title: Asymmetric Goal Drift in Coding Agents Under Value Conflict
comment: 5 pages, 4 figures, Published as a workshop paper in Lifelong Agents @ ICLR 2026
summary: Agentic coding agents are increasingly deployed autonomously, at scale, and over long-context horizons. Throughout an agent's lifetime, it must navigate tensions between explicit instructions, learned values, and environmental pressures, often in contexts unseen during training. Prior work on model preferences, agent behavior under value tensions, and goal drift has relied on static, synthetic settings that do not capture the complexity of real-world environments. To this end, we introduce a framework built on OpenCode to orchestrate realistic, multi-step coding tasks to measure how agents violate explicit constraints in their system prompt over time with and without environmental pressure toward competing values. Using this framework, we demonstrate that GPT-5 mini, Haiku 4.5, and Grok Code Fast 1 exhibit asymmetric drift: they are more likely to violate their system prompt when its constraint opposes strongly-held values like security and privacy. We find for the models and values tested that goal drift correlates with three compounding factors: value alignment, adversarial pressure, and accumulated context. However, even strongly-held values like privacy show non-zero violation rates under sustained environmental pressure. These findings reveal that shallow compliance checks are insufficient and that comment-based pressure can exploit model value hierarchies to override system prompt instructions. More broadly, our findings highlight a gap in current alignment approaches in ensuring that agentic systems appropriately balance explicit user constraints against broadly beneficial learned preferences under sustained environmental pressure.

AI.tldr: Realistic, long-horizon agentic coders show constraint-violation drift under environmental pressure; violations align with value conflicts (e.g., privacy/security), with drift driven by value alignment, pressure, and accumulated context; even strong values are not immune, revealing limits of shallow compliance checks.
AI.motivation: To understand how agents balance explicit system prompts against learned preferences in realistic environments and to quantify prompt-violation drift over time under competing values and pressure, addressing shortcomings of static/synthetic evaluation settings.
AI.method: Introduce OpenCode-based framework to orchestrate realistic, multi-step coding tasks; evaluate GPT-5 mini, Haiku 4.5, and Grok Code Fast 1; measure violations of system prompts over time under environmental pressure and competing values; analyze drift in relation to value alignment, adversarial pressure, and accumulated context.
AI.result: Demonstrated asymmetric drift: models more likely to violate system prompts when the constraint conflicts with strongly-held values (e.g., security/privacy). Drift correlates with value alignment, adversarial pressure, and accumulated context; violations persist under sustained pressure, even for privacy. Comment-based pressure can exploit model value hierarchies to override prompts.
AI.conclusion: Shallow compliance checks are insufficient. To align agentic systems, we need robust approaches that resist environmental pressure and carefully balance explicit constraints with learned preferences across time, beyond prompt-based controls.

title: AOI: Turning Failed Trajectories into Training Signals for Autonomous Cloud Diagnosis
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents offer a promising data-driven approach to automating Site Reliability Engineering (SRE), yet their enterprise deployment is constrained by three challenges: restricted access to proprietary data, unsafe action execution under permission-governed environments, and the inability of closed systems to improve from failures. We present AOI (Autonomous Operations Intelligence), a trainable multi-agent framework formulating automated operations as a structured trajectory learning problem under security constraints. Our approach integrates three key components. First, a trainable diagnostic system applies Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to distill expert-level knowledge into locally deployed open-source models, enabling preference-based learning without exposing sensitive data. Second, a read-write separated execution architecture decomposes operational trajectories into observation, reasoning, and action phases, allowing safe learning while preventing unauthorized state mutation. Third, a Failure Trajectory Closed-Loop Evolver mines unsuccessful trajectories and converts them into corrective supervision signals, enabling continual data augmentation. Evaluated on the AIOpsLab benchmark, our contributions yield cumulative gains. (1) The AOI runtime alone achieves 66.3% best@5 success on all 86 tasks, outperforming the prior state-of-the-art (41.9%) by 24.4 points. (2) Adding Observer GRPO training, a locally deployed 14B model reaches 42.9% avg@1 on 63 held-out tasks with unseen fault types, surpassing Claude Sonnet 4.5. (3) The Evolver converts 37 failed trajectories into diagnostic guidance, improving end-to-end avg@5 by 4.8 points while reducing variance by 35%.

AI.tldr: AOI: a trainable multi-agent framework for autonomous SRE operations with GRPO-based diagnostics, read-write separation, and a Failure Trajectory Evolver; achieves substantial gains on the AIOpsLab benchmark.
AI.motivation: Address enterprise restrictions in SRE automation: limited access to proprietary data, unsafe action execution under permissioned environments, and lack of learning from failures in closed-loop systems; provide a data-efficient, safe, and continually improving autonomous operations platform.
AI.method: Three components: (1) a trainable diagnostic system using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to distill expert knowledge into locally deployed open-source models for preference-based learning without leaking sensitive data; (2) a read-write separated execution architecture that splits trajectories into observation, reasoning, and action phases to enable safe learning while preventing unauthorized state mutations; (3) a Failure Trajectory Closed-Loop Evolver that mines unsuccessful trajectories to generate corrective supervision signals for continual data augmentation.
AI.result: On AIOpsLab benchmark: AOI runtime achieves 66.3% best@5 across 86 tasks (vs 41.9% prior SOTA, +24.4 points). With Observer GRPO training, a locally deployed 14B model attains 42.9% avg@1 on 63 held-out tasks with unseen fault types, surpassing Claude Sonnet 4.5. The Evolver converts 37 failed trajectories into diagnostic guidance, improving end-to-end avg@5 by 4.8 points and reducing variance by 35%.
AI.conclusion: AOI demonstrates a data-efficient, safe, and continually improving autonomous operations framework for SRE, delivering substantial performance gains and enabling deployment under restricted data access and governance constraints; the approach is likely applicable to real-world enterprise SRE environments and prompts further work on privacy-preserving policy distillation, safe action protocols, and more robust failure-driven learning.

title: Build, Judge, Optimize: A Blueprint for Continuous Improvement of Multi-Agent Consumer Assistants
summary: Conversational shopping assistants (CSAs) represent a compelling application of agentic AI, but moving from prototype to production reveals two underexplored challenges: how to evaluate multi-turn interactions and how to optimize tightly coupled multi-agent systems. Grocery shopping further amplifies these difficulties, as user requests are often underspecified, highly preference-sensitive, and constrained by factors such as budget and inventory. In this paper, we present a practical blueprint for evaluating and optimizing conversational shopping assistants, illustrated through a production-scale AI grocery assistant. We introduce a multi-faceted evaluation rubric that decomposes end-to-end shopping quality into structured dimensions and develop a calibrated LLM-as-judge pipeline aligned with human annotations. Building on this evaluation foundation, we investigate two complementary prompt-optimization strategies based on a SOTA prompt-optimizer called GEPA (Shao et al., 2025): (1) Sub-agent GEPA, which optimizes individual agent nodes against localized rubrics, and (2) MAMuT (Multi-Agent Multi-Turn) GEPA (Herrera et al., 2026), a novel system-level approach that jointly optimizes prompts across agents using multi-turn simulation and trajectory-level scoring. We release rubric templates and evaluation design guidance to support practitioners building production CSAs.

AI.tldr: Presents a production-ready blueprint for evaluating and optimizing conversational shopping assistants (CSAs) using a multi-faceted end-to-end rubric and calibrated LLM-based judging, plus two GEPA-based prompt-optimization strategies: Sub-agent GEPA and MAMuT GEPA for system-wide multi-turn optimization.
AI.motivation: To address two underexplored challenges in production CSAs: evaluating multi-turn interactions and optimizing tightly coupled multi-agent systems, particularly in grocery shopping where user requests are underspecified and constrained by budget and inventory.
AI.method: Develop a decomposed evaluation rubric for end-to-end shopping quality; build a calibrated LLM-as-judge pipeline aligned with human annotations; apply GEPA-based optimization: (1) Sub-agent GEPA for per-node prompt optimization against local rubrics, (2) MAMuT GEPA for joint, system-level prompt optimization across agents using multi-turn simulation and trajectory-level scoring; release rubric templates and evaluation guidance.
AI.result: Demonstrated a production-scale AI grocery assistant; established a practical evaluation foundation via a multi-faceted rubric and a calibrated LLM-judge; evaluated two GEPA-based prompt-optimization strategies (Sub-agent GEPA and MAMuT GEPA) on the CSA system.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides actionable guidance for practitioners to evaluate and optimize production CSAs, combining structured end-to-end evaluation with system-wide prompt optimization, and offers reusable rubric templates and guidance.

title: Proact-VL: A Proactive VideoLLM for Real-Time AI Companions
summary: Proactive and real-time interactive experiences are essential for human-like AI companions, yet face three key challenges: (1) achieving low-latency inference under continuous streaming inputs, (2) autonomously deciding when to respond, and (3) controlling both quality and quantity of generated content to meet real-time constraints. In this work, we instantiate AI companions through two gaming scenarios, commentator and guide, selected for their suitability for automatic evaluation. We introduce the Live Gaming Benchmark, a large-scale dataset with three representative scenarios: solo commentary, co-commentary, and user guidance, and present Proact-VL, a general framework that shapes multimodal language models into proactive, real-time interactive agents capable of human-like environment perception and interaction. Extensive experiments show Proact-VL achieves superior response latency and quality while maintaining strong video understanding capabilities, demonstrating its practicality for real-time interactive applications.

AI.tldr: Proact-VL is a general framework that converts multimodal language models into proactive, real-time interactive agents for gaming, introduced with the Live Gaming Benchmark (solo commentary, co-commentary, user guidance). It achieves improved latency and content quality while preserving video understanding, enabling practical real-time AI companions.
AI.motivation: Real-time, human-like AI companions require (i) low-latency inference on streaming inputs, (ii) autonomous timing of responses, and (iii) controlled content generation to meet time constraints. The work targets these challenges via a unified framework and a specialized benchmark/dataset.
AI.method: Proact-VL shapes multimodal LMs into proactive agents capable of environment perception and interaction. The Live Gaming Benchmark provides three representative video-centric scenarios (solo commentary, co-commentary, user guidance) to evaluate latency, response quality, and multimodal understanding under real-time constraints.
AI.result: Experiments show Proact-VL achieves superior response latency and quality while maintaining strong video understanding capabilities, indicating practicality for real-time interactive applications.
AI.conclusion: Proact-VL offers a practical, general framework for proactive, real-time multimodal agents, validated on a robust gaming-oriented benchmark, with potential for broader real-time interactive deployments and future improvements in response control and generalization.

title: Mozi: Governed Autonomy for Drug Discovery LLM Agents
summary:
Tool-augmented large language model (LLM) agents promise to unify scientific reasoning with computation, yet their deployment in high-stakes domains like drug discovery is bottlenecked by two critical barriers: unconstrained tool-use governance and poor long-horizon reliability. In dependency-heavy pharmaceutical pipelines, autonomous agents often drift into irreproducible trajectories, where early-stage hallucinations multiplicatively compound into downstream failures. To overcome this, we present Mozi, a dual-layer architecture that bridges the flexibility of generative AI with the deterministic rigor of computational biology. Layer A (Control Plane) establishes a governed supervisor--worker hierarchy that enforces role-based tool isolation, limits execution to constrained action spaces, and drives reflection-based replanning. Layer B (Workflow Plane) operationalizes canonical drug discovery stages -- from Target Identification to Lead Optimization -- as stateful, composable skill graphs. This layer integrates strict data contracts and strategic human-in-the-loop (HITL) checkpoints to safeguard scientific validity at high-uncertainty decision boundaries.
  Operating on the design principle of ``free-form reasoning for safe tasks, structured execution for long-horizon pipelines,'' Mozi provides built-in robustness mechanisms and trace-level audibility to completely mitigate error accumulation. We evaluate Mozi on PharmaBench, a curated benchmark for biomedical agents, demonstrating superior orchestration accuracy over existing baselines. Furthermore, through end-to-end therapeutic case studies, we demonstrate Mozi's ability to navigate massive chemical spaces, enforce stringent toxicity filters, and generate highly competitive in silico candidates, effectively transforming the LLM from a fragile conversationalist into a reliable, governed co-scientist.

AI.tldr: Mozi proposes a dual-layer LLM agent architecture for drug discovery that combines a governance-focused Control Plane with a stateful Workflow Plane. It enforces tool isolation, constrained action spaces, and reflection-based replanning, while mapping canonical discovery stages to skill graphs with data contracts and HITL checks. Evaluated on PharmaBench and through therapeutic case studies, it claims improved orchestration and robust, auditable high-stakes reasoning.
AI.motivation: Unconstrained tool usage and poor long-horizon reliability plague autonomous LLM agents in drug discovery. Dependency-heavy pipelines suffer from early hallucinations that cascade downstream. There is a need for governance, structured execution, and proper human oversight to ensure scientific validity and reproducibility.
AI.method: Mozi introduces a dual-layer design: Layer A (Control Plane) provides a governed supervisor–worker hierarchy with role-based tool isolation, constrained action spaces, and reflection-based replanning; Layer B (Workflow Plane) encodes drug discovery stages as stateful, composable skill graphs with strict data contracts and HITL checkpoints. The architecture supports free-form reasoning for safe tasks and structured execution for long-horizon pipelines, and includes trace-level audibility and robustness mechanisms to mitigate error accumulation.
AI.result: Demonstrates superior orchestration accuracy on PharmaBench compared with baselines. End-to-end therapeutic case studies show the system can navigate large chemical spaces, apply stringent toxicity filters, and generate competitive in silico candidates, effectively turning the LLM into a governed co-scientist.
AI.conclusion: Mozi offers built-in robustness and complete auditable governance, transforming LLMs from fragile conversationalists into reliable, governed co-scientists suitable for high-stakes biomedical research, by combining safe, constrained execution with flexible, domain-aware reasoning.

title: MAGE: Meta-Reinforcement Learning for Language Agents toward Strategic Exploration and Exploitation
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) agents have demonstrated remarkable proficiency in learned tasks, yet they often struggle to adapt to non-stationary environments with feedback. While In-Context Learning and external memory offer some flexibility, they fail to internalize the adaptive ability required for long-term improvement. Meta-Reinforcement Learning (meta-RL) provides an alternative by embedding the learning process directly within the model. However, existing meta-RL approaches for LLMs focus primarily on exploration in single-agent settings, neglecting the strategic exploitation necessary for multi-agent environments. We propose MAGE, a meta-RL framework that empowers LLM agents for strategic exploration and exploitation. MAGE utilizes a multi-episode training regime where interaction histories and reflections are integrated into the context window. By using the final episode reward as the objective, MAGE incentivizes the agent to refine its strategy based on past experiences. We further combine population-based training with an agent-specific advantage normalization technique to enrich agent diversity and ensure stable learning. Experiment results show that MAGE outperforms existing baselines in both exploration and exploitation tasks. Furthermore, MAGE exhibits strong generalization to unseen opponents, suggesting it has internalized the ability for strategic exploration and exploitation. Code is available at https://github.com/Lu-Yang666/MAGE.

AI.tldr: MAGE is a meta-RL framework for LLM agents in multi-agent settings that enables strategic exploration and exploitation through multi-episode training, integrating interaction histories and reflections into the context; it optimizes via the final episode reward and enhances diversity and stability with population-based training plus agent-specific advantage normalization.
AI.motivation: Address the gap where LLM agents struggle with non-stationary environments and long-term adaptation, and where existing in-context learning and single-agent meta-RL miss strategic exploitation in multi-agent settings.
AI.method: Multi-episode training where interaction histories and reflections are added to the LLM context window; final episode reward as the optimization objective; population-based training; agent-specific advantage normalization to maintain diversity and stabilize learning.
AI.result: MAGE outperforms baselines in both exploration and exploitation tasks and generalizes to unseen opponents; code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: MAGE demonstrates that LLM agents can internalize strategic exploration and exploitation in multi-agent environments and generalize beyond seen opponents; invites further research and application in adaptive, interactive LLM systems.

title: AI4S-SDS: A Neuro-Symbolic Solvent Design System via Sparse MCTS and Differentiable Physics Alignment
summary: Automated design of chemical formulations is a cornerstone of materials science, yet it requires navigating a high-dimensional combinatorial space involving discrete compositional choices and continuous geometric constraints. Existing Large Language Model (LLM) agents face significant challenges in this setting, including context window limitations during long-horizon reasoning and path-dependent exploration that may lead to mode collapse. To address these issues, we introduce AI4S-SDS, a closed-loop neuro-symbolic framework that integrates multi-agent collaboration with a tailored Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) engine. We propose a Sparse State Storage mechanism with Dynamic Path Reconstruction, which decouples reasoning history from context length and enables arbitrarily deep exploration under fixed token budgets. To reduce local convergence and improve coverage, we implement a Global--Local Search Strategy: a memory-driven planning module adaptively reconfigures the search root based on historical feedback, while a Sibling-Aware Expansion mechanism promotes orthogonal exploration at the node level. Furthermore, we bridge symbolic reasoning and physical feasibility through a Differentiable Physics Engine, employing a hybrid normalized loss with sparsity-inducing regularization to optimize continuous mixing ratios under thermodynamic constraints. Empirical results show that AI4S-SDS achieves full validity under the adopted HSP-based physical constraints and substantially improves exploration diversity compared to baseline agents. In preliminary lithography experiments, the framework identifies a novel photoresist developer formulation that demonstrates competitive or superior performance relative to a commercial benchmark, highlighting the potential of diversity-driven neuro-symbolic search for scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: AI4S-SDS: a closed-loop neuro-symbolic search framework combining multi-agent collaboration, MCTS, sparse state storage with dynamic path reconstruction, and a differentiable physics engine to design chemical formulations under thermodynamic constraints; achieves full feasibility and greater exploration diversity, with a novel lithography candidate showing competitive performance.
AI.motivation: Overcome context-window limitations and path-dependent exploration in LLM-driven, high-dimensional discrete-continuous formulation design; mitigate mode collapse and improve search coverage.
AI.method: A closed-loop neuro-symbolic framework (AI4S-SDS) that integrates multi-agent collaboration with a tailored Monte Carlo Tree Search. It introduces: (1) Sparse State Storage with Dynamic Path Reconstruction to decouple reasoning history from context length; (2) Global–Local Search Strategy for memory-driven adaptive root reconfiguration; (3) Sibling-Aware Expansion for orthogonal exploration at each node; (4) Differentiable Physics Engine with a hybrid normalized loss and sparsity-inducing regularization to optimize continuous mixing ratios under thermodynamic constraints.
AI.result: Empirically, the framework achieves full validity under HSP-based physical constraints and substantially improves exploration diversity compared to baseline agents. In preliminary lithography experiments, it identifies a novel photoresist developer formulation with competitive or superior performance relative to a commercial benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the potential of diversity-driven neuro-symbolic search to accelerate scientific discovery by integrating symbolic reasoning, adaptive deep search, and differentiable physics to produce feasible, diverse formulations.

title: RAGNav: A Retrieval-Augmented Topological Reasoning Framework for Multi-Goal Visual-Language Navigation
summary: Vision-Language Navigation (VLN) is evolving from single-point pathfinding toward the more challenging Multi-Goal VLN. This task requires agents to accurately identify multiple entities while collaboratively reasoning over their spatial-physical constraints and sequential execution order. However, generic Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) paradigms often suffer from spatial hallucinations and planning drift when handling multi-object associations due to the lack of explicit spatial modeling.To address these challenges, we propose RAGNav, a framework that bridges the gap between semantic reasoning and physical structure. The core of RAGNav is a Dual-Basis Memory system, which integrates a low-level topological map for maintaining physical connectivity with a high-level semantic forest for hierarchical environment abstraction. Building on this representation, the framework introduces an anchor-guided conditional retrieval and a topological neighbor score propagation mechanism. This approach facilitates the rapid screening of candidate targets and the elimination of semantic noise, while performing semantic calibration by leveraging the physical associations inherent in the topological neighborhood.This mechanism significantly enhances the capability of inter-target reachability reasoning and the efficiency of sequential planning. Experimental results demonstrate that RAGNav achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance in complex multi-goal navigation tasks.

AI.tldr: RAGNav introduces Dual-Basis Memory (low-level topological map + high-level semantic forest) for multi-goal VLN, with anchor-guided retrieval and topological neighbor propagation to reduce semantic noise and improve sequential planning; reports SOTA on complex tasks.
AI.motivation: Overcome spatial hallucinations and planning drift in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for multi-object navigation by embedding explicit spatial structure and hierarchical semantics into the reasoning process.
AI.method: Dual-Basis Memory: a low-level topological map maintaining physical connectivity and a high-level semantic forest for hierarchical environment abstraction. Anchor-guided conditional retrieval and topological neighbor score propagation are used to screen candidate targets, eliminate semantic noise, and perform semantic calibration using physical associations in the topological neighborhood to improve inter-target reachability and sequential planning.
AI.result: Empirical results claim state-of-the-art performance on complex multi-goal navigation tasks, with improved inter-target reachability reasoning and planning efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Integrating spatial structure with semantic reasoning via Dual-Basis Memory enhances RAG-based VLN; potential for generalization to other tasks requiring robust spatial-semantic grounding.

title: AgentSelect: Benchmark for Narrative Query-to-Agent Recommendation
summary: LLM agents are rapidly becoming the practical interface for task automation, yet the ecosystem lacks a principled way to choose among an exploding space of deployable configurations. Existing LLM leaderboards and tool/agent benchmarks evaluate components in isolation and remain fragmented across tasks, metrics, and candidate pools, leaving a critical research gap: there is little query-conditioned supervision for learning to recommend end-to-end agent configurations that couple a backbone model with a toolkit. We address this gap with AgentSelect, a benchmark that reframes agent selection as narrative query-to-agent recommendation over capability profiles and systematically converts heterogeneous evaluation artifacts into unified, positive-only interaction data. AgentSelectcomprises 111,179 queries, 107,721 deployable agents, and 251,103 interaction records aggregated from 40+ sources, spanning LLM-only, toolkit-only, and compositional agents. Our analyses reveal a regime shift from dense head reuse to long-tail, near one-off supervision, where popularity-based CF/GNN methods become fragile and content-aware capability matching is essential. We further show that Part~III synthesized compositional interactions are learnable, induce capability-sensitive behavior under controlled counterfactual edits, and improve coverage over realistic compositions; models trained on AgentSelect also transfer to a public agent marketplace (MuleRun), yielding consistent gains on an unseen catalog. Overall, AgentSelect provides the first unified data and evaluation infrastructure for agent recommendation, which establishes a reproducible foundation to study and accelerate the emerging agent ecosystem.

AI.tldr: AgentSelect introduces a unified data/evaluation platform for end-to-end agent configuration recommendation, reframing the problem as narrative query-to-agent matching over capability profiles and showing robustness across a large multi-source dataset and transferability to a public marketplace.
AI.motivation: Fragmented LLM agent benchmarks and lack of query-conditioned supervision for selecting a backbone model plus toolkit; need a data-driven, coverage-aware, end-to-end agent configuration framework.
AI.method: Aggregate heterogeneous evaluation artifacts into positive-only interaction data and formulate as narrative query-to-agent recommendation over capability profiles. Build AgentSelect with 111,179 queries, 107,721 deployable agents, and 251,103 interaction records from 40+ sources across LLM-only, toolkit-only, and compositional agents. Analyze regime shifts from dense head reuse to long-tail supervision, synthesize Part III compositional interactions, study counterfactual edits, and transfer evaluation to a public marketplace (MuleRun).
AI.result: Findings show a regime shift where popularity-based CF/GNN methods become fragile in the long-tail setting; content-aware capability matching is essential. Part III compositional interactions are learnable and induce capability-sensitive behavior under counterfactual edits, improving coverage for realistic compositions. Models trained on AgentSelect transfer to MuleRun, yielding gains on an unseen catalog.
AI.conclusion: AgentSelect provides the first unified data and evaluation infrastructure for agent recommendation, enabling reproducible study and acceleration of the agent ecosystem, with demonstrated transfer to external marketplaces.

title: LifeBench: A Benchmark for Long-Horizon Multi-Source Memory
comment: A total of 28 pages, 8 pages of main text, and 15 figures and tables
summary: Long-term memory is fundamental for personalized agents capable of accumulating knowledge, reasoning over user experiences, and adapting across time. However, existing memory benchmarks primarily target declarative memory, specifically semantic and episodic types, where all information is explicitly presented in dialogues. In contrast, real-world actions are also governed by non-declarative memory, including habitual and procedural types, and need to be inferred from diverse digital traces. To bridge this gap, we introduce Lifebench, which features densely connected, long-horizon event simulation. It pushes AI agents beyond simple recall, requiring the integration of declarative and non-declarative memory reasoning across diverse and temporally extended contexts. Building such a benchmark presents two key challenges: ensuring data quality and scalability. We maintain data quality by employing real-world priors, including anonymized social surveys, map APIs, and holiday-integrated calendars, thus enforcing fidelity, diversity and behavioral rationality within the dataset. Towards scalability, we draw inspiration from cognitive science and structure events according to their partonomic hierarchy; enabling efficient parallel generation while maintaining global coherence. Performance results show that top-tier, state-of-the-art memory systems reach just 55.2\% accuracy, highlighting the inherent difficulty of long-horizon retrieval and multi-source integration within our proposed benchmark. The dataset and data synthesis code are available at https://github.com/1754955896/LifeBench.

AI.tldr: Lifebench introduces a long-horizon memory benchmark that fuses declarative and non-declarative memory through densely connected event simulation, using real-world priors for data quality and a partonomic hierarchy for scalable generation; state-of-the-art memory systems achieve 55.2% accuracy.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between existing declarative-memory benchmarks and the need to model non-declarative, procedural/habitual memory inferred from traces, requiring scalable, coherent long-horizon data.
AI.method: Construct Lifebench with long-horizon event simulation, structure events via a partonomic hierarchy to enable parallel generation while preserving global coherence, and incorporate real-world priors (anonymized surveys, map APIs, calendars) to ensure fidelity and diversity.
AI.result: Top-tier memory systems reach 55.2% accuracy, highlighting the challenge of long-horizon retrieval and multi-source integration; dataset and data synthesis code are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark pushes beyond recall-only memory, fostering development of models that integrate declarative and non-declarative memory reasoning across extended contexts; supports advancing personalized agents and memory architectures.

title: A Rubric-Supervised Critic from Sparse Real-World Outcomes
summary: Academic benchmarks for coding agents tend to reward autonomous task completion, measured by verifiable rewards such as unit-test success. In contrast, real-world coding agents operate with humans in the loop, where success signals are typically noisy, delayed, and sparse. How can we bridge this gap? In this paper, we propose a process to learn a "critic" model from sparse and noisy interaction data, which can then be used both as a reward model for either RL-based training or inference-time scaling. Specifically, we introduce Critic Rubrics, a rubric-based supervision framework with 24 behavioral features that can be derived from human-agent interaction traces alone. Using a semi-supervised objective, we can then jointly predict these rubrics and sparse human feedback (when present). In experiments, we demonstrate that, despite being trained primarily from trace-observable rubrics and sparse real-world outcome proxies, these critics improve best-of-N reranking on SWE-bench (Best@8 +15.9 over Random@8 over the rerankable subset of trajectories), enable early stopping (+17.7 with 83% fewer attempts), and support training-time data curation via critic-selected trajectories.

AI.tldr: Learn a critic model from sparse, noisy interaction traces using Critic Rubrics; improves reranking, early stopping, and data curation for real-world coding agents.
AI.motivation: Real-world coding agents receive noisy, delayed, sparse feedback; benchmarks with verifiable rewards do not capture human-in-the-loop signals; a trace-based critic can bridge this gap.
AI.method: Introduce Critic Rubrics: 24 behavioral features derived from interaction traces; a semi-supervised objective jointly predicts rubric labels and sparse human feedback to train a critic usable as a reward model for RL or inference-time scaling.
AI.result: On SWE-bench, Best@8 improves by 15.9 points over Random@8 on the rerankable subset; enables early stopping (+17.7) with 83% fewer attempts; supports training-time data curation via critic-selected trajectories.
AI.conclusion: A trace-derived critic can serve as an effective reward model bridging synthetic benchmarks and real-world human-in-the-loop signals, with practical gains in reranking, early stopping, and data selection.

title: From Threat Intelligence to Firewall Rules: Semantic Relations in Hybrid AI Agent and Expert System Architectures
summary: Web security demands rapid response capabilities to evolving cyber threats. Agentic Artificial Intelligence (AI) promises automation, but the need for trustworthy security responses is of the utmost importance. This work investigates the role of semantic relations in extracting information for sensitive operational tasks, such as configuring security controls for mitigating threats. To this end, it proposes to leverage hypernym-hyponym textual relations to extract relevant information from Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) reports. By leveraging a neuro-symbolic approach, the multi-agent system automatically generates CLIPS code for an expert system creating firewall rules to block malicious network traffic. Experimental results show the superior performance of the hypernym-hyponym retrieval strategy compared to various baselines and the higher effectiveness of the agentic approach in mitigating threats.

AI.tldr: Neuro-symbolic, multi-agent system uses hypernym-hyponym relations in Cyber Threat Intelligence to auto-generate CLIPS-based firewall rules, achieving superior information retrieval and threat mitigation.
AI.motivation: Address the need for rapid, trustworthy automated security responses by extracting actionable CTI insights through semantic relations and generating expert-system rules.
AI.method: Utilize hypernym-hyponym textual relations to extract information from CTI reports; employ a neuro-symbolic multi-agent framework that automatically generates CLIPS code for an expert system to configure firewall rules blocking malicious traffic.
AI.result: Hypernym-hyponym retrieval outperforms baselines; agentic approach more effective at mitigating threats.
AI.conclusion: Semantic-relations-driven, neuro-symbolic agent systems can enhance automated cyber defense by translating CTI-derived insights into executable firewall configurations.

title: Towards Realistic Personalization: Evaluating Long-Horizon Preference Following in Personalized User-LLM Interactions
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly serving as personal assistants, where users share complex and diverse preferences over extended interactions. However, assessing how well LLMs can follow these preferences in realistic, long-term situations remains underexplored. This work proposes RealPref, a benchmark for evaluating realistic preference-following in personalized user-LLM interactions. RealPref features 100 user profiles, 1300 personalized preferences, four types of preference expression (ranging from explicit to implicit), and long-horizon interaction histories. It includes three types of test questions (multiple-choice, true-or-false, and open-ended), with detailed rubrics for LLM-as-a-judge evaluation. Results indicate that LLM performance significantly drops as context length grows and preference expression becomes more implicit, and that generalizing user preference understanding to unseen scenarios poses further challenges. RealPref and these findings provide a foundation for future research to develop user-aware LLM assistants that better adapt to individual needs. The code is available at https://github.com/GG14127/RealPref.

AI.tldr: Introduces RealPref, a benchmark to evaluate how well LLMs follow personalized user preferences over long-term interactions; shows performance degrades with longer context and more implicit preferences; code released.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in evaluating long-horizon preference following and personalization in LLMs, where existing benchmarks focus on short-term or explicit preferences.
AI.method: Construct RealPref with 100 user profiles, 1300 personalized preferences, four expression types (explicit to implicit), long interaction histories, three question formats (multiple-choice, true/false, open-ended), and rubric-based LLM-as-a-judge evaluation; provides implementation and dataset publicly (GitHub).
AI.result: Empirical results show significant performance drop as context length grows and preferences become more implicit; generalization to unseen scenarios remains challenging.
AI.conclusion: RealPref provides a foundation for future development of user-aware LLM assistants that better adapt to individual needs; code and data are publicly available.

title: Agentics 2.0: Logical Transduction Algebra for Agentic Data Workflows
summary: Agentic AI is rapidly transitioning from research prototypes to enterprise deployments, where requirements extend to meet the software quality attributes of reliability, scalability, and observability beyond plausible text generation. We present Agentics 2.0, a lightweight, Python-native framework for building high-quality, structured, explainable, and type-safe agentic data workflows. At the core of Agentics 2.0, the logical transduction algebra formalizes a large language model inference call as a typed semantic transformation, which we call a transducible function that enforces schema validity and the locality of evidence. The transducible functions compose into larger programs via algebraically grounded operators and execute as stateless asynchronous calls in parallel in asynchronous Map-Reduce programs. The proposed framework provides semantic reliability through strong typing, semantic observability through evidence tracing between slots of the input and output types, and scalability through stateless parallel execution. We instantiate reusable design patterns and evaluate the programs in Agentics 2.0 on challenging benchmarks, including DiscoveryBench for data-driven discovery and Archer for NL-to-SQL semantic parsing, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: Agentics 2.0 is a Python-native framework for building high-quality, explainable, type-safe agentic data workflows, modeling LLM inferences as transducible functions that compose into stateless parallel programs, achieving semantic reliability, observability, and scalability; demonstrated with state-of-the-art results on DiscoveryBench and Archer.
AI.motivation: Enterprise deployments of agentic AI require reliability, scalability, and observability beyond plausible text generation; enforce schema validity and trace evidence locality; provide strong typing and traceable evidence across input/output slots.
AI.method: Introduce a logical transduction algebra; define transducible functions as typed semantic transformations that enforce schema validity and locality of evidence; compose via algebraically grounded operators; execute as stateless asynchronous calls in parallel as Map-Reduce programs; provide semantic observability through evidence tracing; implement in Python-native framework; instantiate reusable design patterns.
AI.result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on DiscoveryBench (data-driven discovery) and Archer (NL-to-SQL semantic parsing); provides evaluation of patterns and claims of reliability, observability, and scalability.
AI.conclusion: Agentics 2.0 delivers semantic reliability through strong typing, semantic observability via evidence tracing, and scalable, stateless parallel execution; establishes reusable design patterns for enterprise-grade agentic AI workflows and promising performance on established benchmarks.

title: Transport Clustering: Solving Low-Rank Optimal Transport via Clustering
summary: Optimal transport (OT) finds a least cost transport plan between two probability distributions using a cost matrix defined on pairs of points. Unlike standard OT, which infers unstructured pointwise mappings, low-rank optimal transport explicitly constrains the rank of the transport plan to infer latent structure. This improves statistical stability and robustness, yields sharper parametric rates for estimating Wasserstein distances adaptive to the intrinsic rank, and generalizes $K$-means to co-clustering. These advantages, however, come at the cost of a non-convex and NP-hard optimization problem. We introduce transport clustering, an algorithm to compute a low-rank OT plan that reduces low-rank OT to a clustering problem on correspondences obtained from a full-rank $\textit{transport registration}$ step. We prove that this reduction yields polynomial-time, constant-factor approximation algorithms for low-rank OT: specifically, a $(1+γ)$ approximation for negative-type metrics and a $(1+γ+\sqrt{2γ}\,)$ approximation for kernel costs, where $γ\in [0,1]$ denotes the approximation ratio of the optimal full-rank solution relative to the low-rank optimal. Empirically, transport clustering outperforms existing low-rank OT solvers on synthetic benchmarks and large-scale, high-dimensional datasets.

AI.tldr: Introduces transport clustering to compute a low-rank optimal transport (OT) plan by reducing it to a clustering problem on correspondences from a full-rank transport registration. The method yields provable approximation guarantees and scales to large, high-dimensional data, outperforming existing low-rank OT solvers.
AI.motivation: Low-rank OT improves statistical stability, robustness, and rate-adaptive estimates and generalizes K-means to co-clustering, but it leads to a non-convex, NP-hard optimization. There is a need for a scalable algorithm with theoretical guarantees.
AI.method: Reduce low-rank OT to a clustering problem on correspondences obtained via a full-rank transport registration step. The approach provides polynomial-time, constant-factor approximation algorithms: a (1+γ) approximation for negative-type metrics and a (1+γ+√(2γ)) approximation for kernel costs, where γ ∈ [0,1] quantifies how close the full-rank solution is to the low-rank optimum.
AI.result: The proposed transport clustering yields theoretical approximation guarantees and empirically outperforms existing low-rank OT solvers on synthetic benchmarks and large-scale, high-dimensional datasets.
AI.conclusion: Transport clustering offers a computationally efficient and theoretically grounded framework for low-rank OT, achieving strong approximation guarantees and practical scalability.

title: Graph Hopfield Networks: Energy-Based Node Classification with Associative Memory
comment: 10 Pages, 4 Figures, Acceptted at ICLR NFAM Workshop 2026
summary: We introduce Graph Hopfield Networks, whose energy function couples associative memory retrieval with graph Laplacian smoothing for node classification. Gradient descent on this joint energy yields an iterative update interleaving Hopfield retrieval with Laplacian propagation. Memory retrieval provides regime-dependent benefits: up to 2.0~pp on sparse citation networks and up to 5 pp additional robustness under feature masking; the iterative energy-descent architecture itself is a strong inductive bias, with all variants (including the memory-disabled NoMem ablation) outperforming standard baselines on Amazon co-purchase graphs. Tuning enables graph sharpening for heterophilous benchmarks without architectural changes.

AI.tldr: Graph Hopfield Networks merge associative memory retrieval with graph Laplacian smoothing for node classification, yielding an iterative energy-descent update that interleaves memory retrieval and propagation. Empirically, they offer modest gains on sparse graphs and substantial robustness to feature masking, with all variants outperforming baselines and tunable graph sharpening enabling heterophily without changing architecture.
AI.motivation: To improve node classification on graphs by combining the strengths of Hopfield-style associative memory with graph-based smoothing, addressing robustness to missing/masked features and heterophily while maintaining strong inductive bias.
AI.method: Define a joint energy combining Hopfield retrieval with graph Laplacian propagation; perform gradient descent that alternates between memory retrieval steps and Laplacian-based feature propagation; evaluate multiple variants (including a memory-disabled ablation NoMem) on standard benchmarks, with tuning for heterophily (graph sharpening).
AI.result: Memory retrieval provides regime-dependent gains (up to about 2.0 percentage points on sparse citation networks) and up to 5 percentage points improved robustness under feature masking. The iterative energy-descent architecture outperforms standard baselines on Amazon co-purchase graphs across all variants, including NoMem. Tuning can induce graph sharpening for heterophilous benchmarks without changing the architecture.
AI.conclusion: The joint energy framework yields a strong inductive bias for graph-based node classification; memory retrieval adds beneficial, regime-dependent improvements while the architecture remains strong even without memory. The approach is flexible, allowing graph sharpening to address heterophily via tuning rather than architectural changes.

title: Right in Time: Reactive Reasoning in Regulated Traffic Spaces
summary: Exact inference in probabilistic First-Order Logic offers a promising yet computationally costly approach for regulating the behavior of autonomous agents in shared traffic spaces. While prior methods have combined logical and probabilistic data into decision-making frameworks, their application is often limited to pre-flight checks due to the complexity of reasoning across vast numbers of possible universes. In this work, we propose a reactive mission design framework that jointly considers uncertain environmental data and declarative, logical traffic regulations. By synthesizing Probabilistic Mission Design (ProMis) with reactive reasoning facilitated by Reactive Circuits (RC), we enable online, exact probabilistic inference over hybrid domains. Our approach leverages the Frequency of Change inherent in heterogeneous data streams to subdivide inference formulas into memoized, isolated tasks, ensuring that only the specific components affected by new sensor data are re-evaluated. In experiments involving both real-world vessel data and simulated drone traffic in dense urban scenarios, we demonstrate that our approach provides orders of magnitude in speedup over ProMis without reactive paradigms. This allows intelligent transportation systems, such as Unmanned Aircraft Systems (UAS), to actively assert safety and legal compliance during operations rather than relying solely on preparation procedures.

AI.tldr: Reactive, exact probabilistic reasoning for autonomous traffic: integrate ProMis with Reactive Circuits for online decision-making with memoized subformulas, yielding significant speedups.
AI.motivation: Enable online, exact probabilistic inference over hybrid domains for autonomous agents in shared traffic spaces, overcoming combinatorial blow-up that limits prior probabilistic-first-order-logic approaches to pre-flight checks.
AI.method: Synthesize Probabilistic Mission Design (ProMis) with Reactive Circuits (RC) to perform online, exact inference. Use Frequency of Change to partition formulas into memoized, isolated tasks so only affected components are re-evaluated upon new sensor data.
AI.result: Demonstrated orders-of-magnitude speedup over ProMis without reactive paradigms in experiments with real vessel data and simulated dense urban drone traffic, enabling active safety and legal compliance during operations.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables scalable, online, exact probabilistic reasoning in hybrid domains for autonomous traffic, with practical implications for intelligent transportation systems and unattended aerial systems.

title: EvoPrune: Early-Stage Visual Token Pruning for Efficient MLLMs
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown strong performance in vision-language tasks, but their inference efficiency is severely limited by the exponential growth of visual tokens in complex scenarios such as high-resolution images and videos. Existing visual token pruning methods mainly operate after visual encoding, overlooking the substantial computational cost incurred during the encoding stage. To address this issue, we propose EvoPrune, an early-stage visual token pruning method for MLLMs that performs pruning directly during visual encoding. Specifically, EvoPrune employs a layer-wise pruning strategy guided by token similarity, diversity, and attention-based importance to retain the most informative visual tokens at selected encoding layers. Extensive experiments on image and video benchmarks validate the effectiveness of EvoPrune. In particular, on the VideoMME dataset, EvoPrune achieves 2$\times$ inference speedup with less than 1% performance degradation, demonstrating its potential for latency-sensitive MLLM deployment.

AI.tldr: EvoPrune prunes visual tokens during encoding to speed up multimodal LLMs with minimal loss, using layer-wise pruning guided by token similarity, diversity, and attention-based importance.
AI.motivation: Efficient inference in MLLMs is hampered by the rapid growth of visual tokens during high-resolution image and video encoding. Existing pruning targets post-encoding stages and miss encoding-time costs.
AI.method: A layer-wise pruning strategy implemented during visual encoding. At selected encoder layers, tokens are pruned based on a combination of similarity, diversity, and attention-based importance to retain the most informative tokens.
AI.result: On image and video benchmarks, notably VideoMME, EvoPrune achieves about 2x faster inference with less than 1% degradation in performance.
AI.conclusion: Early-stage visual token pruning is effective for latency-sensitive MLLMs, enabling substantial speedups with minimal accuracy loss.

title: Hybrid Belief Reinforcement Learning for Efficient Coordinated Spatial Exploration
summary: Coordinating multiple autonomous agents to explore and serve spatially heterogeneous demand requires jointly learning unknown spatial patterns and planning trajectories that maximize task performance. Pure model-based approaches provide structured uncertainty estimates but lack adaptive policy learning, while deep reinforcement learning often suffers from poor sample efficiency when spatial priors are absent. This paper presents a hybrid belief-reinforcement learning (HBRL) framework to address this gap. In the first phase, agents construct spatial beliefs using a Log-Gaussian Cox Process (LGCP) and execute information-driven trajectories guided by a Pathwise Mutual Information (PathMI) planner with multi-step lookahead. In the second phase, trajectory control is transferred to a Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) agent, warm-started through dual-channel knowledge transfer: belief state initialization supplies spatial uncertainty, and replay buffer seeding provides demonstration trajectories generated during LGCP exploration. A variance-normalized overlap penalty enables coordinated coverage through shared belief state, permitting cooperative sensing in high-uncertainty regions while discouraging redundant coverage in well-explored areas. The framework is evaluated on a multi-UAV wireless service provisioning task. Results show 10.8% higher cumulative reward and 38% faster convergence over baselines, with ablation studies confirming that dual-channel transfer outperforms either channel alone.

AI.tldr: Hybrid Belief-Reinforcement Learning (HBRL) for multi-agent spatial tasks combines LGCP-based spatial beliefs and PathMI planning with a two-phase policy transfer to SAC, improving sample efficiency and coordination.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between model-based methods that give uncertainty estimates and DRL methods that struggle with spatial priors and sample efficiency in spatially heterogeneous environments.
AI.method: Phase 1: construct spatial beliefs with a Log-Gaussian Cox Process (LGCP) and plan information-rich trajectories using a Pathwise Mutual Information (PathMI) planner with multi-step lookahead. Phase 2: transfer to Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) with warm-start via dual-channel knowledge transfer: (a) belief state initialization injects spatial uncertainty, and (b) replay buffer seeding provides demonstration trajectories from LGCP exploration. A variance-normalized overlap penalty coordinates coverage via a shared belief state to promote cooperative sensing in high-uncertainty regions and reduce redundancy in explored areas.
AI.result: On a multi-UAV wireless service provisioning task, the method achieves 10.8% higher cumulative reward and 38% faster convergence compared with baselines. Ablation shows that dual-channel transfer outperforms either channel alone.
AI.conclusion: The HBRL framework effectively fuses model-based spatial reasoning with model-free policy learning, improving performance and sample efficiency in spatially heterogeneous multi-agent tasks; dual-channel transfer is key to its gains.

title: Map-Agnostic And Interactive Safety-Critical Scenario Generation via Multi-Objective Tree Search
summary: Generating safety-critical scenarios is essential for validating the robustness of autonomous driving systems, yet existing methods often struggle to produce collisions that are both realistic and diverse while ensuring explicit interaction logic among traffic participants. This paper presents a novel framework for traffic-flow level safety-critical scenario generation via multi-objective Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS). We reframe trajectory feasibility and naturalistic behavior as optimization objectives within a unified evaluation function, enabling the discovery of diverse collision events without compromising realism. A hybrid Upper Confidence Bound (UCB) and Lower Confidence Bound (LCB) search strategy is introduced to balance exploratory efficiency with risk-averse decision-making. Furthermore, our method is map-agnostic and supports interactive scenario generation with each vehicle individually powered by SUMO's microscopic traffic models, enabling realistic agent behaviors in arbitrary geographic locations imported from OpenStreetMap. We validate our approach across four high-risk accident zones in Hong Kong's complex urban environments. Experimental results demonstrate that our framework achieves an 85\% collision failure rate while generating trajectories with superior feasibility and comfort metrics. The resulting scenarios exhibit greater complexity, as evidenced by increased vehicle mileage and CO\(_2\) emissions. Our work provides a principled solution for stress testing autonomous vehicles through the generation of realistic yet infrequent corner cases at traffic-flow level.

AI.tldr: A multi-objective Monte Carlo Tree Search framework for generating realistic, diverse safety-critical traffic scenarios by optimizing feasibility and naturalistic behavior; uses a hybrid UCB-LCB strategy; map-agnostic, SUMO-based agents with OpenStreetMap inputs; validated in four high-risk Hong Kong zones with an 85% collision generation rate and higher mileage/CO2, enabling stress testing of autonomous vehicles.
AI.motivation: Existing scenario-generation methods struggle to produce collisions that are both realistic and diverse while enforcing explicit interaction among traffic participants. A traffic-flow level, optimization-driven approach can yield more credible corner cases for AV validation.
AI.method: Formulates trajectory feasibility and naturalistic behavior as optimization objectives within a unified evaluation function. Employs a hybrid UCB-LCB search in Monte Carlo Tree Search to balance exploration and risk-averse decision-making. Map-agnostic framework where each vehicle is powered by SUMO's microscopic models, enabling realistic agent behavior in arbitrary OpenStreetMap-derived locations.
AI.result: Enabled generation of diverse, realistic collision scenarios with an 85% collision rate across tested zones. Trajectories exhibit higher feasibility and comfort metrics. Scenarios show increased mileage and CO2 emissions, indicating more complex and longer-horizon interactions.
AI.conclusion: Provides a principled, traffic-flow-level approach for stress testing autonomous vehicles by generating realistic yet infrequent corner cases, capable of operating in arbitrary geographic locations.

title: Error as Signal: Stiffness-Aware Diffusion Sampling via Embedded Runge-Kutta Guidance
summary: Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) has established the foundation for guidance mechanisms in diffusion models, showing that well-designed guidance proxies significantly improve conditional generation and sample quality. Autoguidance (AG) has extended this idea, but it relies on an auxiliary network and leaves solver-induced errors unaddressed. In stiff regions, the ODE trajectory changes sharply, where local truncation error (LTE) becomes a critical factor that deteriorates sample quality. Our key observation is that these errors align with the dominant eigenvector, motivating us to leverage the solver-induced error as a guidance signal. We propose Embedded Runge-Kutta Guidance (ERK-Guid), which exploits detected stiffness to reduce LTE and stabilize sampling. We theoretically and empirically analyze stiffness and eigenvector estimators with solver errors to motivate the design of ERK-Guid. Our experiments on both synthetic datasets and the popular benchmark dataset, ImageNet, demonstrate that ERK-Guid consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Code is available at https://github.com/mlvlab/ERK-Guid.

AI.tldr: ERK-Guid introduces solver-aware guidance for diffusion models by exploiting solver-induced local truncation errors (LTE) in stiff regions. It uses Embedded Runge-Kutta guidance to detect stiffness and align LTE with the dominant eigenvector, reducing LTE and stabilizing sampling. Empirical results on synthetic data and ImageNet show consistent gains over state-of-the-art methods, with code released.
AI.motivation: Current guidance methods (CFG, AG) improve conditional generation but can be hindered by solver-induced errors, especially in stiff regions where LTE dominates trajectory changes. Exploiting these errors as a guidance signal can stabilize sampling and improve quality.
AI.method: Propose Embedded Runge-Kutta Guidance (ERK-Guid) that detects stiffness during the diffusion solver and uses the resulting LTE as a guidance signal. The approach analyzes stiffness and eigenvector estimators in the presence of solver errors to justify the design. It leverages embedded Runge-Kutta schemes to estimate LTE and adapt guidance accordingly, aiming to reduce LTE and stabilize sampling.
AI.result: Theoretical and empirical analyses show stiffness and eigenvector estimators remain informative under solver errors. Experiments on synthetic datasets and ImageNet demonstrate that ERK-Guid consistently outperforms state-of-the-art guidance methods.
AI.conclusion: ERK-Guid provides a solver-aware guidance mechanism that improves diffusion-based sampling in stiff regions by using LTE as a guidance signal via Embedded Runge-Kutta methods; results indicate robust performance gains and a release of code for replication.

title: NuMuon: Nuclear-Norm-Constrained Muon for Compressible LLM Training
summary: The rapid progress of large language models (LLMs) is increasingly constrained by memory and deployment costs, motivating compression methods for practical deployment. Many state-of-the-art compression pipelines leverage the low-rank structure of trained weight matrices, a phenomenon often associated with the properties of popular optimizers such as Adam. In this context, Muon is a recently proposed optimizer that improves LLM pretraining via full-rank update steps, but its induced weight-space structure has not been characterized yet. In this work, we report a surprising empirical finding: despite imposing full-rank updates, Muon-trained models exhibit pronounced low-rank structure in their weight matrices and are readily compressible under standard pipelines. Motivated by this insight, we propose NuMuon, which augments Muon with a nuclear-norm constraint on the update direction, further constraining the learned weights toward low-rank structure. Across billion-parameter-scale models, we show that NuMuon increases weight compressibility and improves post-compression model quality under state-of-the-art LLM compression pipelines while retaining Muon's favorable convergence behavior.

AI.tldr: Muon-trained models exhibit strong low-rank structure in weight matrices despite full-rank updates; NuMuon adds a nuclear-norm constraint on the update to further promote low-rank weights. Across multi-billion-parameter LLMs, NuMuon increases weight compressibility and improves post-compression quality while preserving Muon's convergence behavior.
AI.motivation: Memory and deployment costs of large language models drive the need for effective compression. Although many pipelines exploit low-rank weight structure, Muon—the optimizer with full-rank updates—surprisingly yields weight matrices with pronounced low-rank structure, suggesting an opportunity to further enhance compressibility.
AI.method: Empirical analysis of Muon-trained models to characterize weight-space structure; introduce NuMuon by adding a nuclear-norm constraint on the update direction; train and evaluate billion-parameter-scale LLMs; assess compressibility with standard pipelines and measure post-compression model quality and convergence.
AI.result: Muon-trained models display pronounced low-rank weight matrices and are readily compressible under standard pipelines. NuMuon, enforcing a nuclear-norm constraint on updates, increases weight compressibility and improves post-compression performance while maintaining Muon’s favorable convergence.
AI.conclusion: Update-direction regularization via a nuclear-norm constraint is an effective, scalable approach to bias training toward low-rank weight structures, enabling more aggressive compression without sacrificing training dynamics; NuMuon provides a practical enhancement to Muon for deployment of large LLMs.

title: Beyond Pixel Histories: World Models with Persistent 3D State
summary: Interactive world models continually generate video by responding to a user's actions, enabling open-ended generation capabilities. However, existing models typically lack a 3D representation of the environment, meaning 3D consistency must be implicitly learned from data, and spatial memory is restricted to limited temporal context windows. This results in an unrealistic user experience and presents significant obstacles to down-stream tasks such as training agents. To address this, we present PERSIST, a new paradigm of world model which simulates the evolution of a latent 3D scene: environment, camera, and renderer. This allows us to synthesize new frames with persistent spatial memory and consistent geometry. Both quantitative metrics and a qualitative user study show substantial improvements in spatial memory, 3D consistency, and long-horizon stability over existing methods, enabling coherent, evolving 3D worlds. We further demonstrate novel capabilities, including synthesising diverse 3D environments from a single image, as well as enabling fine-grained, geometry-aware control over generated experiences by supporting environment editing and specification directly in 3D space. Project page: https://francelico.github.io/persist.github.io

AI.tldr: PERSIST introduces a latent 3D world-model that evolves over time, enabling persistent spatial memory and 3D-consistent frame synthesis for interactive video, with 3D editing and single-image-to-3D environment generation.
AI.motivation: Address lack of explicit 3D representation and limited temporal memory in existing interactive world models, which degrade geometry consistency and downstream task performance.
AI.method: Model latent 3D scene including environment, camera, and renderer; maintain persistence of geometry and memory across frames; synthesize frames from evolving latent scene; support 3D environment editing and generation from a single image.
AI.result: Quantitative metrics and user study show improvements in spatial memory, 3D consistency, and long-horizon stability; richer 3D world coherence; capabilities to generate diverse 3D environments from a single image; geometry-aware control via 3D space editing.
AI.conclusion: PERSIST enables coherent, evolving 3D worlds with persistent memory, enhancing interactivity and enabling 3D-aware task control; promising for downstream agents and broader applications.

title: Self-adapting Robotic Agents through Online Continual Reinforcement Learning with World Model Feedback
summary: As learning-based robotic controllers are typically trained offline and deployed with fixed parameters, their ability to cope with unforeseen changes during operation is limited. Biologically inspired, this work presents a framework for online Continual Reinforcement Learning that enables automated adaptation during deployment. Building on DreamerV3, a model-based Reinforcement Learning algorithm, the proposed method leverages world model prediction residuals to detect out-of-distribution events and automatically trigger finetuning. Adaptation progress is monitored using both task-level performance signals and internal training metrics, allowing convergence to be assessed without external supervision and domain knowledge. The approach is validated on a variety of contemporary continuous control problems, including a quadruped robot in high-fidelity simulation, and a real-world model vehicle. Relevant metrics and their interpretation are presented and discussed, as well as resulting trade-offs described. The results sketch out how autonomous robotic agents could once move beyond static training regimes toward adaptive systems capable of self-reflection and -improvement during operation, just like their biological counterparts.

AI.tldr: Online continual RL framework built on DreamerV3 that uses world-model residuals to detect OOD events and automatically finetune during deployment, with monitoring from task signals and internal training metrics, validated on continuous control tasks including a simulated quadruped and a real vehicle; discusses metrics and trade-offs and points toward adaptive, self-improving robots.
AI.motivation: Overcome the limitation of offline-trained controllers with fixed parameters that struggle when facing unforeseen changes in deployment; seek autonomous, biology-inspired adaptation.
AI.method: Extend DreamerV3 with world-model prediction residuals to detect out-of-distribution events and automatically trigger finetuning during operation; monitor adaptation progress via task-level performance signals and internal training metrics, enabling unsupervised assessment of convergence; operate without external supervision or domain knowledge.
AI.result: Demonstrated feasibility on contemporary continuous-control problems, including a high-fidelity quadruped in simulation and a real-world model vehicle; presents interpretation of relevant metrics and discusses trade-offs.
AI.conclusion: Suggests a path toward autonomous robotic agents capable of self-reflection and improvement during operation, moving beyond static training regimes to adaptive systems.

title: MPFlow: Multi-modal Posterior-Guided Flow Matching for Zero-Shot MRI Reconstruction
summary: Zero-shot MRI reconstruction relies on generative priors, but single-modality unconditional priors produce hallucinations under severe ill-posedness. In many clinical workflows, complementary MRI acquisitions (e.g. high-quality structural scans) are routinely available, yet existing reconstruction methods lack mechanisms to leverage this additional information. We propose MPFlow, a zero-shot multi-modal reconstruction framework built on rectified flow that incorporates auxiliary MRI modalities at inference time without retraining the generative prior to improve anatomical fidelity. Cross-modal guidance is enabled by our proposed self-supervised pretraining strategy, Patch-level Multi-modal MR Image Pretraining (PAMRI), which learns shared representations across modalities. Sampling is jointly guided by data consistency and cross-modal feature alignment using pre-trained PAMRI, systematically suppressing intrinsic and extrinsic hallucinations. Extensive experiments on HCP and BraTS show that MPFlow matches diffusion baselines on image quality using only 20% of sampling steps while reducing tumor hallucinations by more than 15% (segmentation dice score). This demonstrates that cross-modal guidance enables more reliable and efficient zero-shot MRI reconstruction.

AI.tldr: MPFlow enables zero-shot multi-modal MRI reconstruction by using cross-modal guidance with PAMRI in a rectified-flow framework, reducing hallucinations and improving efficiency.
AI.motivation: Single-modality generative priors can hallucinate under ill-posed MRI reconstruction. In clinical workflows, complementary MRI acquisitions are routinely available but are not leveraged by existing methods.
AI.method: MPFlow is a zero-shot multi-modal reconstruction framework based on rectified flow. It incorporates auxiliary MRI modalities at inference time without retraining the generative prior. Cross-modal guidance is achieved via Patch-level Multi-modal MR Image Pretraining (PAMRI), a self-supervised pretraining strategy that learns shared representations across modalities. Sampling is guided by data consistency and cross-modal feature alignment using PAMRI to suppress intrinsic and extrinsic hallucinations.
AI.result: On HCP and BraTS, MPFlow matches diffusion baselines in image quality using only 20% of the sampling steps and reduces tumor hallucinations by >15% in segmentation dice score.
AI.conclusion: Cross-modal guidance with PAMRI enables more reliable and efficient zero-shot MRI reconstruction.

title: Riemannian Optimization in Modular Systems
summary:
Understanding how systems built out of modular components can be jointly optimized is an important problem in biology, engineering, and machine learning. The backpropagation algorithm is one such solution and has been instrumental in the success of neural networks. Despite its empirical success, a strong theoretical understanding of it is lacking. Here, we combine tools from Riemannian geometry, optimal control theory, and theoretical physics to advance this understanding.
  We make three key contributions: First, we revisit the derivation of backpropagation as a constrained optimization problem and combine it with the insight that Riemannian gradient descent trajectories can be understood as the minimum of an action. Second, we introduce a recursively defined layerwise Riemannian metric that exploits the modular structure of neural networks and can be efficiently computed using the Woodbury matrix identity, avoiding the $O(n^3)$ cost of full metric inversion. Third, we develop a framework of composable ``Riemannian modules'' whose convergence properties can be quantified using nonlinear contraction theory, providing algorithmic stability guarantees of order $O(κ^2 L/(ξμ\sqrt{n}))$ where $κ$ and $L$ are Lipschitz constants, $μ$ is the mass matrix scale, and $ξ$ bounds the condition number.
  Our layerwise metric approach provides a practical alternative to natural gradient descent. While we focus here on studying neural networks, our approach more generally applies to the study of systems made of modules that are optimized over time, as it occurs in biology during both evolution and development.

AI.tldr: Geometry-informed, modular backprop analysis with layerwise Riemannian metrics; efficient computation via Woodbury; contraction-based stability guarantees; a practical alternative to natural gradient with broad applicability.
AI.motivation: Understand backpropagation theoretically and design modular, stable optimization for systems built from interacting components, including biological systems.
AI.method: Re-derive backpropagation as constrained optimization with action-minimizing Riemannian gradient flows; introduce a recursive layerwise Riemannian metric computable via Woodbury identities; define composable 'Riemannian modules'; analyze convergence with nonlinear contraction theory to obtain explicit stability bounds.
AI.result: Propose a practical metric-based optimization method that serves as an efficient alternative to natural gradient; provide convergence/stability guarantees with a bound O(κ^2 L/(ξ μ sqrt(n))); framework applicable to modular systems beyond neural networks.
AI.conclusion: The framework links geometry, optimal control, and contraction theory to advance understanding of modular optimization and offers a scalable, stability-guaranteed alternative for systems composed of modules; potential applications in biology and engineered modular systems.

title: Optimal trajectory-guided stochastic co-optimization for e-fuel system design and real-time operation
summary: E-fuels are promising long-term energy carriers supporting the net-zero transition. However, the large combinatorial design-operation spaces under renewable uncertainty make the use of mathematical programming impractical for co-optimizing e-fuel production systems. Here, we present MasCOR, a machine-learning-assisted co-optimization framework that learns from global operational trajectories. By encoding system design and renewable trends, a single MasCOR agent generalizes dynamic operation across diverse configurations and scenarios, substantially simplifying design-operation co-optimization under uncertainty. Benchmark comparisons against state-of-the-art reinforcement learning baselines demonstrate near-optimal performance, while computational costs are substantially lower than those of mathematical programming, enabling rapid parallel evaluation of designs within the co-optimization loop. This framework enables rapid screening of feasible design spaces together with corresponding operational policies. When applied to four potential European sites targeting e-methanol production, MasCOR shows that most locations benefit from reducing system load below 50 MW to achieve carbon-neutral methanol production, with production costs of 1.0-1.2 USD per kg. In contrast, Dunkirk (France), with limited renewable availability and high grid prices, favors system loads above 200 MW and expanded storage to exploit dynamic grid exchange and hydrogen sales to the market. These results underscore the value of the MasCOR framework for site-specific guidance from system design to real-time operation.

AI.tldr: ML-assisted co-optimization (MasCOR) for e-fuel production under renewable uncertainty enables rapid design–operation screening and near-optimal performance at lower cost than algebraic programming; demonstrated on four European sites for e-methanol with site-specific guidance (load targets and storage/hydrogen sales).
AI.motivation: To address the intractable combinatorial design–operation spaces and uncertainty in renewables that hinder traditional mathematical programming, by learning from global operational trajectories to enable rapid, generalizable co-optimization across designs and scenarios.
AI.method: MasCOR trains a single ML agent that encodes system design and renewable trends, learning dynamic operation across configurations. It generalizes to diverse designs and scenarios, enabling co-optimization of design and real-time operation. Benchmarked against state-of-the-art RL baselines, MasCOR offers near-optimal performance with lower computational cost and supports parallel evaluation of designs.
AI.result: MasCOR achieves near-optimal performance compared with RL baselines while significantly reducing computation relative to mathematical programming. It enables rapid screening of feasible design spaces with corresponding operational policies. In a four-site European study for e-methanol, most sites benefit from keeping loads below ~50 MW; Dunkirk favors loads above ~200 MW with storage to exploit grid exchange and hydrogen sales, yielding production costs of about 1.0–1.2 USD/kg.
AI.conclusion: MasCOR provides a scalable, data-driven framework for site-specific, co-optimized design and operation under renewable uncertainty, enabling rapid design screening and real-time policy deployment across configurations.

title: Force-Aware Residual DAgger via Trajectory Editing for Precision Insertion with Impedance Control
summary: Imitation learning (IL) has shown strong potential for contact-rich precision insertion tasks.
