However, its practical deployment is often hindered by covariate shift and the need for continuous expert monitoring to recover from failures during execution. In this paper, we propose Trajectory Editing Residual Dataset Aggregation (TER-DAgger), a scalable and force-aware human-in-the-loop imitation learning framework that mitigates covariate shift by learning residual policies through optimization-based trajectory editing. This approach smoothly fuses policy rollouts with human corrective trajectories, providing consistent and stable supervision. Second, we introduce a force-aware failure anticipation mechanism that triggers human intervention only when discrepancies arise between predicted and measured end-effector forces, significantly reducing the requirement for continuous expert monitoring. Third, all learned policies are executed within a Cartesian impedance control framework, ensuring compliant and safe behavior during contact-rich interactions. Extensive experiments in both simulation and real-world precision insertion tasks show that TER-DAgger improves the average success rate by over 37\% compared to behavior cloning, human-guided correction, retraining, and fine-tuning baselines, demonstrating its effectiveness in mitigating covariate shift and enabling scalable deployment in contact-rich manipulation.

AI.tldr: TER-DAgger is a scalable, force-aware imitation learning framework that combines optimization-based trajectory editing with residual policy learning to reduce covariate shift and enable safe, compliant contact-rich manipulation, achieving substantial performance gains.
AI.motivation: In contact-rich precision insertion tasks, imitation learning suffers from covariate shift and often requires continuous expert monitoring. A scalable approach that reduces supervision while ensuring safety is needed.
AI.method: Trajectory Editing Residual Dataset Aggregation (TER-DAgger) learns residual policies via optimization-based trajectory editing and fuses policy rollouts with human corrective trajectories. It includes a force-aware failure anticipation mechanism that cues human intervention when discrepancies between predicted and measured end-effector forces occur. All policies run inside a Cartesian impedance control framework for compliant, safe interactions.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation in simulation and real-world precision insertion tasks shows the average success rate improves by over 37% relative to behavior cloning, human-guided correction, retraining, and fine-tuning baselines, demonstrating improved covariate shift handling and scalable deployment.
AI.conclusion: TER-DAgger provides a robust, scalable solution for contact-rich manipulation by integrating trajectory editing, residual learning, force-aware intervention, and impedance control, achieving significant performance gains and reduced supervision.

title: LDP-Slicing: Local Differential Privacy for Images via Randomized Bit-Plane Slicing
summary: Local Differential Privacy (LDP) is the gold standard trust model for privacy-preserving machine learning by guaranteeing privacy at the data source. However, its application to image data has long been considered impractical due to the high dimensionality of pixel space. Canonical LDP mechanisms are designed for low-dimensional data, resulting in severe utility degradation when applied to high-dimensional pixel spaces. This paper demonstrates that this utility loss is not inherent to LDP, but from its application to an inappropriate data representation. We introduce LDP-Slicing, a lightweight, training-free framework that resolves this domain mismatch. Our key insight is to decompose pixel values into a sequence of binary bit-planes. This transformation allows us to apply the LDP mechanism directly to the bit-level representation. To further strengthen privacy and preserve utility, we integrate a perceptual obfuscation module that mitigates human-perceivable leakage and an optimization-based privacy budget allocation strategy. This pipeline satisfies rigorous pixel-level $\varepsilon$-LDP while producing images that retain high utility for downstream tasks. Extensive experiments on face recognition and image classification demonstrate that LDP-Slicing outperforms existing DP/LDP baselines under comparable privacy budgets, with negligible computational overhead.

AI.tldr: LDP-Slicing decomposes images into binary bit-planes and applies LDP at the bit level, augmented with perceptual obfuscation and optimized privacy-budget allocation, achieving pixel-level ε-LDP with high utility and low overhead.
AI.motivation: The high dimensionality of pixel space makes standard LDP utilities poor; a representation-aligned approach is needed to enable practical LDP for images.
AI.method: Decompose pixel values into binary bit-planes, apply LDP on the bit-level representation, integrate a perceptual obfuscation module to reduce human-perceived leakage, and optimize privacy-budget allocation; the pipeline is training-free.
AI.result: Experiments on face recognition and image classification show LDP-Slicing outperforms existing DP/LDP baselines under similar privacy budgets with negligible computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: Bit-plane decomposition coupled with LDP and perceptual obfuscation resolves the domain mismatch between LDP and image data, enabling practical, high-utility pixel-level ε-LDP for vision tasks.

title: Why Are Linear RNNs More Parallelizable?
comment: Corrected authorship list from initial version
summary: The community is increasingly exploring linear RNNs (LRNNs) as language models, motivated by their expressive power and parallelizability. While prior work establishes the expressivity benefits of LRNNs over transformers, it is unclear what makes LRNNs -- but not traditional, nonlinear RNNs -- as easy to parallelize in practice as transformers. We answer this question by providing a tight connection between types of RNNs and standard complexity classes. We show that LRNNs can be viewed as log-depth (bounded fan-in) arithmetic circuits, which represents only a slight depth overhead relative to log-depth boolean circuits that transformers admit. Furthermore, we show that nonlinear RNNs can solve $\mathsf{L}$-complete problems (and even $\mathsf{P}$-complete ones, under polynomial precision), revealing a fundamental barrier to parallelizing them as efficiently as transformers. Our theory also identifies fine-grained expressivity differences between recent popular LRNN variants: permutation-diagonal LRNNs are $\mathsf{NC}^1$-complete whereas diagonal-plus-low-rank LRNNs are more expressive ($\mathsf{PNC}^1$-complete). We provide further insight by associating each type of RNN with a corresponding automata-theoretic model that it can simulate. Together, our results reveal fundamental tradeoffs between nonlinear RNNs and different variants of LRNNs, providing a foundation for designing LLM architectures that achieve an optimal balance between expressivity and parallelism.

AI.tldr: LRNNs align with log-depth arithmetic circuits, enabling parallelism close to transformers; nonlinear RNNs face fundamental depth-based barriers to parallelization; LRNN variants differ in expressivity (e.g., NC1 vs PNC1); automata-theoretic mappings give insight into tradeoffs for designing efficient LLMs.
AI.motivation: Explain why LRNNs can be parallelized as efficiently as transformers while nonlinear RNNs cannot, and characterize the expressivity and computational limits of LRNN variants.
AI.method: Frame LRNNs as log-depth, bounded-fan-in arithmetic circuits; compare to log-depth Boolean circuits underlying transformers; show nonlinear RNNs solve L-complete (and P-complete under polynomial precision); map each RNN type to corresponding automata-theoretic models; derive fine-grained complexity results for LRNN variants.
AI.result: LRNNs incur only a small depth overhead relative to log-depth Boolean circuits that transformers admit; nonlinear RNNs have inherent parallelization barriers by solving L-complete (and P-complete with precision) problems; permutation-diagonal LRNNs are NC1-complete while diagonal-plus-low-rank LRNNs are PN C1-complete; each type corresponds to an automata model it can simulate.
AI.conclusion: A fundamental tradeoff exists between nonlinear RNNs and LRNN variants in expressivity and parallelizability; the framework guides designing LLMs to balance expressive power with efficient parallel computation, selecting LRNN variants to achieve desired complexity and automata-theoretic properties.

title: Phys4D: Fine-Grained Physics-Consistent 4D Modeling from Video Diffusion
summary: Recent video diffusion models have achieved impressive capabilities as large-scale generative world models. However, these models often struggle with fine-grained physical consistency, exhibiting physically implausible dynamics over time. In this work, we present \textbf{Phys4D}, a pipeline for learning physics-consistent 4D world representations from video diffusion models. Phys4D adopts \textbf{a three-stage training paradigm} that progressively lifts appearance-driven video diffusion models into physics-consistent 4D world representations. We first bootstrap robust geometry and motion representations through large-scale pseudo-supervised pretraining, establishing a foundation for 4D scene modeling. We then perform physics-grounded supervised fine-tuning using simulation-generated data, enforcing temporally consistent 4D dynamics. Finally, we apply simulation-grounded reinforcement learning to correct residual physical violations that are difficult to capture through explicit supervision. To evaluate fine-grained physical consistency beyond appearance-based metrics, we introduce a set of \textbf{4D world consistency evaluation} that probe geometric coherence, motion stability, and long-horizon physical plausibility. Experimental results demonstrate that Phys4D substantially improves fine-grained spatiotemporal and physical consistency compared to appearance-driven baselines, while maintaining strong generative performance. Our project page is available at https://sensational-brioche-7657e7.netlify.app/

AI.tldr: Phys4D lifts video diffusion models into physics-consistent 4D world representations via a three-stage training pipeline and a 4D consistency evaluation suite.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between appearance-focused video diffusion and physically plausible, temporally coherent 4D dynamics in real-world scenes.
AI.method: Three-stage pipeline: (1) large-scale pseudo-supervised pretraining to bootstrap geometry and motion; (2) physics-grounded supervised fine-tuning using simulation-generated data to enforce temporally consistent 4D dynamics; (3) simulation-grounded reinforcement learning to correct residual physical violations. Evaluation includes a dedicated 4D world consistency suite probing geometric coherence, motion stability, and long-horizon plausibility.
AI.result: Significant improvements in fine-grained spatiotemporal and physical consistency over appearance-driven baselines, with generative performance maintained.
AI.conclusion: Phys4D provides a scalable approach to achieving physics-consistent 4D representations from video diffusion models and introduces a comprehensive evaluation framework for 4D world coherence.

title: HE-VPR: Height Estimation Enabled Aerial Visual Place Recognition Against Scale Variance
summary: In this work, we propose HE-VPR, a visual place recognition (VPR) framework that incorporates height estimation. Our system decouples height inference from place recognition, allowing both modules to share a frozen DINOv2 backbone. Two lightweight bypass adapter branches are integrated into our system. The first estimates the height partition of the query image via retrieval from a compact height database, and the second performs VPR within the corresponding height-specific sub-database. The adaptation design reduces training cost and significantly decreases the search space of the database. We also adopt a center-weighted masking strategy to further enhance the robustness against scale differences. Experiments on two self-collected challenging multi-altitude datasets demonstrate that HE-VPR achieves up to 6.1\% Recall@1 improvement over state-of-the-art ViT-based baselines and reduces memory usage by up to 90\%. These results indicate that HE-VPR offers a scalable and efficient solution for height-aware aerial VPR, enabling practical deployment in GNSS-denied environments. All the code and datasets for this work have been released on https://github.com/hmf21/HE-VPR.

AI.tldr: HE-VPR is a height-aware visual place recognition framework that decouples height inference from place recognition, sharing a frozen DINOv2 backbone with two lightweight bypass adapters. One adapter retrieves the height partition from a compact height database, and the other performs VPR within the corresponding height-specific sub-database, enabling reduced training cost and a smaller search space. A center-weighted masking strategy improves robustness to scale differences.
AI.motivation: Tackle GNSS-denied aerial VPR with significant height/scale variation while keeping computational efficiency, by decoupling height inference from place recognition and using a shared backbone.
AI.method: Freeze DINOv2 backbone and introduce two bypass adapters: (1) height partition estimation via retrieval from a compact height database, (2) VPR within the chosen height-specific sub-database; apply center-weighted masking to handle scale differences; reduces training cost and search space.
AI.result: On two challenging multi-altitude datasets, HE-VPR achieves up to 6.1 percentage points improvement in Recall@1 over state-of-the-art ViT-based baselines and reduces memory usage by up to 90%.
AI.conclusion: HE-VPR provides a scalable, efficient height-aware aerial VPR approach suitable for deployment in GNSS-denied environments; code and datasets are released.

title: Glass Segmentation with Fusion of Learned and General Visual Features
summary: Glass surface segmentation from RGB images is a challenging task, since glass as a transparent material distinctly lacks visual characteristics. However, glass segmentation is critical for scene understanding and robotics, as transparent glass surfaces must be identified as solid material. This paper presents a novel architecture for glass segmentation, deploying a dual-backbone producing general visual features as well as task-specific learned visual features. General visual features are produced by a frozen DINOv3 vision foundation model, and the task-specific features are generated with a Swin model trained in a supervised manner. Resulting multi-scale feature representations are downsampled with residual Squeeze-and-Excitation Channel Reduction, and fed into a Mask2Former Decoder, producing the final segmentation masks. The architecture was evaluated on four commonly used glass segmentation datasets, achieving state-of-the-art results on several accuracy metrics. The model also has a competitive inference speed compared to the previous state-of-the-art method, and surpasses it when using a lighter DINOv3 backbone variant. The implementation source code and model weights are available at: https://github.com/ojalar/lgnet

AI.tldr: Dual-backbone LGNet uses frozen DINOv3 for general features and supervised Swin for task-specific features, fused via residual SE channel reduction and a Mask2Former decoder to achieve state-of-the-art glass segmentation across four datasets with competitive speed.
AI.motivation: Glass is transparent and lacks distinctive features, making segmentation difficult; accurate glass detection is important for robotics and scene understanding.
AI.method: Two backbones: frozen DINOv3 for general features; supervised Swin for task-specific features. Features downsampled with residual Squeeze-and-Excitation Channel Reduction, concatenated/fused, then processed by a Mask2Former Decoder to output segmentation masks. Implemented as LGNet; evaluation on four glass datasets; code released.
AI.result: State-of-the-art on several accuracy metrics across four datasets; competitive inference speed, improved speed with lighter DINOv3 backbone; code and weights available.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effective fusion of frozen foundation features with task-specific representations and efficient channel reduction to enhance glass segmentation performance while maintaining efficiency; supports broader use of frozen foundation models in segmentation tasks.

title: Extending Neural Operators: Robust Handling of Functions Beyond the Training Set
comment: related open source software see https://web.atzberger.org/
summary: We develop a rigorous framework for extending neural operators to handle out-of-distribution input functions. We leverage kernel approximation techniques and provide theory for characterizing the input-output function spaces in terms of Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Spaces (RKHSs). We provide theorems on the requirements for reliable extensions and their predicted approximation accuracy. We also establish formal relationships between specific kernel choices and their corresponding Sobolev Native Spaces. This connection further allows the extended neural operators to reliably capture not only function values but also their derivatives. Our methods are empirically validated through the solution of elliptic partial differential equations (PDEs) involving operators on manifolds having point-cloud representations and handling geometric contributions. We report results on key factors impacting the accuracy and computational performance of the extension approaches.

AI.tldr: A kernel/RKHS-based framework extends neural operators to out-of-distribution inputs, linking kernels to Sobolev native spaces to enable derivative-aware extensions, with PDE-on-manifold experiments.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of reliable generalization of neural operators to out-of-distribution input functions and the desire to capture derivatives; use RKHS/kernel theory to characterize input-output spaces and guide extensions.
AI.method: Develop a rigorous kernel-approximation framework; prove theorems on requirements for reliable OOD extension and approximation accuracy; establish relations between kernel choices and Sobolev native spaces; enable neural operators to estimate function values and derivatives; validate empirically on elliptic PDEs on manifolds with point-cloud representations and geometric contributions; analyze factors affecting accuracy and computational performance.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantees for reliable extension and approximation accuracy; explicit mappings between kernel choices and Sobolev native spaces enabling derivative recovery; neural operators extended to also approximate derivatives; empirical validation showing accuracy and efficiency in PDEs on manifolds and point-cloud geometries.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a principled framework combining RKHS theory with neural operators to achieve robust OOD extensions and derivative-aware predictions, with practical guidance on kernel selection and extension strategies for geometric PDE problems.

title: PhyPrompt: RL-based Prompt Refinement for Physically Plausible Text-to-Video Generation
summary: State-of-the-art text-to-video (T2V) generators frequently violate physical laws despite high visual quality. We show this stems from insufficient physical constraints in prompts rather than model limitations: manually adding physics details reliably produces physically plausible videos, but requires expertise and does not scale. We present PhyPrompt, a two-stage reinforcement learning framework that automatically refines prompts for physically realistic generation. First, we fine-tune a large language model on a physics-focused Chain-of-Thought dataset to integrate principles like object motion and force interactions while preserving user intent. Second, we apply Group Relative Policy Optimization with a dynamic reward curriculum that initially prioritizes semantic fidelity, then progressively shifts toward physical commonsense. This curriculum achieves synergistic optimization: PhyPrompt-7B reaches 40.8\% joint success on VideoPhy2 (8.6pp gain), improving physical commonsense by 11pp (55.8\% to 66.8\%) while simultaneously increasing semantic adherence by 4.4pp (43.4\% to 47.8\%). Remarkably, our curriculum exceeds single-objective training on both metrics, demonstrating compositional prompt discovery beyond conventional multi-objective trade-offs. PhyPrompt outperforms GPT-4o (+3.8\% joint) and DeepSeek-V3 (+2.2\%, 100$\times$ larger) using only 7B parameters. The approach transfers zero-shot across diverse T2V architectures (Lavie, VideoCrafter2, CogVideoX-5B) with up to 16.8\% improvement, establishing that domain-specialized reinforcement learning with compositional curricula surpasses general-purpose scaling for physics-aware generation.

AI.tldr: PhyPrompt uses domain-specific RL to auto-refine prompts for physics-consistent T2V generation, achieving state-of-the-art physical reasoning with a 7B model and zero-shot transfer across architectures.
AI.motivation: State-of-the-art T2V generators often violate physical laws because prompts lack robust physical constraints and manual prompting does not scale; automated, physics-aware prompt refinement is needed.
AI.method: Two-stage RL framework: (1) fine-tune a large language model on a physics-focused Chain-of-Thought dataset to encode object motion and force interactions while preserving user intent; (2) apply Group Relative Policy Optimization with a dynamic reward curriculum that first prioritizes semantic fidelity and then emphasizes physical commonsense.
AI.result: PhyPrompt-7B achieves 40.8% joint success on VideoPhy2 (up 8.6 percentage points); physical commonsense improves from 55.8% to 66.8% (+11pp); semantic adherence rises from 43.4% to 47.8% (+4.4pp). The curriculum surpasses single-objective training on both metrics. It outperforms GPT-4o by 3.8% and DeepSeek-V3 by 2.2% (DeepSeek-V3 is 100× larger). Zero-shot transfer across Lavie, VideoCrafter2, and CogVideoX-5B yields up to 16.8% gains.
AI.conclusion: Domain-specialized reinforcement learning with a compositional curriculum enables physics-aware text-to-video generation with modestly sized models, achieving superior joint performance and broad architectural transfer, surpassing general-purpose scaling for physics-aware generation.

title: Sim2Sea: Sim-to-Real Policy Transfer for Maritime Vessel Navigation in Congested Waters
summary: Autonomous navigation in congested maritime environments is a critical capability for a wide range of real-world applications. However, it remains an unresolved challenge due to complex vessel interactions and significant environmental uncertainties. Existing methods often fail in practical deployment due to a substantial sim-to-real gap, which stems from imprecise simulation, inadequate situational awareness, and unsafe exploration strategies. To address these, we propose \textbf{Sim2Sea}, a comprehensive framework designed to bridge simulation and real-world execution. Sim2Sea advances in three key aspects. First, we develop a GPU-accelerated parallel simulator for scalable and accurate maritime scenario simulation. Second, we design a dual-stream spatiotemporal policy that handles complex dynamics and multi-modal perception, augmented with a velocity-obstacle-guided action masking mechanism to ensure safe and efficient exploration. Finally, a targeted domain randomization scheme helps bridge the sim-to-real gap. Simulation results demonstrate that our method achieves faster convergence and safer trajectories than established baselines. In addition, our policy trained purely in simulation successfully transfers zero-shot to a 17-ton unmanned vessel operating in real-world congested waters. These results validate the effectiveness of Sim2Sea in achieving reliable sim-to-real transfer for practical autonomous maritime navigation.

AI.tldr: Sim2Sea provides a GPU-accelerated simulator, a dual-stream spatiotemporal policy with velocity-obstacle-based safety masking, and targeted domain randomization to enable faster training and zero-shot sim-to-real transfer for autonomous maritime navigation.
AI.motivation: Autonomous navigation in congested maritime environments suffers from a large sim-to-real gap due to imperfect simulations, limited situational awareness, and unsafe exploration. A scalable, accurate simulation framework plus safe, transferable policies is needed for real-world deployment.
AI.method: (1) Develop a GPU-accelerated parallel simulator for scalable, accurate maritime scenario simulation. (2) Propose a dual-stream spatiotemporal policy that handles complex dynamics and multi-modal perception, augmented with a velocity-obstacle-guided action masking to ensure safe and efficient exploration. (3) Apply targeted domain randomization to bridge the sim-to-real gap.
AI.result: Simulation experiments show faster convergence and safer trajectories compared with baselines. A policy trained entirely in simulation transfers zero-shot to a 17-ton unmanned vessel operating in real congested waters.
AI.conclusion: Sim2Sea effectively bridges the sim-to-real gap for practical autonomous maritime navigation, enabling reliable, real-world deployment of simulated-trained policies.

title: QD-PCQA: Quality-Aware Domain Adaptation for Point Cloud Quality Assessment
summary: No-Reference Point Cloud Quality Assessment (NR-PCQA) still struggles with generalization, primarily due to the scarcity of annotated point cloud datasets. Since the Human Visual System (HVS) drives perceptual quality assessment independently of media types, prior knowledge on quality learned from images can be repurposed for point clouds. This insight motivates adopting Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA) to transfer quality-relevant priors from labeled images to unlabeled point clouds. However, existing UDA-based PCQA methods often overlook key characteristics of perceptual quality, such as sensitivity to quality ranking and quality-aware feature alignment, thereby limiting their effectiveness. To address these issues, we propose a novel Quality-aware Domain adaptation framework for PCQA, termed QD-PCQA. The framework comprises two main components: i) a Rank-weighted Conditional Alignment (RCA) strategy that aligns features under consistent quality levels and adaptively emphasizes misranked samples to reinforce perceptual quality ranking awareness; and ii) a Quality-guided Feature Augmentation (QFA) strategy, which includes quality-guided style mixup, multi-layer extension, and dual-domain augmentation modules to augment perceptual feature alignment. Extensive cross-domain experiments demonstrate that QD-PCQA significantly improves generalization in NR-PCQA tasks. The code is available at https://github.com/huhu-code/QD-PCQA.

AI.tldr: QD-PCQA proposes rank-weighted conditional alignment and quality-guided feature augmentation to transfer perceptual-quality priors from labeled images to unlabeled point clouds, boosting NR-PCQA generalization.
AI.motivation: NR-PCQA suffers from limited annotated point-cloud datasets. Perceptual quality is driven by the HVS and can be learned from images; cross-domain unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) can transfer quality priors from images to point clouds. Existing UDA-PCQA work misses key quality-sensitive aspects like ranking and alignment.
AI.method: Two core components: (1) Rank-weighted Conditional Alignment (RCA) to align features at consistent quality levels while emphasizing misranked samples; (2) Quality-guided Feature Augmentation (QFA) including quality-guided style mixup, multi-layer extension, and dual-domain augmentation to augment perceptual feature alignment.
AI.result: Cross-domain NR-PCQA experiments show significant generalization gains, outperforming baselines.
AI.conclusion: QD-PCQA effectively leverages HVS-informed priors and novel alignment/augmentation strategies to improve cross-domain NR-PCQA generalization; code is publicly available.

title: Adaptive Sensing of Continuous Physical Systems for Machine Learning
summary: Physical dynamical systems can be viewed as natural information processors: their systems preserve, transform, and disperse input information. This perspective motivates learning not only from data generated by such systems, but also how to measure them in a way that extracts the most useful information for a given task. We propose a general computing framework for adaptive information extraction from dynamical systems, in which a trainable attention module learns both where to probe the system state and how to combine these measurements to optimize prediction performance. As a concrete instantiation, we implement this idea using a spatiotemporal field governed by a partial differential equation as the underlying dynamics, though the framework applies equally to any system whose state can be sampled. Our results show that adaptive spatial sensing significantly improves prediction accuracy on canonical chaotic benchmarks. This work provides a perspective on attention-enhanced reservoir computing as a special case of a broader paradigm: neural networks as trainable measurement devices for extracting information from physical dynamical systems.

AI.tldr: Adaptive information extraction framework for dynamical systems using a trainable attention module to select where to probe and how to fuse measurements, instantiated on a PDE-driven spatiotemporal field; yields improved prediction on chaotic benchmarks; reframes attention-enhanced reservoir computing as neural measurement devices for physical dynamics.
AI.motivation: Dynamical systems can be efficient information processors; learning optimal sensing and fusion strategies can improve task performance while leveraging the system's natural dynamics; unify measurement and prediction in a trainable framework.
AI.method: A trainable attention module learns both probe locations in the system state and the fusion of the obtained measurements to optimize prediction performance; demonstrated on a PDE-governed spatiotemporal field; treats neural networks as trainable measurement devices and situates this within an attention-enhanced reservoir computing perspective.
AI.result: Adaptive spatial sensing significantly improves prediction accuracy on canonical chaotic benchmarks compared to non-adaptive sensing.
AI.conclusion: Frames neural networks as trainable measurement devices for information extraction from physical dynamical systems and positions attention-enhanced reservoir computing as a special case within a broader adaptive sensing paradigm.

title: MMAI Gym for Science: Training Liquid Foundation Models for Drug Discovery
summary: General-purpose large language models (LLMs) that rely on in-context learning do not reliably deliver the scientific understanding and performance required for drug discovery tasks. Simply increasing model size or introducing reasoning tokens does not yield significant performance gains. To address this gap, we introduce the MMAI Gym for Science, a one-stop shop molecular data formats and modalities as well as task-specific reasoning, training, and benchmarking recipes designed to teach foundation models the 'language of molecules' in order to solve practical drug discovery problems. We use MMAI Gym to train an efficient Liquid Foundation Model (LFM) for these applications, demonstrating that smaller, purpose-trained foundation models can outperform substantially larger general-purpose or specialist models on molecular benchmarks. Across essential drug discovery tasks - including molecular optimization, ADMET property prediction, retrosynthesis, drug-target activity prediction, and functional group reasoning - the resulting model achieves near specialist-level performance and, in the majority of settings, surpasses larger models, while remaining more efficient and broadly applicable in the domain.

AI.tldr: Efficient, purpose-trained foundation models (LFM) trained via MMAI Gym outperform larger models on drug-discovery benchmarks while remaining efficient.
AI.motivation: General-purpose LLMs with in-context learning fail to deliver reliable scientific understanding and performance for drug discovery; scaling alone does not yield meaningful gains. There is a need for task-specific data formats, modalities, and reasoning to teach models the language of molecules.
AI.method: MMAI Gym provides standardized molecular data formats, modalities, and task-specific reasoning, training, and benchmarking recipes to teach foundation models the 'language of molecules'. An efficient Liquid Foundation Model (LFM) is trained for molecular tasks.
AI.result: The LFM attains near specialist-level performance across essential drug-discovery tasks (molecular optimization, ADMET prediction, retrosynthesis, drug-target activity, functional group reasoning). In most settings it surpasses larger general-purpose or specialist models while being more efficient and broadly applicable in the domain.
AI.conclusion: Smaller, purpose-trained foundation models can rival or surpass larger models in drug discovery tasks; the MMAI Gym approach enables practical, efficient AI-assisted drug discovery.

title: SaFeR: Safety-Critical Scenario Generation for Autonomous Driving Test via Feasibility-Constrained Token Resampling
summary: Safety-critical scenario generation is crucial for evaluating autonomous driving systems. However, existing approaches often struggle to balance three conflicting objectives: adversarial criticality, physical feasibility, and behavioral realism. To bridge this gap, we propose SaFeR: safety-critical scenario generation for autonomous driving test via feasibility-constrained token resampling. We first formulate traffic generation as a discrete next token prediction problem, employing a Transformer-based model as a realism prior to capture naturalistic driving distributions. To capture complex interactions while effectively mitigating attention noise, we propose a novel differential attention mechanism within the realism prior. Building on this prior, SaFeR implements a novel resampling strategy that induces adversarial behaviors within a high-probability trust region to maintain naturalism, while enforcing a feasibility constraint derived from the Largest Feasible Region (LFR). By approximating the LFR via offline reinforcement learning, SaFeR effectively prevents the generation of theoretically inevitable collisions. Closed-loop experiments on the Waymo Open Motion Dataset and nuPlan demonstrate that SaFeR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving a higher solution rate and superior kinematic realism while maintaining strong adversarial effectiveness.

AI.tldr: SaFeR is a feasibility-constrained token-resampling framework for safety-critical scenario generation in autonomous driving, balancing realism, feasibility, and adversariality.
AI.motivation: To address the triad of conflicting objectives—adversarial criticality, physical feasibility, and behavioral realism—in safety-critical scenario generation and evaluation of autonomous driving systems.
AI.method: Formulate traffic generation as discrete next-token prediction using a Transformer-based realism prior. Introduce a differential attention mechanism to better model interactions. Implement a resampling strategy that introduces adversarial behaviors within a high-probability trust region while enforcing feasibility via Largest Feasible Region (LFR), approximated with offline reinforcement learning.
AI.result: SaFeR achieves higher solution rate and superior kinematic realism while preserving strong adversarial effectiveness, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines on Waymo Open Motion Dataset and nuPlan in closed-loop experiments.
AI.conclusion: SaFeR provides a feasible, realistic, and adversarially capable safety-critical scenario generator for autonomous driving testing, with the LFR-based feasibility constraint and resampling within a trust region contributing to improved safety evaluation.

title: PROSPECT: Unified Streaming Vision-Language Navigation via Semantic--Spatial Fusion and Latent Predictive Representation
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have advanced zero-shot end-to-end Vision-Language Navigation (VLN), yet robust navigation requires not only semantic understanding but also predictive modeling of environment dynamics and spatial structure. We propose PROSPECT, a unified streaming navigation agent that couples a streaming Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policy with latent predictive representation learning. PROSPECT uses CUT3R as a streaming 3D foundation spatial encoder to produce long-context, absolute-scale spatial features, and fuses them with SigLIP semantic features via cross-attention. During training, we introduce learnable stream query tokens that query the streaming context and predict next-step 2D and 3D latent features (rather than pixels or explicit modalities), supervised in the latent spaces of frozen SigLIP and CUT3R teachers. The predictive branch shapes internal representations without inference overhead. Experiments on VLN-CE benchmarks and real-robot deployment demonstrate state-of-the-art performance and improved long-horizon robustness under diverse lighting. We will release code for the community soon.

AI.tldr: PROSPECT is a streaming vision-language navigation agent that integrates a streaming VLA policy with latent predictive learning, using CUT3R for 3D spatial features and SigLIP semantics, to predict next-step latent representations and improve long-horizon robustness and cross-domain deployment.
AI.motivation: To address robust VLN by combining semantic understanding with predictive modeling of environment dynamics and spatial structure, enabling long-horizon planning under varied lighting and real-world conditions.
AI.method: A unified streaming VLA policy paired with a latent predictive branch. It leverages CUT3R as a streaming 3D spatial encoder to obtain absolute-scale spatial features, fused with SigLIP semantic features via cross-attention. Trainable stream query tokens query the streaming context and predict next-step 2D/3D latent features, supervised in the latent spaces of frozen SigLIP and CUT3R teachers. The predictive branch shapes representations without adding inference overhead.
AI.result: On VLN-CE benchmarks and real-robot deployment, PROSPECT achieves state-of-the-art performance and enhanced long-horizon robustness, including under diverse lighting conditions.
AI.conclusion: Integrating streaming perception with latent predictive learning yields robust, scalable navigation. The approach promises strong generalization and will release code.

title: Freezing of Gait Prediction using Proactive Agent that Learns from Selected Experience and DDQN Algorithm
comment: Accepted on Activity and Behavior Computing (ABC) 2026 Conference (https://autocare.ai/abc2026) and will be published on International Journal of Activity and Behavior Computing (IJABC) (International Journal of Activity and Behavior Computing)
summary: Freezing of Gait (FOG) is a debilitating motor symptom commonly experienced by individuals with Parkinson's Disease (PD) which often leads to falls and reduced mobility. Timely and accurate prediction of FOG episodes is essential for enabling proactive interventions through assistive technologies. This study presents a reinforcement learning-based framework designed to identify optimal pre-FOG onset points, thereby extending the prediction horizon for anticipatory cueing systems. The model implements a Double Deep Q-Network (DDQN) architecture enhanced with Prioritized Experience Replay (PER) allowing the agent to focus learning on high-impact experiences and refine its policy. Trained over 9000 episodes with a reward shaping strategy that promotes cautious decision-making, the agent demonstrated robust performance in both subject-dependent and subject-independent evaluations. The model achieved a prediction horizon of up to 8.72 seconds prior to FOG onset in subject-independent scenarios and 7.89 seconds in subject-dependent settings. These results highlight the model's potential for integration into wearable assistive devices, offering timely and personalized interventions to mitigate FOG in PD patients.

AI.tldr: Reinforcement learning with a DDQN and PER extends prediction horizon for pre-FOG onset in Parkinson's disease, achieving up to ~8.72 s before onset, enabling anticipatory wearable interventions.
AI.motivation: To extend the prediction horizon for freezing of gait (FOG) to allow timely, proactive interventions in PD by learning high-impact pre-onset cues using prioritized experience replay.
AI.method: A Double Deep Q-Network (DDQN) with Prioritized Experience Replay (PER) trained over 9,000 episodes with reward shaping to promote cautious decisions; evaluated in both subject-dependent and subject-independent settings for pre-FOG onset detection.
AI.result: Prediction horizon up to 8.72 seconds prior to FOG onset in subject-independent tests and 7.89 seconds in subject-dependent tests; demonstrated robust performance and potential for wearable integration.
AI.conclusion: RL-based anticipatory cueing for FOG is viable, offering longer pre-onset horizons and personalized intervention opportunities; future work should address real-time deployment, generalization, and user-specific adaptation.

title: Test-Time Meta-Adaptation with Self-Synthesis
comment: 5 pages, 2 figures, 1 table. Accepted to ICLR 2026 LIT and Data-FM Workshops
summary: As strong general reasoners, large language models (LLMs) encounter diverse domains and tasks, where the ability to adapt and self-improve at test time is valuable. We introduce MASS, a meta-learning framework that enables LLMs to self-adapt by generating problem-specific synthetic training data and performing targeted self-updates optimized for downstream performance at inference time. We train this behavior end-to-end via bilevel optimization: an inner loop adapts on self-generated examples while an outer loop meta-learns data-attribution signals and rewards post-update task performance. The synthetic data is optimized with scalable meta-gradients, backpropagating the downstream loss through the inner updates to reward useful generations. Experiments on mathematical reasoning show that MASS learns to synthesize per-instance curricula that yield effective, data-efficient test-time adaptation.

AI.tldr: MASS enables LLMs to self-adapt at test time by generating synthetic, problem-specific training data and performing targeted self-updates. It uses bilevel optimization where an inner loop updates on self-generated data and an outer loop learns data-attribution signals and rewards based on post-update performance. Demonstrated on mathematical reasoning, MASS creates per-instance curricula that improve data-efficient test-time adaptation.
AI.motivation: Address the inability of generalist LLMs to adapt efficiently to new tasks and domains at test time. Leverage self-generated data and meta-learning to guide beneficial updates that improve downstream performance.
AI.method: Train end-to-end via bilevel optimization: inner loop adapts on self-generated examples; outer loop meta-learns data-attribution signals and rewards for post-update task performance. Synthetic data are optimized with scalable meta-gradients by backpropagating downstream loss through inner updates to encourage useful generations.
AI.result: On mathematical reasoning tasks, MASS learns to synthesize per-instance curricula that enable effective and data-efficient test-time adaptation.
AI.conclusion: A scalable meta-learning framework enabling LLMs to self-adapt at test time through self-generated data and bilevel optimization, demonstrating improved data efficiency in reasoning tasks.

title: Swimming Under Constraints: A Safe Reinforcement Learning Framework for Quadrupedal Bio-Inspired Propulsion
summary: Bio-inspired aquatic propulsion offers high thrust and maneuverability but is prone to destabilizing forces such as lift fluctuations, which are further amplified by six-degree-of-freedom (6-DoF) fluid coupling. We formulate quadrupedal swimming as a constrained optimization problem that maximizes forward thrust while minimizing destabilizing fluctuations. Our proposed framework, Accelerated Constrained Proximal Policy Optimization with a PID-regulated Lagrange multiplier (ACPPO-PID), enforces constraints with a PID-regulated Lagrange multiplier, accelerates learning via conditional asymmetric clipping, and stabilizes updates through cycle-wise geometric aggregation. Initialized with imitation learning and refined through on-hardware towing-tank experiments, ACPPO-PID produces control policies that transfer effectively to quadrupedal free-swimming trials. Results demonstrate improved thrust efficiency, reduced destabilizing forces, and faster convergence compared with state-of-the-art baselines, underscoring the importance of constraint-aware safe RL for robust and generalizable bio-inspired locomotion in complex fluid environments.

AI.tldr: Introduces ACPPO-PID, a constrained RL framework for quadrupedal, bio-inspired swimming that maximizes forward thrust while minimizing destabilizing lift fluctuations under 6-DoF fluid coupling; augmented with PID-regulated Lagrange multipliers, conditional asymmetric clipping, and cycle-wise geometric aggregation; demonstrates hardware transfer and superior performance vs baselines.
AI.motivation: Address destabilizing hydrodynamic forces in bio-inspired aquatic locomotion and the need for constraint-aware safe reinforcement learning to ensure robustness and generalization in complex fluid environments.
AI.method: Formulates quadrupedal swimming as a constrained optimization problem. Proposes ACPPO-PID (Accelerated Constrained Proximal Policy Optimization with a PID-regulated Lagrange multiplier) to enforce constraints via a PID-regulated Lagrange multiplier, accelerate learning through conditional asymmetric clipping, and stabilize updates through cycle-wise geometric aggregation. The approach is initialized with imitation learning and refined through on-hardware towing-tank experiments, enabling transfer to quadrupedal free-swimming trials.
AI.result: Achieves improved thrust efficiency and reduced destabilizing forces, with faster convergence compared to state-of-the-art baselines. Demonstrates effective transfer from towing-tank-trained policies to quadrupedal free-swimming trials.
AI.conclusion: Constraint-aware safe RL is crucial for robust and generalizable bio-inspired locomotion in complex fluid environments. The ACPPO-PID framework yields superior performance and transferability, highlighting the importance of integrating safety constraints into learning for aquatic propulsion.

title: DAGE: Dual-Stream Architecture for Efficient and Fine-Grained Geometry Estimation
summary: Estimating accurate, view-consistent geometry and camera poses from uncalibrated multi-view/video inputs remains challenging - especially at high spatial resolutions and over long sequences. We present DAGE, a dual-stream transformer whose main novelty is to disentangle global coherence from fine detail. A low-resolution stream operates on aggressively downsampled frames with alternating frame/global attention to build a view-consistent representation and estimate cameras efficiently, while a high-resolution stream processes the original images per-frame to preserve sharp boundaries and small structures. A lightweight adapter fuses these streams via cross-attention, injecting global context without disturbing the pretrained single-frame pathway. This design scales resolution and clip length independently, supports inputs up to 2K, and maintains practical inference cost. DAGE delivers sharp depth/pointmaps, strong cross-view consistency, and accurate poses, establishing new state-of-the-art results for video geometry estimation and multi-view reconstruction.

AI.tldr: DAGE is a dual-stream transformer that separates global view-consistency from fine detail to enable accurate, scalable video geometry estimation and camera pose from uncalibrated multi-view inputs, combining a low-res global stream with a high-res per-frame stream via a lightweight cross-attention adapter.
AI.motivation: Accurately estimating geometry and camera poses from uncalibrated multi-view videos is computationally hard at high resolutions and long sequences due to the need for global coherence and fine detail preservation. Existing methods struggle to balance view-consistency, detail, and efficiency.
AI.method: A dual-stream architecture: (1) a low-resolution stream on aggressively downsampled frames with alternating frame/global attention to capture global coherence and estimate cameras efficiently; (2) a high-resolution stream processing original frames per-frame to preserve sharp boundaries and small structures. A lightweight adapter fuses the streams via cross-attention, injecting global context into the single-frame pathway without overwriting it. The design allows independent scaling of resolution and clip length and supports inputs up to 2K while maintaining practical inference cost.
AI.result: DAGE achieves sharp depth/pointmaps, strong cross-view consistency, and accurate poses, and sets new state-of-the-art results for video geometry estimation and multi-view reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: Disentangling global coherence from fine detail with a dual-stream transformer enables scalable, efficient video geometry estimation and multi-view reconstruction at high resolutions, preserving per-frame detail while maintaining view-consistent global context.

title: Graph Negative Feedback Bias Correction Framework for Adaptive Heterophily Modeling
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have emerged as a powerful framework for processing graph-structured data. However, conventional GNNs and their variants are inherently limited by the homophily assumption, leading to degradation in performance on heterophilic graphs. Although substantial efforts have been made to mitigate this issue, they remain constrained by the message-passing paradigm, which is inherently rooted in homophily. In this paper, a detailed analysis of how the underlying label autocorrelation of the homophily assumption introduces bias into GNNs is presented. We innovatively leverage a negative feedback mechanism to correct the bias and propose Graph Negative Feedback Bias Correction (GNFBC), a simple yet effective framework that is independent of any specific aggregation strategy. Specifically, we introduce a negative feedback loss that penalizes the sensitivity of predictions to label autocorrelation. Furthermore, we incorporate the output of graph-agnostic models as a feedback term, leveraging independent node feature information to counteract correlation-induced bias guided by Dirichlet energy. GNFBC can be seamlessly integrated into existing GNN architectures, improving overall performance with comparable computational and memory overhead.

AI.tldr: GNFBC introduces negative feedback bias correction for GNNs to address label autocorrelation under homophily assumptions, using a negative feedback loss and graph-agnostic feedback grounded in Dirichlet energy, enabling integration with existing GNNs with low overhead.
AI.motivation: Conventional GNNs rely on homophily, causing performance degradation on heterophilic graphs due to bias from label autocorrelation. A need exists for a framework that reduces this bias without depending on specific aggregation schemes.
AI.method: Propose Graph Negative Feedback Bias Correction (GNFBC). Introduce a negative feedback loss that penalizes sensitivity of predictions to label autocorrelation. Incorporate feedback from graph-agnostic model outputs as an auxiliary term, leveraging independent node features and guiding correction via Dirichlet energy. GNFBC is designed to be plug-in to existing GNN architectures with minimal computational and memory overhead.
AI.result: Claimed enhancement in overall performance on heterogeneous graphs with comparable computational/memory overhead to standard GNNs, without being tied to any particular aggregation strategy.
AI.conclusion: GNFBC offers a simple, effective, and architecture-agnostic bias correction mechanism for heterophilic graphs by suppressing label autocorrelation effects through negative feedback and leveraging graph-agnostic feedback, enabling broader applicability across GNN variants.

title: mlx-snn: Spiking Neural Networks on Apple Silicon via MLX
summary: We introduce mlx-snn, the first spiking neural network (SNN) library built natively on Apple's MLX framework. As SNN research grows rapidly, all major libraries -- snnTorch, Norse, SpikingJelly, Lava -- target PyTorch or custom backends, leaving Apple Silicon users without a native option. mlx-snn provides six neuron models (LIF, IF, Izhikevich, Adaptive LIF, Synaptic, Alpha), four surrogate gradient functions, four spike encoding methods (including an EEG-specific encoder), and a complete backpropagation-through-time training pipeline. The library leverages MLX's unified memory architecture, lazy evaluation, and composable function transforms (mx.grad, mx.compile) to enable efficient SNN research on Apple Silicon hardware. We validate mlx-snn on MNIST digit classification across five hyperparameter configurations and three backends, achieving up to 97.28% accuracy with 2.0--2.5 times faster training and 3--10 times lower GPU memory than snnTorch on the same M3 Max hardware. mlx-snn is open-source under the MIT license and available on PyPI. https://github.com/D-ST-Sword/mlx-snn

AI.tldr: First native SNN library for Apple's MLX framework; supports 6 neuron models, 4 surrogate gradients, 4 spike encodings (including EEG-specific); efficient BPTT training on Apple Silicon with significant speed/memory gains; MIT-licensed and open-source on PyPI.
AI.motivation: Existing SNN libraries target PyTorch or custom backends, leaving Apple Silicon users without a native SNN toolchain and hindering native MLX ecosystem adoption.
AI.method: Implement mlx-snn with six neuron models (LIF, IF, Izhikevich, Adaptive LIF, Synaptic, Alpha), four surrogate gradients, four spike encoding methods (including EEG-specific encoder), and a full backpropagation-through-time training pipeline. Leverage MLX features (unified memory, lazy evaluation, mx.grad, mx.compile) and validate on MNIST across five hyperparameter configurations and three backends.
AI.result: Demonstrates MNIST accuracy up to 97.28% with 2.0–2.5× faster training and 3–10× reduced GPU memory compared to snnTorch on the same M3 Max hardware.
AI.conclusion: mlx-snn delivers a native, efficient SNN research platform for Apple Silicon, enabling faster experimentation with reduced memory footprint; open-source under MIT and available on PyPI (GitHub link provided).

title: Modeling and Control of a Pneumatic Soft Robotic Catheter Using Neural Koopman Operators
comment: 8 pages, 6 figures. Accepted to IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA) 2026
summary: Catheter-based interventions are widely used for the diagnosis and treatment of cardiac diseases. Recently, robotic catheters have attracted attention for their ability to improve precision and stability over conventional manual approaches. However, accurate modeling and control of soft robotic catheters remain challenging due to their complex, nonlinear behavior. The Koopman operator enables lifting the original system data into a linear "lifted space", offering a data-driven framework for predictive control; however, manually chosen basis functions in the lifted space often oversimplify system behaviors and degrade control performance. To address this, we propose a neural network-enhanced Koopman operator framework that jointly learns the lifted space representation and Koopman operator in an end-to-end manner. Moreover, motivated by the need to minimize radiation exposure during X-ray fluoroscopy in cardiac ablation, we investigate open-loop control strategies using neural Koopman operators to reliably reach target poses without continuous imaging feedback. The proposed method is validated in two experimental scenarios: interactive position control and a simulated cardiac ablation task using an atrium-like cavity. Our approach achieves average errors of 2.1 +- 0.4 mm in position and 4.9 +- 0.6 degrees in orientation, outperforming not only model-based baselines but also other Koopman variants in targeting accuracy and efficiency. These results highlight the potential of the proposed framework for advancing soft robotic catheter systems and improving catheter-based interventions.

AI.tldr: End-to-end neural Koopman framework that jointly learns lifted-space representation and operator for soft robotic catheter control, enabling open-loop, radiation-minimizing targeting; validated in interactive and simulated atrial ablation tasks with improved accuracy.
AI.motivation: Nonlinear, soft robotic catheter dynamics challenge accurate modeling and control; manually selected Koopman bases often oversimplify behavior, limiting performance especially for radiation-free open-loop control.
AI.method: Neural network jointly learns the lifted-space representation and the Koopman operator in an end-to-end manner. Applies open-loop control to reach target poses with reduced fluoroscopy. Evaluated in two experimental scenarios: interactive position control and simulated atrial ablation in an atrium-like cavity.
AI.result: Average position error 2.1 ± 0.4 mm and orientation error 4.9 ± 0.6 degrees. Outperforms model-based baselines and other Koopman variants in targeting accuracy and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the potential of a neural Koopman-based framework to improve soft robotic catheter control and enable safer, radiation-minimized interventions; suggests benefits of end-to-end learned lifted representations for complex biomedical robotics.

title: WSI-INR: Implicit Neural Representations for Lesion Segmentation in Whole-Slide Images
summary: Whole-slide images (WSIs) are fundamental for computational pathology, where accurate lesion segmentation is critical for clinical decision making. Existing methods partition WSIs into discrete patches, disrupting spatial continuity and treating multi-resolution views as independent samples, which leads to spatially fragmented segmentation and reduced robustness to resolution variations. To address the issues, we propose WSI-INR, a novel patch-free framework based on Implicit Neural Representations (INRs). WSI-INR models the WSI as a continuous implicit function mapping spatial coordinates directly to tissue semantics features, outputting segmentation results while preserving intrinsic spatial information across the entire slide. In the WSI-INR, we incorporate multi-resolution hash grid encoding to regard different resolution levels as varying sampling densities of the same continuous tissue, achieving a consistent feature representation across resolutions. In addition, by jointly training a shared INR decoder, WSI-INR can capture general priors across different cases. Experimental results showed that WSI-INR maintains robust segmentation performance across resolutions; at Base/4, our resolution-specific optimization improves Dice score by +26.11%, while U-Net and TransUNet decrease by 54.28% and 36.18%, respectively. Crucially, this work enables INRs to segment highly heterogeneous pathological lesions beyond structurally consistent anatomical tissues, offering a fresh perspective for pathological analysis.

AI.tldr: Patch-free WSI segmentation via Implicit Neural Representations (INR) that preserves spatial continuity and enables cross-resolution consistency, outperforming patch-based baselines across resolutions.
AI.motivation: Patch-based WSIs break spatial continuity and treat multi-resolution views as independent, causing fragmented segmentation and reduced robustness to resolution changes.
AI.method: Model WSIs as a continuous implicit function mapping 2D coordinates to tissue-semantic features; use multi-resolution hash-grid encoding to handle varying sampling densities; train a shared INR decoder jointly across cases.
AI.result: Robust segmentation across resolutions; at Base/4, Dice improves by +26.11% while U-Net and TransUNet degrade by -54.28% and -36.18%; demonstrates ability to segment heterogeneous lesions and preserve spatial information.
AI.conclusion: WSI-INR offers a novel, patch-free INR-based paradigm for pathology that maintains spatial continuity across scales and generalizes across cases; potential for broader lesion analysis.

title: Directional Neural Collapse Explains Few-Shot Transfer in Self-Supervised Learning
summary: Frozen self-supervised representations often transfer well with only a few labels across many semantic tasks. We argue that a single geometric quantity, \emph{directional} CDNV (decision-axis variance), sits at the core of two favorable behaviors: strong few-shot transfer within a task, and low interference across many tasks. We show that both emerge when variability \emph{along} class-separating directions is small. First, we prove sharp non-asymptotic multiclass generalization bounds for downstream classification whose leading term is the directional CDNV. The bounds include finite-shot corrections that cleanly separate intrinsic decision-axis variability from centroid-estimation error. Second, we link decision-axis collapse to multitask geometry: for independent balanced labelings, small directional CDNV across tasks forces the corresponding decision axes to be nearly orthogonal, helping a single representation support many tasks with minimal interference. Empirically, across SSL objectives, directional CDNV collapses during pretraining even when classical CDNV remains large, and our bounds closely track few-shot error at practical shot sizes. Additionally, on synthetic multitask data, we verify that SSL learns representations whose induced decision axes are nearly orthogonal. The code and project page of the paper are available at [\href{https://dlfundamentals.github.io/directional-neural-collapse/}{project page}].

AI.tldr: Directional CDNV is identified as the central geometric quantity governing both strong few-shot transfer within tasks and low interference across many tasks in frozen SSL representations. The paper shows non-asymptotic multiclass generalization bounds where the leading term is directional CDNV, decomposes error into decision-axis variability and centroid-estimation error, and links small directional CDNV to near-orthogonal decision axes across tasks. Empirically, directional CDNV collapses during pretraining (even when classical CDNV remains large), and synthetic multitask experiments confirm nearly orthogonal decision axes. Code available on the project page.
AI.motivation: To identify a single geometric quantity that explains both large within-task few-shot transfer and low cross-task interference in frozen self-supervised representations, unifying generalization and multitask geometry.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis yielding sharp non-asymptotic multiclass generalization bounds with the leading term being directional CDNV; decomposition separating decision-axis variability from centroid-estimation error; a multitask geometry argument showing that small directional CDNV induces near-orthogonality of decision axes across tasks; empirical verification across SSL objectives and synthetic data, with tracking of directional CDNV during pretraining.
AI.result: Leading-term bounds depend on directional CDNV and align with observed few-shot error; directional CDNV collapses during pretraining even when classical CDNV does not; decision axes become nearly orthogonal across independent balanced tasks under small directional CDNV; empirical and synthetic data support the proposed interpretation.
AI.conclusion: Directional CDNV captures the essential geometry linking few-shot transfer and multitask robustness in frozen SSL representations. The results provide both theoretical guarantees and empirical evidence that variability along class-separating directions governs generalization and interference, suggesting a geometric lens for SSL representation analysis and potential design implications.

title: HBRB-BoW: A Retrained Bag-of-Words Vocabulary for ORB-SLAM via Hierarchical BRB-KMeans
summary: In visual simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM), the quality of the visual vocabulary is fundamental to the system's ability to represent environments and recognize locations. While ORB-SLAM is a widely used framework, its binary vocabulary, trained through the k-majority-based bag-of-words (BoW) approach, suffers from inherent precision loss. The inability of conventional binary clustering to represent subtle feature distributions leads to the degradation of visual words, a problem that is compounded as errors accumulate and propagate through the hierarchical tree structure. To address these structural deficiencies, this paper proposes hierarchical binary-to-real-and-back (HBRB)-BoW, a refined hierarchical binary vocabulary training algorithm. By integrating a global real-valued flow within the hierarchical clustering process, our method preserves high-fidelity descriptor information until the final binarization at the leaf nodes. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach yields a more discriminative and well-structured vocabulary than traditional methods, significantly enhancing the representational integrity of the visual dictionary in complex environments. Furthermore, replacing the default ORB-SLAM vocabulary file with our HBRB-BoW file is expected to improve performance in loop closing and relocalization tasks.

AI.tldr: Hierarchical binary-to-real-and-back BoW (HBRB-BoW) improves ORB-SLAM vocabularies by preserving descriptor fidelity during hierarchical clustering, yielding a more discriminative and well-structured vocabulary and improving loop closure/relocalization potential.
AI.motivation: Binary BoW in ORB-SLAM suffers precision loss from binary clustering; errors propagate in the hierarchical tree degrading visual words; need a vocabulary training method that preserves descriptor information to improve SLAM robustness in complex environments.
AI.method: Introduce HBRB-BoW by integrating a global real-valued flow within the hierarchical clustering process, maintaining high-fidelity descriptor information until final leaf binarization; train a refined hierarchical vocabulary and replace the default binary vocabulary with HBRB-BoW.
AI.result: Experimental results show more discriminative, better-structured vocabulary than traditional methods; improved representational integrity of the visual dictionary; expected improvements in loop closing and relocalization when using HBRB-BoW in place of standard vocab.
AI.conclusion: HBRB-BoW enhances visual vocabulary quality for ORB-SLAM, improving SLAM robustness in complex environments by mitigating precision loss in binary BoW and aiding loop closure and relocalization tasks.

title: Seeing as Experts Do: A Knowledge-Augmented Agent for Open-Set Fine-Grained Visual Understanding
summary: Fine-grained visual understanding is shifting from static classification to knowledge-augmented reasoning, where models must justify as well as recognise. Existing approaches remain limited by closed-set taxonomies and single-label prediction, leading to significant degradation under open-set or context-dependent conditions. We present the Knowledge-Augmented Fine-Grained Reasoning Agent (KFRA), a unified framework that transforms fine-grained perception into evidence-driven reasoning. KFRA operates through a three-stage closed reasoning loop that emulates expert analysis. It first performs open-vocabulary detection and web-scale retrieval to generate category hypotheses. It then conducts discriminative regions localisation by aligning textual knowledge with visual evidence through a global-to-local focusing mechanism. Finally, it integrates all multimodal evidence within a large multimodal model to perform interpretable reasoning. Unlike existing agents that treat retrieval and reasoning as independent processes, KFRA establishes a retrieval-grounding coupling that converts retrieved knowledge into spatially grounded evidence for verification. This design enables factual, interpretable, and task-agnostic reasoning across diverse fine-grained scenarios. To evaluate this capability, we construct FGExpertBench, a benchmark designed to assess reasoning depth and cross-task generalisation across six knowledge dimensions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that KFRA consistently surpasses both standalone large multimodal models and current agent frameworks, achieving up to 19 percent improvement in reasoning accuracy and delivering evidence-grounded interpretability in open-set fine-grained visual understanding.

AI.tldr: KFRA is a three-stage knowledge-augmented reasoning agent for fine-grained visual understanding that combines open-set detection, knowledge-grounded region localization, and multimodal reasoning, evaluated on FGExpertBench with demonstrated improvements.
AI.motivation: Overcome limitations of closed-set, single-label fine-grained recognition and the lack of integrated, explainable reasoning by enabling evidence-driven, open-world inference and interpretability.
AI.method: A three-stage closed reasoning loop: (1) open-vocabulary detection and web-scale retrieval to form category hypotheses; (2) discriminative region localization by aligning textual knowledge with visual evidence via a global-to-local focusing mechanism; (3) integration of all multimodal evidence in a large multimodal model to perform interpretable reasoning. A retrieval-grounding coupling converts retrieved knowledge into spatially grounded evidence for verification.
AI.result: KFRA outperforms standalone large multimodal models and existing agent frameworks, with up to 19% relative improvement in reasoning accuracy and evidence-grounded interpretability across open-set fine-grained tasks, demonstrated on FGExpertBench spanning six knowledge dimensions.
AI.conclusion: KFRA enables factual, interpretable, and task-agnostic reasoning for open-set fine-grained vision, with the retrieval-grounding coupling being central to robust reasoning and cross-task generalization; FGExpertBench provides a rigorous evaluation of reasoning depth and generalization.

title: A Stein Identity for q-Gaussians with Bounded Support
summary: Stein's identity is a fundamental tool in machine learning with applications in generative models, stochastic optimization, and other problems involving gradients of expectations under Gaussian distributions. Less attention has been paid to problems with non-Gaussian expectations. Here, we consider the class of bounded-support $q$-Gaussians and derive a new Stein identity leading to gradient estimators which have nearly identical forms to the Gaussian ones, and which are similarly easy to implement. We do this by extending the previous results of Landsman, Vanduffel, and Yao (2013) to prove new Bonnet- and Price-type theorems for q-Gaussians. We also simplify their forms by using escort distributions. Our experiments show that bounded-support distributions can reduce the variance of gradient estimators, which can potentially be useful for Bayesian deep learning and sharpness-aware minimization. Overall, our work simplifies the application of Stein's identity for an important class of non-Gaussian distributions.

AI.tldr: Extends Stein's identity to bounded-support q-Gaussians, deriving Bonnet- and Price-type theorems and using escort distributions to yield Gaussian-like gradient estimators with potential variance reduction; practical for Bayesian DL and sharpness-aware minimization.
AI.motivation: Broaden Stein-methods beyond Gaussian expectations to non-Gaussian, bounded-support distributions to enable efficient gradient estimators in learning and optimization.
AI.method: Generalize Landsman–Vanduffel–Yao (2013) results to q-Gaussians; prove Bonnet- and Price-type theorems for q-Gaussians; simplify via escort distributions; derive practical gradient estimators with implementation-friendly form.
AI.result: Derived a Stein identity for bounded-support q-Gaussians; gradient estimators maintain a form close to the Gaussian case; potential variance reduction observed in experiments; estimators are easy to implement.
AI.conclusion: Provides a simpler, more general Stein framework for non-Gaussian distributions (q-Gaussians); enhances applicability to Bayesian deep learning and sharpness-aware minimization by potentially reducing estimator variance and maintaining computational simplicity.

title: Role-Aware Conditional Inference for Spatiotemporal Ecosystem Carbon Flux Prediction
summary: Accurate prediction of terrestrial ecosystem carbon fluxes (e.g., CO$_2$, GPP, and CH$_4$) is essential for understanding the global carbon cycle and managing its impacts. However, prediction remains challenging due to strong spatiotemporal heterogeneity: ecosystem flux responses are constrained by slowly varying regime conditions, while short-term fluctuations are driven by high-frequency dynamic forcings. Most existing learning-based approaches treat environmental covariates as a homogeneous input space, implicitly assuming a global response function, which leads to brittle generalization across heterogeneous ecosystems. In this work, we propose Role-Aware Conditional Inference (RACI), a process-informed learning framework that formulates ecosystem flux prediction as a conditional inference problem. RACI employs hierarchical temporal encoding to disentangle slow regime conditioners from fast dynamic drivers, and incorporates role-aware spatial retrieval that supplies functionally similar and geographically local context for each role. By explicitly modeling these distinct functional roles, RACI enables a model to adapt its predictions across diverse environmental regimes without training separate local models or relying on fixed spatial structures. We evaluate RACI across multiple ecosystem types (wetlands and agricultural systems), carbon fluxes (CO$_2$, GPP, CH$_4$), and data sources, including both process-based simulations and observational measurements. Across all settings, RACI consistently outperforms competitive spatiotemporal baselines, demonstrating improved accuracy and spatial generalization under pronounced environmental heterogeneity.

AI.tldr: RACI introduces a Role-Aware Conditional Inference framework for predicting ecosystem carbon fluxes under heterogeneous regimes, using hierarchical temporal encoding and role-aware spatial retrieval to adapt to different environmental roles. This yields improved accuracy and spatial generalization across diverse ecosystems (wetlands and agriculture) for CO2, GPP, and CH4, validated on both process-based simulations and observations.
AI.motivation: Prediction of terrestrial ecosystem carbon fluxes is hindered by strong spatiotemporal heterogeneity. Existing learning-based models treat covariates as a homogeneous input space, assuming a global response function, which fails to generalize across heterogeneous ecosystems. There is a need for a process-informed, conditional inference approach that can adapt to different environmental regimes without requiring many local models or fixed spatial structures.
AI.method: RACI frames flux prediction as a conditional inference problem. It uses hierarchical temporal encoding to disentangle slow regime conditioners from fast dynamic drivers and employs role-aware spatial retrieval to provide functionally similar and geographically local context for each role. This enables a single model to adapt predictions across diverse environmental regimes without training separate local models or relying on fixed spatial structures.
AI.result: Across multiple ecosystem types (wetlands and agricultural systems), carbon fluxes (CO2, GPP, CH4) and data sources (process-based simulations and observations), RACI consistently outperforms competitive spatiotemporal baselines, showing improved accuracy and spatial generalization in the presence of pronounced environmental heterogeneity.
AI.conclusion: Modeling distinct functional roles explicitly through conditional inference and context-aware retrieval enables robust, generalizable flux predictions across heterogeneous ecosystems without resorting to numerous local models or rigid spatial priors.

title: GarmentPile++: Affordance-Driven Cluttered Garments Retrieval with Vision-Language Reasoning
summary: Garment manipulation has attracted increasing attention due to its critical role in home-assistant robotics. However, the majority of existing garment manipulation works assume an initial state consisting of only one garment, while piled garments are far more common in real-world settings. To bridge this gap, we propose a novel garment retrieval pipeline that can not only follow language instruction to execute safe and clean retrieval but also guarantee exactly one garment is retrieved per attempt, establishing a robust foundation for the execution of downstream tasks (e.g., folding, hanging, wearing). Our pipeline seamlessly integrates vision-language reasoning with visual affordance perception, fully leveraging the high-level reasoning and planning capabilities of VLMs alongside the generalization power of visual affordance for low-level actions. To enhance the VLM's comprehensive awareness of each garment's state within a garment pile, we employ visual segmentation model (SAM2) to execute object segmentation on the garment pile for aiding VLM-based reasoning with sufficient visual cues. A mask fine-tuning mechanism is further integrated to address scenarios where the initial segmentation results are suboptimal. In addition, a dual-arm cooperation framework is deployed to address cases involving large or long garments, as well as excessive garment sagging caused by incorrect grasping point determination, both of which are strenuous for a single arm to handle. The effectiveness of our pipeline are consistently demonstrated across diverse tasks and varying scenarios in both real-world and simulation environments. Project page: https://garmentpile2.github.io/.

AI.tldr: Proposes a garment retrieval pipeline for piles that guarantees exactly one garment per attempt by combining vision-language reasoning with visual affordance perception, enhanced by SAM2 segmentation and a mask-fine-tuning mechanism, plus a dual-arm cooperation framework; evaluated in real and simulated settings.
AI.motivation: Real-world garment manipulation often involves piles of garments, not single items, making safe, single-item retrieval difficult. A robust, generalizable pipeline is needed to enable downstream tasks (folding, hanging, wearing) with guaranteed one-item retrieval per attempt.
AI.method: Integrates vision-language reasoning (VLMs) with visual affordance perception; uses SAM2-based garment pile segmentation to provide visual cues for VLM reasoning; applies mask fine-tuning to improve segmentation when needed; employs a dual-arm cooperation framework to handle large/long garments and sagging due to grasping point errors.
