AI.result: Demonstrates effectiveness across diverse tasks and scenarios in both real-world and simulation environments, showcasing robust single-garment retrieval from piles and supporting downstream manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: The pipeline provides a robust foundation for downstream garment manipulation tasks by combining high-level VLM planning with low-level visual affordance cues, segmentation refinement, and dual-arm coordination to handle complex pile states.

title: LiDAR Prompted Spatio-Temporal Multi-View Stereo for Autonomous Driving
summary:
Accurate metric depth is critical for autonomous driving perception and simulation, yet current approaches struggle to achieve high metric accuracy, multi-view and temporal consistency, and cross-domain generalization. To address these challenges, we present DriveMVS, a novel multi-view stereo framework that reconciles these competing objectives through two key insights:
  (1) Sparse but metrically accurate LiDAR observations can serve as geometric prompts to anchor depth estimation in absolute scale, and (2) deep fusion of diverse cues is essential for resolving ambiguities and enhancing robustness, while a spatio-temporal decoder ensures consistency across frames. Built upon these principles, DriveMVS embeds the LiDAR prompt in two ways: as a hard geometric prior that anchors the cost volume, and as soft feature-wise guidance fused by a triple-cue combiner. Regarding temporal consistency, DriveMVS employs a spatio-temporal decoder that jointly leverages geometric cues from the MVS cost volume and temporal context from neighboring frames. Experiments show that DriveMVS achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks, excelling in metric accuracy, temporal stability, and zero-shot cross-domain transfer, demonstrating its practical value for scalable, reliable autonomous driving systems.

AI.tldr: DriveMVS is a multi-view stereo framework that uses sparse LiDAR observations as both hard geometric priors and soft feature prompts, fused with a triple-cue combiner, plus a spatio-temporal decoder to ensure metric, robust, and temporally consistent depth estimation, achieving state-of-the-art results and cross-domain transfer.
AI.motivation: To overcome metric depth accuracy, multi-view/temporal consistency, and cross-domain generalization gaps in MVS for autonomous driving by leveraging LiDAR as geometric prompts and deep fusion of diverse cues.
AI.method: Embed LiDAR prompts in two ways: (1) as a hard geometric prior anchoring the cost volume; (2) as soft feature-wise guidance via a triple-cue combiner. Use a spatio-temporal decoder that fuses MVS cost-volume geometry with temporal context from neighboring frames for consistency.
AI.result: Reported state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks, excelling in metric accuracy, temporal stability, and zero-shot cross-domain transfer.
AI.conclusion: Combining sparse, metrically accurate LiDAR prompts with deep fusion and a spatio-temporal decoder yields robust, scalable, and well-generalizing metric depth for autonomous driving.

title: Why Do Unlearnable Examples Work: A Novel Perspective of Mutual Information
summary: The volume of freely scraped data on the Internet has driven the tremendous success of deep learning. Along with this comes the growing concern about data privacy and security. Numerous methods for generating unlearnable examples have been proposed to prevent data from being illicitly learned by unauthorized deep models by impeding generalization. However, the existing approaches primarily rely on empirical heuristics, making it challenging to enhance unlearnable examples with solid explanations. In this paper, we analyze and improve unlearnable examples from a novel perspective: mutual information reduction. We demonstrate that effective unlearnable examples always decrease mutual information between clean features and poisoned features, and when the network gets deeper, the unlearnability goes better together with lower mutual information. Further, we prove from a covariance reduction perspective that minimizing the conditional covariance of intra-class poisoned features reduces the mutual information between distributions. Based on the theoretical results, we propose a novel unlearnable method called Mutual Information Unlearnable Examples (MI-UE) that reduces covariance by maximizing the cosine similarity among intra-class features, thus impeding the generalization effectively. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms the previous methods, even under defense mechanisms.

AI.tldr: MI-UE provides a principled, mutual-information–based framework for generating unlearnable examples by reducing intra-class feature covariance; increasing intra-class cosine similarity to lower MI and impede generalization, with strong empirical results.
AI.motivation: To move beyond heuristic unlearnable examples by grounding the method in information-theoretic principles; show that reducing mutual information between clean and poisoned features yields stronger unlearnability, especially as networks deepen.
AI.method: The authors analyze unlearnable examples through mutual information and covariance reductions. They prove that decreasing conditional covariance of intra-class poisoned features reduces MI between distributions. Building on this, they introduce MI-UE, which maximizes cosine similarity among intra-class features to reduce covariance and thereby MI, hindering generalization.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate MI-UE significantly outperforms prior methods, including under defense mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: A principled MI-based framework for unlearnable examples with theoretical support and robust empirical performance; deeper models amplify unlearnability via lower mutual information.

title: Image-based Prompt Injection: Hijacking Multimodal LLMs through Visually Embedded Adversarial Instructions
comment: 7 pages, published in 2025 3rd International Conference on Foundation and Large Language Models (FLLM), Vienna, Austria
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) integrate vision and text to power applications, but this integration introduces new vulnerabilities. We study Image-based Prompt Injection (IPI), a black-box attack in which adversarial instructions are embedded into natural images to override model behavior. Our end-to-end IPI pipeline incorporates segmentation-based region selection, adaptive font scaling, and background-aware rendering to conceal prompts from human perception while preserving model interpretability. Using the COCO dataset and GPT-4-turbo, we evaluate 12 adversarial prompt strategies and multiple embedding configurations. The results show that IPI can reliably manipulate the output of the model, with the most effective configuration achieving up to 64\% attack success under stealth constraints. These findings highlight IPI as a practical threat in black-box settings and underscore the need for defenses against multimodal prompt injection.

AI.tldr: End-to-end image-based prompt injection can reliably manipulate multimodal models in black-box settings, achieving up to 64% success under stealth, underscoring a practical security threat and the need for defenses.
AI.motivation: Exposes vulnerability of Multimodal Large Language Models to image-based prompt injection and evaluates attack viability in realistic, black-box scenarios.
AI.method: An end-to-end attack pipeline with segmentation-based region selection, adaptive font scaling, and background-aware rendering. Evaluation on COCO with GPT-4-turbo across 12 adversarial prompt strategies and multiple embedding configurations.
AI.result: IPI attacks can significantly influence model outputs, with up to 64% success under stealth constraints, demonstrating practicality and generalizability across configurations.
AI.conclusion: IPI constitutes a practical threat to multimodal systems; highlights urgent need for robust defenses and defense-aware evaluation in MLLMs.

title: Learning Hip Exoskeleton Control Policy via Predictive Neuromusculoskeletal Simulation
summary: Developing exoskeleton controllers that generalize across diverse locomotor conditions typically requires extensive motion-capture data and biomechanical labeling, limiting scalability beyond instrumented laboratory settings. Here, we present a physics-based neuromusculoskeletal learning framework that trains a hip-exoskeleton control policy entirely in simulation, without motion-capture demonstrations, and deploys it on hardware via policy distillation. A reinforcement learning teacher policy is trained using a muscle-synergy action prior over a wide range of walking speeds and slopes through a two-stage curriculum, enabling direct comparison between assisted and no-exoskeleton conditions. In simulation, exoskeleton assistance reduces mean muscle activation by up to 3.4% and mean positive joint power by up to 7.0% on level ground and ramp ascent, with benefits increasing systematically with walking speed. On hardware, the assistance profiles learned in simulation are preserved across matched speed-slope conditions (r: 0.82, RMSE: 0.03 Nm/kg), providing quantitative evidence of sim-to-real transfer without additional hardware tuning. These results demonstrate that physics-based neuromusculoskeletal simulation can serve as a practical and scalable foundation for exoskeleton controller development, substantially reducing experimental burden during the design phase.

AI.tldr: A physics-based neuromusculoskeletal framework trained in simulation with a muscle-synergy prior enables sim-to-real transfer of hip-exoskeleton controllers through policy distillation, reducing muscle activation and joint power and preserving learned policies on hardware across speed–slope conditions.
AI.motivation: Overcome the need for lengthy motion-capture data and biomechanical labeling to generalize exoskeleton control across locomotor conditions, enabling scalable design beyond instrumented labs.
AI.method: Train a reinforcement learning teacher policy using a muscle-synergy action prior over diverse speeds and slopes with a two-stage curriculum; simulate hip-exoskeleton assistance; distill the policy to hardware; compare assisted vs no-exoskeleton; evaluate sim-to-real transfer using correlation and RMSE between simulation and hardware.
AI.result: In simulation, exoskeleton assistance reduces mean muscle activation by up to 3.4% and mean positive joint power by up to 7.0% on level ground and ramp ascent, with larger benefits at higher speeds. On hardware, learned assistance profiles are preserved across matched speed-slope conditions (r=0.82, RMSE=0.03 Nm/kg).
AI.conclusion: Physics-based neuromusculoskeletal simulation provides a practical, scalable foundation for exoskeleton controller development, substantially reducing experimental burden during the design phase.

title: DMD-augmented Unpaired Neural Schrödinger Bridge for Ultra-Low Field MRI Enhancement
summary: Ultra Low Field (64 mT) brain MRI improves accessibility but suffers from reduced image quality compared to 3 T. As paired 64 mT - 3 T scans are scarce, we propose an unpaired 64 mT $\rightarrow$ 3 T translation framework that enhances realism while preserving anatomy. Our method builds upon the Unpaired Neural Schrödinge Bridge (UNSB) with multi-step refinement. To strengthen target distribution alignment, we augment the adversarial objective with DMD2-style diffusion-guided distribution matching using a frozen 3T diffusion teacher. To explicitly constrain global structure beyond patch-level correspondence, we combine PatchNCE with an Anatomical Structure Preservation (ASP) regularizer that enforces soft foreground background consistency and boundary aware constraints. Evaluated on two disjoint cohorts, the proposed framework achieves an improved realism structure trade-off, enhancing distribution level realism on unpaired benchmarks while increasing structural fidelity on the paired cohort compared to unpaired baselines.

AI.tldr: Unpaired 64 mT → 3 T MRI translation using UNSB with diffusion-guided distribution matching and Anatomical Structure Preservation to boost realism while preserving anatomy.
AI.motivation: Scarcity of paired 64 mT–3 T scans creates a domain gap; a translation framework is needed to improve realism at 3 T without sacrificing anatomical fidelity across unpaired data.
AI.method: Builds on Unpaired Neural Schrödinger Bridge (UNSB) with multi-step refinement; adds DMD2-style diffusion-guided distribution matching via a frozen 3 T diffusion teacher; incorporates PatchNCE with an Anatomical Structure Preservation (ASP) regularizer enforcing soft foreground-background consistency and boundary-aware constraints.
AI.result: Evaluated on two disjoint cohorts; achieves an improved realism-structure trade-off, with higher distribution-level realism on unpaired benchmarks and increased structural fidelity on the paired cohort relative to unpaired baselines.
AI.conclusion: The framework delivers a favorable realism–structure balance for cross-field brain MRI translation, enabling more convincing 64 mT reconstructions that respect anatomical structure.

title: JANUS: Structured Bidirectional Generation for Guaranteed Constraints and Analytical Uncertainty
summary: High-stakes synthetic data generation faces a fundamental Quadrilemma: achieving Fidelity to the original distribution, Control over complex logical constraints, Reliability in uncertainty estimation, and Efficiency in computational cost -- simultaneously. State-of-the-art Deep Generative Models (CTGAN, TabDDPM) excel at fidelity but rely on inefficient rejection sampling for continuous range constraints. Conversely, Structural Causal Models offer logical control but struggle with high-dimensional fidelity and complex noise inversion. We introduce JANUS (Joint Ancestral Network for Uncertainty and Synthesis), a framework that unifies these capabilities using a DAG of Bayesian Decision Trees. Our key innovation is Reverse-Topological Back-filling, an algorithm that propagates constraints backwards through the causal graph, achieving 100% constraint satisfaction on feasible constraint sets without rejection sampling. This is paired with an Analytical Uncertainty Decomposition derived from Dirichlet priors, enabling 128x faster uncertainty estimation than Monte Carlo methods. Across 15 datasets and 523 constrained scenarios, JANUS achieves state-of-the-art fidelity (Detection Score 0.497), eliminates mode collapse on imbalanced data, and provides exact handling of complex inter-column constraints (e.g., Salary_offered >= Salary_requested) where baselines fail entirely.

AI.tldr: JANUS unifies high-fidelity synthetic data with strict constraint satisfaction and fast uncertainty estimation using a DAG of Bayesian Decision Trees. It employs Reverse-Topological Back-filling to enforce constraints without rejection sampling and Analytical Uncertainty Decomposition (from Dirichlet priors) to speed up uncertainty estimation (128x). Demonstrates state-of-the-art fidelity and exact inter-column constraint handling across 15 datasets and 523 scenarios.
AI.motivation: Addresses the Quadrilemma in high-stakes synthetic data: maintaining fidelity to the original distribution, enforcing complex logical constraints, obtaining reliable uncertainty estimates, and doing so efficiently. Current methods trade fidelity against constraint control or uncertainty or efficiency.
AI.method: JANUS constructs a DAG of Bayesian Decision Trees. It introduces Reverse-Topological Back-filling to propagate constraints backwards through the causal graph to achieve 100% constraint satisfaction on feasible sets without rejection sampling. It also uses Analytical Uncertainty Decomposition derived from Dirichlet priors to enable fast (128x) uncertainty estimation compared to Monte Carlo methods.
AI.result: Empirically validated across 15 datasets and 523 constrained scenarios. Achieves state-of-the-art fidelity (Detection Score ~0.497). Eliminates mode collapse on imbalanced data. Handles complex inter-column constraints exactly (e.g., Salary_offered >= Salary_requested) where baselines fail.
AI.conclusion: JANUS delivers a unified framework that simultaneously achieves fidelity, constraint control, uncertainty reliability, and efficiency. It outperforms CTGAN, TabDDPM, and Structural Causal Models on constrained synthetic data tasks, with exact constraint satisfaction on feasible sets and substantially faster uncertainty estimation.

title: Field imaging framework for morphological characterization of aggregates with computer vision: Algorithms and applications
summary: Construction aggregates, including sand and gravel, crushed stone and riprap, are the core building blocks of the construction industry. State-of-the-practice characterization methods mainly relies on visual inspection and manual measurement. State-of-the-art aggregate imaging methods have limitations that are only applicable to regular-sized aggregates under well-controlled conditions. This dissertation addresses these major challenges by developing a field imaging framework for the morphological characterization of aggregates as a multi-scenario solution. For individual and non-overlapping aggregates, a field imaging system was designed and the associated segmentation and volume estimation algorithms were developed. For 2D image analyses of aggregates in stockpiles, an automated 2D instance segmentation and morphological analysis approach was established. For 3D point cloud analyses of aggregate stockpiles, an integrated 3D Reconstruction-Segmentation-Completion (RSC-3D) approach was established: 3D reconstruction procedures from multi-view images, 3D stockpile instance segmentation, and 3D shape completion to predict the unseen sides. First, a 3D reconstruction procedure was developed to obtain high-fidelity 3D models of collected aggregate samples, based on which a 3D aggregate particle library was constructed. Next, two datasets were derived from the 3D particle library for 3D learning: a synthetic dataset of aggregate stockpiles with ground-truth instance labels, and a dataset of partial-complete shape pairs, developed with varying-view raycasting schemes. A state-of-the-art 3D instance segmentation network and a 3D shape completion network were trained on the datasets, respectively. The application of the integrated approach was demonstrated on real stockpiles and validated with ground-truth, showing good performance in capturing and predicting the unseen sides of aggregates.

AI.tldr: A field imaging framework for characterizing construction aggregates across 2D and 3D data, combining reconstruction, segmentation, and shape completion to address limitations of current methods and enable unseen-side prediction in stockpiles.
AI.motivation: Current methods rely on visual inspection and manual measurement and struggle with non-regular aggregates and field conditions; existing imaging techniques are constrained to regular-sized aggregates under controlled settings. A field-ready, multi-scenario framework is needed for robust morphological characterization of aggregates.
AI.method: Developed a field imaging system for single, non-overlapping aggregates with segmentation and volume estimation; 2D automated instance segmentation and morphological analysis for stockpiles; and a 3D integrated Reconstruction-Segmentation-Completion (RSC-3D) pipeline: multi-view 3D reconstruction, 3D stockpile instance segmentation, and 3D shape completion to predict unseen sides. Built a 3D aggregate particle library from high-fidelity reconstructions; created two datasets (synthetic stockpiles with ground-truth labels and partial-complete shape pairs from varying-view raycasting); trained a state-of-the-art 3D instance segmentation network and a 3D shape completion network on these datasets.
AI.result: Applied the integrated approach to real stockpiles with ground-truth validation, achieving good performance in capturing and predicting the unseen sides of aggregates.
AI.conclusion: The integrated field imaging framework (RSC-3D) addresses key challenges in field-based aggregate morphology characterization, enabling accurate 2D/3D analysis and robust unseen-side prediction, with practical validation on real stockpiles.

title: GSeg3D: A High-Precision Grid-Based Algorithm for Safety-Critical Ground Segmentation in LiDAR Point Clouds
summary: Ground segmentation in point cloud data is the process of separating ground points from non-ground points. This task is fundamental for perception in autonomous driving and robotics, where safety and reliable operation depend on the precise detection of obstacles and navigable surfaces. Existing methods often fall short of the high precision required in safety-critical environments, leading to false detections that can compromise decision-making. In this work, we present a ground segmentation approach designed to deliver consistently high precision, supporting the stringent requirements of autonomous vehicles and robotic systems operating in real-world, safety-critical scenarios.

AI.tldr: A high-precision ground segmentation method for point clouds aimed at safety-critical autonomous systems.
AI.motivation: Need for accurate separation of ground and non-ground points to ensure safe obstacle detection and navigable surface planning in autonomous driving and robotics; existing methods produce false detections that can impair decision-making in safety-critical contexts.
AI.method: An algorithmic ground segmentation framework designed to consistently deliver very high precision, with emphasis on real-world, safety-critical operation for autonomous vehicles and robots.
AI.result: Claims of consistently high precision that meet the stringent requirements of safety-critical applications, reducing false detections and improving reliability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach provides a dependable ground-segmentation solution suitable for real-world autonomous systems operating in safety-critical environments.

title: MOOSE-Star: Unlocking Tractable Training for Scientific Discovery by Breaking the Complexity Barrier
summary: While large language models (LLMs) show promise in scientific discovery, existing research focuses on inference or feedback-driven training, leaving the direct modeling of the generative reasoning process, $P(\text{hypothesis}|\text{background})$ ($P(h|b)$), unexplored. We demonstrate that directly training $P(h|b)$ is mathematically intractable due to the combinatorial complexity ($O(N^k)$) inherent in retrieving and composing inspirations from a vast knowledge base. To break this barrier, we introduce MOOSE-Star, a unified framework enabling tractable training and scalable inference. In the best case, MOOSE-Star reduces complexity from exponential to logarithmic ($O(\log N)$) by (1) training on decomposed subtasks derived from the probabilistic equation of discovery, (2) employing motivation-guided hierarchical search to enable logarithmic retrieval and prune irrelevant subspaces, and (3) utilizing bounded composition for robustness against retrieval noise. To facilitate this, we release TOMATO-Star, a dataset of 108,717 decomposed papers (38,400 GPU hours) for training. Furthermore, we show that while brute-force sampling hits a ''complexity wall,'' MOOSE-Star exhibits continuous test-time scaling.

AI.tldr: Proposes MOOSE-Star for tractable training and scalable inference of P(h|b) in scientific discovery; reduces combinatorial retrieval/composition from exponential to logarithmic; introduces TOMATO-Star dataset; demonstrates scalability vs brute-force sampling.
AI.motivation: Directly modeling P(h|b) is intractable due to O(N^k) combinatorial complexity in retrieving and composing inspirations from large knowledge bases; need scalable methods for generative reasoning.
AI.method: Train on decomposed subtasks from the probabilistic equation of discovery; use motivation-guided hierarchical search for logarithmic retrieval; apply bounded composition to mitigate retrieval noise; release TOMATO-Star dataset (108,717 decomposed papers) to support training.
AI.result: Achieves potential complexity reduction from exponential to O(log N) in the best case; brute-force sampling hits a complexity wall; MOOSE-Star shows continuous test-time scaling as N grows.
AI.conclusion: MOOSE-Star offers a unified, tractable framework for training P(h|b) and scalable inference in scientific discovery tasks, with dataset support and implications for robust generative reasoning.

title: InEdit-Bench: Benchmarking Intermediate Logical Pathways for Intelligent Image Editing Models
summary: Multimodal generative models have made significant strides in image editing, demonstrating impressive performance on a variety of static tasks. However, their proficiency typically does not extend to complex scenarios requiring dynamic reasoning, leaving them ill-equipped to model the coherent, intermediate logical pathways that constitute a multi-step evolution from an initial state to a final one. This capacity is crucial for unlocking a deeper level of procedural and causal understanding in visual manipulation. To systematically measure this critical limitation, we introduce InEdit-Bench, the first evaluation benchmark dedicated to reasoning over intermediate pathways in image editing. InEdit-Bench comprises meticulously annotated test cases covering four fundamental task categories: state transition, dynamic process, temporal sequence, and scientific simulation. Additionally, to enable fine-grained evaluation, we propose a set of assessment criteria to evaluate the logical coherence and visual naturalness of the generated pathways, as well as the model's fidelity to specified path constraints. Our comprehensive evaluation of 14 representative image editing models on InEdit-Bench reveals significant and widespread shortcomings in this domain. By providing a standardized and challenging benchmark, we aim for InEdit-Bench to catalyze research and steer development towards more dynamic, reason-aware, and intelligent multimodal generative models.

AI.tldr: Introduces InEdit-Bench, a benchmark to evaluate dynamic, multi-step image-editing reasoning across four task categories; discovers current multimodal models struggle with coherent intermediate pathways; aims to push progress in dynamic, reason-aware generation.
AI.motivation: Address the gap that state-of-the-art multimodal image editors perform well on static tasks but fail to model coherent, multi-step evolutions and intermediate reasoning; provide a standardized benchmark to quantify this capability.
AI.method: Develop InEdit-Bench with annotated test cases spanning four categories (state transition, dynamic process, temporal sequence, scientific simulation); propose evaluation criteria for logical coherence, visual naturalness, and fidelity to path constraints; benchmark 14 representative image-editing models.
AI.result: 14 models show significant deficiencies in dynamic reasoning and pathway coherence; InEdit-Bench reveals widespread limitations and offers a rigorous, standardized evaluation framework.
AI.conclusion: InEdit-Bench is a challenging benchmark designed to catalyze research toward dynamic, reason-aware multimodal generative models by quantifying their ability to produce coherent intermediate pathways.

title: PRAM-R: A Perception-Reasoning-Action-Memory Framework with LLM-Guided Modality Routing for Adaptive Autonomous Driving
summary: Multimodal perception enables robust autonomous driving but incurs unnecessary computational cost when all sensors remain active. This paper presents PRAM-R, a unified Perception-Reasoning-Action-Memory framework with LLM-Guided Modality Routing for adaptive autonomous driving. PRAM-R adopts an asynchronous dual-loop design: a fast reactive loop for perception and control, and a slow deliberative loop for reasoning-driven modality selection and memory updates. An LLM router selects and weights modalities using environmental context and sensor diagnostics, while a hierarchical memory module preserves temporal consistency and supports long-term adaptation. We conduct a two-stage evaluation: (1) synthetic stress tests for stability analysis and (2) real-world validation on the nuScenes dataset. Synthetic stress tests confirm 87.2% reduction in routing oscillations via hysteresis-based stabilization. Real-world validation on nuScenes shows 6.22% modality reduction with 20% memory recall while maintaining comparable trajectory accuracy to full-modality baselines in complex urban scenarios. Our work demonstrates that LLM-augmented architectures with hierarchical memory achieve efficient, adaptive multimodal perception in autonomous driving.

AI.tldr: PRAM-R proposes an adaptive multimodal perception framework for autonomous driving that uses an LLM-guided modality router with asynchronous fast and slow loops and hierarchical memory; it reduces routing oscillations and modality usage while maintaining trajectory accuracy on nuScenes.
AI.motivation: Reduce computational cost of multimodal perception without sacrificing safety and accuracy; achieve stable, adaptive sensor selection in dynamic driving environments.
AI.method: Asynchronous dual-loop design: fast reactive loop for perception/control; slow deliberative loop for LLM-guided modality routing and memory updates. LLM router uses environmental context and sensor diagnostics to select/weight modalities; hierarchical memory supports temporal consistency and long-term adaptation; evaluate with synthetic stress tests (routing stability) and real-world nuScenes.
AI.result: 87.2% reduction in routing oscillations via hysteresis stabilization; 6.22% modality reduction with 20% memory recall on nuScenes; trajectory accuracy comparable to full-modality baselines in complex urban scenarios.
AI.conclusion: LLM-augmented architectures with hierarchical memory enable efficient, adaptive multimodal perception in autonomous driving.

title: TAP: A Token-Adaptive Predictor Framework for Training-Free Diffusion Acceleration
summary: Diffusion models achieve strong generative performance but remain slow at inference due to the need for repeated full-model denoising passes. We present Token-Adaptive Predictor (TAP), a training-free, probe-driven framework that adaptively selects a predictor for each token at every sampling step. TAP uses a single full evaluation of the model's first layer as a low-cost probe to compute proxy losses for a compact family of candidate predictors (instantiated primarily with Taylor expansions of varying order and horizon), then assigns each token the predictor with the smallest proxy error. This per-token "probe-then-select" strategy exploits heterogeneous temporal dynamics, requires no additional training, and is compatible with various predictor designs. TAP incurs negligible overhead while enabling large speedups with little or no perceptual quality loss. Extensive experiments across multiple diffusion architectures and generation tasks show that TAP substantially improves the accuracy-efficiency frontier compared to fixed global predictors and caching-only baselines.

AI.tldr: A training-free, per-token adaptive predictor selection (TAP) for diffusion models that uses a single-layer probe to proxy losses for multiple predictor candidates, enabling per-token choice of predictor to accelerate sampling with minimal quality loss.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models are slow at inference due to iterative full-model denoising. Existing speedups rely on fixed predictors or caching and require training. TAP aims to dramatically speed up sampling without additional training by exploiting per-token temporal dynamics.
AI.method: Compute a single full evaluation of the model's first layer as a cheap probe. For a compact family of predictor candidates (e.g., Taylor-expansion-based predictors of varying order/horizon), compute proxy losses from the probe. Assign each token the predictor with the smallest proxy error. This per-token probe-then-select strategy is training-free and compatible with different predictor designs.
AI.result: TAP yields substantial speedups with little or no perceptual quality loss compared to fixed global predictors and caching baselines, across multiple diffusion architectures and generation tasks.
AI.conclusion: A training-free, per-token adaptive predictor framework can efficiently accelerate diffusion sampling by exploiting heterogeneous token dynamics, offering strong accuracy-efficiency gains without extra training and with broad compatibility.

title: Harmonic Dataset Distillation for Time Series Forecasting
summary: Time Series forecasting (TSF) in the modern era faces significant computational and storage cost challenges due to the massive scale of real-world data. Dataset Distillation (DD), a paradigm that synthesizes a small, compact dataset to achieve training performance comparable to that of the original dataset, has emerged as a promising solution. However, conventional DD methods are not tailored for time series and suffer from architectural overfitting and limited scalability. To address these issues, we propose Harmonic Dataset Distillation for Time Series Forecasting (HDT). HDT decomposes the time series into its sinusoidal basis through the FFT and aligns the core periodic structure by Harmonic Matching. Since this process operates in the frequency domain, all updates during distillation are applied globally without disrupting temporal dependencies of time series. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HDT achieves strong cross-architecture generalization and scalability, validating its practicality for large-scale, real-world applications.

AI.tldr: Introduce Harmonic Dataset Distillation for Time Series Forecasting (HDT), a frequency-domain dataset distillation approach that uses FFT to extract sinusoidal bases and Harmonic Matching to align core periodic structure, enabling global updates in the frequency domain to preserve temporal dependencies and improve scalability.
AI.motivation: Time series data are vast and costly to store/train on; conventional dataset distillation (DD) for TSF suffers from architectural overfitting and poor scalability. A frequency-domain approach could preserve temporal structure while enabling compact, transferable distilled data across architectures.
AI.method: Decompose time series into sinusoidal basis via FFT; perform Harmonic Matching to align the core periodic structure; conduct distillation updates in the frequency domain globally to avoid disrupting temporal dependencies, ensuring cross-architecture compatibility.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows strong cross-architecture generalization and scalability of HDT on large-scale, real-world TSF tasks, validating its practical effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: HDT offers a scalable, architecture-agnostic DD framework for time series forecasting by leveraging frequency-domain representations to preserve temporal patterns and improve distillation efficiency.

title: AMP2026: A Multi-Platform Marine Robotics Dataset for Tracking and Mapping
summary: Marine environments present significant challenges for perception and autonomy due to dynamic surfaces, limited visibility, and complex interactions between aerial, surface, and submerged sensing modalities. This paper introduces the Aerial Marine Perception Dataset (AMP2026), a multi-platform marine robotics dataset collected across multiple field deployments designed to support research in two primary areas: multi-view tracking and marine environment mapping. The dataset includes synchronized data from aerial drones, boat-mounted cameras, and submerged robotic platforms, along with associated localization and telemetry information. The goal of this work is to provide a publicly available dataset enabling research in marine perception and multi-robot observation scenarios. This paper describes the data collection methodology, sensor configurations, dataset organization, and intended research tasks supported by the dataset.

AI.tldr: Introduces AMP2026, a publicly available multi-platform marine perception dataset for multi-view tracking and marine environment mapping, with synchronized data from aerial drones, boat-mounted cameras, and submerged robots plus localization/telemetry.
AI.motivation: Marine perception is challenged by dynamic water surfaces, low visibility, and cross-modality sensing; a large, diverse, and synchronized dataset is needed to develop and benchmark perception and multi-robot systems in marine environments.
AI.method: Field deployments across multiple platforms collecting synchronized sensor data from aerial drones, surface cameras, and submerged robots, with localization and telemetry information; dataset organization and sensor configurations described; intended research tasks include multi-view tracking and mapping.
AI.result: Public release of the AMP2026 dataset enabling research in marine perception and multi-robot observation; documentation of data collection methodology, sensor setups, and dataset organization.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a publicly available dataset and the methodological framework to support ongoing research in marine perception and multi-platform observation, addressing key challenges in marine robotics.

title: Separators in Enhancing Autoregressive Pretraining for Vision Mamba
summary: The state space model Mamba has recently emerged as a promising paradigm in computer vision, attracting significant attention due to its efficient processing of long sequence tasks. Mamba's inherent causal mechanism renders it particularly suitable for autoregressive pretraining. However, current autoregressive pretraining methods are constrained to short sequence tasks, failing to fully exploit Mamba's prowess in handling extended sequences. To address this limitation, we introduce an innovative autoregressive pretraining method for Vision Mamba that substantially extends the input sequence length. We introduce new \textbf{S}epara\textbf{T}ors for \textbf{A}uto\textbf{R}egressive pretraining to demarcate and differentiate between different images, known as \textbf{STAR}. Specifically, we insert identical separators before each image to demarcate its inception. This strategy enables us to quadruple the input sequence length of Vision Mamba while preserving the original dimensions of the dataset images. Employing this long sequence pretraining technique, our STAR-B model achieved an impressive accuracy of 83.5\% on ImageNet-1k, which is highly competitive in Vision Mamba. These results underscore the potential of our method in enhancing the performance of vision models through improved leveraging of long-range dependencies.

AI.tldr: STAR enables long-sequence autoregressive pretraining for Vision Mamba by inserting identical separators before each image to demarcate instances; quadruples input sequence length while preserving image dimensions; STAR-B achieves 83.5% on ImageNet-1k, demonstrating strong potential for long-range dependency modeling.
AI.motivation: Current autoregressive pretraining for Vision Mamba is constrained to short sequences, underutilizing the model's capacity for long-range dependencies. The work aims to extend effective sequence length to fully exploit Mamba in vision tasks.
AI.method: Introduce STAR separators—identical tokens placed before each image—to delineate image boundaries. This increases the input sequence length without changing image dimensions. The approach is applied to Vision Mamba, yielding a long-sequence pretraining paradigm, with a model variant named STAR-B.
AI.result: Long-sequence pretraining enabled by STAR leads to a reported accuracy of 83.5% on ImageNet-1k using the STAR-B model, indicating competitive performance within Vision Mamba frameworks.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that explicit boundary separators can effectively extend sequence length for autoregressive vision models, enabling better utilization of long-range dependencies and improving performance. Points to potential for further gains and scalability considerations.

title: LEA: Label Enumeration Attack in Vertical Federated Learning
summary:
A typical Vertical Federated Learning (VFL) scenario involves several participants collaboratively training a machine learning model, where each party has different features for the same samples, with labels held exclusively by one party. Since labels contain sensitive information, VFL must ensure the privacy of labels. However, existing VFL-targeted label inference attacks are either limited to specific scenarios or require auxiliary data, rendering them impractical in real-world applications.
  We introduce a novel Label Enumeration Attack (LEA) that, for the first time, achieves applicability across multiple VFL scenarios and eschews the need for auxiliary data. Our intuition is that an adversary, employing clustering to enumerate mappings between samples and labels, ascertains the accurate label mappings by evaluating the similarity between the benign model and the simulated models trained under each mapping. To achieve that, the first challenge is how to measure model similarity, as models trained on the same data can have different weights. Drawing from our findings, we propose an efficient approach for assessing congruence based on the cosine similarity of the first-round loss gradients, which offers superior efficiency and precision compared to the comparison of parameter similarities. However, the computational cost may be prohibitive due to the necessity of training and comparing the vast number of simulated models generated through enumeration. To overcome this challenge, we propose Binary-LEA from the perspective of reducing the number of models and eliminating futile training, which lowers the number of enumerations from n! to n^3. Moreover, LEA is resilient against common defense mechanisms such as gradient noise and gradient compression.

AI.tldr: Introduces Label Enumeration Attack (LEA) for vertical federated learning that maps samples to labels via clustering, without auxiliary data; uses first-round gradient loss cosine similarity for model congruence; Binary-LEA reduces enumeration from factorial to cubic; claims resilience to gradient noise/compression.
AI.motivation: Privacy risk: labels in VFL can leak sensitive information. Prior VFL-targeted label inference attacks are limited to specific scenarios or require auxiliary data, hindering real-world applicability.
AI.method: Attackers cluster possible sample-label mappings and train simulated models per mapping; evaluate congruence between the benign model and simulated models using cosine similarity of first-round loss gradients; introduce Binary-LEA to prune the search, reducing enumerations from n! to n^3; claim robustness to gradient noise and gradient compression.
AI.result: LEA is applicable across multiple VFL scenarios without auxiliary data; effective in identifying correct label mappings; Binary-LEA significantly reduces search complexity. Demonstrated resilience to common defenses.
AI.conclusion: LEA reveals a substantive privacy risk to VFL label privacy, motivating the need for defenses. It provides a general, efficient attack framework and suggests future work on defense strategies and broader evaluations.

title: RoboLight: A Dataset with Linearly Composable Illumination for Robotic Manipulation
summary: In this paper, we introduce RoboLight, the first real-world robotic manipulation dataset capturing synchronized episodes under systematically varied lighting conditions. RoboLight consists of two components. (a) RoboLight-Real contains 2,800 real-world episodes collected in our custom Light Cube setup, a calibrated system equipped with eight programmable RGB LED lights. It includes structured illumination variation along three independently controlled dimensions: color, direction, and intensity. Each dimension is paired with a dedicated task featuring objects of diverse geometries and materials to induce perceptual challenges. All image data are recorded in high-dynamic-range (HDR) format to preserve radiometric accuracy. Leveraging the linearity of light transport, we introduce (b) RoboLight-Synthetic, comprising 196,000 episodes synthesized through interpolation in the HDR image space of RoboLight-Real. In principle, RoboLight-Synthetic can be arbitrarily expanded by refining the interpolation granularity. We further verify the dataset quality through qualitative analysis and real-world policy roll-outs, analyzing task difficulty, distributional diversity, and the effectiveness of synthesized data. We additionally demonstrate three representative use cases of the proposed dataset. The full dataset, along with the system software and hardware design, will be released as open-source to support continued research.

AI.tldr: A real-world robotic manipulation dataset (RoboLight) with synchronized lighting variation, plus a large HDR-based synthetic expansion; two components: RoboLight-Real (2,800 HDR RGB-E episodes with variations in color, direction, intensity) and RoboLight-Synthetic (196k interpolated HDR episodes).
AI.motivation: To address perceptual challenges in robotic manipulation under varied lighting and to provide radiometric-accurate data for robust perception and learning, enabling reliable evaluation and synthesis of lighting effects.
AI.method: Build a Light Cube with eight programmable RGB LEDs to generate controlled, tri-axial lighting variation (color, direction, intensity). Collect RoboLight-Real (2,800 real episodes in HDR) and synthesize RoboLight-Synthetic (196k episodes) by interpolating in HDR space, leveraging light transport linearity. Evaluate via qualitative analysis, real-world policy roll-outs, assess task difficulty and distributional diversity, and demonstrate three use cases; release as open-source.
AI.result: Qualitative validation and real-policy roll-outs indicate the dataset captures perceptual challenges under varied lighting; synthetic data via HDR interpolation expands coverage; three use cases illustrate practical applicability; the open-source release supports broader research.
AI.conclusion: RoboLight provides a scalable, open dataset for studying and mitigating lighting-induced perception gaps in robotic manipulation, with HDR fidelity and a pathway to expand synthetic coverage through HDR-space interpolation.

title: Adaptive Enhancement and Dual-Pooling Sequential Attention for Lightweight Underwater Object Detection with YOLOv10
comment: Accepted in 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence, and Networking (QPAIN)
summary: Underwater object detection constitutes a pivotal endeavor within the realms of marine surveillance and autonomous underwater systems; however, it presents significant challenges due to pronounced visual impairments arising from phenomena such as light absorption, scattering, and diminished contrast. In response to these formidable challenges, this manuscript introduces a streamlined yet robust framework for underwater object detection, grounded in the YOLOv10 architecture. The proposed method integrates a Multi-Stage Adaptive Enhancement module to improve image quality, a Dual-Pooling Sequential Attention (DPSA) mechanism embedded into the backbone to strengthen multi-scale feature representation, and a Focal Generalized IoU Objectness (FGIoU) loss to jointly improve localization accuracy and objectness prediction under class imbalance. Comprehensive experimental evaluations conducted on the RUOD and DUO benchmark datasets substantiate that the proposed DPSA_FGIoU_YOLOv10n attains exceptional performance, achieving mean Average Precision (mAP) scores of 88.9% and 88.0% at IoU threshold 0.5, respectively. In comparison to the baseline YOLOv10n, this represents enhancements of 6.7% for RUOD and 6.2% for DUO, all while preserving a compact model architecture comprising merely 2.8M parameters. These findings validate that the proposed framework establishes an efficacious equilibrium among accuracy, robustness, and real-time operational efficiency, making it suitable for deployment in resource-constrained underwater settings.

AI.tldr: A compact YOLOv10n-based underwater object detector with a Multi-Stage Adaptive Enhancement module, DPSA attention in the backbone, and FGIoU loss; achieves high mAP on RUOD/DUO with 2.8M parameters, balancing accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Underwater imaging suffers from absorption, scattering, and reduced contrast. The work aims to boost detection performance in resource-constrained underwater environments by improving image quality and feature representation while maintaining a lightweight model.
AI.method: A streamlined pipeline built on YOLOv10n includes (1) a Multi-Stage Adaptive Enhancement module to improve image quality; (2) a Dual-Pooling Sequential Attention (DPSA) mechanism embedded in the backbone to strengthen multi-scale features; (3) a Focal Generalized IoU (FGIoU) loss to jointly optimize localization and objectness under class imbalance; and (4) evaluation on RUOD and DUO datasets.
AI.result: The DPSA_FGIoU_YOLOv10n achieves mAP of 88.9% (RUOD) and 88.0% (DUO) at IoU 0.5, improving over the baseline YOLOv10n by 6.7% and 6.2% respectively, with 2.8M parameters, indicating strong accuracy with a compact model.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework offers a favorable balance among accuracy, robustness, and real-time efficiency, suitable for deployment in resource-constrained underwater settings.

title: Inverse Contextual Bandits without Rewards: Learning from a Non-Stationary Learner via Suffix Imitation
summary: We study the Inverse Contextual Bandit (ICB) problem, in which a learner seeks to optimize a policy while an observer, who cannot access the learner's rewards and only observes actions, aims to recover the underlying problem parameters. During the learning process, the learner's behavior naturally transitions from exploration to exploitation, resulting in non-stationary action data that poses significant challenges for the observer. To address this issue, we propose a simple and effective framework called Two-Phase Suffix Imitation. The framework discards data from an initial burn-in phase and performs empirical risk minimization using only data from a subsequent imitation phase. We derive a predictive decision loss bound that explicitly characterizes the bias-variance trade-off induced by the choice of burn-in length. Despite the severe information deficit, we show that a reward-free observer can achieve a convergence rate of $\tilde O(1/\sqrt{N})$, matching the asymptotic efficiency of a fully reward-aware learner. This result demonstrates that a passive observer can effectively uncover the optimal policy from actions alone, attaining performance comparable to that of the learner itself.

AI.tldr: Proposes Two-Phase Suffix Imitation to recover policy parameters in Inverse Contextual Bandits using only action data after a burn-in, with risk bounds and a reward-free observer achieving near-learner performance.
AI.motivation: Address learning from actions when rewards are hidden and data is non-stationary due to exploration-exploitation; enable an observer to infer underlying problem parameters.
AI.method: Discard initial burn-in data and perform empirical risk minimization on the suffix data from the imitation phase; derive a predictive decision loss bound that captures the bias-variance trade-off caused by burn-in length.
AI.result: Even with severe information deficit, a reward-free observer attains a convergence rate of ~O(1/√N), matching the asymptotic efficiency of a reward-aware learner.
AI.conclusion: A simple, effective framework shows a passive observer can recover the optimal policy from actions alone, achieving learner-like performance despite non-stationary data.

title: VANGUARD: Vehicle-Anchored Ground Sample Distance Estimation for UAVs in GPS-Denied Environments
summary: Autonomous aerial robots operating in GPS-denied or communication-degraded environments frequently lose access to camera metadata and telemetry, leaving onboard perception systems unable to recover the absolute metric scale of the scene. As LLM/VLM-based planners are increasingly adopted as high-level agents for embodied systems, their ability to reason about physical dimensions becomes safety-critical -- yet our experiments show that five state-of-the-art VLMs suffer from spatial scale hallucinations, with median area estimation errors exceeding 50%. We propose VANGUARD, a lightweight, deterministic Geometric Perception Skill designed as a callable tool that any LLM-based agent can invoke to recover Ground Sample Distance (GSD) from ubiquitous environmental anchors: small vehicles detected via oriented bounding boxes, whose modal pixel length is robustly estimated through kernel density estimation and converted to GSD using a pre-calibrated reference length. The tool returns both a GSD estimate and a composite confidence score, enabling the calling agent to autonomously decide whether to trust the measurement or fall back to alternative strategies. On the DOTA~v1.5 benchmark, VANGUARD achieves 6.87% median GSD error on 306~images. Integrated with SAM-based segmentation for downstream area measurement, the pipeline yields 19.7% median error on a 100-entry benchmark -- with 2.6x lower category dependence and 4x fewer catastrophic failures than the best VLM baseline -- demonstrating that equipping agents with deterministic geometric tools is essential for safe autonomous spatial reasoning.

AI.tldr: VANGUARD: a deterministic geometric perception tool to recover ground sample distance (GSD) for LLM/VLM planners in GPS-denied aerial robotics, mitigating scale hallucination.
AI.motivation: Address safety-critical scale estimation failure in autonomous, GPS-denied environments where onboard perception lacks absolute scene scale, compromising metric reasoning.
AI.method: Detect small vehicles with oriented bounding boxes; estimate modal pixel length via kernel density estimation; convert to GSD using a pre-calibrated reference length; return GSD and a composite confidence score; designed as a callable tool for LLM-based agents; integrate with SAM-based segmentation for downstream area measurement.
AI.result: On DOTA v1.5: 6.87% median GSD error over 306 images. When integrated with SAM-based segmentation for area measurement, achieves 19.7% median error on 100-entry benchmark; 2.6x reduced category dependence; 4x fewer catastrophic failures than the best VLM baseline.
AI.conclusion: Deterministic geometric tools are essential for safe autonomous spatial reasoning; VANGUARD demonstrates practical gains in metric accuracy and robustness for LLM-guided perception, enabling safer decision-making in GPS-denied scenarios.

title: Vector-Quantized Soft Label Compression for Dataset Distillation
summary: Dataset distillation is an emerging technique for reducing the computational and storage costs of training machine learning models by synthesizing a small, informative subset of data that captures the essential characteristics of a much larger dataset. Recent methods pair synthetic samples and their augmentations with soft labels from a teacher model, enabling student models to generalize effectively despite the small size of the distilled dataset. While soft labels are critical for effective distillation, the storage and communication overhead they incur, especially when accounting for augmentations, is often overlooked. In practice, each distilled sample is associated with multiple soft labels, making them the dominant contributor to storage costs, particularly in large-class settings such as ImageNet-1K. In this paper, we present a rigorous analysis of bit requirements across dataset distillation frameworks, quantifying the storage demands of both distilled samples and their soft labels. To address the overhead, we introduce a vector-quantized autoencoder (VQAE) for compressing soft labels, achieving substantial compression while preserving the effectiveness of the distilled data. We validate our method on both vision and language distillation benchmarks. On ImageNet-1K, our proposed VQAE achieves 30--40x additional compression over RDED, LPLD, SRE2L, and CDA baselines while retaining over $90\%$ of their original performance.

AI.tldr: We propose a vector-quantized autoencoder (VQAE) to compress soft labels in dataset distillation, addressing the dominant storage cost of per-sample soft labels (including augmentations). The method analyzes bit requirements and demonstrates large compression gains, achieving 30–40x additional compression over several baselines (RDED, LPLD, SRE2L, CDA) on ImageNet-1K, while retaining over 90% of original performance, and validating on vision and language distillation benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Dataset distillation often relies on synthetic samples and their augmentations with soft labels from a teacher model. Soft-label storage and communication are major overheads, especially with many augmentations and large-class datasets like ImageNet-1K, limiting practicality. There is a need for effective compression of soft labels to reduce bit requirements without sacrificing distillation efficacy.
AI.method: Introduce a vector-quantized autoencoder (VQAE) to compress soft labels associated with distilled samples and their augmentations. Provide a rigorous analysis of bit requirements for both distilled samples and soft labels. Evaluate on vision and language distillation benchmarks, comparing against baselines (RDED, LPLD, SRE2L, CDA) and reporting compression gains and retained performance.
AI.result: VQAE achieves substantial compression of soft labels, delivering 30–40x additional compression over the cited baselines on ImageNet-1K, while preserving over 90% of their original performance. The method is validated across vision and language distillation benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Compressing soft labels with VQAE significantly reduces storage and communication overhead in dataset distillation without materially degrading distillation effectiveness, enabling more scalable and practical deployment in large-class settings. The results suggest the approach generalizes to both vision and language distillation tasks.

title: When and Where to Reset Matters for Long-Term Test-Time Adaptation
summary: When continual test-time adaptation (TTA) persists over the long term, errors accumulate in the model and further cause it to predict only a few classes for all inputs, a phenomenon known as model collapse. Recent studies have explored reset strategies that completely erase these accumulated errors. However, their periodic resets lead to suboptimal adaptation, as they occur independently of the actual risk of collapse. Moreover, their full resets cause catastrophic loss of knowledge acquired over time, even though such knowledge could be beneficial in the future. To this end, we propose (1) an Adaptive and Selective Reset (ASR) scheme that dynamically determines when and where to reset, (2) an importance-aware regularizer to recover essential knowledge lost due to reset, and (3) an on-the-fly adaptation adjustment scheme to enhance adaptability under challenging domain shifts. Extensive experiments across long-term TTA benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, particularly under challenging conditions. Our code is available at https://github.com/YonseiML/asr.

AI.tldr: Dynamic Adaptive Selective Reset (ASR) with an importance-aware regularizer and on-the-fly adaptation adjustment improves long-term test-time adaptation by preventing model collapse and preserving useful knowledge.
AI.motivation: In long-term continual TTA, accumulated errors cause model collapse to predicting only a few classes. Existing resets are either periodic (suboptimal) or total (catastrophic forgetting). A dynamic, selective reset strategy, plus memory-preserving regularization and adaptive control, is needed.
AI.method: Proposes ASR to dynamically determine when and where to reset during test-time. Introduces an importance-aware regularizer to recover critical knowledge lost due to resets. Adds an on-the-fly adaptation adjustment scheme to maintain robustness under challenging domain shifts.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on long-term TTA benchmarks show that ASR with the regularizer and adaptation adjustment outperforms baselines, especially under difficult domain shifts, by reducing model collapse and preserving knowledge.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ASR framework provides a principled approach to long-term TTA, balancing timely resetting with knowledge retention and adaptive flexibility; code is released at the provided GitHub link.

title: OmniPlanner: Universal Exploration and Inspection Path Planning across Robot Morphologies
comment: The code for this paper is open-sourced and released at: https://github.com/ntnu-arl/gbplanner_ros/tree/gbplanner3
summary: Autonomous robotic systems are increasingly deployed for mapping, monitoring, and inspection in complex and unstructured environments. However, most existing path planning approaches remain domain-specific (i.e., either on air, land, or sea), limiting their scalability and cross-platform applicability. This article presents OmniPlanner, a unified planning framework for autonomous exploration and inspection across aerial, ground, and underwater robots. The method integrates volumetric exploration and viewpoint-based inspection, alongside target reach behaviors within a single modular architecture, complemented by a platform abstraction layer that captures morphology-specific sensing, traversability and motion constraints. This enables the same planning strategy to generalize across distinct mobility domains with minimal retuning. The framework is validated through extensive simulation studies and field deployments in underground mines, industrial facilities, forests, submarine bunkers, and structured outdoor environments. Across these diverse scenarios, OmniPlanner demonstrates robust performance, consistent cross-domain generalization, and improved exploration and inspection efficiency compared to representative state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: OmniPlanner: a unified cross-domain planner for aerial, ground, and underwater robots, combining exploration, inspection, and target-reach within a modular architecture and platform abstraction, achieving cross-domain generalization and higher efficiency in simulations and real deployments.
AI.motivation: Domain-specific planners limit scalability and cross-platform applicability; a unified planning framework is needed to enable autonomous exploration and inspection across diverse mobility platforms.
AI.method: Integrates volumetric exploration, viewpoint-based inspection, and target reach behaviors within a single modular architecture, augmented by a platform abstraction layer that captures morphology-specific sensing, traversability, and motion constraints to enable minimal retuning across domains.
AI.result: Validated through extensive simulations and field deployments in diverse environments (mines, facilities, forests, bunkers, outdoor settings); demonstrates robust performance, consistent cross-domain generalization, and improved exploration/inspection efficiency over representative baselines.
AI.conclusion: A unified cross-domain planning framework is feasible and effective for cross-platform autonomous exploration and inspection, enabling scalable deployment across aerial, ground, and underwater robots.

title: Structure-aware Prompt Adaptation from Seen to Unseen for Open-Vocabulary Compositional Zero-Shot Learning
summary: The goal of Open-Vocabulary Compositional Zero-Shot Learning (OV-CZSL) is to recognize attribute-object compositions in the open-vocabulary setting, where compositions of both seen and unseen attributes and objects are evaluated. Recently, prompt tuning methods have demonstrated strong generalization capabilities in the closed setting, where only compositions of seen attributes and objects are evaluated, i.e., Compositional Zero-Shot Learning (CZSL). However, directly applying these methods to OV-CZSL may not be sufficient to generalize to unseen attributes, objects and their compositions, as it is limited to seen attributes and objects. Normally, when faced with unseen concepts, humans adopt analogies with seen concepts that have the similar semantics thereby inferring their meaning (e.g., "wet" and "damp", "shirt" and "jacket"). In this paper, we experimentally show that the distribution of semantically related attributes or objects tends to form consistent local structures in the embedding space. Based on the above structures, we propose Structure-aware Prompt Adaptation (SPA) method, which enables models to generalize from seen to unseen attributes and objects. Specifically, in the training stage, we design a Structure-aware Consistency Loss (SCL) that encourages the local structure's consistency of seen attributes and objects in each iteration. In the inference stage, we devise a Structure-guided Adaptation Strategy (SAS) that adaptively aligns the structures of unseen attributes and objects with those of trained seen attributes and objects with similar semantics. Notably, SPA is a plug-and-play method that can be seamlessly integrated into existing CZSL prompt tuning methods. Extensive experiments on OV-CZSL benchmarks demonstrate that SPA achieves competitive closed-set performance while significantly improving open-vocabulary results.

AI.tldr: Proposes Structure-aware Prompt Adaptation (SPA) for Open-Vocabulary Compositional Zero-Shot Learning (OV-CZSL); uses structure-consistency during training and structure-guided adaptation during inference to generalize from seen to unseen attributes/objects; plug-and-play with existing CZSL prompts; improves open-vocabulary results while retaining competitive closed-set performance.
AI.motivation: Overcome the failure of standard prompt-tuning in OV-CZSL to generalize to unseen attributes/objects by leveraging the semantic structure of the embedding space, inspired by human analogy-based reasoning.
AI.method: Introduce Structure-aware Consistency Loss (SCL) to enforce local semantic structure consistency among seen attributes/objects during training. Propose Structure-guided Adaptation Strategy (SAS) to align unseen attributes/objects with semantically similar seen concepts at inference. SPA is a plug-and-play module that can be integrated with existing CZSL prompt-tuning methods.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on OV-CZSL benchmarks show SPA achieves competitive closed-set performance and significantly improves open-vocabulary results.
AI.conclusion: SPA demonstrates effective use of semantic structure for generalization in OV-CZSL, enabling better handling of unseen attribute-object combinations while preserving closed-set accuracy; compatible with existing prompt-tuning pipelines.

title: Relational In-Context Learning via Synthetic Pre-training with Structural Prior
summary: Relational Databases (RDBs) are the backbone of modern business, yet they lack foundation models comparable to those in text or vision. A key obstacle is that high-quality RDBs are private, scarce and structurally heterogeneous, making internet-scale pre-training infeasible. To overcome this data scarcity, We introduce $\textbf{RDB-PFN}$, the first relational foundation model trained purely via $\textbf{synthetic data}$. Inspired by Prior-Data Fitted Networks (PFNs) where synthetic data generated from Structural Causal Models (SCMs) enables reasoning on single tables, we design a $\textbf{Relational Prior Generator}$ to create an infinite stream of diverse RDBs from scratch. Pre-training on $\textbf{over 2 million}$ synthetic single-table and relational tasks, RDB-PFN learns to adapt to any new database instantly via genuine $\textbf{in-context learning}$. Experiments verify RDB-PFN achieves strong few-shot performance on 19 real-world relational prediction tasks, outperforming graph-based and single-table foundation-model baselines (given the same DFS-linearized inputs), while using a lightweight architecture and fast inference. The code is available at https://github.com/MuLabPKU/RDBPFN

AI.tldr: A relational foundation model, RDB-PFN, is trained solely on synthetic data to enable in-context learning for relational tasks, achieving strong few-shot results on real data.
AI.motivation: RDBs are central but lack foundation models due to private, scarce, and heterogeneous data; internet-scale pre-training is infeasible; synthetic data via SCMs and PFNs offers a path to generalization.
AI.method: Develop Relational Prior Generator to produce infinite synthetic RDBs from scratch. Pre-train on over 2 million synthetic single-table and relational tasks. Use in-context learning for adaptation. Lightweight architecture with DFS-linearized inputs.
AI.result: RDB-PFN achieves strong few-shot performance on 19 real-world relational prediction tasks, outperforming graph-based and single-table baselines under same inputs; fast inference.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic-data-driven relational foundation modeling is viable, enabling instant adaptation to new databases; code released.

title: UrbanHuRo: A Two-Layer Human-Robot Collaboration Framework for the Joint Optimization of Heterogeneous Urban Services
comment: 8 pages, 15 figures. This paper has been accepted by ICRA'26 as a regular paper
summary: In the vision of smart cities, technologies are being developed to enhance the efficiency of urban services and improve residents' quality of life. However, most existing research focuses on optimizing individual services in isolation, without adequately considering reciprocal interactions among heterogeneous urban services that could yield higher efficiency and improved resource utilization. For example, human couriers could collect traffic and air quality data along their delivery routes, while sensing robots could assist with on-demand delivery during peak hours, enhancing both sensing coverage and delivery efficiency. However, the joint optimization of different urban services is challenging due to potentially conflicting objectives and the need for real-time coordination in dynamic environments. In this paper, we propose UrbanHuRo, a two-layer human-robot collaboration framework for joint optimization of heterogeneous urban services, demonstrated through crowdsourced delivery and urban sensing. UrbanHuRo includes two key designs: (i) a scalable distributed MapReduce-based K-submodular maximization module for efficient order dispatch, and (ii) a deep submodular reward reinforcement learning algorithm for sensing route planning. Experimental evaluations on real-world datasets from a food delivery platform demonstrate that UrbanHuRo improves sensing coverage by 29.7% and courier income by 39.2% on average in most settings, while also significantly reducing the number of overdue orders.

AI.tldr: A two-layer human–robot collaboration framework (UrbanHuRo) jointly optimizes crowdsourced delivery and urban sensing using (i) a MapReduce-based K-submodular dispatch module and (ii) a deep submodular reward RL planner for sensing routes; it achieves notable gains on real data (sensing coverage +, courier income +) and fewer overdue orders.
AI.motivation: Existing research optimizes urban services in isolation, overlooking reciprocal interactions that could improve efficiency and resource use. Coordinating heterogeneous services (delivery + sensing) can enhance data collection, route efficiency, and service quality in dynamic city environments.
AI.method: Two-layer design: (i) scalable distributed MapReduce-based K-submodular maximization for order dispatch; (ii) deep submodular reward reinforcement learning for sensing route planning. Evaluation on real-world food delivery data shows improved sensing coverage and courier income while reducing overdue orders.
AI.result: Demonstrates substantial performance gains (e.g., ~30% higher sensing coverage and ~40% higher courier income) and improved overdue-order reduction, validating joint optimization viability on real datasets.
AI.conclusion: UrbanHuRo shows that joint optimization across heterogeneous urban services is feasible and beneficial with submodular optimization and RL components. The approach highlights scalability and effectiveness but leaves open questions about baseline comparisons, generalizability to other cities/services, computational overhead, and deployment challenges.

title: Compliant In-hand Rolling Manipulation Using Tactile Sensing
summary: We investigate in-hand rolling manipulation using a multifingered robot hand, where each finger is compliant and equipped with a tactile fingertip providing contact location and wrench information. We derive the equations of motion for compliant quasistatic in-hand rolling manipulation and formulate a fingertip rolling manipulation controller for multiple fingers to achieve a desired object twist within a grasp. The contact mechanics are demonstrated in simulation and the controller is tested on an experimental robot system.

AI.tldr: A multi-finger, compliant, tactile-enabled in-hand rolling manipulation framework with quasistatic motion equations and a multi-finger fingertip rolling controller, validated in simulation and on a real robot.
AI.motivation: Enable dexterous in-hand manipulation by exploiting compliant contacts and tactile sensing to achieve precise object twists while maintaining a grasp.
AI.method: Derive compliant quasistatic equations of motion for in-hand rolling; formulate a multi-finger fingertip rolling controller to realize a desired object twist within the grasp; leverage tactile fingertips for contact location and wrench information; validate contact mechanics in simulation and test the controller on an experimental robot system.
AI.result: Established equations of motion for the system; designed a fingertip rolling controller for multiple fingers to achieve specified object twist; demonstrated contact mechanics in simulation; validated the controller experimentally.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the feasibility of compliant, tactile-based in-hand rolling manipulation with multi-finger control and provides a framework for integrating contact sensing into robust object pose regulation within a grasp.

title: From Narrow to Panoramic Vision: Attention-Guided Cold-Start Reshapes Multimodal Reasoning
summary: The cold-start initialization stage plays a pivotal role in training Multimodal Large Reasoning Models (MLRMs), yet its mechanisms remain insufficiently understood. To analyze this stage, we introduce the Visual Attention Score (VAS), an attention-based metric that quantifies how much a model attends to visual tokens. We find that reasoning performance is strongly correlated with VAS (r=0.9616): models with higher VAS achieve substantially stronger multimodal reasoning. Surprisingly, multimodal cold-start fails to elevate VAS, resulting in attention distributions close to the base model, whereas text-only cold-start leads to a clear increase. We term this counter-intuitive phenomenon Lazy Attention Localization. To validate its causal role, we design training-free interventions that directly modulate attention allocation during inference, performance gains of 1$-$2% without any retraining. Building on these insights, we further propose Attention-Guided Visual Anchoring and Reflection (AVAR), a comprehensive cold-start framework that integrates visual-anchored data synthesis, attention-guided objectives, and visual-anchored reward shaping. Applied to Qwen2.5-VL-7B, AVAR achieves an average gain of 7.0% across 7 multimodal reasoning benchmarks. Ablation studies further confirm that each component of AVAR contributes step-wise to the overall gains. The code, data, and models are available at https://github.com/lrlbbzl/Qwen-AVAR.

AI.tldr: Introduces Visual Attention Score (VAS) to analyze cold-start in Multimodal Large Reasoning Models (MLRMs), reveals Lazy Attention Localization where multimodal cold-start fails to raise attention to visual tokens, and presents AVAR (visual-anchored data synthesis, attention-guided objectives, and reward shaping) that improves multimodal reasoning. Achieves 7.0% average gains on Qwen2.5-VL-7B across 7 benchmarks; includes training-free attention interventions and ablations; code available.
AI.motivation: Understand and improve the cold-start initialization stage in Multimodal Large Reasoning Models, where multimodal reasoning performance correlates with how much the model attends to visual tokens but traditional cold-start attempts fail to boost visual attention.
AI.method: Define Visual Attention Score (VAS) as an attention-based metric over visual tokens; analyze correlation with reasoning performance; identify Lazy Attention Localization; implement training-free inference-time interventions to modulate attention; propose AVAR framework combining visual-anchored data synthesis, attention-guided objectives, and visual-anchored reward shaping; apply to Qwen2.5-VL-7B and run ablations across 7 multimodal reasoning benchmarks.
AI.result: Find a strong positive correlation between VAS and reasoning performance (r=0.9616); multimodal cold-start does not elevate VAS and yields attention distributions similar to base model, while text-only cold-start increases VAS; training-free interventions yield 1–2% gains; AVAR yields an average 7.0% improvement across 7 benchmarks; ablations show each component contributes.
AI.conclusion: Attention-focused cold-start strategies, particularly AVAR, can meaningfully improve multimodal reasoning by steering visual attention and anchoring visual data during training-free and designed objectives; code, data, and models are released for reproducibility.

title: Pretrained Vision-Language-Action Models are Surprisingly Resistant to Forgetting in Continual Learning
summary: Continual learning is a long-standing challenge in robot policy learning, where a policy must acquire new skills over time without catastrophically forgetting previously learned ones. While prior work has extensively studied continual learning in relatively small behavior cloning (BC) policy models trained from scratch, its behavior in modern large-scale pretrained Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models remains underexplored. In this work, we found that pretrained VLAs are remarkably resistant to forgetting compared with smaller policy models trained from scratch. Simple Experience Replay (ER) works surprisingly well on VLAs, sometimes achieving zero forgetting even with a small replay data size. Our analysis reveals that pretraining plays a critical role in downstream continual learning performance: large pretrained models mitigate forgetting with a small replay buffer size while maintaining strong forward learning capabilities. Furthermore, we found that VLAs can retain relevant knowledge from prior tasks despite performance degradation during learning new tasks. This knowledge retention enables rapid recovery of seemingly forgotten skills through finetuning. Together, these insights imply that large-scale pretraining fundamentally changes the dynamics of continual learning, enabling models to continually acquire new skills over time with simple replay. Code and more information can be found at https://ut-austin-rpl.github.io/continual-vla
