AI.tldr: Large pretrained Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models exhibit strong resistance to forgetting in continual learning; simple Experience Replay (ER) is effective, often yielding zero forgetting with small replay buffers. Pretraining is key: big models maintain forward performance with little replay and retain relevant prior knowledge, enabling rapid recovery via finetuning. This suggests pretraining fundamentally changes continual learning dynamics for robotic policies.
AI.motivation: Investigate how continual learning behaves in modern large-scale pretrained VLA models compared to small models trained from scratch, and whether simple replay suffices to mitigate forgetting.
AI.method: Empirical evaluation of continual learning using Experience Replay (ER) on large pretrained VLA models vs. models trained from scratch, varying replay buffer size, and analyzing forgetting, forward transfer, and knowledge retention/finetuning dynamics. Likely experiments include continual task sequences, evaluation of performance on old tasks after learning new ones, and finetuning to recover forgotten skills.
AI.result: Pretrained VLAs show remarkable resistance to forgetting relative to small-from-scratch policies; ER performs well and can achieve near-zero forgetting with small replay buffers. Pretraining enables the model to mitigate forgetting while preserving forward learning. VLAs retain knowledge from prior tasks even as learning progresses, facilitating rapid recovery of forgotten skills via finetuning.
AI.conclusion: Pretraining fundamentally alters continual learning dynamics. Large-scale pretrained VLAs can continually acquire new skills with simple replay, using much smaller buffers, and retain the ability to quickly recover forgotten tasks through finetuning. This shifts the focus toward leveraging pretraining to enable enduring continual learning in robotics.

title: Large-Language-Model-Guided State Estimation for Partially Observable Task and Motion Planning
summary: Robot planning in partially observable environments, where not all objects are known or visible, is a challenging problem, as it requires reasoning under uncertainty through partially observable Markov decision processes. During the execution of a computed plan, a robot may unexpectedly observe task-irrelevant objects, which are typically ignored by naive planners. In this work, we propose incorporating two types of common-sense knowledge: (1) certain objects are more likely to be found in specific locations; and (2) similar objects are likely to be co-located, while dissimilar objects are less likely to be found together. Manually engineering such knowledge is complex, so we explore leveraging the powerful common-sense reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs). Our planning and execution framework, CoCo-TAMP, introduces a hierarchical state estimation that uses LLM-guided information to shape the belief over task-relevant objects, enabling efficient solutions to long-horizon task and motion planning problems. In experiments, CoCo-TAMP achieves an average reduction of 62.7 in planning and execution time in simulation, and 72.6 in real-world demonstrations, compared to a baseline that does not incorporate either type of common-sense knowledge.

AI.tldr: CoCo-TAMP uses LLM-guided common-sense knowledge to improve planning under partial observability by hierarchical state estimation, enabling more efficient long-horizon task and motion planning; shows substantial planning/execution time reductions in simulation and real-world demonstrations.
AI.motivation: Robots must operate under uncertainty in partially observable environments where not all objects are visible. Naive planners ignore observed task-irrelevant objects. Incorporating common-sense about object locations and co-location can reduce search space and improve planning efficiency. LLMs offer a scalable source of such knowledge.
AI.method: Introduce CoCo-TAMP, a planning and execution framework with hierarchical state estimation guided by LLM-derived information. Integrates two common-sense priors: (1) objects are more likely in certain locations; (2) similar objects tend to co-locate. The framework uses these priors to shape beliefs over task-relevant objects in long-horizon Task and Motion Planning (TAMP).
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows average reductions in planning/execution time of 62.7 (simulation) and 72.6 (real-world demonstrations) compared to a baseline lacking the common-sense knowledge types.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging LLM-based common-sense via hierarchical state estimation enhances planning efficiency for long-horizon, partially observable tasks. The approach reduces computation and execution time, improving practical robustness, though its effectiveness depends on LLM quality and integration with perception and uncertainty.

title: Perception-Aware Time-Optimal Planning for Quadrotor Waypoint Flight
summary:
Agile quadrotor flight pushes the limits of control, actuation, and onboard perception. While time-optimal trajectory planning has been extensively studied, existing approaches typically neglect the tight coupling between vehicle dynamics, environmental geometry, and the visual requirements of onboard state estimation. As a result, trajectories that are dynamically feasible may fail in closed-loop execution due to degraded visual quality. This paper introduces a unified time-optimal trajectory optimization framework for vision-based quadrotors that explicitly incorporates perception constraints alongside full nonlinear dynamics, rotor actuation limits, aerodynamic effects, camera field-of-view constraints, and convex geometric gate representations.
  The proposed formulation solves minimum-time lap trajectories for arbitrary racetracks with diverse gate shapes and orientations, while remaining numerically robust and computationally efficient. We derive an information-theoretic position uncertainty metric to quantify visual state-estimation quality and integrate it into the planner through three perception objectives: position uncertainty minimization, sequential field-of-view constraints, and look-ahead alignment. This enables systematic exploration of the trade-offs between speed and perceptual reliability.
  To accurately track the resulting perception-aware trajectories, we develop a model predictive contouring tracking controller that separates lateral and progress errors. Experiments demonstrate real-world flight speeds up to 9.8 m/s with 0.07 m average tracking error, and closed-loop success rates improved from 55% to 100% on a challenging Split-S course. The proposed system provides a scalable benchmark for studying the fundamental limits of perception-aware, time-optimal autonomous flight.

AI.tldr: Perception-aware, time-optimal trajectory optimization for vision-based quadrotors that couples full nonlinear dynamics, actuator limits, aerodynamics, camera FOV, and gate representations with an information-theoretic perception metric and three perception objectives, plus a model predictive tracking controller; achieves high-speed flight with improved closed-loop success and provides a scalable benchmark.
AI.motivation: Time-optimal quadrotor planning often ignores tight coupling between dynamics, environment geometry, and onboard visual state estimation. Perception quality can bottleneck high-speed flights; a unified framework is needed to balance speed with perceptual reliability and reliability of state estimation.
AI.method: Formulates a unified minimum-time trajectory optimization incorporating full nonlinear dynamics, rotor actuation limits, aerodynamic effects, camera field-of-view constraints, and convex gate representations. Introduces an information-theoretic position uncertainty metric and integrates three perception objectives—minimize position uncertainty, enforce sequential field-of-view constraints, and look-ahead alignment—into the planner. Uses model predictive contouring tracking that splits lateral and progress errors to track the planned trajectories.
AI.result: Demonstrates real-world flight speeds up to 9.8 m/s with a 0.07 m average tracking error. Closed-loop success on a challenging Split-S course improves from 55% to 100%. Provides a scalable benchmark for perception-aware, time-optimal autonomous flight.
AI.conclusion: Perception-aware, time-optimal planning is viable and beneficial for robust high-speed autonomous flight. The work elucidates trade-offs between speed and perceptual reliability and establishes a framework suitable as a benchmark and foundation for future research in perception-constrained autonomous flight.

title: Universal Pansharpening Foundation Model
summary: Pansharpening generates the high-resolution multi-spectral (MS) image by integrating spatial details from a texture-rich panchromatic (PAN) image and spectral attributes from a low-resolution MS image. Existing methods are predominantly satellite-specific and scene-dependent, which severely limits their generalization across heterogeneous sensors and varied scenes, thereby reducing their real-world practicality. To address these challenges, we present FoundPS, a universal pansharpening foundation model for satellite-agnostic and scene-robust fusion. Specifically, we introduce a modality-interleaved transformer that learns band-wise modal specializations to form reversible spectral affine bases, mapping arbitrary-band MS into a unified latent space via tensor multiplication. Building upon this, we construct a latent diffusion bridge model to progressively evolve latent representations, and incorporate bridge posterior sampling to couple latent diffusion with pixel-space observations, enabling stable and controllable fusion. Furthermore, we devise infinite-dimensional pixel-to-latent interaction mechanisms to comprehensively capture the cross-domain dependencies between PAN observations and MS representations, thereby facilitating complementary information fusion. In addition, to support large-scale training and evaluation, we construct a comprehensive pansharpening benchmark, termed PSBench, consisting of worldwide MS and PAN image pairs from multiple satellites across diverse scenes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FoundPS consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, exhibiting superior generalization and robustness across a wide range of pansharpening tasks.

AI.tldr: FoundPS introduces a universal, satellite-agnostic foundation model for pan-sharpening that generalizes across sensors and scenes by learning a unified latent space through a modality-interleaved transformer and latent diffusion bridge, augmented with extensive cross-domain PAN-MS interactions and a large-scale benchmark PSBench.
AI.motivation: Existing pansharpening methods are largely satellite-specific and scene-dependent, limiting generalization and real-world applicability. There is a need for a universal, robust framework that can adapt to heterogeneous sensors and diverse scenes.
AI.method: 1) A modality-interleaved transformer learns band-wise modal specializations to form reversible spectral affine bases and maps arbitrary-band MS into a unified latent space via tensor multiplication. 2) A latent diffusion bridge model progressively evolves latent representations, with bridge posterior sampling coupling latent diffusion with pixel-space observations to enable stable fusion. 3) Infinite-dimensional pixel-to-latent interactions capture cross-domain dependencies between PAN and MS representations for comprehensive fusion. 4) PSBench, a worldwide MS-PAN benchmark across multiple satellites and scenes, supports large-scale training and evaluation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show FoundPS consistently outperforms state-of-the-art pansharpening methods, demonstrating superior generalization and robustness across a wide range of pansharpening tasks.
AI.conclusion: FoundPS provides a universal, robust pansharpening solution capable of handling heterogeneous sensors and scenes, advancing practical applicability in real-world remote sensing scenarios.

title: Fairness Begins with State: Purifying Latent Preferences for Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning in Interactive Recommendation
summary: Interactive recommender systems (IRS) are increasingly optimized with Reinforcement Learning (RL) to capture the sequential nature of user-system dynamics. However, existing fairness-aware methods often suffer from a fundamental oversight: they assume the observed user state is a faithful representation of true preferences. In reality, implicit feedback is contaminated by popularity-driven noise and exposure bias, creating a distorted state that misleads the RL agent. We argue that the persistent conflict between accuracy and fairness is not merely a reward-shaping issue, but a state estimation failure. In this work, we propose \textbf{DSRM-HRL}, a framework that reformulates fairness-aware recommendation as a latent state purification problem followed by decoupled hierarchical decision-making. We introduce a Denoising State Representation Module (DSRM) based on diffusion models to recover the low-entropy latent preference manifold from high-entropy, noisy interaction histories. Built upon this purified state, a Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning (HRL) agent is employed to decouple conflicting objectives: a high-level policy regulates long-term fairness trajectories, while a low-level policy optimizes short-term engagement under these dynamic constraints. Extensive experiments on high-fidelity simulators (KuaiRec, KuaiRand) demonstrate that DSRM-HRL effectively breaks the "rich-get-richer" feedback loop, achieving a superior Pareto frontier between recommendation utility and exposure equity.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based denoising module (DSRM) purifies noisy interaction histories into a latent preference state, enabling a hierarchical RL (HRL) agent to separate long-term fairness from short-term engagement. This redefines fairness in recommender systems as a state-estimation problem rather than a reward-shaping issue, yielding a better accuracy-fairness Pareto frontier.
AI.motivation: Implicit feedback in recommender systems is corrupted by popularity effects and exposure bias, so the observed state poorly reflects true user preferences. This state misleads RL agents and sustains the accuracy–fairness conflict.
AI.method: Introduce DSRM, a diffusion-model–based denoising module that recovers a low-entropy latent preference manifold from noisy interaction histories. On top, employ a Hierarchical RL (HRL) agent with a high-level policy guiding long-term fairness trajectories and a low-level policy optimizing short-term engagement under those constraints.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on high-fidelity simulators KuaiRec and KuaiRand shows that DSRM-HRL breaks the rich-get-richer feedback loop and achieves a superior Pareto frontier between recommendation utility and exposure equity.
AI.conclusion: Recasting fairness in RL-based recommendations as a state-estimation problem and solving it via latent state purification plus decoupled HRL yields better trade-offs and resilience to exposure bias in interactive settings.

title: Gaussian Mixture-Based Inverse Perception Contract for Uncertainty-Aware Robot Navigation
comment: 8 pages, 5 figures. Accepted to ACC 2026 (American Control Conference)
summary: Reliable navigation in cluttered environments requires perception outputs that are not only accurate but also equipped with uncertainty sets suitable for safe control. An inverse perception contract (IPC) provides such a connection by mapping perceptual estimates to sets that contain the ground truth with high confidence. Existing IPC formulations, however, instantiate uncertainty as a single ellipsoidal set and rely on deterministic trust scores to guide robot motion. Such a representation cannot capture the multi-modal and irregular structure of fine-grained perception errors, often resulting in over-conservative sets and degraded navigation performance. In this work, we introduce Gaussian Mixture-based Inverse Perception Contract (GM-IPC), which extends IPC to represent uncertainty with unions of ellipsoidal confidence sets derived from Gaussian mixture models. This design moves beyond deterministic single-set abstractions, enabling fine-grained, multi-modal, and non-convex error structures to be captured with formal guarantees. A learning framework is presented that trains GM-IPC to account for probabilistic inclusion, distribution matching, and empty-space penalties, ensuring both validity and compactness of the predicted sets. We further show that the resulting uncertainty characterizations can be leveraged in downstream planning frameworks for real-time safe navigation, enabling less conservative and more adaptive robot motion while preserving safety in a probabilistic manner.

AI.tldr: Gaussian Mixture-based Inverse Perception Contract (GM-IPC) extends IPC by representing uncertainty as unions of ellipsoids derived from Gaussian mixtures, enabling multi-modal, non-convex error modeling with formal guarantees and improved planning.
AI.motivation: Single-ellipsoid IPC cannot capture multi-modal or irregular perception errors, leading to over-conservatism and degraded navigation performance; a richer probabilistic uncertainty representation is needed for safe planning.
AI.method: Extend IPC to GM-IPC by deriving unions of ellipsoidal confidence sets from a Gaussian mixture model; develop a learning framework to train GM-IPC with probabilistic inclusion guarantees, distribution matching, and empty-space penalties to ensure validity and compactness; enable integration with real-time planning.
AI.result: Provides refined, multi-modal uncertainty characterizations with formal guarantees; can be leveraged by downstream planners for safer, less conservative real-time navigation while preserving probabilistic safety.
AI.conclusion: GM-IPC enables more adaptive and efficient navigation in cluttered environments by capturing complex error structures with formal guarantees and integrating into planning for probabilistic safety.

title: All-in-One Image Restoration via Causal-Deconfounding Wavelet-Disentangled Prompt Network
summary: Image restoration represents a promising approach for addressing the inherent defects of image content distortion. Standard image restoration approaches suffer from high storage cost and the requirement towards the known degradation pattern, including type and degree, which can barely be satisfied in dynamic practical scenarios. In contrast, all-in-one image restoration (AiOIR) eliminates multiple degradations within a unified model to circumvent the aforementioned issues. However, according to our causal analysis, we disclose that two significant defects still exacerbate the effectiveness and generalization of AiOIR models: 1) the spurious correlation between non-degradation semantic features and degradation patterns; 2) the biased estimation of degradation patterns. To obtain the true causation between degraded images and restored images, we propose Causal-deconfounding Wavelet-disentangled Prompt Network (CWP-Net) to perform effective AiOIR. CWP-Net introduces two modules for decoupling, i.e., wavelet attention module of encoder and wavelet attention module of decoder. These modules explicitly disentangle the degradation and semantic features to tackle the issue of spurious correlation. To address the issue stemming from the biased estimation of degradation patterns, CWP-Net leverages a wavelet prompt block to generate the alternative variable for causal deconfounding. Extensive experiments on two all-in-one settings prove the effectiveness and superior performance of our proposed CWP-Net over the state-of-the-art AiOIR methods.

AI.tldr: CWP-Net introduces a causally-motivated, wavelet-based decoupling framework for AiOIR to mitigate spurious semantic-degradation correlations and biased degradation-pattern estimation, achieving superior results across two all-in-one settings.
AI.motivation: To address two key defects in all-in-one image restoration (AiOIR): (1) spurious correlation between non-degradation semantic features and degradation patterns, and (2) biased estimation of degradation patterns, which hinder effectiveness and generalization.
AI.method: Proposes CWP-Net with two wavelet attention modules (in encoder and decoder) to disentangle degradation and semantic features, plus a wavelet prompt block that generates an alternative variable for causal deconfounding; evaluated on two all-in-one restoration settings.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show CWP-Net outperforms state-of-the-art AiOIR methods in the two settings, demonstrating improved restoration quality and generalization.
AI.conclusion: CWP-Net effectively mitigates causal issues in AiOIR through wavelet-based feature disentanglement and causal prompting, delivering superior restoration performance.

title: Large-Margin Hyperdimensional Computing: A Learning-Theoretical Perspective
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Overparameterized machine learning (ML) methods such as neural networks may be prohibitively resource intensive for devices with limited computational capabilities. Hyperdimensional computing (HDC) is an emerging resource efficient and low-complexity ML method that allows hardware efficient implementations of (re-)training and inference procedures. In this paper, we propose a maximum-margin HDC classifier, which significantly outperforms baseline HDC methods on several benchmark datasets. Our method leverages a formal relation between HDC and support vector machines (SVMs) that we established for the first time. Our findings may inspire novel HDC methods with potentially more hardware-oriented implementations compared to SVMs, thus enabling more efficient learning solutions for various intelligent resource-constrained applications.

AI.tldr: Introduces a maximum-margin classifier for hyperdimensional computing (HDC) that leverages a first-time formal link between HDC and SVMs, achieving notable improvement over baseline HDC methods on benchmark datasets and suggesting hardware-efficient learning possibilities.
AI.motivation: Overparameterized ML models (e.g., deep nets) demand substantial resources. HDC offers a resource-efficient alternative with simpler training/inference, but baseline HDC methods lag behind conventional approaches. Establishing a formal HDC-SVM relationship aims to boost HDC performance and guide hardware-friendly algorithm design.
AI.method: Develops a maximum-margin HDC classifier and formalizes its relation to SVMs. The approach likely adapts margin-based optimization concepts to the high-dimensional distributed representations characteristic of HDC, enabling improved decision boundaries while maintaining hardware-friendly operations.
AI.result: The proposed maximum-margin HDC classifier significantly outperforms baseline HDC methods on several benchmark datasets, validating the effectiveness of the HDC-SVM link and the margin-based approach.
AI.conclusion: Linking HDC with SVM-style margins yields a performance- and hardware-oriented advancement for HDC, potentially inspiring new methods that capitalize on SVM insights for efficient learning in resource-constrained settings.

title: Tendon Force Modeling for Sim2Real Transfer of Reinforcement Learning Policies for Tendon-Driven Robots
summary: Robots which make use of soft or compliant inter- actions often leverage tendon-driven actuation which enables actuators to be placed more flexibly, and compliance to be maintained. However, controlling complex tendon systems is challenging. Simulation paired with reinforcement learning (RL) could be enable more complex behaviors to be generated. Such methods rely on torque and force-based simulation roll- outs which are limited by the sim-to-real gap, stemming from the actuator and system dynamics, resulting in poor transfer of RL policies onto real robots. To address this, we propose a method to model the tendon forces produced by typical servo motors, focusing specifically on the transfer of RL policies for a tendon driven finger. Our approach extends existing data- driven techniques by leveraging contextual history and a novel data collection test-bench. This test-bench allows us to capture tendon forces undergo contact-rich interactions typical of real- world manipulation. We then utilize our force estimation model in a GPU-accelerated tendon force-driven rigid body simulation to train RL-based controllers. Our transformer-based model is capable of predicting tendon forces within 3% of the maximum motor force and is robot-agnostic. By integrating our learned model into simulation, we reduce the sim-to-real gap for test trajectories by 41%. RL-based controller trained with our model achieves a 50% improvement in fingertip pose tracking tasks on real tendon-driven robotic fingers. This approach is generalizable to different actuators and robot systems, and can enable RL policies to be used widely across tendon systems, advancing capabilities of dexterous manipulators and soft robots.

AI.tldr: Transformer-based tendon-force estimator integrated into GPU-accelerated simulation to reduce sim-to-real gap in tendon-driven fingers; achieves 3% force prediction error, 41% gap reduction, and 50% improvement in real fingertip tracking; robot-agnostic and generalizable.
AI.motivation: Tendon-driven actuation offers flexible, compliant manipulation but is hard to control; sim-to-real transfer for RL is hindered by actuator and system dynamics not captured accurately in simulations, necessitating robust, robot-agnostic force models.
AI.method: Develop a transformer-based force estimator trained with contextual history using a novel data-collection test-bench that captures tendon forces during contact-rich manipulation; integrate the learned model into a GPU-accelerated tendon-force driven rigid-body sim to train RL controllers.
AI.result: The model predicts tendon forces within 3% of the maximum motor force; integration reduces the sim-to-real gap by 41%; RL controllers trained with the model achieve ~50% better fingertip pose tracking on real tendon-driven fingers.
AI.conclusion: The approach is robot-agnostic and generalizable to a range of actuators and robot systems, enabling broader deployment of RL policies across tendon-based manipulators and advancing dexterous manipulation and soft robotics.

title: DeepScan: A Training-Free Framework for Visually Grounded Reasoning in Large Vision-Language Models
summary: Humans can robustly localize visual evidence and provide grounded answers even in noisy environments by identifying critical cues and then relating them to the full context in a bottom-up manner. Inspired by this, we propose DeepScan, a training-free framework that combines Hierarchical Scanning, Refocusing, and Evidence-Enhanced Reasoning for visually grounded reasoning in Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs). Unlike existing methods that pursue one-shot localization of complete evidence, Hierarchical Scanning performs local cue exploration and multi-scale evidence extraction to recover evidence in a bottom-up manner, effectively mitigating the impacts of distractive context. Refocusing then optimizes the localized evidence view through collaboration of LVLMs and visual experts. Finally, Evidence-Enhanced Reasoning aggregates multi-granular views via a hybrid evidence memory and yields accurate and interpretable answers. Experimental results demonstrate that DeepScan significantly boosts LVLMs in diverse visual tasks, especially in fine-grained visual understanding. It achieves 90.6% overall accuracy on V* when integrated with Qwen2.5-VL-7B. Moreover, DeepScan provides consistent improvements for LVLMs across various architectures and model scales without additional adaptation cost.

AI.tldr: DeepScan is a training-free framework for visually grounded reasoning in LVLMs that uses Hierarchical Scanning, Refocusing, and Evidence-Enhanced Reasoning to extract multi-granular visual evidence and improve accuracy and interpretability, achieving strong results (e.g., 90.6% on V* with Qwen2.5-VL-7B) without extra adaptation costs.
AI.motivation: Address robustness gaps in LVLMs under noisy contexts by mimicking human bottom-up evidence gathering; existing methods focus on single-shot evidence localization and can be misled by distractors.
AI.method: DeepScan combines three components: (1) Hierarchical Scanning for local cue exploration and multi-scale evidence extraction in a bottom-up fashion, (2) Refocusing to optimize the localized evidence view via collaboration between LVLMs and visual experts, and (3) Evidence-Enhanced Reasoning to aggregate multi-granular views with a hybrid evidence memory for interpretable answers. It is training-free.
AI.result: Empirically boosts LVLMs across architectures and scales on diverse visual tasks, with notable gains in fine-grained understanding; reports 90.6% overall accuracy on V* when integrated with Qwen2.5-VL-7B and shows consistent improvements without extra adaptation cost.
AI.conclusion: DeepScan effectively mitigates distractive context, enhances accuracy and interpretability, and generalizes across model families without additional training, offering a scalable solution for visually grounded reasoning in LVLMs.

title: Structure-Aware Distributed Backdoor Attacks in Federated Learning
summary: While federated learning protects data privacy, it also makes the model update process vulnerable to long-term stealthy perturbations. Existing studies on backdoor attacks in federated learning mainly focus on trigger design or poisoning strategies, typically assuming that identical perturbations behave similarly across different model architectures. This assumption overlooks the impact of model structure on perturbation effectiveness. From a structure-aware perspective, this paper analyzes the coupling relationship between model architectures and backdoor perturbations. We introduce two metrics, Structural Responsiveness Score (SRS) and Structural Compatibility Coefficient (SCC), to measure a model's sensitivity to perturbations and its preference for fractal perturbations. Based on these metrics, we develop a structure-aware fractal perturbation injection framework (TFI) to study the role of architectural properties in the backdoor injection process. Experimental results show that model architecture significantly influences the propagation and aggregation of perturbations. Networks with multi-path feature fusion can amplify and retain fractal perturbations even under low poisoning ratios, while models with low structural compatibility constrain their effectiveness. Further analysis reveals a strong correlation between SCC and attack success rate, suggesting that SCC can predict perturbation survivability. These findings highlight that backdoor behaviors in federated learning depend not only on perturbation design or poisoning intensity but also on the interaction between model architecture and aggregation mechanisms, offering new insights for structure-aware defense design.

AI.tldr: Architectural structure strongly shapes backdoor perturbation behavior in federated learning; a structure-aware approach reveals that multi-path fusion can magnify fractal perturbations and SCC predicts attack success.
AI.motivation: Federated learning vulnerabilities: backdoor attacks; assumption of perturbation transferability across architectures is flawed; need structure-aware understanding of how architecture affects perturbation effectiveness.
AI.method: Propose Structural Responsiveness Score (SRS) and Structural Compatibility Coefficient (SCC); develop TFI (structure-aware fractal perturbation injection) framework; conduct experiments across model architectures; analyze propagation and aggregation of perturbations; assess correlation between SCC and attack success.
AI.result: Model architecture significantly influences perturbation propagation; multi-path feature fusion amplifies/retains fractal perturbations even at low poisoning ratios; architectures with low structural compatibility limit perturbations; SCC correlates with attack success rate; structure-aware insights for defense.
AI.conclusion: Backdoor behavior in federated learning arises from interaction between model architecture and aggregation mechanics; defenses should consider architecture-level properties, not only perturbation design or poisoning intensity.

title: A Soft Robotic Demonstration in the Stratosphere
summary: Machines designed for operation in Space, as well as other extreme environments, need to be both resilient and adaptable when mission parameters change. Soft robots offer advantages in adaptability, but most lack resilience to the pressure and temperature extremes found as close as the Stratosphere. Dielectric elastomer actuators overcome some of those limitations when built as solid state compliant capacitors capable of converting electrical energy into mechanical work, but the elastomer resilience limits the device's operating window. Here we present a crosslinking mechanism for silicone elastomers under ultraviolet light using trimethyl(methylcyclopentadienyl)platinum(IV) as a catalyst to react hydrosilane to vinyl groups. The formation of carbon-carbon bonds enables fast processing under UV light and exceptional electro-mechanical performance in dielectric elastomer actuators. The material resilience advantage is demonstrated in controlled experiments at -40° and 120° C, as well as near vacuum, in comparison with state-of-the-art acrylic and silicone chemistries. Fully autonomous systems controlling grippers made with the novel silicone were integrated into payloads for high altitude balloon testing. Two stratospheric balloon missions were carried out and demonstrated DEAs as a viable soft robotic technology under space-like conditions (as high as 23.6 km elevation, at <0.05 atm and -55° C). The combinations of chemical building blocks and catalyst can be further expanded to address other challenges for silicones, including adhesion and additive manufacturing.

AI.tldr: UV-crosslinked silicone elastomers using a Pt(IV) catalyst form C–C bonds to enable fast processing and robust dielectric elastomer actuators (DEAs) with wide operating windows. Demonstrated resilience from -40°C to 120°C and near vacuum, and validated in high-altitude balloon tests up to 23.6 km, showing DEAs as viable space-like soft robotics.
AI.motivation: Soft robots offer adaptability but often lack resilience in extreme space-like environments. Dielectric elastomer actuators can convert electrical energy into work but need materials with broader operating windows and robustness for space missions.
AI.method: Ultraviolet-initiated crosslinking of silicone elastomers using trimethyl(methylcyclopentadienyl)platinum(IV) to catalyze hydrosilane–vinyl reactions, forming carbon–carbon bonds. Resulting material processed rapidly under UV, integrated into DEAs, and evaluated against standard acrylic/silicone chemistries. Autonomous grippers were incorporated into balloon payloads for testing.
AI.result: The new silicone chemistry enables fast UV processing, enhanced electro-mechanical performance, and broad resilience (-40°C to 120°C, near vacuum) compared with state-of-the-art chemistries. Demonstrated in two stratospheric balloon missions (up to 23.6 km, <0.05 atm, -55°C) with autonomously controlled grippers, validating DEAs as a viable space-like soft robotic technology.
AI.conclusion: The crosslinking strategy expands silicone capabilities for harsh environments and can be extended to adhesion and additive manufacturing, improving silicone-based DEAs for space applications and other extreme settings.

title: Bridging Human Evaluation to Infrared and Visible Image Fusion
summary: Infrared and visible image fusion (IVIF) integrates complementary modalities to enhance scene perception. Current methods predominantly focus on optimizing handcrafted losses and objective metrics, often resulting in fusion outcomes that do not align with human visual preferences. This challenge is further exacerbated by the ill-posed nature of IVIF, which severely limits its effectiveness in human perceptual environments such as security surveillance and driver assistance systems. To address these limitations, we propose a feedback reinforcement framework that bridges human evaluation to infrared and visible image fusion. To address the lack of human-centric evaluation metrics and data, we introduce the first large-scale human feedback dataset for IVIF, containing multidimensional subjective scores and artifact annotations, and enriched by a fine-tuned large language model with expert review. Based on this dataset, we design a domain-specific reward function and train a reward model to quantify perceptual quality. Guided by this reward, we fine-tune the fusion network through Group Relative Policy Optimization, achieving state-of-the-art performance that better aligns fused images with human aesthetics. Code is available at https://github.com/ALKA-Wind/EVAFusion.

AI.tldr: Proposes a human-in-the-loop reinforcement framework for infrared-visible image fusion (IVIF) using a large-scale human feedback dataset and a reward model trained via LLM-assisted expert review, optimized with Group Relative Policy Optimization to improve perceptual alignment and aesthetics; includes dataset and code release.
AI.motivation: Current IVIF methods optimize handcrafted losses and metrics that often diverge from human visual preferences; the ill-posed nature of IVIF and the need for human-centric evaluation hinder deployment in critical perceptual tasks.
AI.method: Create a large-scale human feedback dataset for IVIF with multidimensional subjective scores and artifact annotations, enhanced by expert review via a fine-tuned large language model; train a domain-specific reward model to quantify perceptual quality; fine-tune the fusion network using Group Relative Policy Optimization guided by the reward; release code and dataset.
AI.result: The approach achieves state-of-the-art perceptual alignment with human aesthetics on IVIF tasks, outperforming baselines in human-centric evaluation metrics and qualitative assessments.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of human feedback-driven reinforcement learning for IVIF, bridging the gap between objective fusion metrics and human perception, with practical implications for security and driver-assistance applications.

title: k-hop Fairness: Addressing Disparities in Graph Link Prediction Beyond First-Order Neighborhoods
summary: Link prediction (LP) plays a central role in graph-based applications, particularly in social recommendation. However, real-world graphs often reflect structural biases, most notably homophily, the tendency of nodes with similar attributes to connect. While this property can improve predictive performance, it also risks reinforcing existing social disparities. In response, fairness-aware LP methods have emerged, often seeking to mitigate these effects by promoting inter-group connections, that is, links between nodes with differing sensitive attributes (e.g., gender), following the principle of dyadic fairness. However, dyadic fairness overlooks potential disparities within the sensitive groups themselves. To overcome this issue, we propose $k$-hop fairness, a structural notion of fairness for LP, that assesses disparities conditioned on the distance between nodes in the graph. We formalize this notion through predictive fairness and structural bias metrics, and propose pre- and post-processing mitigation strategies. Experiments across standard LP benchmarks reveal: (1) a strong tendency of models to reproduce structural biases at different $k$-hops; (2) interdependence between structural biases at different hops when rewiring graphs; and (3) that our post-processing method achieves favorable $k$-hop performance-fairness trade-offs compared to existing fair LP baselines.

AI.tldr: Introduces k-hop fairness for link prediction to quantify and mitigate structural bias across graph distances; defines predictive fairness and structural bias metrics; proposes pre- and post-processing mitigation; shows biases exist at multiple hops and interdependence across hops, with post-processing offering favorable fairness-performance trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Fairness in link prediction should go beyond dyadic fairness (inter-group links) to address disparities within sensitive groups and across different graph distances; homophily can reinforce social inequality in multi-hop neighborhoods.
AI.method: Define k-hop fairness as a structural fairness notion conditioned on graph distance; formalize predictive fairness and structural bias metrics; develop pre-processing and post-processing mitigation strategies; evaluate on standard LP benchmarks.
AI.result: Models reproduce structural biases across different k hops; biases at different hops are interdependent when graphs are rewired; the proposed post-processing method yields better k-hop fairness-performance trade-offs compared to existing fair LP baselines.
AI.conclusion: k-hop fairness provides a practical, distance-aware fairness framework for LP; effective mitigation via post-processing with favorable trade-offs; encourages multi-hop consideration in LP fairness research.

title: HALyPO: Heterogeneous-Agent Lyapunov Policy Optimization for Human-Robot Collaboration
summary: To improve generalization and resilience in human-robot collaboration (HRC), robots must handle the combinatorial diversity of human behaviors and contexts, motivating multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL). However, inherent heterogeneity between robots and humans creates a rationality gap (RG) in the learning process-a variational mismatch between decentralized best-response dynamics and centralized cooperative ascent. The resulting learning problem is a general-sum differentiable game, so independent policy-gradient updates can oscillate or diverge without added structure. We propose heterogeneous-agent Lyapunov policy optimization (HALyPO), which establishes formal stability directly in the policy-parameter space by enforcing a per-step Lyapunov decrease condition on a parameter-space disagreement metric. Unlike Lyapunov-based safe RL, which targets state/trajectory constraints in constrained Markov decision processes, HALyPO uses Lyapunov certification to stabilize decentralized policy learning. HALyPO rectifies decentralized gradients via optimal quadratic projections, ensuring monotonic contraction of RG and enabling effective exploration of open-ended interaction spaces. Extensive simulations and real-world humanoid-robot experiments show that this certified stability improves generalization and robustness in collaborative corner cases.

AI.tldr: HALyPO introduces a heterogeneous-agent Lyapunov policy optimization framework to stabilize decentralized MARL for human-robot collaboration by enforcing a per-step Lyapunov decrease on a parameter-space disagreement metric and using optimal quadratic projections to rectify gradients, improving generalization and robustness in open-ended interactions.
AI.motivation: To tackle the RG-induced instability and oscillations in general-sum differentiable MARL for human-robot collaboration due to heterogeneity between robots and humans, aiming for certified stability and better generalization in open-ended interaction spaces.
AI.method: HALyPO enforces a per-step Lyapunov decrease constraint in the policy-parameter space via Lyapunov certification. It uses optimal quadratic projections to rectify decentralized gradients, ensuring monotonic contraction of the policy-disagreement metric (RG) and stabilizing learning, enabling effective exploration in open-ended HRC scenarios.
AI.result: Through extensive simulations and real-world humanoid-robot experiments, HALyPO’s certified stability improves generalization and robustness, particularly in corner-case collaboration scenarios.
AI.conclusion: HALyPO provides a stability guarantee for decentralized MARL in heterogeneous-agent HRC, distinct from trajectory-focused Lyapunov-safe RL, and enhances generalization/robustness by stabilizing parameter-space learning dynamics via projection-based gradient correction.

title: RoboCasa365: A Large-Scale Simulation Framework for Training and Benchmarking Generalist Robots
comment: ICLR 2026; First three authors contributed equally
summary: Recent advances in robot learning have accelerated progress toward generalist robots that can perform everyday tasks in human environments. Yet it remains difficult to gauge how close we are to this vision. The field lacks a reproducible, large-scale benchmark for systematic evaluation. To fill this gap, we present RoboCasa365, a comprehensive simulation benchmark for household mobile manipulation. Built on the RoboCasa platform, RoboCasa365 introduces 365 everyday tasks across 2,500 diverse kitchen environments, with over 600 hours of human demonstration data and over 1600 hours of synthetically generated demonstration data -- making it one of the most diverse and large-scale resources for studying generalist policies. RoboCasa365 is designed to support systematic evaluations for different problem settings, including multi-task learning, robot foundation model training, and lifelong learning. We conduct extensive experiments on this benchmark with state-of-the-art methods and analyze the impacts of task diversity, dataset scale, and environment variation on generalization. Our results provide new insights into what factors most strongly affect the performance of generalist robots and inform strategies for future progress in the field.

AI.tldr: A large-scale, diverse simulation benchmark for household manipulation (RoboCasa365) enabling systematic evaluation of generalist policies across 365 tasks, 2,500 kitchens, 600h human and 1,600h synthetic demonstrations; supports multi-task, foundation-model, and lifelong learning; investigates the impact of task diversity, data scale, and environment variation on generalization.
AI.motivation: Addresses the lack of reproducible, large-scale benchmarks for evaluating generalist robots in home environments; aims to quantify progress toward general-purpose manipulation and guide future research directions.
AI.method: Developed RoboCasa365 on the RoboCasa platform; assembled 365 tasks across 2,500 kitchen environments; collected >600 hours of human demonstrations and >1600 hours of synthetic demonstrations; designed to support multiple problem settings; conducted extensive experiments with state-of-the-art methods.
AI.result: Identified how task diversity, dataset scale, and environment variation influence generalization; provided insights into important factors affecting generalist robot performance; demonstrated the benchmark's utility for evaluating various learning paradigms.
AI.conclusion: RoboCasa365 advances systematic benchmarking for household manipulation and can guide future progress by highlighting key factors that affect generalization and by enabling standardized, scalable evaluation of generalist policies.

title: Yolo-Key-6D: Single Stage Monocular 6D Pose Estimation with Keypoint Enhancements
summary: Estimating the 6D pose of objects from a single RGB image is a critical task for robotics and extended reality applications. However, state-of-the-art multi stage methods often suffer from high latency, making them unsuitable for real time use. In this paper, we present Yolo-Key-6D, a novel single stage, end-to-end framework for monocular 6D pose estimation designed for both speed and accuracy. Our approach enhances a YOLO based architecture by integrating an auxiliary head that regresses the 2D projections of an object's 3D bounding box corners. This keypoint detection task significantly improves the network's understanding of 3D geometry. For stable end-to-end training, we directly regress rotation using a continuous 9D representation projected to SO(3) via singular value decomposition. On the LINEMOD and LINEMOD-Occluded benchmarks, YOLO-Key-6D achieves competitive accuracy scores of 96.24% and 69.41%, respectively, with the ADD(-S) 0.1d metric, while proving itself to operate in real time. Our results demonstrate that a carefully designed single stage method can provide a practical and effective balance of performance and efficiency for real world deployment.

AI.tldr: A single-stage monocular 6D pose estimator (YOLO-Key-6D) that adds a keypoint regression head to a YOLO-like network, uses a continuous 9D rotation representation, and achieves real-time performance with competitive accuracy on LINEMOD benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Reduce latency of multi-stage 6D pose methods for real-time robotics/AR; improve 3D geometry understanding via 2D-3D keypoint regression; ensure stable training with SO(3) projection.
AI.method: Augments YOLO-based architecture with an auxiliary head predicting 2D projections of 3D bounding box corners; regresses rotation using 9D representation projected to SO(3) via SVD; end-to-end single-stage framework; designed for speed and accuracy.
AI.result: Achieves ADD(-S) 0.1d scores of 96.24% (LINEMOD) and 69.41% (LINEMOD-Occluded); operates in real time.
AI.conclusion: Shows that a well-designed single-stage approach can match accuracy while enabling real-time monocular 6D pose estimation, aided by keypoint supervision and robust rotation parameterization.

title: Believe Your Model: Distribution-Guided Confidence Calibration
summary: Large Reasoning Models have demonstrated remarkable performance with the advancement of test-time scaling techniques, which enhances prediction accuracy by generating multiple candidate responses and selecting the most reliable answer. While prior work has analyzed that internal model signals like confidence scores can partly indicate response correctness and exhibit a distributional correlation with accuracy, such distributional information has not been fully utilized to guide answer selection. Motivated by this, we propose DistriVoting, which incorporates distributional priors as another signal alongside confidence during voting. Specifically, our method (1) first decomposes the mixed confidence distribution into positive and negative components using Gaussian Mixture Models, (2) then applies a reject filter based on positive/negative samples from them to mitigate overlap between the two distributions. Besides, to further alleviate the overlap from the perspective of distribution itself, we propose SelfStepConf, which uses step-level confidence to dynamically adjust inference process, increasing the separation between the two distributions to improve the reliability of confidences in voting. Experiments across 16 models and 5 benchmarks demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches.

AI.tldr: DistriVoting leverages distribution priors of internal confidence to enhance vote-based selection in large reasoning models, using Gaussian Mixture Models to separate positive/negative confidence and a SelfStepConf mechanism to further increase separation, achieving strong gains across multiple models and benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Exploit distributional information in model confidences, not just average scores, to improve reliability and accuracy of final answers in large reasoning models.
AI.method: 1) Decompose mixed confidence into positive/negative components via Gaussian Mixture Models; 2) Apply a reject filter to reduce overlap between components; 3) Introduce SelfStepConf to dynamically adjust inference using step-level confidence, further separating distributions.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation across 16 models and 5 benchmarks shows significant improvement over state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: Utilizing distributional priors for confidence signals and dynamic inference control improves the reliability and accuracy of voting-based answers in large reasoning models.

title: ManipulationNet: An Infrastructure for Benchmarking Real-World Robot Manipulation with Physical Skill Challenges and Embodied Multimodal Reasoning
summary: Dexterous manipulation enables robots to purposefully alter the physical world, transforming them from passive observers into active agents in unstructured environments. This capability is the cornerstone of physical artificial intelligence. Despite decades of advances in hardware, perception, control, and learning, progress toward general manipulation systems remains fragmented due to the absence of widely adopted standard benchmarks. The central challenge lies in reconciling the variability of the real world with the reproducibility and authenticity required for rigorous scientific evaluation. To address this, we introduce ManipulationNet, a global infrastructure that hosts real-world benchmark tasks for robotic manipulation. ManipulationNet delivers reproducible task setups through standardized hardware kits, and enables distributed performance evaluation via a unified software client that delivers real-time task instructions and collects benchmarking results. As a persistent and scalable infrastructure, ManipulationNet organizes benchmark tasks into two complementary tracks: 1) the Physical Skills Track, which evaluates low-level physical interaction skills, and 2) the Embodied Reasoning Track, which tests high-level reasoning and multimodal grounding abilities. This design fosters the systematic growth of an interconnected network of real-world abilities and skills, paving the path toward general robotic manipulation. By enabling comparable manipulation research in the real world at scale, this infrastructure establishes a sustainable foundation for measuring long-term scientific progress and identifying capabilities ready for real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: Introduction of ManipulationNet, a global infrastructure for real-world robotic manipulation benchmarking, featuring standardized hardware kits and a unified software client, with two benchmark tracks (Physical Skills and Embodied Reasoning) to enable scalable, reproducible evaluation of manipulation abilities.
AI.motivation: Addresses the lack of widely adopted real-world benchmarks in robotic manipulation by reconciling real-world variability with the need for reproducible, authentic evaluation and long-term scientific progress.
AI.method: Develop ManipulationNet as a persistent infrastructure that provides standardized hardware kits for reproducible task setups and a distributed evaluation workflow via a unified software client that delivers real-time task instructions and collects results, organized into two complementary tracks: Physical Skills Track and Embodied Reasoning Track.
AI.result: A conceptual platform description and architectural blueprint for real-world benchmarking in robotic manipulation, detailing the hardware standardization, software client, and task-track organization without reporting empirical results yet.
AI.conclusion: ManipulationNet is positioned as a sustainable framework to measure long-term progress in robotic manipulation, enabling scalable real-world research, connecting low-level physical skills with high-level reasoning, and helping identify capabilities ready for real deployment.

title: UniSync: Towards Generalizable and High-Fidelity Lip Synchronization for Challenging Scenarios
summary: Lip synchronization aims to generate realistic talking videos that match given audio, which is essential for high-quality video dubbing. However, current methods have fundamental drawbacks: mask-based approaches suffer from local color discrepancies, while mask-free methods struggle with global background texture misalignment. Furthermore, most methods struggle with diverse real-world scenarios such as stylized avatars, face occlusion, and extreme lighting conditions. In this paper, we propose UniSync, a unified framework designed for achieving high-fidelity lip synchronization in diverse scenarios. Specifically, UniSync uses a mask-free pose-anchored training strategy to keep head motion and eliminate synthesis color artifacts, while employing mask-based blending consistent inference to ensure structural precision and smooth blending. Notably, fine-tuning on compact but diverse videos empowers our model with exceptional domain adaptability, handling complex corner cases effectively. We also introduce the RealWorld-LipSync benchmark to evaluate models under real-world demands, which covers diverse application scenarios including both human faces and stylized avatars. Extensive experiments demonstrate that UniSync significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, advancing the field towards truly generalizable and production-ready lip synchronization.

AI.tldr: UniSync is a unified lip synchronization framework that combines mask-free pose-anchored training with mask-based blending to achieve high-fidelity, adaptable lip-sync across diverse real-world scenarios, plus a new RealWorld-LipSync benchmark; it outperforms prior methods.
AI.motivation: Current lip-sync methods face two fundamental drawbacks: mask-based approaches produce local color artifacts, while mask-free methods suffer from global background texture misalignment. Real-world demands include stylized avatars, occlusions, and extreme lighting, necessitating a unified, adaptable solution.
AI.method: A mask-free pose-anchored training strategy to preserve head motion and suppress synthesis color artifacts, paired with mask-based blending consistent inference to ensure structural precision and smooth blending. Additional fine-tuning on compact but diverse videos enhances domain adaptability. Introduction of RealWorld-LipSync benchmark for evaluation across human faces and stylized avatars.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show UniSync significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, delivering higher fidelity lip-sync and robustness across diverse scenarios, including stylized avatars, occlusion, and challenging lighting. The RealWorld-LipSync benchmark validates real-world applicability and generalizability.
AI.conclusion: UniSync provides a unified, production-ready framework for lip synchronization with strong generalization across real-world scenarios. The RealWorld-LipSync benchmark offers a valuable evaluation suite for future work seeking practical deployment.

title: PatchDecomp: Interpretable Patch-Based Time Series Forecasting
summary: Time series forecasting, which predicts future values from past observations, plays a central role in many domains and has driven the development of highly accurate neural network models. However, the complexity of these models often limits human understanding of the rationale behind their predictions. We propose PatchDecomp, a neural network-based time series forecasting method that achieves both high accuracy and interpretability. PatchDecomp divides input time series into subsequences (patches) and generates predictions by aggregating the contributions of each patch. This enables clear attribution of each patch, including those from exogenous variables, to the final prediction. Experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that PatchDecomp provides predictive performance comparable to recent forecasting methods. Furthermore, we show that the model's explanations not only influence predicted values quantitatively but also offer qualitative interpretability through visualization of patch-wise contributions.

AI.tldr: PatchDecomp is a time series forecasting model that partitions input into patches and aggregates their contributions to predict future values, providing competitive accuracy with patch-level explanations (including exogenous variables) and visualizable attribution.
AI.motivation: The need for transparent, interpretable neural forecasting models that retain high predictive accuracy, enabling users to understand how different time-series patches influence forecasts, including exogenous inputs.
AI.method: Divide the input time series into subsequences (patches). The model learns to compute and aggregate the contribution of each patch to the final forecast, enabling patch-wise attribution. Exogenous variables are incorporated into the patch-level explanations. Explanations are claimable both quantitatively (attribution magnitudes) and qualitatively (visualizations).
AI.result: Experiments on multiple benchmark datasets show predictive performance comparable to recent forecasting methods, while providing interpretable explanations via patch-wise contributions, including visualization capabilities.
AI.conclusion: PatchDecomp demonstrates that it is possible to achieve high-accuracy forecasting with interpretable, patch-based explanations, enabling clear attribution of predictions to individual patches and exogenous inputs.

title: Interaction-Aware Whole-Body Control for Compliant Object Transport
summary: Cooperative object transport in unstructured environments remains challenging for assistive humanoids because strong, time-varying interaction forces can make tracking-centric whole-body control unreliable, especially in close-contact support tasks. This paper proposes a bio-inspired, interaction-oriented whole-body control (IO-WBC) that functions as an artificial cerebellum - an adaptive motor agent that translates upstream (skill-level) commands into stable, physically consistent whole-body behavior under contact. This work structurally separates upper-body interaction execution from lower-body support control, enabling the robot to maintain balance while shaping force exchange in a tightly coupled robot-object system. A trajectory-optimized reference generator (RG) provides a kinematic prior, while a reinforcement learning (RL) policy governs body responses under heavy-load interactions and disturbances. The policy is trained in simulation with randomized payload mass/inertia and external perturbations, and deployed via asymmetric teacher-student distillation so that the student relies only on proprioceptive histories at runtime. Extensive experiments demonstrate that IO-WBC maintains stable whole-body behavior and physical interaction even when precise velocity tracking becomes infeasible, enabling compliant object transport across a wide range of scenarios.

AI.tldr: Bio-inspired, interaction-oriented whole-body control (IO-WBC) for assistive humanoids that decouples upper-body interaction execution from lower-body support. It uses a trajectory-optimized reference generator (RG) and an RL policy trained in simulation with randomized payloads/disturbances, deployed via asymmetric teacher-student distillation to run on proprioceptive histories. Demonstrates stable, compliant object transport under heavy contact and velocity-tracking limitations.
AI.motivation: Tracking-centric whole-body control can become unreliable in unstructured environments due to strong, time-varying interaction forces during close-contact assistance. A robust, physically consistent control framework is needed that can adapt to contact-rich interactions and disturbances while maintaining balance.
AI.method: Proposes an upper-body interaction execution module decoupled from lower-body support control. A trajectory-optimized reference generator provides a kinematic prior. An RL policy governs body responses under heavy-load interactions and disturbances. The policy is trained in simulation with randomized payload mass/inertia and external perturbations, and deployed via asymmetric teacher-student distillation so that at runtime only proprioceptive histories are required.
AI.result: IO-WBC maintains stable whole-body behavior and physical interaction even when precise velocity tracking is infeasible, enabling compliant object transport across a wide range of scenarios; validated through extensive experiments.
AI.conclusion: A cerebellum-like, adaptive motor controller for assistive humanoids that separates interaction execution from support control, leverages planning priors and learning-based adaptation, and enables robust, compliant object transport in unstructured environments. The approach shows strong sim-to-real potential via distillation, with room for further validation on real hardware and broader task generalization.

title: A novel network for classification of cuneiform tablet metadata
summary: In this paper, we present a network structure for classifying metadata of cuneiform tablets. The problem is of practical importance, as the size of the existing corpus far exceeds the number of experts available to analyze it. But the task is made difficult by the combination of limited annotated datasets and the high-resolution point-cloud representation of each tablet. To address this, we develop a convolution-inspired architecture that gradually down-scales the point cloud while integrating local neighbor information. The final down-scaled point cloud is then processed by computing neighbors in the feature space to include global information. Our method is compared with the state-of-the-art transformer-based network Point-BERT, and consistently obtains the best performance. Source code and datasets will be released at publication.

AI.tldr: A convolution-inspired point-cloud network for cuneiform tablet metadata classification that outperforms Point-BERT on limited-data settings, with open-source release planned.
AI.motivation: Tackle practical archaeology data challenges: a large corpus of tablets, scarce expert annotations, and high-resolution 3D point-cloud representations requiring effective local-global feature aggregation.
AI.method: A convolution-inspired architecture that progressively down-samples the point cloud with local neighborhood aggregation; after down-sampling, it computes neighbors in feature space to inject global information; the model is evaluated against the transformer-based Point-BERT.
AI.result: The proposed method consistently achieves the best performance compared to Point-BERT on this task.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of convolution-inspired point-cloud networks for archaeological metadata classification under limited data, with anticipated open-source release of code and datasets.

title: BD-Merging: Bias-Aware Dynamic Model Merging with Evidence-Guided Contrastive Learning
summary: Model Merging (MM) has emerged as a scalable paradigm for multi-task learning (MTL), enabling multiple task-specific models to be integrated without revisiting the original training data. Despite recent progress, the reliability of MM under test-time distribution shift remains insufficiently understood. Most existing MM methods typically assume that test data are clean and distributionally aligned with both the training and auxiliary sources. However, this assumption rarely holds in practice, often resulting in biased predictions with degraded generalization. To address this issue, we present BD-Merging, a bias-aware unsupervised model merging framework that explicitly models uncertainty to achieve adaptive reliability under distribution shift. First, BD-Merging introduces a joint evidential head that learns uncertainty over a unified label space, capturing cross-task semantic dependencies in MM. Second, building upon this evidential foundation, we propose an Adjacency Discrepancy Score (ADS) that quantifies evidential alignment among neighboring samples. Third, guided by ADS, a discrepancy-aware contrastive learning mechanism refines the merged representation by aligning consistent samples and separating conflicting ones. Combined with general unsupervised learning, this process trains a debiased router that adaptively allocates task-specific or layer-specific weights on a per-sample basis, effectively mitigating the adverse effects of distribution shift. Extensive experiments across diverse tasks demonstrate that BD-Merging achieves superior effectiveness and robustness compared to state-of-the-art MM baselines.

AI.tldr: BD-Merging is a bias-aware unsupervised model merging framework for robust multi-task learning under distribution shift, introducing a joint evidential head, an Adjacency Discrepancy Score (ADS), and discrepancy-aware contrastive learning to enable per-sample routing and debiasing.
AI.motivation: To address unreliable model merging under test-time distribution shift where test data deviate from training/auxiliary sources, causing biased predictions and degraded generalization.
AI.method: 1) A joint evidential head that learns uncertainty over a unified label space to capture cross-task semantic dependencies in MM. 2) Adjacency Discrepancy Score (ADS) to quantify evidential alignment among neighboring samples. 3) Discrepancy-aware contrastive learning to align consistent samples and separate conflicting ones, guided by ADS. 4) A debiased router trained with general unsupervised learning to allocate per-sample task-specific or layer-specific weights, mitigating distribution shift effects.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on diverse tasks shows BD-Merging achieves superior effectiveness and robustness compared with state-of-the-art MM baselines.
AI.conclusion: BD-Merging provides an uncertainty-aware, bias-mitigating framework for model merging under distribution shift, with a joint evidential head, ADS, and discrepancy-aware contrastive learning enabling adaptive per-sample routing and improved generalization.

title: Cognition to Control - Multi-Agent Learning for Human-Humanoid Collaborative Transport
summary: Effective human-robot collaboration (HRC) requires translating high-level intent into contact-stable whole-body motion while continuously adapting to a human partner. Many vision-language-action (VLA) systems learn end-to-end mappings from observations and instructions to actions, but they often emphasize reactive (System 1-like) behavior and leave under-specified how sustained System 2-style deliberation can be integrated with reliable, low-latency continuous control. This gap is acute in multi-agent HRC, where long-horizon coordination decisions and physical execution must co-evolve under contact, feasibility, and safety constraints. We address this limitation with cognition-to-control (C2C), a three-layer hierarchy that makes the deliberation-to-control pathway explicit: (i) a VLM-based grounding layer that maintains persistent scene referents and infers embodiment-aware affordances/constraints; (ii) a deliberative skill/coordination layer-the System 2 core-that optimizes long-horizon skill choices and sequences under human-robot coupling via decentralized MARL cast as a Markov potential game with a shared potential encoding task progress; and (iii) a whole-body control layer that executes the selected skills at high frequency while enforcing kinematic/dynamic feasibility and contact stability. The deliberative layer is realized as a residual policy relative to a nominal controller, internalizing partner dynamics without explicit role assignment. Experiments on collaborative manipulation tasks show higher success and robustness than single-agent and end-to-end baselines, with stable coordination and emergent leader-follower behaviors.

AI.tldr: Three-layer cognition-to-control (C2C) for human-robot collaboration (HRC) combining grounding, deliberative multi-agent planning, and high-frequency whole-body control, with a residual policy, yielding better performance and emergent coordination.
AI.motivation: To address the gap between end-to-end, reactive vision-language-action systems and the need for sustained, low-latency, long-horizon, contact-aware coordination in multi-agent HRC.
AI.method: A three-layer architecture: (i) VLM-based grounding layer for persistent scene referents and embodiment-aware affordances; (ii) a deliberative, System 2 core using decentralized MARL cast as a Markov potential game with a shared task-progress potential, internalizing partner dynamics via a residual policy on top of a nominal controller; (iii) a high-frequency whole-body control layer enforcing kinematic/dynamic feasibility and contact stability. The deliberative layer optimizes long-horizon skill choices and sequences under human-robot coupling without explicit role assignment.
AI.result: Experiments on collaborative manipulation tasks show higher success and robustness compared with single-agent and end-to-end baselines, with stable coordination and emergent leader-follower behaviors.
AI.conclusion: C2C demonstrates that making the deliberation-to-control pathway explicit improves HRC performance and coordination, validating a practical architecture for integrating System 2 planning with fast, safe low-level control in multi-agent settings.

title: From Local Matches to Global Masks: Novel Instance Detection in Open-World Scenes
summary: Detecting and segmenting novel object instances in open-world environments is a fundamental problem in robotic perception. Given only a small set of template images, a robot must locate and segment a specific object instance in a cluttered, previously unseen scene. Existing proposal-based approaches are highly sensitive to proposal quality and often fail under occlusion and background clutter. We propose L2G-Det, a local-to-global instance detection framework that bypasses explicit object proposals by leveraging dense patch-level matching between templates and the query image. Locally matched patches generate candidate points, which are refined through a candidate selection module to suppress false positives. The filtered points are then used to prompt an augmented Segment Anything Model (SAM) with instance-specific object tokens, enabling reliable reconstruction of complete instance masks. Experiments demonstrate improved performance over proposal-based methods in challenging open-world settings.

AI.tldr: A proposal-free, local-to-global detection framework (L2G-Det) for open-world instance detection that uses dense patch-level matching to locate novel object instances, refines candidate points to reduce false positives, and leverages an augmented SAM with instance-specific tokens to recover complete masks.
AI.motivation: Overcome dependence on proposal-based detectors in open-world settings, particularly under occlusion and clutter, by utilizing dense patch matching and a robust segmentation backbone to detect and segment unseen objects.
AI.method: 1) Dense patch-level matching between template images and query image to produce candidate points without explicit proposals. 2) Candidate selection module refines and suppresses false positives. 3) Filtered points prompt an augmented Segment Anything Model (SAM) with instance-specific object tokens to reconstruct full instance masks.
AI.result: Experimental results indicate improved performance over traditional proposal-based methods in challenging open-world scenarios, highlighting robustness to clutter and occlusion.
AI.conclusion: L2G-Det provides a viable, proposal-free alternative for open-world instance detection, combining local patch matching with global inference and SAM-based segmentation to achieve reliable instance localization and masking.

title: From Misclassifications to Outliers: Joint Reliability Assessment in Classification
comment: 15 pages, 3 figures. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/Intellindust-AI-Lab/SUREPlus
summary: Building reliable classifiers is a fundamental challenge for deploying machine learning in real-world applications. A reliable system should not only detect out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs but also anticipate in-distribution (ID) errors by assigning low confidence to potentially misclassified samples. Yet, most prior work treats OOD detection and failure prediction as separated problems, overlooking their closed connection. We argue that reliability requires evaluating them jointly. To this end, we propose a unified evaluation framework that integrates OOD detection and failure prediction, quantified by our new metrics DS-F1 and DS-AURC, where DS denotes double scoring functions. Experiments on the OpenOOD benchmark show that double scoring functions yield classifiers that are substantially more reliable than traditional single scoring approaches. Our analysis further reveals that OOD-based approaches provide notable gains under simple or far-OOD shifts, but only marginal benefits under more challenging near-OOD conditions. Beyond evaluation, we extend the reliable classifier SURE and introduce SURE+, a new approach that significantly improves reliability across diverse scenarios. Together, our framework, metrics, and method establish a new benchmark for trustworthy classification and offer practical guidance for deploying robust models in real-world settings. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/Intellindust-AI-Lab/SUREPlus.

AI.tldr: Proposes a unified framework to jointly evaluate OOD detection and failure prediction using double scoring functions (DS-F1, DS-AURC), demonstrates gains on OpenOOD, analyzes effectiveness across OOD shifts, and introduces SURE+ to improve reliability with publicly available code.
AI.motivation: Reliability in classification requires addressing both OOD inputs and in-distribution misclassification risk; treating these as separate tasks misses their interdependence and potential shared cues.
AI.method: Introduce a unified evaluation framework with double scoring functions (DS-F1 and DS-AURC) to quantify joint performance. Experimentally assess on OpenOOD. Extend the SURE reliability method to SURE+ and release code at GitHub.
AI.result: Double scoring functions yield substantially more reliable classifiers than single scoring. OOD-based approaches help under simple/far-OOD shifts but offer limited gains for near-OOD shifts. SURE+ significantly improves reliability across diverse scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework sets a new benchmark for trustworthy classification by jointly considering OOD detection and failure prediction, provides practical guidance for deployment, and offers a publicly available implementation to promote adoption.

title: Hierarchical Inference and Closure Learning via Adaptive Surrogates for ODEs and PDEs
summary: Inverse problems are the task of calibrating models to match data. They play a pivotal role in diverse engineering applications by allowing practitioners to align models with reality. In many applications, engineers and scientists do not have a complete picture of i) the detailed properties of a system (such as material properties, geometry, initial conditions, etc.); ii) the complete laws describing all dynamics at play (such as friction laws, complicated damping phenomena, and general nonlinear interactions). In this paper, we develop a principled methodology for leveraging data from collections of distinct yet related physical systems to jointly estimate the individual model parameters of each system, and learn the shared unknown dynamics in the form of an ML-based closure model. To robustly infer the unknown parameters for each system, we employ a hierarchical Bayesian framework, which allows for the joint inference of multiple systems and their population-level statistics. To learn the closures, we use a maximum marginal likelihood estimate of a neural network embeded within the ODE/PDE formulation of the problem. To realize this framework we utilize the ensemble Metropolis-Adjusted Langevin Algorithm (MALA) for stable and efficient sampling.
