To mitigate the computational bottleneck of repetitive forward evaluations in solving inverse problems, we introduce a bilevel optimization strategy to simultaneously train a surrogate forward model alongside the inference. Within this framework, we evaluate and compare distinct surrogate architectures, specifically Fourier Neural Operators (FNO) and parametric Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINNs).

AI.tldr: Hierarchical Bayesian multi-system inverse problem with an ML-based closure learned inside ODE/PDE, using MALA sampling and bilevel surrogate training; evaluates Fourier Neural Operators vs parametric PINNs.
AI.motivation: In many problems, system properties and governing dynamics are incomplete. Leveraging data from related systems enables joint parameter inference and learning of shared, data-driven closures at scale, addressing identifiability and computational challenges.
AI.method: A hierarchical Bayesian framework is used to jointly infer each system's parameters and population-level statistics. The ML closure is learned via maximum marginal likelihood with the neural network embedded in the ODE/PDE. Ensemble MALA performs sampling. A bilevel optimization strategy trains a surrogate forward model to reduce forward solves during inference. Surrogates tested: Fourier Neural Operators (FNO) and parametric Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs).
AI.result: The abstract indicates that the framework is evaluated and compared using FNO and PINN surrogates, but provides no quantitative results in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: The work contributes a principled, scalable approach for joint parameter inference and data-driven closure learning across related systems, leveraging hierarchical Bayes, MALA-based sampling, and surrogate-assisted forward models; it assesses two distinct surrogate architectures (FNO and PINN).

title: Architecture and evaluation protocol for transformer-based visual object tracking in UAV applications
summary: Object tracking from Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) is challenged by platform dynamics, camera motion, and limited onboard resources. Existing visual trackers either lack robustness in complex scenarios or are too computationally demanding for real-time embedded use. We propose an Modular Asynchronous Tracking Architecture (MATA) that combines a transformer-based tracker with an Extended Kalman Filter, integrating ego-motion compensation from sparse optical flow and an object trajectory model. We further introduce a hardware-independent, embedded oriented evaluation protocol and a new metric called Normalized time to Failure (NT2F) to quantify how long a tracker can sustain a tracking sequence without external help. Experiments on UAV benchmarks, including an augmented UAV123 dataset with synthetic occlusions, show consistent improvements in Success and NT2F metrics across multiple tracking processing frequency. A ROS 2 implementation on a Nvidia Jetson AGX Orin confirms that the evaluation protocol more closely matches real-time performance on embedded systems.

AI.tldr: MATA is a modular architecture for UAV tracking that fuses a transformer-based tracker with an EKF, augmented by ego-motion compensation and a trajectory model, plus a hardware-oriented evaluation protocol and NT2F metric to assess sustained tracking without external input. Demonstrates improved success and NT2F on UAV benchmarks, including augmented UAV123, and shows real-time feasibility on Nvidia Jetson AGX Orin via ROS 2.
AI.motivation: To address UAV tracking under platform dynamics, camera motion, and limited onboard resources. Existing trackers trade robustness for speed or vice versa; there is a need for a real-time, robust, embedded-friendly tracker with an evaluation framework that reflects onboard constraints.
AI.method: Modular Asynchronous Tracking Architecture (MATA) combines: (i) a transformer-based visual tracker; (ii) an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) for state estimation; (iii) ego-motion compensation using sparse optical flow; (iv) an object trajectory model; (v) a hardware-independent, embedded-oriented evaluation protocol; (vi) a new Normalized Time to Failure (NT2F) metric. Evaluation includes augmented UAV123 with synthetic occlusions; ROS 2 implementation on Nvidia Jetson AGX Orin.
AI.result: The approach yields consistent improvements in Success and NT2F metrics across multiple tracking processing frequencies on UAV benchmarks, and the evaluation protocol aligns with real-time embedded performance, validated on ROS 2 running on Jetson AGX Orin.
AI.conclusion: MATA offers a robust, real-time capable UAV tracker with an embedded-focused evaluation framework and a novel NT2F metric. It demonstrates practical deployment feasibility and sets the stage for more extensive ablations and benchmarking across diverse UAV scenarios.

title: Lang2Str: Two-Stage Crystal Structure Generation with LLMs and Continuous Flow Models
summary: Generative models hold great promise for accelerating material discovery but are often limited by their inflexible single-stage generative process in designing valid and diverse materials. To address this, we propose a two-stage generative framework, Lang2Str, that combines the strengths of large language models (LLMs) and flow-based models for flexible and precise material generation. Our method frames the generative process as a conditional generative task, where an LLM provides high-level conditions by generating descriptions of material unit cells' geometric layouts and properties. These descriptions, informed by the LLM's extensive background knowledge, ensure reasonable structure designs. A conditioned flow model then decodes these textual conditions into precise continuous coordinates and unit cell parameters. This staged approach combines the structured reasoning of LLMs and the distribution modeling capabilities of flow models. Experimental results show that our method achieves competitive performance on \textit{ab initio} material generation and crystal structure prediction tasks, with generated structures exhibiting closer alignment to ground truth in both geometry and energy levels, surpassing state-of-the-art models. The flexibility and modularity of our framework further enable fine-grained control over the generation process, potentially leading to more efficient and customizable material design.

AI.tldr: Two-stage Lang2Str combines LLM-guided high-level design with a conditioned flow decoder to generate material unit cells, achieving competitive ab initio generation and crystal structure prediction with enhanced control and near-ground-truth geometry/energies.
AI.motivation: Overcome inflexible single-stage generative models that struggle to ensure validity and diversity in material design; exploit LLMs' reasoning and domain knowledge plus flow models' distribution modeling.
AI.method: LLM generates textual descriptions of unit cell geometry/properties; conditioned flow model translates descriptions into continuous coordinates and unit cell parameters; conditional generative framework.
AI.result: Competitive performance on ab initio generation and crystal structure prediction; generated structures closer to ground truth in geometry and energy levels; surpasses SOTA; modular design enables fine-grained control.
AI.conclusion: Lang2Str offers a flexible, modular pipeline for material generation, enabling targeted design and potential efficiency gains; further work could extend to broader material families and evaluate control fidelity and scalability.

title: Fine-grained Image Aesthetic Assessment: Learning Discriminative Scores from Relative Ranks
comment: The paper has been accepted by CVPR 2026
summary: Image aesthetic assessment (IAA) has extensive applications in content creation, album management, and recommendation systems, etc. In such applications, it is commonly needed to pick out the most aesthetically pleasing image from a series of images with subtle aesthetic variations, a topic we refer to as fine-grained IAA. Unfortunately, state-of-the-art IAA models are typically designed for coarse-grained evaluation, where images with notable aesthetic differences are evaluated independently on an absolute scale. These models are inherently limited in discriminating fine-grained aesthetic differences. To address the dilemma, we contribute FGAesthetics, a fine-grained IAA database with 32,217 images organized into 10,028 series, which are sourced from diverse categories including Natural, AIGC, and Cropping. Annotations are collected via pairwise comparisons within each series. We also devise Series Refinement and Rank Calibration to ensure the reliability of data and labels. Based on FGAesthetics, we further propose FGAesQ, a novel IAA framework that learns discriminative aesthetic scores from relative ranks through Difference-preserved Tokenization (DiffToken), Comparative Text-assisted Alignment (CTAlign), and Rank-aware Regression (RankReg). FGAesQ enables accurate aesthetic assessment in fine-grained scenarios while still maintains competitive performance in coarse-grained evaluation. Extensive experiments and comparisons demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: Introduce FGAesthetics, a large fine-grained IAA dataset (32,217 images in 10,028 series) with pairwise comparisons, and FGAesQ, a framework that learns from relative ranks using DiffToken, CTAlign, and RankReg to improve fine-grained aesthetic discrimination while remaining strong in coarse-grained tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing IAA models excel at coarse-grained evaluation and struggle with subtle aesthetic differences. A dedicated fine-grained dataset and a rank-informed model are needed to discriminate small aesthetic variations and enable reliable ranking within series.
AI.method: Dataset construction: collect pairwise comparisons within series and apply Series Refinement and Rank Calibration for reliability. Model: DiffToken preserves difference signals between ranks; CTAlign leverages comparative text to align visual and textual cues; RankReg performs rank-aware regression to predict aesthetic scores.
AI.result: Empirical studies show FGAesQ achieves superior performance in fine-grained IAA and maintains competitive results in coarse-grained tasks. Ablation studies confirm contributions from DiffToken, CTAlign, and RankReg.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a new benchmark and a novel, effective framework for fine-grained image aesthetics assessment, enabling accurate ranking within subtle aesthetic variations and robust performance across granularity levels.

title: GIPO: Gaussian Importance Sampling Policy Optimization
summary: Post-training with reinforcement learning (RL) has recently shown strong promise for advancing multimodal agents beyond supervised imitation. However, RL remains limited by poor data efficiency, particularly in settings where interaction data are scarce and quickly become outdated. To address this challenge, GIPO (Gaussian Importance sampling Policy Optimization) is proposed as a policy optimization objective based on truncated importance sampling, replacing hard clipping with a log-ratio-based Gaussian trust weight to softly damp extreme importance ratios while maintaining non-zero gradients. Theoretical analysis shows that GIPO introduces an implicit, tunable constraint on the update magnitude, while concentration bounds guarantee robustness and stability under finite-sample estimation. Experimental results show that GIPO achieves state-of-the-art performance among clipping-based baselines across a wide range of replay buffer sizes, from near on-policy to highly stale data, while exhibiting superior bias--variance trade-off, high training stability and improved sample efficiency.

AI.tldr: GIPO introduces a Gaussian importance sampling objective for policy optimization that soft-clips importance ratios via a log-ratio-based Gaussian trust weight, yielding stable, data-efficient updates across replay buffers and achieving state-of-the-art performance among clipping-based baselines.
AI.motivation: Improve data efficiency in post-training RL for multimodal agents, particularly with scarce or stale interaction data, by avoiding hard clipping of importance weights and ensuring non-zero gradients and finite-sample robustness.
AI.method: Define a policy optimization objective using truncated importance sampling with a Gaussian trust weight based on the log ratio of policies. Replace hard clipping with soft, Gaussian-based damping of extreme importance ratios. Provide theoretical analysis showing an implicit, tunable constraint on update magnitude and concentration bounds for finite-sample robustness. Empirically evaluate across varying replay buffer sizes, from near on-policy to highly stale data.
AI.result: GIPO achieves state-of-the-art performance among clipping-based baselines across a wide range of replay buffer sizes, with improved bias–variance trade-off, training stability, and sample efficiency, particularly in settings with scarce or aged data.
AI.conclusion: GIPO offers a principled, tunable constraint on policy updates via Gaussian-IS weighting, delivering stable, data-efficient post-training RL for multimodal agents and broad robustness to replay-buffer aging.

title: DISC: Dense Integrated Semantic Context for Large-Scale Open-Set Semantic Mapping
summary: Open-set semantic mapping enables language-driven robotic perception, but current instance-centric approaches are bottlenecked by context-depriving and computationally expensive crop-based feature extraction. To overcome this fundamental limitation, we introduce DISC (Dense Integrated Semantic Context), featuring a novel single-pass, distance-weighted extraction mechanism. By deriving high-fidelity CLIP embeddings directly from the vision transformer's intermediate layers, our approach eliminates the latency and domain-shift artifacts of traditional image cropping, yielding pure, mask-aligned semantic representations. To fully leverage these features in large-scale continuous mapping, DISC is built upon a fully GPU-accelerated architecture that replaces periodic offline processing with precise, on-the-fly voxel-level instance refinement. We evaluate our approach on standard benchmarks (Replica, ScanNet) and a newly generated large-scale-mapping dataset based on Habitat-Matterport 3D (HM3DSEM) to assess scalability across complex scenes in multi-story buildings. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that DISC significantly surpasses current state-of-the-art zero-shot methods in both semantic accuracy and query retrieval, providing a robust, real-time capable framework for robotic deployment. The full source code, data generation and evaluation pipelines will be made available at https://github.com/DFKI-NI/DISC.

AI.tldr: Dense Integrated Semantic Context (DISC) introduces a single-pass, dense, voxel-level semantic mapping that derives CLIP-style embeddings from ViT intermediate layers to produce mask-aligned representations, enabling real-time, open-set perception and improved zero-shot performance.
AI.motivation: Overcome the limitations of crop-based, instance-centric methods that lose context, incur high computation, and suffer latency and domain shift in open-set semantic mapping.
AI.method: Propose DISC: a distance-weighted, single-pass extraction that derives high-fidelity CLIP embeddings from vision transformer intermediate layers to yield dense, mask-aligned semantic features; implemented on a fully GPU-accelerated pipeline that performs on-the-fly voxel-level instance refinement instead of offline processing; evaluated on Replica, ScanNet, and HM3DSEM (a new large-scale HM3D-based dataset).
AI.result: Disc significantly surpasses current state-of-the-art zero-shot methods in semantic accuracy and query retrieval; demonstrates real-time capable dense semantic mapping suitable for robotic deployment across complex scenes.
AI.conclusion: DISC enables robust, scalable open-set semantic mapping with dense, mask-aligned representations and real-time performance, and the authors provide source code, data generation, and evaluation pipelines.

title: N-gram Injection into Transformers for Dynamic Language Model Adaptation in Handwritten Text Recognition
summary: Transformer-based encoder-decoder networks have recently achieved impressive results in handwritten text recognition, partly thanks to their auto-regressive decoder which implicitly learns a language model. However, such networks suffer from a large performance drop when evaluated on a target corpus whose language distribution is shifted from the source text seen during training. To retain recognition accuracy despite this language shift, we propose an external n-gram injection (NGI) for dynamic adaptation of the network's language modeling at inference time. Our method allows switching to an n-gram language model estimated on a corpus close to the target distribution, therefore mitigating bias without any extra training on target image-text pairs. We opt for an early injection of the n-gram into the transformer decoder so that the network learns to fully leverage text-only data at the low additional cost of n-gram inference. Experiments on three handwritten datasets demonstrate that the proposed NGI significantly reduces the performance gap between source and target corpora.

AI.tldr: External n-gram injection (NGI) enables inference-time adaptation of language modeling in Transformer-based handwritten text recognition, reducing accuracy loss when language distribution shifts, without target-domain training.
AI.motivation: Handwritten text recognition with auto-regressive decoders relies on an implicit language model. Performance degrades when the target corpus language distribution differs from the training data; a lightweight adaptation mechanism at inference time is needed.
AI.method: Introduce external NGI by injecting an n-gram language model into the transformer decoder at an early stage. The n-gram LM is estimated from a corpus close to the target distribution and used during inference, without any training on target image-text pairs.
AI.result: Experiments on three handwritten datasets show NGI significantly reduces the performance gap between source and target corpora.
AI.conclusion: NGI provides a practical, training-free way to adapt the model’s language modeling to target distributions at inference time, mitigating bias from language shift and improving robustness.

title: TFWaveFormer: Temporal-Frequency Collaborative Multi-level Wavelet Transformer for Dynamic Link Prediction
summary: Dynamic link prediction plays a crucial role in diverse applications including social network analysis, communication forecasting, and financial modeling. While recent Transformer-based approaches have demonstrated promising results in temporal graph learning, their performance remains limited when capturing complex multi-scale temporal dynamics. In this paper, we propose TFWaveFormer, a novel Transformer architecture that integrates temporal-frequency analysis with multi-resolution wavelet decomposition to enhance dynamic link prediction. Our framework comprises three key components: (i) a temporal-frequency coordination mechanism that jointly models temporal and spectral representations, (ii) a learnable multi-resolution wavelet decomposition module that adaptively extracts multi-scale temporal patterns through parallel convolutions, replacing traditional iterative wavelet transforms, and (iii) a hybrid Transformer module that effectively fuses local wavelet features with global temporal dependencies. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that TFWaveFormer achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming existing Transformer-based and hybrid models by significant margins across multiple metrics. The superior performance of TFWaveFormer validates the effectiveness of combining temporal-frequency analysis with wavelet decomposition in capturing complex temporal dynamics for dynamic link prediction tasks.

AI.tldr: Introduces TFWaveFormer, a Transformer architecture that merges temporal-frequency coordination with multi-resolution wavelet decomposition for dynamic link prediction, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Dynamic link prediction requires modeling complex, multi-scale temporal dynamics; existing Transformer-based approaches struggle to capture such patterns comprehensively.
AI.method: Three components: (i) temporal-frequency coordination mechanism for joint temporal and spectral modeling; (ii) learnable multi-resolution wavelet decomposition via parallel convolutions to extract multi-scale temporal patterns; (iii) a hybrid Transformer module that fuses local wavelet features with global temporal dependencies.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on benchmark datasets shows state-of-the-art performance, with significant improvements over existing Transformer-based and hybrid models across multiple metrics.
AI.conclusion: Combining temporal-frequency analysis with wavelet decomposition effectively captures complex temporal dynamics, validating the approach for dynamic link prediction.

title: Long-Term Visual Localization in Dynamic Benthic Environments: A Dataset, Footprint-Based Ground Truth, and Visual Place Recognition Benchmark
summary: Long-term visual localization has the potential to reduce cost and improve mapping quality in optical benthic monitoring with autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs). Despite this potential, long-term visual localization in benthic environments remains understudied, primarily due to the lack of curated datasets for benchmarking. Moreover, limited georeferencing accuracy and image footprints necessitate precise geometric information for accurate ground-truthing. In this work, we address these gaps by presenting a curated dataset for long-term visual localization in benthic environments and a novel method to ground-truth visual localization results for near-nadir underwater imagery. Our dataset comprises georeferenced AUV imagery from five benthic reference sites, revisited over periods up to six years, and includes raw and color-corrected stereo imagery, camera calibrations, and sub-decimeter registered camera poses. To our knowledge, this is the first curated underwater dataset for long-term visual localization spanning multiple sites and photic-zone habitats. Our ground-truthing method estimates 3D seafloor image footprints and links camera views with overlapping footprints, ensuring that ground-truth links reflect shared visual content. Building on this dataset and ground truth, we benchmark eight state-of-the-art visual place recognition (VPR) methods and find that Recall@K is significantly lower on our dataset than on established terrestrial and underwater benchmarks. Finally, we compare our footprint-based ground truth to a traditional location-based ground truth and show that distance-threshold ground-truthing can overestimate VPR Recall@K at sites with rugged terrain and altitude variations. Together, the curated dataset, ground-truthing method, and VPR benchmark provide a stepping stone for advancing long-term visual localization in dynamic benthic environments.

AI.tldr: A curated, long-term underwater visual localization dataset and a footprint-based ground-truthing method for near-nadir imagery, benchmarking eight VPR methods and revealing challenges in rugged benthic environments; highlights limitations of distance-based GT and need for robust VPR in dynamic underwater settings.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of curated, long-term underwater VPR datasets and the need for precise, content-based ground truth to enable reliable benchmarking in dynamic benthic habitats.
AI.method: (1) Curate georeferenced AUV imagery from five benthic sites revisited over up to six years, providing raw/color-corrected stereo imagery, calibrations, and sub-decimeter camera poses. (2) Develop a 3D seafloor footprint-based ground-truthing approach that links camera views via overlapping footprints to reflect shared visual content. (3) Benchmark eight state-of-the-art VPR methods on the dataset.
AI.result: Recall@K is significantly lower on the benthic dataset than on existing terrestrial/underwater benchmarks, indicating higher long-term localization difficulty in dynamic benthic environments. Footprint-based GT differs from traditional location-based GT, with distance-threshold GT potentially overestimating Recall@K at rugged sites. The dataset spans multiple sites and habitats, enabling broad benchmarking.
AI.conclusion: The curated dataset, footprint-based ground truth, and VPR benchmarks constitute a valuable resource for advancing long-term visual localization in dynamic underwater environments and reveal limitations of standard GT schemes and VPR methods in complex benthic scenes.

title: Dual-Solver: A Generalized ODE Solver for Diffusion Models with Dual Prediction
comment: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2026. 36 pages, 18 figures
summary: Diffusion models achieve state-of-the-art image quality. However, sampling is costly at inference time because it requires a large number of function evaluations (NFEs). To reduce NFEs, classical ODE numerical methods have been adopted. Yet, the choice of prediction type and integration domain leads to different sampling behaviors. To address these issues, we introduce Dual-Solver, which generalizes multistep samplers through learnable parameters that continuously (i) interpolate among prediction types, (ii) select the integration domain, and (iii) adjust the residual terms. It retains the standard predictor-corrector structure while preserving second-order local accuracy. These parameters are learned via a classification-based objective using a frozen pretrained classifier (e.g., MobileNet or CLIP). For ImageNet class-conditional generation (DiT, GM-DiT) and text-to-image generation (SANA, PixArt-$α$), Dual-Solver improves FID and CLIP scores in the low-NFE regime ($3 \le$ NFE $\le 9$) across backbones.

AI.tldr: Dual-Solver generalizes multistep diffusion samplers with learnable parameters that interpolate among prediction types, select the integration domain, and adjust residual terms, trained via a frozen classifier objective, achieving better FID and CLIP in low-NFE regimes across ImageNet-conditioned and text-to-image backbones.
AI.motivation: Reduce inference-time sampling cost (low NFEs) of diffusion models while preserving or improving image quality; address sensitivity to prediction type and integration domain in ODE-based samplers.
AI.method: Introduce Dual-Solver within a predictor-corrector framework with learnable parameters that continuously interpolate between prediction types, select the integration domain, and adjust residual terms; preserve second-order local accuracy; train parameters via a classification-based objective using a frozen pretrained classifier (e.g., MobileNet or CLIP).
AI.result: Empirically improves FID and CLIP scores in the low-NFE regime (3 ≤ NFE ≤ 9) across backbones for ImageNet class-conditional generation (DiT, GM-DiT) and text-to-image generation (SANA, PixArt-α).
AI.conclusion: Dual-Solver enables flexible, efficient diffusion sampling with fewer NFEs while maintaining accuracy, showing cross-backbone gains for both conditional and text-to-image generation.

title: Cross-Modal Mapping and Dual-Branch Reconstruction for 2D-3D Multimodal Industrial Anomaly Detection
summary: Multimodal industrial anomaly detection benefits from integrating RGB appearance with 3D surface geometry, yet existing \emph{unsupervised} approaches commonly rely on memory banks, teacher-student architectures, or fragile fusion schemes, limiting robustness under noisy depth, weak texture, or missing modalities. This paper introduces \textbf{CMDR-IAD}, a lightweight and modality-flexible unsupervised framework for reliable anomaly detection in 2D+3D multimodal as well as single-modality (2D-only or 3D-only) settings. \textbf{CMDR-IAD} combines bidirectional 2D$\leftrightarrow$3D cross-modal mapping to model appearance-geometry consistency with dual-branch reconstruction that independently captures normal texture and geometric structure. A two-part fusion strategy integrates these cues: a reliability-gated mapping anomaly highlights spatially consistent texture-geometry discrepancies, while a confidence-weighted reconstruction anomaly adaptively balances appearance and geometric deviations, yielding stable and precise anomaly localization even in depth-sparse or low-texture regions. On the MVTec 3D-AD benchmark, CMDR-IAD achieves state-of-the-art performance while operating without memory banks, reaching 97.3\% image-level AUROC (I-AUROC), 99.6\% pixel-level AUROC (P-AUROC), and 97.6\% AUPRO. On a real-world polyurethane cutting dataset, the 3D-only variant attains 92.6\% I-AUROC and 92.5\% P-AUROC, demonstrating strong effectiveness under practical industrial conditions. These results highlight the framework's robustness, modality flexibility, and the effectiveness of the proposed fusion strategies for industrial visual inspection. Our source code is available at https://github.com/ECGAI-Research/CMDR-IAD/

AI.tldr: CMDR-IAD is a lightweight unsupervised framework for 2D+3D multi- and single-modality anomaly detection, using cross-modal mapping and dual-branch reconstruction with two fusion strategies, achieving state-of-the-art results without memory banks.
AI.motivation: To achieve robust, unsupervised industrial anomaly detection that leverages appearance and geometry while remaining robust to depth noise, weak texture, and missing modalities—addressing limitations of memory-bank, teacher-student, and fragile fusion approaches.
AI.method: Bidirectional 2D↔3D cross-modal mapping to enforce appearance–geometry consistency, plus dual-branch reconstruction that separately models texture and geometry. A two-part fusion strategy combines a reliability-gated mapping anomaly (textural-geometry discrepancies) with a confidence-weighted reconstruction anomaly (adaptive balancing of appearance and geometry deviations). The framework is modality-flexible (supports 2D+3D, 2D-only, 3D-only) and memory-bank-free.
AI.result: On MVTec 3D-AD, achieves state-of-the-art without memory banks: I-AUROC 97.3%, P-AUROC 99.6%, AUPRO 97.6%. On a real-world polyurethane cutting dataset (3D-only), I-AUROC 92.6%, P-AUROC 92.5%. The approach demonstrates robust anomaly localization in depth-sparse or low-texture regions and generalizes to practical industrial scenarios.
AI.conclusion: CMDR-IAD offers robustness, modality flexibility, and effective fusion strategies for industrial visual inspection without memory banks, achieving strong benchmark results and practical applicability; code is publicly available.

title: Specialization of softmax attention heads: insights from the high-dimensional single-location model
summary: Multi-head attention enables transformer models to represent multiple attention patterns simultaneously. Empirically, head specialization emerges in distinct stages during training, while many heads remain redundant and learn similar representations. We propose a theoretical model capturing this phenomenon, based on the multi-index and single-location regression frameworks. In the first part, we analyze the training dynamics of multi-head softmax attention under SGD, revealing an initial unspecialized phase followed by a multi-stage specialization phase in which different heads sequentially align with latent signal directions. In the second part, we study the impact of attention activation functions on performance. We show that softmax-1 significantly reduces noise from irrelevant heads. Finally, we introduce the Bayes-softmax attention, which achieves optimal prediction performance in this setting.

AI.tldr: The abstract presents a theoretical model for multi-head attention dynamics showing staged head specialization under SGD, analyzes activation-function effects on performance, and introduces Bayes-softmax as an optimal attention mechanism.
AI.motivation: Explain why multiple attention heads specialize and why many heads stay redundant, providing a principled framework to understand training dynamics and to improve performance.
AI.method: Proposes a theoretical framework based on multi-index and single-location regression. Analyzes SGD dynamics of multi-head softmax attention, examines the impact of activation functions, and introduces Bayes-softmax attention.
AI.result: Finds an initial unspecialized phase followed by a multi-stage specialization where heads align with latent signal directions. Softmax-1 reduces noise from irrelevant heads. Bayes-softmax achieves optimal prediction performance in this setting.
AI.conclusion: The work offers a mechanistic account of head specialization in multi-head attention and introduces Bayes-softmax as an effective, theoretically grounded attention mechanism.

title: Slice-wise quality assessment of high b-value breast DWI via deep learning-based artifact detection
summary: Diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) can support lesion detection and characterization in breast magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), however especially high b-value diffusion-weighted acquisitions can be prone to intensity artifacts that can affect diagnostic image assessment. This study aims to detect both hyper- and hypointense artifacts on high b-value diffusion-weighted images (b=1500 s/mm2) using deep learning, employing either a binary classification (artifact presence) or a multiclass classification (artifact intensity) approach on a slice-wise dataset.This IRB-approved retrospective study used the single-center dataset comprising n=11806 slices from routine 3T breast MRI examinations performed between 2022 and mid-2023. Three convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures (DenseNet121, ResNet18, and SEResNet50) were trained for binary classification of hyper- and hypointense artifacts. The best performing model (DenseNet121) was applied to an independent holdout test set and was further trained separately for multiclass classification. Evaluation included area under receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC), area under precision recall curve (AUPRC), precision, and recall, as well as analysis of predicted bounding box positions, derived from the network Grad-CAM heatmaps. DenseNet121 achieved AUROCs of 0.92 and 0.94 for hyper- and hypointense artifact detection, respectively, and weighted AUROCs of 0.85 and 0.88 for multiclass classification on single-slice high b-value diffusion-weighted images. A radiologist evaluated bounding box precision on a 1-5 Likert-like scale across 200 slices, achieving mean scores of 3.33+-1.04 for hyperintense artifacts and 2.62+-0.81 for hypointense artifacts. Hyper- and hypointense artifact detection in slice-wise breast DWI MRI dataset (b=1500 s/mm2) using CNNs particularly DenseNet121, seems promising and requires further validation.

AI.tldr: CNN-based detection of hyper- and hypointense artifacts on high-b-value breast DWI (b=1500 s/mm2); DenseNet121 achieved AUROC ~0.92–0.94 for binary artifact detection and 0.85–0.88 for multiclass severity, with radiologist-judged localization.
AI.motivation: High-b-value diffusion-weighted imaging in breast MRI is prone to intensity artifacts that can hinder interpretation; automating artifact detection could improve image quality control and downstream diagnostic confidence.
AI.method: Retrospective single-center study on 11,806 slices from 3T breast MRI exams. Three CNNs (DenseNet121, ResNet18, SEResNet50) trained for binary classification of hyper- and hypointense artifacts. DenseNet121 selected for holdout test, then trained for multiclass artifact severity. Grad-CAM heatmaps used to derive predicted bounding boxes. Radiologist assessed localization on 200 slices (1–5 Likert scale).
AI.result: DenseNet121 achieved AUROCs of 0.92 (hyper) and 0.94 (hypo). Multiclass (weighted AUROC) 0.85 and 0.88. Bounding-box localization scored 3.33±1.04 (hyperintense) and 2.62±0.81 (hypointense) on radiologist evaluation.
AI.conclusion: DenseNet121-based detection of high-b-value DWI artifacts in breast MRI is promising but requires external validation and broader multicenter testing.

title: Spectral Surgery: Training-Free Refinement of LoRA via Gradient-Guided Singular Value Reweighting
summary: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) improves downstream performance by restricting task updates to a low-rank parameter subspace, yet how this limited capacity is allocated within a trained adapter remains unclear. Through a geometric and empirical study across multiple tasks and backbones, we find that trained LoRA updates often exhibit an inefficient spectrum: task effects concentrate in a small subset of singular directions, while many remaining components are neutral or detrimental, motivating post-hoc refinement within the learned subspace. We propose Spectral Surgery, a training-free refinement that decomposes a LoRA update with SVD, estimates per-component sensitivity using gradients on a small calibration set, and reweights singular values under a magnitude constraint while keeping the learned directions fixed. Across Llama-3.1-8B and Qwen3-8B on four benchmarks, Spectral Surgery yields consistent gains (up to +4.4 points on CommonsenseQA and +2.4 pass@1 on HumanEval) by adjusting only $\approx 1{,}000$ scalar coefficients. These results demonstrate that SVD-structured, low-cost parameter editing can serve as a practical route to improving trained LoRA adapters in a purely post-hoc manner.

AI.tldr: Spectral Surgery is a post-hoc, training-free refinement of trained LoRA adapters using SVD to reweight singular directions, yielding consistent gains on large LLM backbones with minimal parameter changes.
AI.motivation: LoRA updates exhibit an inefficient spectrum: task effects concentrate in a few singular directions, while many components are neutral or detrimental, indicating potential gains from subspace refinement after training.
AI.method: Decompose the LoRA update with SVD, estimate per-component sensitivity from gradients on a small calibration set, and reweight singular values under a magnitude constraint while keeping directions fixed (training-free).
AI.result: Applied to Llama-3.1-8B and Qwen3-8B across four benchmarks, achieving gains up to +4.4 points on CommonsenseQA and +2.4 pass@1 on HumanEval by adjusting roughly 1,000 scalar coefficients.
AI.conclusion: SVD-structured, low-cost parameter editing enables practical post-hoc improvements of trained LoRA adapters without additional training.

title: Spatial Causal Prediction in Video
summary: Spatial reasoning, the ability to understand spatial relations, causality, and dynamic evolution, is central to human intelligence and essential for real-world applications such as autonomous driving and robotics. Existing studies, however, primarily assess models on visible spatio-temporal understanding, overlooking their ability to infer unseen past or future spatial states. In this work, we introduce Spatial Causal Prediction (SCP), a new task paradigm that challenges models to reason beyond observation and predict spatial causal outcomes. We further construct SCP-Bench, a benchmark comprising 2,500 QA pairs across 1,181 videos spanning diverse viewpoints, scenes, and causal directions, to support systematic evaluation. Through comprehensive experiments on {23} state-of-the-art models, we reveal substantial gaps between human and model performance, limited temporal extrapolation, and weak causal grounding. We further analyze key factors influencing performance and propose perception-enhancement and reasoning-guided strategies toward advancing spatial causal intelligence. The project page is https://guangstrip.github.io/SCP-Bench.

AI.tldr: SCP introduces spatial causal prediction and SCP-Bench, a benchmark of 2,500 QA pairs over 1,181 videos to benchmark models' ability to infer unseen past/future spatial states; reveals human-model gaps and suggests enhancement strategies.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation emphasizes visible spatio-temporal understanding; there is a need to test models' ability to infer unseen spatial states and underlying causality, which is critical for autonomy in real-world settings. SCP-Bench provides a standardized platform for systematic evaluation.
AI.method: Define the Spatial Causal Prediction task; construct SCP-Bench containing 2,500 QA pairs across 1,181 videos with diverse viewpoints, scenes, and causal directions; conduct comprehensive experiments on 23 state-of-the-art models to assess performance, extrapolation, and causal grounding; analyze factors influencing results and propose enhancement strategies (perception-enhancement and reasoning-guided).
AI.result: There are substantial gaps between human and model performance in spatial causal reasoning, limited temporal extrapolation by models, and weak causal grounding; performance is influenced by various factors identified in the analysis.
AI.conclusion: The work points toward perception-enhancement and reasoning-guided approaches to advance spatial causal intelligence and provides SCP-Bench as a resource to drive progress; a dedicated project page is provided for access and benchmarking.

title: On the Learnability of Offline Model-Based Optimization: A Ranking Perspective
summary: Offline model-based optimization (MBO) seeks to discover high-performing designs using only a fixed dataset of past evaluations. Most existing methods rely on learning a surrogate model via regression and implicitly assume that good predictive accuracy leads to good optimization performance. In this work, we challenge this assumption and study offline MBO from a learnability perspective. We argue that offline optimization is fundamentally a problem of ranking high-quality designs rather than accurate value prediction. Specifically, we introduce an optimization-oriented risk based on ranking between near-optimal and suboptimal designs, and develop a unified theoretical framework that connects surrogate learning to final optimization. We prove the theoretical advantages of ranking over regression, and identify distributional mismatch between the training data and near-optimal designs as the dominant error. Inspired by this, we design a distribution-aware ranking method to reduce this mismatch. Empirical results across various tasks show that our approach outperforms twenty existing methods, validating our theoretical findings. Additionally, both theoretical and empirical results reveal intrinsic limitations in offline MBO, showing a regime in which no offline method can avoid over-optimistic extrapolation.

AI.tldr: Ranking-based offline MBO with distribution-aware ranking outperforms regression-based surrogates, revealing theoretical advantages and fundamental extrapolation limits.
AI.motivation: Challenging the assumption that predictive accuracy in regression suffices for optimization; offline MBO is fundamentally a ranking problem between near-optimal and suboptimal designs.
AI.method: Define an optimization-oriented risk based on ranking performance; build a unified theory linking surrogate learning to final optimization; prove ranking can outperform regression; identify distributional mismatch as the dominant error source; develop a distribution-aware ranking algorithm to mitigate this mismatch.
AI.result: Theoretical advantages of ranking over regression are established; empirical results across tasks show the proposed distribution-aware ranking method outperforms twenty existing methods; the work demonstrates the mismatch between training data and near-optimal designs and the effectiveness of targeted mitigation.
AI.conclusion: A ranking-focused perspective improves offline MBO and exposes intrinsic limits: there exists a regime of extrapolation where no offline method can avoid over-optimistic results; distribution-aware ranking reduces but cannot eliminate this fundamental challenge.

title: Towards Generalized Multimodal Homography Estimation
summary: Supervised and unsupervised homography estimation methods depend on image pairs tailored to specific modalities to achieve high accuracy. However, their performance deteriorates substantially when applied to unseen modalities. To address this issue, we propose a training data synthesis method that generates unaligned image pairs with ground-truth offsets from a single input image. Our approach renders the image pairs with diverse textures and colors while preserving their structural information. These synthetic data empower the trained model to achieve greater robustness and improved generalization across various domains. Additionally, we design a network to fully leverage cross-scale information and decouple color information from feature representations, thus improving estimation accuracy. Extensive experiments show that our training data synthesis method improves generalization performance. The results also confirm the effectiveness of the proposed network.

AI.tldr: Data-synthesis-based training for robust, cross-domain homography estimation with a color-agnostic, cross-scale network.
AI.motivation: Homography estimation models trained on modality-specific data struggle when faced with unseen modalities; improving cross-domain generalization is essential for practical deployment.
AI.method: Generate unaligned image pairs with ground-truth offsets from a single input image, preserving structure while varying textures/colors; introduce a network that exploits cross-scale information and decouples color information from feature representations to improve accuracy.
AI.result: Empirical experiments demonstrate improved generalization across domains and enhanced estimation accuracy due to the proposed data synthesis and network design.
AI.conclusion: The approach enhances both training-data diversity and model robustness, enabling better cross-modality performance for supervised and unsupervised homography estimation.

title: Fixed-Budget Constrained Best Arm Identification in Grouped Bandits
summary: We study fixed budget constrained best-arm identification in grouped bandits, where each arm consists of multiple independent attributes with stochastic rewards. An arm is considered feasible only if all its attributes' means are above a given threshold. The aim is to find the feasible arm with the largest overall mean. We first derive a lower bound on the error probability for any algorithm on this setting. We then propose Feasibility Constrained Successive Rejects (FCSR), a novel algorithm that identifies the best arm while ensuring feasibility. We show it attains optimal dependence on problem parameters up to constant factors in the exponent. Empirically, FCSR outperforms natural baselines while preserving feasibility guarantees.

AI.tldr: Derives a lower bound for fixed-budget, feasibility-constrained best-arm identification in grouped bandits and presents Feasibility Constrained Successive Rejects (FCSR), an algorithm achieving near-optimal exponent and preserving feasibility; shows favorable empirical performance.
AI.motivation: In settings where each arm is a vector of attributes and an arm is feasible only if all attribute means exceed a threshold, identify the best feasible arm under a fixed sample budget. This extends constrained best-arm identification to multi-attribute, grouped-bandit scenarios.
AI.method: First establish a lower bound on the error probability for any algorithm under the fixed-budget, feasibility-constrained setting. Then design Feasibility Constrained Successive Rejects (FCSR), a sequential elimination algorithm that enforces feasibility at each step and analyzes its error probability and sample complexity, proving optimality of the exponent up to constant factors.
AI.result: A problem-parameter dependent lower bound on error probability is derived. FCSR achieves the optimal dependence on problem parameters (up to constant factors in the exponent) and maintains feasibility throughout. Empirical experiments show FCSR outperforms natural baselines while preserving feasibility guarantees.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a tight fundamental limit and a practical, feasible-algorithm for constrained best-arm identification in grouped bandits, with strong theoretical guarantees and empirical validation.

title: ProFound: A moderate-sized vision foundation model for multi-task prostate imaging
summary: Many diagnostic and therapeutic clinical tasks for prostate cancer increasingly rely on multi-parametric MRI. Automating these tasks is challenging because they necessitate expert interpretations, which are difficult to scale to capitalise on modern deep learning. Although modern automated systems achieve expert-level performance in isolated tasks, their general clinical utility remains limited by the requirement of large task-specific labelled datasets. In this paper, we present ProFound, a domain-specialised vision foundation model for volumetric prostate mpMRI. ProFound is pre-trained using several variants of self-supervised approaches on a diverse, multi-institutional collection of 5,000 patients, with a total of over 22,000 unique 3D MRI volumes (over 1,800,000 2D image slices). We conducted a systematic evaluation of ProFound across a broad spectrum of $11$ downstream clinical tasks on over 3,000 independent patients, including prostate cancer detection, Gleason grading, lesion localisation, gland volume estimation, zonal and surrounding structure segmentation. Experimental results demonstrate that finetuned ProFound consistently outperforms or remains competitive with state-of-the-art specialised models and existing medical vision foundation models trained/finetuned on the same data.

AI.tldr: ProFound is a domain-specific vision foundation model for volumetric prostate mpMRI, trained with self-supervised learning on a large multi-institution dataset, achieving strong performance across 11 downstream prostate MRI tasks, and outperforming or matching task-specific and existing medical vision models when fine-tuned on the same data.
AI.motivation: To overcome the need for large task-specific labelled datasets in clinical mpMRI tasks by leveraging domain specialization and self-supervised pretraining to enable broad, multi-task utility.
AI.method: Pretrain ProFound with several self-supervised objectives on a diverse dataset of 5,000 patients (over 22,000 3D mpMRI volumes, >1.8 million 2D slices) from multiple institutions. Evaluate via fine-tuning across 11 downstream tasks (cancer detection, Gleason grading, lesion localization, gland volume estimation, zonal/surrounding structure segmentation) on 3,000 independent patients, comparing to state-of-the-art task-specific models and existing medical vision foundation models trained/finetuned on the same data.
AI.result: Fine-tuned ProFound consistently outperforms or remains competitive with state-of-the-art specialized models and existing medical vision foundation models trained/finetuned on the same data across the 11 tasks and 3,000-patient evaluation set.
AI.conclusion: Domain-specialised vision foundation models trained with self-supervised pretraining on large, diverse prostate mpMRI datasets can provide broad clinical utility across multiple tasks, reducing the need for large task-specific labelled data while achieving competitive or superior performance relative to task-specific or generic medical foundation models.

title: A Multi-Dimensional Quality Scoring Framework for Decentralized LLM Inference with Proof of Quality
summary: Decentralized large language model (LLM) inference networks can pool heterogeneous compute to scale serving, but they require lightweight and incentive-compatible mechanisms to assess output quality. Prior work introduced cost-aware Proof of Quality (PoQ) and adaptive robust PoQ to allocate rewards under evaluator heterogeneity and adversarial behavior. In this paper, we focus on the quality signal itself and propose a multi-dimensional quality scoring framework that decomposes output quality into modular dimensions, including model and cost priors, structure quality, semantic quality, query-output alignment, and agreement/uncertainty. Using logged outputs from QA and summarization tasks, we systematically audit dimension reliability and show that seemingly reasonable dimensions can be task-dependent and even negatively correlated with reference quality without calibration. While the default composite underperforms a strong single semantic evaluator, ablations reveal that removing unreliable dimensions and re-normalizing weights yields a calibrated composite that matches or exceeds the best single- evaluator and consensus baselines. Finally, we integrate the composite score as a drop-in quality signal in PoQ and demonstrate complementary benefits with robust aggregation and adaptive trust weighting under adversarial evaluator attacks.

AI.tldr: A multi-dimensional quality scoring framework for LLM outputs is proposed, auditing dimension reliability across QA and summarization tasks; after removing unreliable dimensions and re-normalizing, the calibrated composite matches or beats single evaluators and consensus baselines. The score is integrated into PoQ for robust, incentive-compatible quality signaling.
AI.motivation: Provide a reliable, calibrated quality signal for decentralized LLM inference by decomposing quality into modular dimensions and ensuring dimension reliability across tasks, reducing susceptibility to task-specific biases and adversarial manipulation.
AI.method: Decompose output quality into dimensions (model/cost priors, structure quality, semantic quality, query-output alignment, agreement/uncertainty). Audit dimension reliability using logged QA and summarization outputs; perform ablations to remove unreliable dimensions and re-normalize weights; evaluate composite vs single evaluators and consensus baselines; integrate calibrated composite into PoQ with robust aggregation and adaptive trust weighting under adversarial conditions.
AI.result: The default composite can underperform strongest semantic evaluator; pruning unreliable dimensions and re-normalizing yields a calibrated composite that matches or surpasses the best single evaluator and consensus baselines.
AI.conclusion: A calibrated multi-dimensional quality score can serve as an effective signal in PoQ, but dimension reliability is task-dependent and careful dimension selection and normalization are essential for achieving or exceeding strong baselines; complements robust aggregation and adaptive trust under adversarial evaluator threats.

title: IROSA: Interactive Robot Skill Adaptation using Natural Language
comment: Accepted IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters (RA-L) journal, 8 pages, 5 figures, 3 tables, 1 listing
summary: Foundation models have demonstrated impressive capabilities across diverse domains, while imitation learning provides principled methods for robot skill adaptation from limited data. Combining these approaches holds significant promise for direct application to robotics, yet this combination has received limited attention, particularly for industrial deployment. We present a novel framework that enables open-vocabulary skill adaptation through a tool-based architecture, maintaining a protective abstraction layer between the language model and robot hardware. Our approach leverages pre-trained LLMs to select and parameterize specific tools for adapting robot skills without requiring fine-tuning or direct model-to-robot interaction. We demonstrate the framework on a 7-DoF torque-controlled robot performing an industrial bearing ring insertion task, showing successful skill adaptation through natural language commands for speed adjustment, trajectory correction, and obstacle avoidance while maintaining safety, transparency, and interpretability.

AI.tldr: Open-vocabulary, tool-based framework enabling LLM-driven adaptation of robot skills without fine-tuning, demonstrated on a 7-DoF torque-controlled manipulator for bearing ring insertion.
AI.motivation: Bridge foundation models and imitation learning to support flexible, language-driven robot skill adaptation in industrial settings, while preserving safety, transparency, and interpretability.
AI.method: Protective abstraction layer between language model and hardware; LLM selects and parameterizes tools to adapt robot skills, avoiding direct model-to-robot interaction or fine-tuning; demonstrates on a 7-DoF torque-controlled robot with natural-language commands for speed, trajectory, and obstacle avoidance.
AI.result: Successful skill adaptation via natural-language commands, enabling speed adjustment, trajectory correction, and obstacle avoidance; maintains safety, transparency, and interpretability.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables open-vocabulary skill adaptation for robotics through a tool-based architecture, supporting safe, interpretable, and potentially deployable industrial applications; future work may expand tool sets and tasks.

title: BLOCK: An Open-Source Bi-Stage MLLM Character-to-Skin Pipeline for Minecraft
summary: We present \textbf{BLOCK}, an open-source bi-stage character-to-skin pipeline that generates pixel-perfect Minecraft skins from arbitrary character concepts. BLOCK decomposes the problem into (i) a \textbf{3D preview synthesis stage} driven by a large multimodal model (MLLM) with a carefully designed prompt-and-reference template, producing a consistent dual-panel (front/back) oblique-view Minecraft-style preview; and (ii) a \textbf{skin decoding stage} based on a fine-tuned FLUX.2 model that translates the preview into a skin atlas image. We further propose \textbf{EvolveLoRA}, a progressive LoRA curriculum (text-to-image $\rightarrow$ image-to-image $\rightarrow$ preview-to-skin) that initializes each phase from the previous adapter to improve stability and efficiency. BLOCK is released with all prompt templates and fine-tuned weights to support reproducible character-to-skin generation.

AI.tldr: BLOCK introduces a bi-stage, open-source pipeline that converts arbitrary character concepts into Minecraft skins via a 3D preview stage with an MLLM and a separate skin-decoding stage using FLUX.2, plus EvolveLoRA—a progressive LoRA curriculum—to initialize each stage from the previous adapter, enabling reproducible character-to-skin generation.
AI.motivation: Automate and standardize creation of pixel-perfect Minecraft skins from text/concept inputs, improving consistency across front/back views while enabling end-to-end reproducibility with open-source templates and weights.
AI.method: Two-stage pipeline: (i) 3D preview synthesis using a large multimodal model with a purpose-built prompt/reference template to produce a dual-panel oblique front/back Minecraft-style preview; (ii) skin decoding via a fine-tuned FLUX.2 model translating the preview into a skin atlas. EvolveLoRA progressively trains adapters in three stages (text-to-image, image-to-image, preview-to-skin), initializing each phase from the previous adapter. The release includes prompt templates and fine-tuned weights.
AI.result: Demonstrates a functional end-to-end pipeline producing skin atlases from concepts; provides reproducible prompts and weights to enable replication of results. The abstract does not report quantitative metrics, but implies pixel-accurate, consistent previews and skin translations.
AI.conclusion: BLOCK offers an open-source, modular solution for character-to-skin generation with a stability/efficiency-centric LoRA curriculum (EvolveLoRA) and full reproducibility support through released artifacts.

title: mlx-vis: GPU-Accelerated Dimensionality Reduction and Visualization on Apple Silicon
summary: mlx-vis is a Python library that implements six dimensionality reduction methods and a k-nearest neighbor graph algorithm entirely in MLX, Apple's array framework for Apple Silicon. The library provides UMAP, t-SNE, PaCMAP, TriMap, DREAMS, CNE, and NNDescent, all executing on Metal GPU through a unified fit_transform interface. Beyond embedding computation, mlx-vis includes a GPU-accelerated circle-splatting renderer that produces scatter plots and smooth animations without matplotlib, composing frames via scatter-add alpha blending on GPU and piping them to hardware H.264 encoding. On Fashion-MNIST with 70,000 points, all methods complete embedding in 2.1-3.8 seconds and render 800-frame animations in 1.4 seconds on an M3 Ultra, with the full pipeline from raw data to rendered video finishing in 3.6-5.2 seconds. The library depends only on MLX and NumPy, is released under the Apache 2.0 license, and is available at https://github.com/hanxiao/mlx-vis.

AI.tldr: mlx-vis delivers end-to-end GPU-accelerated dimensionality reduction and visualization on Apple Silicon, unifying six DR methods and a kNN graph with a Metal-based renderer and H.264 encoding, achieving very fast end-to-end performance.
AI.motivation: To enable fast, end-to-end dimensionality reduction and visualization on Apple Silicon by leveraging MLX/Metal GPU acceleration, reducing CPU overhead and providing a single unified API.
AI.method: Implement six dimensionality reduction methods (UMAP, t-SNE, PaCMAP, TriMap, DREAMS, CNE) and NNDescent on MLX, executing on Metal GPU with a unified fit_transform interface. Include a GPU-accelerated circle-splatting renderer for scatter plots/animations, composed via GPU alpha blending and piped to hardware H.264 encoding. End-to-end pipeline minimizes dependencies to MLX and NumPy.
AI.result: On Fashion-MNIST (70,000 points), all DR methods complete embeddings in 2.1–3.8 s; render 800-frame animations in 1.4 s on an M3 Ultra; end-to-end (raw data to rendered video) 3.6–5.2 s.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates a practical, GPU-accelerated, end-to-end DR and visualization stack on Apple Silicon with a minimal dependency footprint, enabling rapid exploratory data analysis and publication-ready visuals.

title: UniRain: Unified Image Deraining with RAG-based Dataset Distillation and Multi-objective Reweighted Optimization
comment: Accepted by CVPR 2026; Project Page: https://github.com/QianfengY/UniRain
summary: Despite significant progress has been made in image deraining, we note that most existing methods are often developed for only specific types of rain degradation and fail to generalize across diverse real-world rainy scenes. How to effectively model different rain degradations within a universal framework is important for real-world image deraining. In this paper, we propose UniRain, an effective unified image deraining framework capable of restoring images degraded by rain streak and raindrop under both daytime and nighttime conditions. To better enhance unified model generalization, we construct an intelligent retrieval augmented generation (RAG)-based dataset distillation pipeline that selects high-quality training samples from all public deraining datasets for better mixed training. Furthermore, we incorporate a simple yet effective multi-objective reweighted optimization strategy into the asymmetric mixture-of-experts (MoE) architecture to facilitate consistent performance and improve robustness across diverse scenes. Extensive experiments show that our framework performs favorably against the state-of-the-art models on our proposed benchmarks and multiple public datasets.

AI.tldr: UniRain is a unified deraining framework for rain streaks and raindrops under day/night conditions, using retrieval-augmented dataset distillation (RAG) and a multi-objective reweighted asymmetric mixture-of-experts (MoE) to enhance generalization and robustness, with strong results on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing deraining methods are often specialized to specific rain degradations and struggle to generalize to diverse real-world scenes. A universal framework capable of handling multiple degradation types and illumination conditions is needed.
AI.method: 1) UniRain architecture for universal rain removal. 2) RAG-based dataset distillation to select high-quality samples from public deraining datasets to create effective mixed training data. 3) Multi-objective reweighted optimization within an asymmetric MoE to improve robustness across diverse scenes.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show UniRain achieves favorable performance against state-of-the-art methods on the proposed benchmarks and multiple public datasets.
AI.conclusion: A unified deraining framework equipped with intelligent data distillation and MoE-based optimization can generalize across rain streaks and raindrops under day and night and improve robustness in diverse scenes.

title: Scaling Dense Event-Stream Pretraining from Visual Foundation Models
summary: Learning versatile, fine-grained representations from irregular event streams is pivotal yet nontrivial, primarily due to the heavy annotation that hinders scalability in dataset size, semantic richness, and application scope. To mitigate this dilemma, we launch a novel self-supervised pretraining method that distills visual foundation models (VFMs) to push the boundaries of event representation at scale. Specifically, we curate an extensive synchronized image-event collection to amplify cross-modal alignment. Nevertheless, due to inherent mismatches in sparsity and granularity between image-event domains, existing distillation paradigms are prone to semantic collapse in event representations, particularly at high resolutions. To bridge this gap, we propose to extend the alignment objective to semantic structures provided off-the-shelf by VFMs, indicating a broader receptive field and stronger supervision. The key ingredient of our method is a structure-aware distillation loss that grounds higher-quality image-event correspondences for alignment, optimizing dense event representations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach takes a great leap in downstream benchmarks, significantly surpassing traditional methods and existing pretraining techniques. This breakthrough manifests in enhanced generalization, superior data efficiency and elevated transferability.

AI.tldr: A self-supervised pretraining framework distills visual foundation models (VFMs) for irregular event streams by aligning events with images using a structure-aware distillation loss, addressing semantic mismatch and sparsity to produce dense, high-quality event representations with strong generalization and transferability.
AI.motivation: To overcome annotation bottlenecks in learning high-quality, fine-grained representations for event streams and to leverage large-scale VFMs through cross-modal self-supervised pretraining.
AI.method: Extend cross-modal alignment to semantic structures from VFMs by introducing a structure-aware distillation loss that grounds higher-quality image–event correspondences. Build a large synchronized image–event dataset and distill VFMs into dense event representations, mitigating semantic collapse at high resolutions.
AI.result: Experiments show significant improvements over traditional and prior pretraining methods on downstream tasks, with enhanced generalization, data efficiency, and transferability.
AI.conclusion: Structure-aware distillation of VFMs enables scalable, dense, semantically rich event representation learning from irregular streams, advancing cross-modal pretraining and downstream performance.

title: FedCova: Robust Federated Covariance Learning Against Noisy Labels
summary: Noisy labels in distributed datasets induce severe local overfitting and consequently compromise the global model in federated learning (FL). Most existing solutions rely on selecting clean devices or aligning with public clean datasets, rather than endowing the model itself with robustness. In this paper, we propose FedCova, a dependency-free federated covariance learning framework that eliminates such external reliances by enhancing the model's intrinsic robustness via a new perspective on feature covariances. Specifically, FedCova encodes data into a discriminative but resilient feature space to tolerate label noise. Built on mutual information maximization, we design a novel objective for federated lossy feature encoding that relies solely on class feature covariances with an error tolerance term. Leveraging feature subspaces characterized by covariances, we construct a subspace-augmented federated classifier. FedCova unifies three key processes through the covariance: (1) training the network for feature encoding, (2) constructing a classifier directly from the learned features, and (3) correcting noisy labels based on feature subspaces. We implement FedCova across both symmetric and asymmetric noisy settings under heterogeneous data distribution. Experimental results on CIFAR-10/100 and real-world noisy dataset Clothing1M demonstrate the superior robustness of FedCova compared with the state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: FedCova is a covariance-based, federated approach that enhances robustness to noisy labels by learning discriminative feature covariances and building a subspace-augmented classifier, avoiding reliance on external clean data.
AI.motivation: Noisy labels in federated learning cause local overfitting and degrade global performance. Existing solutions rely on clean-device selection or public clean data, which limits practicality. A method that strengthens the model’s intrinsic robustness based on feature covariances is desirable.
AI.method: Foundation on mutual information maximization, FedCova introduces a loss for federated lossy feature encoding that depends only on class feature covariances with an error tolerance term. It constructs subspace-augmented federated classifiers from covariance-derived feature subspaces, integrating three processes: feature encoding, classifier construction, and noisy-label correction. The framework supports symmetric/asymmetric noise under heterogeneous data distributions.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on CIFAR-10/100 and Clothing1M shows FedCova achieving superior robustness to label noise compared with state-of-the-art methods across symmetric and asymmetric noise settings and data heterogeneity.
AI.conclusion: FedCova provides a unified, intrinsic robustness mechanism for FL by leveraging covariance-informed feature encoding and subspace-based classification, enabling improved performance under noisy labels without external clean data sources.

title: GeoSeg: Training-Free Reasoning-Driven Segmentation in Remote Sensing Imagery
summary: Recent advances in MLLMs are reframing segmentation from fixed-category prediction to instruction-grounded localization. While reasoning based segmentation has progressed rapidly in natural scenes, remote sensing lacks a generalizable solution due to the prohibitive cost of reasoning-oriented data and domain-specific challenges like overhead viewpoints. We present GeoSeg, a zero-shot, training-free framework that bypasses the supervision bottleneck for reasoning-driven remote sensing segmentation. GeoSeg couples MLLM reasoning with precise localization via: (i) bias-aware coordinate refinement to correct systematic grounding shifts and (ii) a dual-route prompting mechanism to fuse semantic intent with fine-grained spatial cues. We also introduce GeoSeg-Bench, a diagnostic benchmark of 810 image--query pairs with hierarchical difficulty levels. Experiments show that GeoSeg consistently outperforms all baselines, with extensive ablations confirming the effectiveness and necessity of each component.

AI.tldr: GeoSeg is a zero-shot, training-free framework for reasoning-driven remote sensing segmentation that integrates MLLM-based reasoning with precise localization via bias-aware coordinate refinement and dual-route prompting; it is paired with GeoSeg-Bench, a diagnostic benchmark of 810 image–query pairs with hierarchical difficulty; experiments show state-of-the-art performance with ablations validating each component.
AI.motivation: Remote sensing lacks a generalizable, reasoning-driven segmentation approach due to data-collection costs, domain-specific geometry (e.g., overhead views), and annotation bottlenecks. Leveraging vision-language models offers zero-shot generalization but requires addressing grounding shifts and integrating semantic intent with spatial localization.
AI.method: GeoSeg comprises: (i) bias-aware coordinate refinement to correct systematic grounding shifts between language-guided queries and image coordinates; (ii) a dual-route prompting mechanism that fuses semantic query intent with fine-grained spatial cues to guide localization; (iii) training-free, zero-shot inference using large multimodal language models for reasoning and localization; (iv) GeoSeg-Bench, a diagnostic benchmark of 810 image–query pairs with hierarchical difficulty to assess reasoning-driven localization in remote sensing.
AI.result: GeoSeg consistently outperforms all baselines on GeoSeg-Bench and other tasks; extensive ablations demonstrate that both the bias refinement and dual-route prompting are necessary components for performance gains.
AI.conclusion: GeoSeg provides a practical, supervision-free route to reasoning-driven segmentation in remote sensing by aligning MLLM reasoning with precise localization, and GeoSeg-Bench enables structured evaluation of such capabilities across hierarchical difficulties.

title: Tuning Just Enough: Lightweight Backdoor Attacks on Multi-Encoder Diffusion Models
summary: As text-to-image diffusion models become increasingly deployed in real-world applications, concerns about backdoor attacks have gained significant attention. Prior work on text-based backdoor attacks has largely focused on diffusion models conditioned on a single lightweight text encoder. However, more recent diffusion models that incorporate multiple large-scale text encoders remain underexplored in this context. Given the substantially increased number of trainable parameters introduced by multiple text encoders, an important question is whether backdoor attacks can remain both efficient and effective in such settings. In this work, we study Stable Diffusion 3, which uses three distinct text encoders and has not yet been systematically analyzed for text-encoder-based backdoor vulnerabilities. To understand the role of text encoders in backdoor attacks, we define four categories of attack targets and identify the minimal sets of encoders required to achieve effective performance for each attack objective. Based on this, we further propose Multi-Encoder Lightweight aTtacks (MELT), which trains only low-rank adapters while keeping the pretrained text encoder weight frozen. We demonstrate that tuning fewer than 0.2% of the total encoder parameters is sufficient for successful backdoor attacks on Stable Diffusion 3, revealing previously underexplored vulnerabilities in practical attack scenarios in multi-encoder settings.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates backdoor vulnerabilities in multi-encoder text-to-image diffusion models, showing backdoors can be injected efficiently by training low-rank adapters while freezing most encoders in Stable Diffusion 3, enabling effective attacks with under 0.2% of encoder parameters.
AI.motivation: With multiple large text encoders, diffusion models present new backdoor surfaces. Prior work focused on single-encoder setups; it remains unclear how multi-encoder architectures affect attack feasibility and efficiency.
AI.method: The study defines four attack-target categories, identifies the minimal subset of encoders required for each target, and introduces MELT (Multi-Encoder Lightweight aTtacks) which trains only low-rank adapters while keeping pretrained encoders frozen. Evaluations are conducted on Stable Diffusion 3.
AI.result: Backdoors remain effective in multi-encoder setups with very limited parameter tuning: tuning fewer than 0.2% of total encoder parameters suffices to achieve successful backdoor performance across the defined targets.
AI.conclusion: Multi-encoder diffusion models present elevated backdoor risk. Attackers can compromise outputs with minimal fine-tuning by inserting adapters, highlighting the need for defenses that monitor and mitigate backdoor signals across all encoders in multi-encoder architectures.

title: RVN-Bench: A Benchmark for Reactive Visual Navigation
summary: Safe visual navigation is critical for indoor mobile robots operating in cluttered environments. Existing benchmarks, however, often neglect collisions or are designed for outdoor scenarios, making them unsuitable for indoor visual navigation. To address this limitation, we introduce the reactive visual navigation benchmark (RVN-Bench), a collision-aware benchmark for indoor mobile robots. In RVN-Bench, an agent must reach sequential goal positions in previously unseen environments using only visual observations and no prior map, while avoiding collisions. Built on the Habitat 2.0 simulator and leveraging high-fidelity HM3D scenes, RVN-Bench provides large-scale, diverse indoor environments, defines a collision-aware navigation task and evaluation metrics, and offers tools for standardized training and benchmarking. RVN-Bench supports both online and offline learning by offering an environment for online reinforcement learning, a trajectory image dataset generator, and tools for producing negative trajectory image datasets that capture collision events. Experiments show that policies trained on RVN-Bench generalize effectively to unseen environments, demonstrating its value as a standardized benchmark for safe and robust visual navigation. Code and additional materials are available at: https://rvn-bench.github.io/.

AI.tldr: A collision-aware indoor visual navigation benchmark (RVN-Bench) built on Habitat 2.0 with HM3D scenes, enabling map-less, vision-only navigation with sequential goals and collision avoidance; supports online/offline learning, trajectory/negative dataset generation, and standardized benchmarking; demonstrates good generalization to unseen environments.
AI.motivation: Indoor mobile robots require safe navigation in cluttered environments, but existing benchmarks either ignore collisions or target outdoor settings. A standardized, scalable, collision-aware indoor benchmark is needed to evaluate and train robust, safe visual navigation policies.
AI.method: Introduce RVN-Bench on Habitat 2.0 using high-fidelity HM3D scenes. Define a collision-aware navigation task where an agent must reach sequential goals using only visual observations without a prior map. Provide large-scale, diverse environments, evaluation metrics, and tools for standardized training/benchmarking. Support online RL, a trajectory image dataset generator, and tools to produce negative trajectory datasets capturing collision events.
AI.result: Experiments show policies trained on RVN-Bench generalize effectively to unseen environments, validating RVN-Bench as a useful standardized benchmark for safe and robust visual navigation.
AI.conclusion: RVN-Bench offers a standardized, scalable framework for collision-aware indoor visual navigation, enabling online and offline learning and facilitating fair comparisons across methods. Code and materials are publicly available.

title: RIVER: A Real-Time Interaction Benchmark for Video LLMs
summary: The rapid advancement of multimodal large language models has demonstrated impressive capabilities, yet nearly all operate in an offline paradigm, hindering real-time interactivity. Addressing this gap, we introduce the Real-tIme Video intERaction Bench (RIVER Bench), designed for evaluating online video comprehension.
