A Transformer-based sequential policy is then trained on this enriched dataset, complemented by a value-guided objective that promotes the distillation of the optimal policy. By replacing the conventional, manually-tuned return-to-go with the learned quasi-optimal value function, IPD improves both decision-making stability and performance during inference. Empirical evaluations on the D4RL benchmark demonstrate that IPD significantly outperforms several state-of-the-art value-based and transformer-based offline RL methods across diverse tasks.

AI.tldr: IPD introduces offline planning into data generation, training, and inference for offline RL using a world model, MPC-imagined trajectories, and a value-guided distillation to replace return-to-go, yielding strong results on D4RL.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of decision transformer-based offline RL, namely difficulty leveraging suboptimal data and lack of explicit planning, by integrating offline planning and a quasi-optimal value into the learning loop.
AI.method: Learn a world model with uncertainty and a quasi-optimal value function from offline data. Identify suboptimal trajectories, augment them with imagined optimal rollouts via Model Predictive Control (MPC). Train a Transformer-based sequential policy on the augmented data with a value-guided objective that distills the optimal policy; replace return-to-go with the learned value. Evaluate on D4RL.
AI.result: IPD significantly outperforms several state-of-the-art value-based and transformer-based offline RL methods across diverse tasks on the D4RL benchmark.
AI.conclusion: IPD bridges offline planning with data generation and supervised training, improving decision-making stability and performance, and demonstrating the benefit of planning-aware data augmentation and value-guided distillation in offline RL.

title: Any2Any: Unified Arbitrary Modality Translation for Remote Sensing
summary: Multi-modal remote sensing imagery provides complementary observations of the same geographic scene, yet such observations are frequently incomplete in practice. Existing cross-modal translation methods treat each modality pair as an independent task, resulting in quadratic complexity and limited generalization to unseen modality combinations. We formulate Any-to-Any translation as inference over a shared latent representation of the scene, where different modalities correspond to partial observations of the same underlying semantics. Based on this formulation, we propose Any2Any, a unified latent diffusion framework that projects heterogeneous inputs into a geometrically aligned latent space. Such structure performs anchored latent regression with a shared backbone, decoupling modality-specific representation learning from semantic mapping. Moreover, lightweight target-specific residual adapters are used to correct systematic latent mismatches without increasing inference complexity. To support learning under sparse but connected supervision, we introduce RST-1M, the first million-scale remote sensing dataset with paired observations across five sensing modalities, providing supervision anchors for any-to-any translation. Experiments across 14 translation tasks show that Any2Any consistently outperforms pairwise translation methods and exhibits strong zero-shot generalization to unseen modality pairs. Code and models will be available at https://github.com/MiliLab/Any2Any.

AI.tldr: Any2Any is a unified latent diffusion framework for any-to-any translation across remote-sensing modalities via a shared latent space, with lightweight adapters and a large multi-modal dataset, enabling strong zero-shot generalization.
AI.motivation: Cross-modal remote sensing data are often incomplete and pairwise translation methods scale poorly (quadratic in modality count). A unified approach that leverages shared semantics across modalities can improve translation quality and generalization to unseen modality pairs.
AI.method: Any2Any projects heterogeneous inputs into a geometrically aligned latent space using a shared backbone with anchored latent regression. Modality-specific residual adapters correct latent mismatches without increasing inference cost. Training leverages RST-1M, a large-scale dataset with five modalities and sparse but connected supervision, to support any-to-any translation.
AI.result: Across 14 translation tasks, Any2Any outperforms pairwise translation methods and demonstrates strong zero-shot generalization to unseen modality pairs.
AI.conclusion: A decoupled, unified latent-diffusion framework with lightweight adapters and a large-scale dataset enables scalable, generalizable any-to-any translation in multi-modal remote sensing, outperforming traditional pairwise approaches.

title: LUMINA: Foundation Models for Topology Transferable ACOPF
summary: Foundation models in general promise to accelerate scientific computation by learning reusable representations across problem instances, yet constrained scientific systems, where predictions must satisfy physical laws and safety limits, pose unique challenges that stress conventional training paradigms. We derive design principles for constrained scientific foundation models through systematic investigation of AC optimal power flow (ACOPF), a representative optimization problem in power grid operations where power balance equations and operational constraints are non-negotiable. Through controlled experiments spanning architectures, training objectives, and system diversity, we extract three empirically grounded principles governing scientific foundation model design. These principles characterize three design trade-offs: learning physics-invariant representations while respecting system-specific constraints, optimizing accuracy while ensuring constraint satisfaction, and ensuring reliability in high-impact operating regimes. We present the LUMINA framework, including data processing and training pipelines to support reproducible research on physics-informed, feasibility-aware foundation models across scientific applications.

AI.tldr: Three principles for constrained scientific foundation models derived from ACOPF: learn physics-invariant representations, enforce hard operational constraints, and ensure reliability in critical regimes; instantiated via the LUMINA framework for reproducible physics-informed learning.
AI.motivation: Scientific systems impose non-negotiable physical laws and safety limits; ACOPF provides a representative testbed with balance equations and constraints to study constraint-aware foundation models.
AI.method: Systematic, controlled experiments across architectures, training objectives, and system diversity; empirical extraction of design principles; development of data processing and training pipelines; introduction of the LUMINA framework.
AI.result: Identified three empirically grounded design principles that articulate the trade-offs among physics-invariant representation, constraint satisfaction, and reliability; presented LUMINA as a reproducible data/training platform for physics-informed, feasibility-aware foundation models.
AI.conclusion: Constrained scientific foundation models must balance physical fidelity, feasibility, and reliability; the LUMINA framework enables reproducible research and broader applicability across scientific domains.

title: TextBoost: Boosting Scene Text Fidelity in Ultra-low Bitrate Image Compression
summary: Ultra-low bitrate image compression faces a critical challenge: preserving small-font scene text while maintaining overall visual quality. Region-of-interest (ROI) bit allocation can prioritize text but often degrades global fidelity, leading to a trade-off between local accuracy and overall image quality. Instead of relying on ROI coding, we incorporate auxiliary textual information extracted by OCR and transmitted with negligible overhead, enabling the decoder to leverage this semantic guidance. Our method, TextBoost, operationalizes this idea through three strategic designs: (i) adaptively filtering OCR outputs and rendering them into a guidance map; (ii) integrating this guidance with decoder features in a calibrated manner via an attention-guided fusion block; and (iii) enforcing guidance-consistent reconstruction in text regions with a regularizing loss that promotes natural blending with the scene. Extensive experiments on TextOCR and ICDAR 2015 demonstrate that TextBoost yields up to 60.6% higher text-recognition F1 at comparable Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR) and bits per pixel (bpp), producing sharper small-font text while preserving global image quality and effectively decoupling text enhancement from global rate-distortion optimization.

AI.tldr: TextBoost uses OCR-derived textual guidance to improve ultra-low bitrate image compression, achieving better small-font text fidelity without sacrificing global quality, by adaptive OCR filtering, attention-based fusion, and guidance-consistent regularization.
AI.motivation: ROI coding improves local text fidelity at the expense of global image quality. The authors propose to transmit OCR-derived text with negligible overhead and use it to semantically guide decoding, decoupling text enhancement from rate-distortion optimization.
AI.method: Three design components: (i) adaptive filtering of OCR outputs rendered as a guidance map; (ii) attention-guided fusion to integrate guidance into decoder features; (iii) a regularization loss enforcing guidance-consistent reconstruction in text regions to blend text naturally with the scene.
AI.result: On TextOCR and ICDAR 2015 datasets, TextBoost achieves up to 60.6% higher text-recognition F1 at comparable PSNR and bpp, producing sharper small-font text while preserving global image quality and decoupling text enhancement from RD optimization.
AI.conclusion: Auxiliary textual guidance via OCR enables substantial gains in text fidelity without harming overall image quality, demonstrating effective semantic-guided compression and broad applicability to text-rich scenes.

title: Activation Outliers in Transformer Quantization: Reproduction, Statistical Analysis, and Deployment Tradeoffs
comment: 10 pages, 3 tables. Reproducible study of transformer PTQ activation outliers based on Bondarenko et al. (EMNLP 2021, Qualcomm AI Research). Code: https://github.com/pranavkkp4/TransQuant-Edge
summary: Post-training quantization (PTQ) of transformers is known to suffer from severe accuracy degradation due to structured activation outliers, as originally analyzed by Bondarenko et al. (EMNLP 2021) in work associated with Qualcomm AI Research. This paper provides a reproducible empirical reproduction and systems-level extension of that phenomenon in BERT-base fine-tuned on QNLI. When global W8A8 quantization is applied, validation accuracy drops sharply from 89.66% (FP32) to 54.33%, a decrease of 35.33 points. Statistical analysis of FP32 activations shows strongly heavy-tailed behavior that intensifies with model depth: kurtosis reaches 271 in the final layers and approximately 55% of activation energy is concentrated in the top 1% of channels. We evaluate several mitigation strategies. Mixed precision PTQ restores accuracy close to the FP32 baseline (89.42%). Per-embedding-group (PEG) quantization shows strong sensitivity to grouping structure, improving accuracy from 66.12% with three groups to 86.18% with four groups. In contrast, percentile-based calibration, even at thresholds between 99.0 and 99.99, fails to recover accuracy (about 50.54%), indicating that large activation channels encode structured signal rather than rare noise. Deployment profiling on an RTX 3050 GPU shows minimal differences in latency and memory usage across methods (median latency about 58-59 ms; VRAM usage about 484-486 MB), highlighting the importance of hardware-aware evaluation. Overall, the results show that PTQ failure in transformers is primarily driven by structured channel dominance amplified through residual connections. Effective mitigation therefore requires channel-aware precision allocation rather than scalar clipping alone.

AI.tldr: PTQ of transformers fails due to structured activation outliers; mixed-precision and channel-aware strategies mitigate; group-based PEG improves with more groups; percentile calibration ineffective; hardware profiling shows minimal latency/memory differences.
AI.motivation: Reproduce and quantify the observed PTQ failure mode in transformers, quantify activation tail behavior, and evaluate mitigation strategies for robust post-training quantization of BERT-base on QNLI.
AI.method: Fine-tune BERT-base on QNLI, apply global W8A8 PTQ, compare FP32 baseline. Analyze activation statistics (kurtosis, energy concentration). Evaluate mitigation: mixed-precision PTQ, Per-Embedding-Group (PEG) quantization with varying group counts, percentile-based calibration. Deploy profiling on an RTX 3050 to assess latency and VRAM.
AI.result: Global W8A8 quantization drastically degrades validation accuracy (89.66% FP32 → 54.33%). Activations exhibit heavy tails (kurtosis up to 271 in final layers); ~55% of activation energy concentrated in top 1% channels. Mixed-precision PTQ recovers accuracy to ~89.4%. PEG quantization improves with more groups (3 groups: 66.12%; 4 groups: 86.18%). Percentile calibration (thresholds 99.0–99.99) fails (~50.54%). RTX 3050 profiling shows median latency ~58–59 ms and VRAM ~484–486 MB, with minimal across-method differences. Conclusion: PTQ failure is driven by structured channel dominance amplified by residual connections; channel-aware precision allocation is essential, rather than scalar clipping.
AI.conclusion: PTQ failures in transformers arise from structured, channel-dominated activations amplified by residual connections. Effective mitigation requires channel-aware precision allocation (e.g., finer-grained or per-channel or per-group quantization) rather than simple clipping or global thresholds.

title: A Baseline Study and Benchmark for Few-Shot Open-Set Action Recognition with Feature Residual Discrimination
summary: Few-Shot Action Recognition (FS-AR) has shown promising results but is often limited by a closed-set assumption that fails in real-world open-set scenarios. While Few-Shot Open-Set (FSOS) recognition is well-established for images, its extension to spatio-temporal video data remains underexplored. To address this, we propose an architectural extension based on a Feature-Residual Discriminator (FR-Disc), adapting previous work on skeletal data to the more complex video domain. Extensive experiments on five datasets demonstrate that while common open-set techniques provide only marginal gains, our FR-Disc significantly enhances unknown rejection capabilities without compromising closed-set accuracy, setting a new state-of-the-art for FSOS-AR. The project website, code, and benchmark are available at: https://hsp-iit.github.io/fsosar/.

AI.tldr: FR-Disc: a video FSOS-AR method using a Feature-Residual Discriminator to improve unknown rejection without hurting closed-set accuracy, achieving state-of-the-art on five datasets.
AI.motivation: FS-AR suffers from closed-set limitations; while FSOS recognition is established for images, its extension to spatio-temporal video remains underexplored; a robust open-set mechanism is needed for real-world video action recognition.
AI.method: Proposes an architectural extension based on a Feature-Residual Discriminator (FR-Disc), adapting prior skeletal-data open-set work to the more complex video domain, and integrating it into video action representations to better separate unseen classes from known ones.
AI.result: FR-Disc significantly enhances unknown rejection capabilities without compromising closed-set accuracy, achieving new state-of-the-art performance for Few-Shot Open-Set Action Recognition on five datasets; project code and benchmark available.
AI.conclusion: FR-Disc demonstrates effective transfer of skeletal-based open-set discriminators to video, advancing FSOS-AR and providing a practical benchmark and resources for the community.

title: CRESTomics: Analyzing Carotid Plaques in the CREST-2 Trial with a New Additive Classification Model
comment: 4 pages, 3 figures, 1 table, accepted to ISBI 2026
summary: Accurate characterization of carotid plaques is critical for stroke prevention in patients with carotid stenosis. We analyze 500 plaques from CREST-2, a multi-center clinical trial, to identify radiomics-based markers from B-mode ultrasound images linked with high-risk. We propose a new kernel-based additive model, combining coherence loss with group-sparse regularization for nonlinear classification. Group-wise additive effects of each feature group are visualized using partial dependence plots. Results indicate our method accurately and interpretably assesses plaques, revealing a strong association between plaque texture and clinical risk.

AI.tldr: Radiomics-based risk stratification of carotid plaques from B-mode ultrasound using a kernel-based additive model with coherence loss and group-sparse regularization; interpretable group-wise effects via partial dependence plots; findings show texture features strongly associated with high-risk plaques in CREST-2 data.
AI.motivation: To achieve accurate and interpretable classification of plaque risk from radiomic features, addressing nonlinearity and feature grouping while preserving interpretability.
AI.method: Kernel-based additive model with coherence loss and group-sparse regularization. Features grouped by type; group-wise additive effects estimated and visualized with partial dependence plots to interpret non-linear associations between feature groups and risk.
AI.result: High-risk plaques identified with high accuracy; texture features show strong association with clinical risk; the model provides interpretable insights through group-wise PD plots.
AI.conclusion: Proposes an effective and interpretable nonlinear radiomics framework for carotid plaque assessment that leverages multi-center CREST-2 data; texture is a key risk indicator.

title: Crab$^{+}$: A Scalable and Unified Audio-Visual Scene Understanding Model with Explicit Cooperation
summary: Developing Audio-Visual Large Language Models (AV-LLMs) for unified scene understanding is pivotal in multimodal intelligence. While instruction tuning enables pre-trained models with multi-task abilities, we observe that conventional multi-task unification methods often suffer from severe negative transfer, where nearly 55% of tasks degrade compared to single-task training. We attribute this phenomenon to audio-visual task heterogeneity, characterized by disparate task granularity and divergent capability demands, which lead to negative interference under joint training. To tackle this, we present Crab$^{+}$, a scalable and unified audio-visual scene understanding model that addresses task heterogeneity through explicit cooperation from both data and model perspectives. On the data side, we introduce AV-UIE v2, a comprehensive Audio-Visual Unified Instruction-tuning dataset with Explicit reasoning processes. It contains approximately 222K samples spanning 17 datasets and 7 tasks, enabling the model to capture cross-task relationships at different levels of granularity. On the model side, we design a unified interface to align heterogeneous task formulations, and propose Interaction-aware LoRA (I-LoRA), which explicitly models inter-task relationships via dynamic routing to coordinate distinct audio-visual interaction patterns, mitigating parameter interference. Extensive experiments show Crab$^{+}$ covers broader tasks than existing unified models while outperforming specialized models on various benchmarks. We successfully reverse the negative transfer trend, achieving positive transfer where multi-task learning surpasses single-task baselines in nearly 88% of tasks. These results hold across diverse AV-LLM paradigms and are validated through in-depth visualization, positioning Crab$^{+}$ as a robust step towards holistic audio-visual scene understanding.

AI.tldr: Crab+ tackles negative transfer in audio-visual multi-task learning by combining a data-side AV-UIE v2 dataset with explicit reasoning and a model-side I-LoRA module and unified task interface, achieving broad task coverage and positive transfer in about 88% of tasks, outperforming both unified and specialized baselines.
AI.motivation: Task heterogeneity in unified audio-visual scene understanding causes negative transfer during joint training. A scalable, unified AV-LLM requires alignment across diverse task granularities and interaction patterns.
AI.method: Data: AV-UIE v2 — ~222k samples, 17 datasets, 7 tasks, with explicit reasoning processes to capture cross-task relationships at multiple granularity levels. Model: a unified interface to normalize heterogeneous task formulations and Interaction-aware LoRA (I-LoRA) with dynamic routing to coordinate distinct audio-visual interaction patterns and reduce parameter interference.
AI.result: Crab+ achieves broader task coverage than existing unified models and outperforms specialized models on multiple benchmarks. It reverses the negative transfer trend, with positive transfer observed in ~88% of tasks, and validation across diverse AV-LLM paradigms supported by visualization-based analyses.
AI.conclusion: Crab+ advances holistic AV scene understanding by effectively coupling data and model innovations to address task heterogeneity, offering a scalable path toward unified, high-performance AV-LLMs.

title: PTOPOFL: Privacy-Preserving Personalised Federated Learning via Persistent Homology
summary: Federated learning (FL) faces two structural tensions: gradient sharing enables data-reconstruction attacks, while non-IID client distributions degrade aggregation quality. We introduce PTOPOFL, a framework that addresses both challenges simultaneously by replacing gradient communication with topological descriptors derived from persistent homology (PH). Clients transmit only 48-dimensional PH feature vectors-compact shape summaries whose many-to-one structure makes inversion provably ill-posed-rather than model gradients. The server performs topology-guided personalised aggregation: clients are clustered by Wasserstein similarity between their PH diagrams, intra-cluster models are topology-weighted,and clusters are blended with a global consensus. We prove an information-contraction theorem showing that PH descriptors leak strictly less mutual information per sample than gradients under strongly convex loss functions, and we establish linear convergence of the Wasserstein-weighted aggregation scheme with an error floor strictly smaller than FedAvg. Evaluated against FedAvg, FedProx, SCAFFOLD, and pFedMe on a non-IID healthcare scenario (8 hospitals, 2 adversarial) and a pathological benchmark (10 clients), PTOPOFL achieves AUC 0.841 and 0.910 respectively-the highest in both settings-while reducing reconstruction risk by a factor of 4.5 relative to gradient sharing. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/MorillaLab/TopoFederatedL and data at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18827595.

AI.tldr: PTOPOFL replaces gradient exchange in federated learning with 48-dimensional persistent-homology features and uses topology-guided, Wasserstein-based clustering for personalized aggregation, achieving higher AUC and lower reconstruction risk than several baselines.
AI.motivation: Mitigate two core FL challenges simultaneously: (i) data-reconstruction risk from gradient leakage, and (ii) degraded aggregation quality under non-IID client distributions.
AI.method: Compute compact PH descriptors (48-d vectors) per client and exchange them instead of gradients. Server clusters clients by Wasserstein similarity of PH diagrams, applies topology-weighted intra-cluster aggregation, and blends clusters with a global consensus. The paper proves an information-contraction theorem for PH descriptors and shows linear convergence with a favorable error floor. Empirical evaluation compares PTOPOFL to FedAvg, FedProx, SCAFFOLD, and pFedMe on non-IID healthcare data (8 hospitals with 2 adversarial) and a 10-client pathological benchmark.
AI.result: PTOPOFL achieves AUC 0.841 (non-IID healthcare) and 0.910 (pathological benchmark), the highest in both settings, and reduces reconstruction risk by a factor of 4.5 compared with gradient-sharing baselines.
AI.conclusion: PH-based descriptors enable privacy-preserving, topology-guided aggregation with convergence guarantees and superior performance in challenging non-IID FL settings, validated by publicly available code and data.

title: Mask-Guided Attention Regulation for Anatomically Consistent Counterfactual CXR Synthesis
summary: Counterfactual generation for chest X-rays (CXR) aims to simulate plausible pathological changes while preserving patient-specific anatomy. However, diffusion-based editing methods often suffer from structural drift, where stable anatomical semantics propagate globally through attention and distort non-target regions, and unstable pathology expression, since subtle and localized lesions induce weak and noisy conditioning signals. We present an inference-time attention regulation framework for reliable counterfactual CXR synthesis. An anatomy-aware attention regularization module gates self-attention and anatomy-token cross-attention with organ masks, confining structural interactions to anatomical ROIs and reducing unintended distortions. A pathology-guided module enhances pathology-token cross-attention within target lung regions during early denoising and performs lightweight latent corrections driven by an attention-concentration energy, enabling controllable lesion localization and extent. Extensive evaluations on CXR datasets show improved anatomical consistency and more precise, controllable pathological edits compared with standard diffusion editing, supporting localized counterfactual analysis and data augmentation for downstream tasks.

AI.tldr: An attention-regulated diffusion editing framework for chest X-rays improves anatomical fidelity and allows controllable, localized pathological edits in counterfactual synthesis.
AI.motivation: To address diffusion-based editing weaknesses in counterfactual CXR generation: (i) structural drift that propagates anatomy globally, and (ii) unstable, weakly conditioned pathology expression for localized lesions.
AI.method: Inference-time framework with two modules: (1) anatomy-aware attention regularization gating self-attention and anatomy-token cross-attention with organ masks to confine interactions to anatomical ROIs; (2) pathology-guided module enhancing pathology-token cross-attention within target lung regions during early denoising, plus lightweight latent corrections driven by an attention-concentration energy to localize lesions controllably.
AI.result: Compared with standard diffusion editing, the method yields improved anatomical consistency and more precise, controllable pathological edits on CXR datasets; supports localized counterfactual analysis and augmentation for downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: Attention-regulated diffusion editing can provide reliable, anatomically constrained and controllable counterfactual CXR synthesis, mitigating structural drift and unstable pathology expression.

title: Algorithmic Compliance and Regulatory Loss in Digital Assets
summary: We study the deployment performance of machine learning based enforcement systems used in cryptocurrency anti money laundering (AML). Using forward looking and rolling evaluations on Bitcoin transaction data, we show that strong static classification metrics substantially overstate real world regulatory effectiveness. Temporal nonstationarity induces pronounced instability in cost sensitive enforcement thresholds, generating large and persistent excess regulatory losses relative to dynamically optimal benchmarks. The core failure arises from miscalibration of decision rules rather than from declining predictive accuracy per se. These findings underscore the fragility of fixed AML enforcement policies in evolving digital asset markets and motivate loss-based evaluation frameworks for regulatory oversight.

AI.tldr: Static ML AML metrics overstate real-world effectiveness; temporal nonstationarity causes miscalibration of decision rules, leading to persistent excess regulatory losses vs dynamic benchmarks; advocates loss-based evaluation for oversight.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether ML-based enforcement for cryptocurrency AML translates into regulatory value in practice, accounting for time-varying market dynamics.
AI.method: Forward-looking and rolling evaluations on Bitcoin transaction data; analysis of cost-sensitive enforcement thresholds; comparison of static metrics vs dynamic benchmarks; emphasis on calibration over predictive accuracy.
AI.result: Strong static classification metrics inflate perceived regulatory effectiveness; thresholds exhibit instability due to temporal nonstationarity; significant excess regulatory losses compared to dynamically optimal benchmarks; miscalibration of decision rules identified as primary failure mode.
AI.conclusion: Fixed AML enforcement policies are fragile in evolving digital asset markets; loss-based evaluation frameworks are needed for regulatory oversight and policy design; dynamic calibration and evaluation should be prioritized.

title: LISTA-Transformer Model Based on Sparse Coding and Attention Mechanism and Its Application in Fault Diagnosis
summary: Driven by the continuous development of models such as Multi-Layer Perceptron, Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), and Transformer, deep learning has made breakthrough progress in fields such as computer vision and natural language processing, and has been successfully applied in practical scenarios such as image classification and industrial fault diagnosis. However, existing models still have certain limitations in local feature modeling and global dependency capture. Specifically, CNN is limited by local receptive fields, while Transformer has shortcomings in effectively modeling local structures, and both face challenges of high model complexity and insufficient interpretability. In response to the above issues, we proposes the following innovative work: A sparse Transformer based on Learnable Iterative Shrinkage Threshold Algorithm (LISTA-Transformer) was designed, which deeply integrates LISTA sparse encoding with visual Transformer to construct a model architecture with adaptive local and global feature collaboration mechanism. This method utilizes continuous wavelet transform to convert vibration signals into time-frequency maps and inputs them into LISTA-Transformer for more effective feature extraction. On the CWRU dataset, the fault recognition rate of our method reached 98.5%, which is 3.3% higher than traditional methods and exhibits certain superiority over existing Transformer-based approaches.

AI.tldr: A LISTA-Transformer integrates LISTA sparse encoding with a Transformer for adaptive local/global feature fusion, using continuous wavelet transform on vibration signals; achieves 98.5% fault recognition on CWRU, outperforming traditional and Transformer baselines.
AI.motivation: CNNs have limited local receptive fields and Transformers may struggle with local structure; together with high model complexity and limited interpretability. A model enabling adaptive local-global feature interaction could improve fault diagnosis performance and efficiency.
AI.method: Proposes a sparse LISTA-Transformer architecture that embeds LISTA-based sparse coding within Transformer blocks to enable adaptive local-global feature collaboration. Vibration signals are converted to time-frequency maps via continuous wavelet transform and fed into the LISTA-Transformer for feature extraction and classification on the CWRU dataset.
AI.result: Fault recognition rate of 98.5% on CWRU, 3.3 percentage points higher than traditional methods; claims superiority over existing Transformer-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates effective integration of local sparse encoding with global Transformer modeling, yielding strong fault-diagnosis performance on vibration data. Further work should assess generalization across datasets, interpretability, and computational efficiency.

title: What Does Flow Matching Bring To TD Learning?
summary: Recent work shows that flow matching can be effective for scalar Q-value function estimation in reinforcement learning (RL), but it remains unclear why or how this approach differs from standard critics. Contrary to conventional belief, we show that their success is not explained by distributional RL, as explicitly modeling return distributions can reduce performance. Instead, we argue that the use of integration for reading out values and dense velocity supervision at each step of this integration process for training improves TD learning via two mechanisms. First, it enables robust value prediction through \emph{test-time recovery}, whereby iterative computation through integration dampens errors in early value estimates as more integration steps are performed. This recovery mechanism is absent in monolithic critics. Second, supervising the velocity field at multiple interpolant values induces more \emph{plastic} feature learning within the network, allowing critics to represent non-stationary TD targets without discarding previously learned features or overfitting to individual TD targets encountered during training. We formalize these effects and validate them empirically, showing that flow-matching critics substantially outperform monolithic critics (2$\times$ in final performance and around 5$\times$ in sample efficiency) in settings where loss of plasticity poses a challenge e.g., in high-UTD online RL problems, while remaining stable during learning.

AI.tldr: Flow-matching critics improve TD learning by reading values via integration and training a dense velocity field at multiple interpolants, enabling test-time recovery and increased representation plasticity; this yields superior performance over monolithic critics, especially in high-UTD online RL settings.
AI.motivation: Clarify why flow matching succeeds and how it differs from standard critics; challenge the belief that distributional modeling is the key to performance gains.
AI.method: Introduce integration-based value readout and dense velocity supervision across multiple interpolants during training; formalize the effects on TD learning; compare to monolithic critics; provide empirical validation, particularly in high-UTD online RL problems.
AI.result: Flow-matching critics substantially outperform monolithic critics (about 2x final performance, ~5x sample efficiency) in challenging online RL scenarios where plasticity is hard to maintain, while remaining stable during learning.
AI.conclusion: Integration-based readout and multi-point velocity supervision yield robust TD learning via test-time recovery and increased feature plasticity, challenging the view that distributional modeling is essential for performance gains; the approach offers practical improvements in sample efficiency and stability.

title: Degradation-based augmented training for robust individual animal re-identification
summary: Wildlife re-identification aims to recognise individual animals by matching query images to a database of previously identified individuals, based on their fine-scale unique morphological characteristics. Current state-of-the-art models for multispecies re- identification are based on deep metric learning representing individual identities by fea- ture vectors in an embedding space, the similarity of which forms the basis for a fast automated identity retrieval. Yet very often, the discriminative information of individual wild animals gets significantly reduced due to the presence of several degradation factors in images, leading to reduced retrieval performance and limiting the downstream eco- logical studies. Here, starting by showing that the extent of this performance reduction greatly varies depending on the animal species (18 wild animal datasets), we introduce an augmented training framework for deep feature extractors, where we apply artificial but diverse degradations in images in the training set. We show that applying this augmented training only to a subset of individuals, leads to an overall increased re-identification performance, under the same type of degradations, even for individuals not seen during training. The introduction of diverse degradations during training leads to a gain of up to 8.5% Rank-1 accuracy to a dataset of real-world degraded animal images, selected using human re-ID expert annotations provided here for the first time. Our work is the first to systematically study image degradation in wildlife re-identification, while introducing all the necessary benchmarks, publicly available code and data, enabling further research on this topic.

AI.tldr: Augmented training with diverse image degradations improves wildlife re-ID robustness; applying degradations to only a subset of individuals yields up to 8.5% Rank-1 gain on real degraded wildlife images; introduces benchmarks, code, and data; first systematic study of degradation effects in wildlife re-ID.
AI.motivation: Wildlife re-ID performance degrades under image degradation; performance varies across species; need robust, generalizable feature representations for ecological studies; establish benchmarks.
AI.method: Propose augmented training framework for deep metric learning; simulate artificial degradations (blur, noise, compression, etc.) during training; apply degradations to only a subset of individuals to boost robustness; evaluate on 18 wild animal datasets with real degradations; release benchmarks and code.
AI.result: Demonstrates up to 8.5% improvement in Rank-1 accuracy under degradations; gains extend to individuals not seen during training; first to systematically study degradation in wildlife re-ID; provides publicly available code and data.
AI.conclusion: Diversity of training degradations enhances robustness of multispecies wild-ID models; targeted augmentation strategy is effective and generalizes; establishes benchmarks and resources for future research; potential limitations to be explored.

title: Out-of-distribution transfer of PDE foundation models to material dynamics under extreme loading
summary: Most PDE foundation models are pretrained and fine-tuned on fluid-centric benchmarks. Their utility under extreme-loading material dynamics remains unclear. We benchmark out-of-distribution transfer on two discontinuity-dominated regimes in which shocks, evolving interfaces, and fracture produce highly non-smooth fields: shock-driven multi-material interface dynamics (perturbed layered interface or PLI) and dynamic fracture/failure evolution (FRAC). We formulate the downstream task as terminal-state prediction, i.e., learning a long-horizon map that predicts the final state directly from the first snapshot without intermediate supervision. Using a unified training and evaluation protocol, we evaluate two open-source pretrained PDE foundation models, POSEIDON and MORPH, and compare fine-tuning from pretrained weights against training from scratch across training-set sizes to quantify sample efficiency under distribution shift.

AI.tldr: Benchmarks two PDE foundation models (POSEIDON, MORPH) on two discontinuity-dominated regimes (PLI shock-driven interfaces and FRAC dynamic fracture) for terminal-state prediction, comparing fine-tuning from pretrained weights versus training from scratch across data sizes to assess sample efficiency under distribution shift.
AI.motivation: PDE foundation models pretrained on fluid benchmarks may not generalize to extreme-loading material dynamics with shocks, interfaces, and fractures. Evaluating OOD transfer and data efficiency is essential to determine utility in non-smooth, discontinuity-dominated regimes.
AI.method: Two regimes (PLI and FRAC) with discontinuities; downstream task is long-horizon terminal-state prediction from the first snapshot. Use a unified training/evaluation protocol to assess two open-source pretrained PDE foundation models (POSEIDON, MORPH). Compare fine-tuning from pretrained weights against training from scratch across varying training-set sizes to quantify sample efficiency under distribution shift.
AI.result: Systematic evaluation comparing pretrained fine-tuning vs scratch across two regimes and training sizes. The study quantifies how pretraining affects sample efficiency and transfer under distribution shift for discontinuity-dominated PDE tasks, with model and regime-dependent outcomes.
AI.conclusion: Pretraining on fluid benchmarks can improve OOD transfer for discontinuity-dominated PDE tasks, enabling more sample-efficient terminal-state prediction under distribution shift. The effectiveness depends on regime and model; results inform model choice and data requirements for robust generalization to shock/interface/fracture dynamics.

title: PlaneCycle: Training-Free 2D-to-3D Lifting of Foundation Models Without Adapters
summary: Large-scale 2D foundation models exhibit strong transferable representations, yet extending them to 3D volumetric data typically requires retraining, adapters, or architectural redesign. We introduce PlaneCycle, a training-free, adapter-free operator for architecture-agnostic 2D-to-3D lifting of foundation models. PlaneCycle reuses the original pretrained 2D backbone by cyclically distributing spatial aggregation across orthogonal HW, DW, and DH planes throughout network depth, enabling progressive 3D fusion while preserving pretrained inductive biases. The method introduces no additional parameters and is applicable to arbitrary 2D networks. Using pretrained DINOv3 models, we evaluate PlaneCycle on six 3D classification and three 3D segmentation benchmarks. Without any training, the lifted models exhibit intrinsic 3D fusion capability and, under linear probing, outperform slice-wise 2D baselines and strong 3D counterparts, approaching the performance of fully trained models. With full fine-tuning, PlaneCycle matches standard 3D architectures, highlighting its potential as a seamless and practical 2D-to-3D lifting operator. These results demonstrate that 3D capability can be unlocked from pretrained 2D foundation models without structural modification or retraining. Code is available at https://github.com/HINTLab/PlaneCycle.

AI.tldr: PlaneCycle is a training-free, adapter-free operator that lifts 2D foundation models to 3D by cyclically distributing spatial aggregation across HW, DW, DH planes, enabling 3D fusion without retraining; it preserves 2D inductive biases and matches/tracks 3D architectures after fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: To extend large-scale 2D foundation models to 3D data without retraining or architectural changes, enabling 3D capabilities in a model- and data-efficient way.
AI.method: Reuses pretrained 2D backbone; introduces PlaneCycle operator that cycles spatial aggregation across orthogonal planes (HW, DW, DH) through network depth; no additional parameters; architecture-agnostic; evaluated using DINOv3 on 3D tasks.
AI.result: Without training, lifted models show 3D fusion; linear probing outperforms slice-wise 2D baselines and strong 3D counterparts; close to fully trained models; with full fine-tuning, matches standard 3D architectures.
AI.conclusion: 3D capability can be unlocked from pretrained 2D foundation models without structural modification or retraining; PlaneCycle provides a practical 2D-to-3D lifting operator; code available.

title: Efficient Refusal Ablation in LLM through Optimal Transport
summary: Safety-aligned language models refuse harmful requests through learned refusal behaviors encoded in their internal representations. Recent activation-based jailbreaking methods circumvent these safety mechanisms by applying orthogonal projections to remove refusal directions, but these approaches treat refusal as a one-dimensional phenomenon and ignore the rich distributional structure of model activations. We introduce a principled framework based on optimal transport theory that transforms the entire distribution of harmful activations to match harmless ones. By combining PCA with closed-form Gaussian optimal transport, we achieve efficient computation in high-dimensional representation spaces while preserving essential geometric structure. Across six models (Llama-2, Llama-3.1, Qwen-2.5; 7B-32B parameters), our method achieves up to 11% higher attack success rates than state-of-the-art baselines while maintaining comparable perplexity, demonstrating superior preservation of model capabilities. Critically, we discover that layer-selective intervention (applying optimal transport to 1-2 carefully chosen layers at approximately 40-60% network depth) substantially outperforms full-network interventions, revealing that refusal mechanisms may be localized rather than distributed. Our analysis provides new insights into the geometric structure of safety representations and suggests that current alignment methods may be vulnerable to distributional attacks beyond simple direction removal.

AI.tldr: A distribution-level OT framework maps harmful activations to harmless ones, using PCA and closed-form Gaussian OT for efficiency. Layer-selective interventions (1–2 layers at ~40–60% depth) outperform full-network interventions, revealing localized safety refusals and distributional vulnerabilities in alignment methods.
AI.motivation: Address the limitation of activation-based jailbreaking that treats refusal as a single direction. Recognize that safety refusals occupy a distributional structure in activations, and exploit this geometry to study robustness and vulnerabilities of alignment methods.
AI.method: Compile a workflow integrating PCA-dimensionality reduction with Gaussian optimal transport to transform the entire distribution of harmful activations to align with harmless activations. Compute OT in reduced space, apply selectively to 1–2 layers (~40–60% depth) in six models (Llama-2, Llama-3.1, Qwen-2.5; 7B–32B). Compare against baselines on attack success rate and perplexity.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves up to 11% higher attack success rates than state-of-the-art baselines while preserving perplexity. Layer-selective transport substantially outperforms full-network interventions, implying the safety refusal mechanism is localized rather than distributed; the framework preserves model capabilities while enabling stronger distributional attacks.
AI.conclusion: Distributional structure of safety representations is central to alignment robustness. Current methods may be vulnerable to distributional attacks, and targeted layer-wise interventions reveal localization of refusal mechanics, guiding future defenses and analyses of safety-aligned models.

title: NOVA3R: Non-pixel-aligned Visual Transformer for Amodal 3D Reconstruction
comment: Accepted to ICLR 2026. Project Page: https://wrchen530.github.io/nova3r
summary: We present NOVA3R, an effective approach for non-pixel-aligned 3D reconstruction from a set of unposed images in a feed-forward manner. Unlike pixel-aligned methods that tie geometry to per-ray predictions, our formulation learns a global, view-agnostic scene representation that decouples reconstruction from pixel alignment. This addresses two key limitations in pixel-aligned 3D: (1) it recovers both visible and invisible points with a complete scene representation, and (2) it produces physically plausible geometry with fewer duplicated structures in overlapping regions. To achieve this, we introduce a scene-token mechanism that aggregates information across unposed images and a diffusion-based 3D decoder that reconstructs complete, non-pixel-aligned point clouds. Extensive experiments on both scene-level and object-level datasets demonstrate that NOVA3R outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of reconstruction accuracy and completeness.

AI.tldr: NOVA3R enables non-pixel-aligned 3D reconstruction from unposed images via a scene-token + diffusion-based 3D decoder, achieving more complete and plausible geometry than pixel-aligned methods.
AI.motivation: Pixel-aligned 3D methods tie geometry to per-ray predictions, failing to recover invisible points and causing duplication in overlaps; a global, view-agnostic representation is needed.
AI.method: Introduce a scene-token mechanism to aggregate information across unposed images and a diffusion-based 3D decoder to reconstruct complete, non-pixel-aligned point clouds in a feed-forward pipeline.
AI.result: Empirical results on scene- and object-level datasets show NOVA3R outperforms state-of-the-art methods in reconstruction accuracy and completeness.
AI.conclusion: By decoupling reconstruction from pixel alignment, NOVA3R yields physically plausible geometry with fewer duplicates and robustly reconstructs complete scenes from unposed imagery.

title: Dissecting Quantization Error: A Concentration-Alignment Perspective
summary: Quantization can drastically increase the efficiency of large language and vision models, but typically incurs an accuracy drop. Recently, function-preserving transforms (e.g. rotations, Hadamard transform, channel-wise scaling) have been successfully applied to reduce post-training quantization error, yet a principled explanation remains elusive. We analyze linear-layer quantization via the signal-to-quantization-noise ratio (SQNR), showing that for uniform integer quantization at a fixed bit width, SQNR decomposes into (i) the concentration of weights and activations (capturing spread and outliers), and (ii) the alignment of their dominant variation directions. This reveals an actionable insight: beyond concentration - the focus of most prior transforms (e.g. rotations or Hadamard) - improving alignment between weight and activation can further reduce quantization error. Motivated by this, we introduce block Concentration-Alignment Transforms (CAT), a lightweight linear transformation that uses a covariance estimate from a small calibration set to jointly improve concentration and alignment, approximately maximizing SQNR. Experiments across several LLMs show that CAT consistently matches or outperforms prior transform-based quantization methods at 4-bit precision, confirming the insights gained in our framework.

AI.tldr: A lightweight block Concentration-Alignment Transform (CAT) improves 4-bit post-training quantization by jointly optimizing concentration and alignment of weights/activations to maximize SQNR, outperforming prior transforms across several LLMs.
AI.motivation: Quantization enables efficiency but often harms accuracy. A principled explanation is needed for the success of function-preserving transforms. Analyze linear-layer quantization via SQNR to separate effects of weight/activation spread (concentration) and variation direction alignment.
AI.method: Derive SQNR decomposition for uniform integer quantization to show dependence on concentration and alignment. Propose block CAT: a light linear transform that uses a small calibration-set covariance estimate to jointly improve concentration and alignment, aiming to maximize SQNR. Apply to LLMs and compare with prior transform methods.
AI.result: CAT consistently matches or outperforms prior transform-based quantization methods at 4-bit precision across several LLMs.
AI.conclusion: The framework clarifies why transforms help in quantization by highlighting alignment in addition to concentration. CAT provides a practical, calibration-efficient method that improves 4-bit quantization performance.

title: Real5-OmniDocBench: A Full-Scale Physical Reconstruction Benchmark for Robust Document Parsing in the Wild
summary: While Vision-Language Models (VLMs) achieve near-perfect scores on digital document benchmarks like OmniDocBench, their performance in the unpredictable physical world remains largely unknown due to the lack of controlled yet realistic evaluations. We introduce Real5-OmniDocBench, the first benchmark that performs a full-scale, one-to-one physical reconstruction of the entire OmniDocBench v1.5 (1,355 images) across five critical real-world scenarios: Scanning, Warping, Screen-Photography, Illumination, and Skew. Unlike prior benchmark that either lack digital correspondence or employ partial sampling, our complete ground-truth mapping enables, for the first time, rigorous factor-wise attribution of performance degradation-allowing us to pinpoint whether failures stem from geometric distortions, optical artifacts, or model limitations. Our benchmark establishes a challenging new standard for the community, demonstrating that the 'reality gap' in document parsing is far from closed, and provides a diagnostic tool to guide the development of truly resilient document intelligence.

AI.tldr: New Real5-OmniDocBench benchmark performs full-scale physical reconstruction of OmniDocBench v1.5 across five real-world scenarios, enabling one-to-one ground-truth mapping and factor-wise attribution of performance, highlighting persistent reality gap in document parsing.
AI.motivation: Digital benchmarks fail to reflect real-world variability; need controlled yet realistic evaluation with complete ground-truth correspondence to diagnose sources of error (geometry, optics, model limits).
AI.method: Construct a physical replica/reconstruction of 1,355 images from OmniDocBench v1.5 across Scanning, Warping, Screen-Photography, Illumination, Skew; create full ground-truth mapping between real captures and digital ground truth; enable factor-wise analysis.
AI.result: Establishes a new challenging standard; shows the reality gap in document parsing remains wide; provides a diagnostic tool to guide resilient document intelligence development.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark will drive the development of robust document understanding systems by enabling targeted error attribution and reproducible evaluation in physical-world conditions.

title: Robust Unscented Kalman Filtering via Recurrent Meta-Adaptation of Sigma-Point Weights
comment: 8 pages, 3 figures, Submitted to the 29th International Conference on Information Fusion (FUSION 2026)
summary: The Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) is a ubiquitous tool for nonlinear state estimation; however, its performance is limited by the static parameterization of the Unscented Transform (UT). Conventional weighting schemes, governed by fixed scaling parameters, assume implicit Gaussianity and fail to adapt to time-varying dynamics or heavy-tailed measurement noise. This work introduces the Meta-Adaptive UKF (MA-UKF), a framework that reformulates sigma-point weight synthesis as a hyperparameter optimization problem addressed via memory-augmented meta-learning. Unlike standard adaptive filters that rely on instantaneous heuristic corrections, our approach employs a Recurrent Context Encoder to compress the history of measurement innovations into a compact latent embedding. This embedding informs a policy network that dynamically synthesizes the mean and covariance weights of the sigma points at each time step, effectively governing the filter's trust in the prediction versus the measurement. By optimizing the system end-to-end through the filter's recursive logic, the MA-UKF learns to maximize tracking accuracy while maintaining estimation consistency. Numerical benchmarks on maneuvering targets demonstrate that the MA-UKF significantly outperforms standard baselines, exhibiting superior robustness to non-Gaussian glint noise and effective generalization to out-of-distribution (OOD) dynamic regimes unseen during training.

AI.tldr: Meta-Adaptive UKF uses memory-augmented meta-learning to adapt sigma-point weights in the UKF, improving robustness to non-Gaussian noise and OOD dynamics via end-to-end optimization of a Recurrent Context Encoder and a policy network.
AI.motivation: Fixed Unscented Transform weights assume Gaussianity and static dynamics, leading to degraded performance under time-varying dynamics and heavy-tailed noise. Adaptive, data-driven weight synthesis aims to maintain accuracy and consistency.
AI.method: Reframe sigma-point weight selection as a hyperparameter optimization within a memory-augmented meta-learning framework. A Recurrent Context Encoder compresses the history of measurement innovations into a latent embedding, which informs a policy network that dynamically outputs mean and covariance weights for the sigma points at each time step, thus balancing trust between prediction and measurement. End-to-end optimization integrates this within the filter's recursive loop.
AI.result: Benchmarks on maneuvering targets show MA-UKF outperforms standard baselines, with enhanced robustness to non-Gaussian glint noise and better generalization to out-of-distribution dynamic regimes not seen during training.
AI.conclusion: MA-UKF demonstrates improved tracking accuracy and estimation consistency by adaptively synthesizing sigma-point weights via memory-augmented meta-learning, enabling robust nonlinear state estimation under non-Gaussian and time-varying conditions.

title: DiverseDiT: Towards Diverse Representation Learning in Diffusion Transformers
comment: To appear in CVPR 2026, GitHub Code: https://github.com/kobeshegu/DiverseDiT, Project Page: https://forevermamba.work/projects/DiverseDiT/
summary: Recent breakthroughs in Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) have revolutionized the field of visual synthesis due to their superior scalability. To facilitate DiTs' capability of capturing meaningful internal representations, recent works such as REPA incorporate external pretrained encoders for representation alignment. However, the underlying mechanisms governing representation learning within DiTs are not well understood. To this end, we first systematically investigate the representation dynamics of DiTs. Through analyzing the evolution and influence of internal representations under various settings, we reveal that representation diversity across blocks is a crucial factor for effective learning. Based on this key insight, we propose DiverseDiT, a novel framework that explicitly promotes representation diversity. DiverseDiT incorporates long residual connections to diversify input representations across blocks and a representation diversity loss to encourage blocks to learn distinct features. Extensive experiments on ImageNet 256x256 and 512x512 demonstrate that our DiverseDiT yields consistent performance gains and convergence acceleration when applied to different backbones with various sizes, even when tested on the challenging one-step generation setting. Furthermore, we show that DiverseDiT is complementary to existing representation learning techniques, leading to further performance gains. Our work provides valuable insights into the representation learning dynamics of DiTs and offers a practical approach for enhancing their performance.

AI.tldr: DiverseDiT boosts diffusion transformers by explicitly promoting representation diversity across blocks using long residual connections and a diversity loss, achieving consistent gains and faster convergence across backbones and image sizes, including one-step generation.
AI.motivation: Understand how internal representations form and evolve in diffusion transformers (DiTs) and identify factors driving effective learning. While external encoders (e.g., REPA) exist for alignment, the mechanisms of representation learning within DiTs remain unclear; the authors hypothesize that diversity of block-wise representations is crucial for performance.
AI.method: Systematically analyze the dynamics of internal representations in DiTs across settings to identify key factors. Propose DiverseDiT, which 1) adds long residual connections to diversify inputs across blocks, and 2) introduces a representation diversity loss to encourage blocks to learn distinct features. Evaluate on ImageNet 256×256 and 512×512, across different backbones and sizes, including one-step generation; show compatibility and complementarity with existing representation learning methods.
AI.result: DiverseDiT yields consistent performance gains and faster convergence across backbones and image scales; improvements persist even in challenging one-step generation; the method is complementary to other representation learning techniques and provides practical gains.
AI.conclusion: Block-wise representation diversity is a key factor in DiT learning dynamics. The DiverseDiT framework offers a practical, effective approach to enhance DiTs by explicitly promoting diversity, broadening the applicability and performance of diffusion transformers.

title: Dual-Modality Multi-Stage Adversarial Safety Training: Robustifying Multimodal Web Agents Against Cross-Modal Attacks
summary: Multimodal web agents that process both screenshots and accessibility trees are increasingly deployed to interact with web interfaces, yet their dual-stream architecture opens an underexplored attack surface: an adversary who injects content into the webpage DOM simultaneously corrupts both observation channels with a consistent deceptive narrative. Our vulnerability analysis on MiniWob++ reveals that attacks including a visual component far outperform text-only injections, exposing critical gaps in text-centric VLM safety training. Motivated by this finding, we propose Dual-Modality Multi-Stage Adversarial Safety Training (DMAST), a framework that formalizes the agent-attacker interaction as a two-player zero-sum Markov game and co-trains both players through a three-stage pipeline: (1) imitation learning from a strong teacher model, (2) oracle-guided supervised fine-tuning that uses a novel zero-acknowledgment strategy to instill task-focused reasoning under adversarial noise, and (3) adversarial reinforcement learning via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) self-play. On out-of-distribution tasks, DMAST substantially mitigates adversarial risks while simultaneously doubling task completion efficiency. Our approach significantly outperforms established training-based and prompt-based defenses, demonstrating genuine co-evolutionary progress and robust generalization to complex, unseen environments.

AI.tldr: Proposes DMAST to defend multimodal web agents against adversarial DOM/content injections by co-training dual observation streams through a three-stage, two-player zero-sum Markov-game framework, achieving robust generalization and doubled task efficiency on out-of-distribution tasks.
AI.motivation: Dual-stream web agents (screenshots + accessibility trees) are vulnerable to adversarial content injections that contaminate both observation channels; text-only safety training is insufficient for multimodal settings.
AI.method: Modeling agent- attacker interaction as a two-player zero-sum Markov game and training via a three-stage pipeline: (1) imitation learning from a strong teacher, (2) oracle-guided supervised fine-tuning with a zero-acknowledgment strategy to emphasize task-focused reasoning under adversarial noise, (3) adversarial reinforcement learning through Group Relative Policy Optimization self-play.
AI.result: DMAST substantially reduces adversarial risk on out-of-distribution tasks and doubles task completion efficiency, outperforming existing training-based and prompt-based defenses.
AI.conclusion: Co-evolutionary training across dual modalities yields improved robustness and generalization to unseen, complex environments, indicating effective defense against sophisticated multimodal adversaries.

title: DeNuC: Decoupling Nuclei Detection and Classification in Histopathology
summary: Pathology Foundation Models (FMs) have shown strong performance across a wide range of pathology image representation and diagnostic tasks. However, FMs do not exhibit the expected performance advantage over traditional specialized models in Nuclei Detection and Classification (NDC). In this work, we reveal that jointly optimizing nuclei detection and classification leads to severe representation degradation in FMs. Moreover, we identify that the substantial intrinsic disparity in task difficulty between nuclei detection and nuclei classification renders joint NDC optimization unnecessarily computationally burdensome for the detection stage. To address these challenges, we propose DeNuC, a simple yet effective method designed to break through existing bottlenecks by Decoupling Nuclei detection and Classification. DeNuC employs a lightweight model for accurate nuclei localization, subsequently leveraging a pathology FM to encode input images and query nucleus-specific features based on the detected coordinates for classification. Extensive experiments on three widely used benchmarks demonstrate that DeNuC effectively unlocks the representational potential of FMs for NDC and significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Notably, DeNuC improves F1 scores by 4.2% and 3.6% (or higher) on the BRCAM2C and PUMA datasets, respectively, while using only 16% (or fewer) trainable parameters compared to other methods. Code is available at https://github.com/ZijiangY1116/DeNuC.

AI.tldr: Decoupling nuclei detection and classification (DeNuC) improves nucleus detection/classification (NDC) performance using a lightweight detector for localization and a pathology foundation model for nucleus-specific classification, yielding superior F1 gains and parameter efficiency on BRCAM2C/PUMA datasets.
AI.motivation: Joint optimization of detection and classification degrades FM representations and is computationally inefficient due to task imbalance; a decoupled approach can preserve localization accuracy and leverage FMs solely for classification.
AI.method: DeNuC uses a two-stage approach: (1) a lightweight detector localizes nuclei; (2) a pathology FM encodes input and queries nucleus-specific features using the detected coordinates for classification, effectively decoupling detection from classification.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on three benchmarks shows significant improvements in NDC, notably +4.2% F1 on BRCAM2C and +3.6% F1 on PUMA, with 16% or fewer trainable parameters, and a public code release.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling NDC via DeNuC unlocks the representational potential of FMs for pathology tasks, delivering better accuracy with greater parameter efficiency; the approach mitigates degradation from joint optimization and reduces computational burden for detection.

title: Robustness of Agentic AI Systems via Adversarially-Aligned Jacobian Regularization
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) transition into autonomous multi-agent ecosystems, robust minimax training becomes essential yet remains prone to instability when highly non-linear policies induce extreme local curvature in the inner maximization. Standard remedies that enforce global Jacobian bounds are overly conservative, suppressing sensitivity in all directions and inducing a large Price of Robustness. We introduce Adversarially-Aligned Jacobian Regularization (AAJR), a trajectory-aligned approach that controls sensitivity strictly along adversarial ascent directions. We prove that AAJR yields a strictly larger admissible policy class than global constraints under mild conditions, implying a weakly smaller approximation gap and reduced nominal performance degradation. Furthermore, we derive step-size conditions under which AAJR controls effective smoothness along optimization trajectories and ensures inner-loop stability. These results provide a structural theory for agentic robustness that decouples minimax stability from global expressivity restrictions.

AI.tldr: AAJR improves robustness in minimax training for autonomous agents by aligning Jacobian regularization with adversarial directions, enabling a larger policy class and stable inner optimization without global Jacobian bounds.
AI.motivation: To overcome instability and excessive conservatism of global Jacobian bounds in minimax training for highly non-linear policies, aiming to maintain robustness with less performance degradation.
AI.method: Propose Adversarially-Aligned Jacobian Regularization (AAJR), a trajectory-aligned regularization that constrains sensitivity only along adversarial ascent directions. Provide theoretical analysis showing AAJR expands the admissible policy class relative to global bounds, reduces the approximation gap, and derives step-size conditions ensuring effective smoothness and inner-loop stability.
AI.result: Theoretical results show AAJR yields a strictly larger admissible policy class under mild conditions, implying a weaker approximation gap and reduced nominal performance degradation. Step-size conditions are derived to ensure trajectory-aligned smoothness and inner-loop stability, offering a structural theory separating minimax stability from global expressivity constraints.
AI.conclusion: AAJR provides a structural framework for agentic robustness by decoupling minimax stability from global expressivity limits, enabling robust minimax performance without unduly restricting expressivity.

title: A Unified Framework for Joint Detection of Lacunes and Enlarged Perivascular Spaces
summary: Cerebral small vessel disease (CSVD) markers, specifically enlarged perivascular spaces (EPVS) and lacunae, present a unique challenge in medical image analysis due to their radiological mimicry. Standard segmentation networks struggle with feature interference and extreme class imbalance when handling these divergent targets simultaneously. To address these issues, we propose a morphology-decoupled framework where Zero-Initialized Gated Cross-Task Attention exploits dense EPVS context to guide sparse lacune detection. Furthermore, biological and topological consistency are enforced via a mixed-supervision strategy integrating Mutual Exclusion and Centerline Dice losses. Finally, we introduce an Anatomically-Informed Inference Calibration mechanism to dynamically suppress false positives based on tissue semantics. Extensive 5-folds cross-validation on the VALDO 2021 dataset (N=40) demonstrates state-of-the-art performance, notably surpassing task winners in lacunae detection precision (71.1%, p=0.01) and F1-score (62.6%, p=0.03). Furthermore, evaluation on the external EPAD cohort (N=1762) confirms the model's robustness for large-scale population studies. Code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: A morphology-decoupled cross-task framework for simultaneous EPVS and lacunae segmentation in CSVD, using zero-initialized gated cross-task attention, mixed-supervision losses (Mutual Exclusion and Centerline Dice), and anatomy-informed inference calibration; validated with strong results on VALDO 2021 and EPAD datasets; code to be released.
AI.motivation: CSVD features EPVS and lacunae that mimic radiologically and exhibit extreme class imbalance and feature interference when segmented together. Existing networks struggle to disentangle sparse lacunae from dense EPVS contexts, compromising precision and F1.
AI.method: Introduce Zero-Initialized Gated Cross-Task Attention to leverage dense EPVS context for guiding sparse lacune detection. Enforce biological and topological consistency with a mixed-supervision strategy using Mutual Exclusion and Centerline Dice losses. Implement Anatomically-Informed Inference Calibration to suppress false positives based on tissue semantics. Evaluate with 5-fold cross-validation on VALDO 2021 (N=40) and external EPAD cohort (N=1762).
AI.result: Achieved state-of-the-art performance in lacunae detection precision (71.1%, p=0.01) and F1-score (62.6%, p=0.03) on VALDO 2021, surpassing task winners. Demonstrated robustness and generalization on the large EPAD cohort for population-scale studies.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively mitigates morphology-related interference and cross-task conflicts, with mixed supervision enforcing structural/topological consistency and the anatomical calibration reducing false positives, enabling scalable and accurate CSVD lesion quantification across cohorts.

title: Accurate and Efficient Hybrid-Ensemble Atmospheric Data Assimilation in Latent Space with Uncertainty Quantification
summary: Data assimilation (DA) combines model forecasts and observations to estimate the optimal state of the atmosphere with its uncertainty, providing initial conditions for weather prediction and reanalyses for climate research. Yet, existing traditional and machine-learning DA methods struggle to achieve accuracy, efficiency and uncertainty quantification simultaneously. Here, we propose HLOBA (Hybrid-Ensemble Latent Observation-Background Assimilation), a three-dimensional hybrid-ensemble DA method that operates in an atmospheric latent space learned via an autoencoder (AE). HLOBA maps both model forecasts and observations into a shared latent space via the AE encoder and an end-to-end Observation-to-Latent-space mapping network (O2Lnet), respectively, and fuses them through a Bayesian update with weights inferred from time-lagged ensemble forecasts. Both idealized and real-observation experiments demonstrate that HLOBA matches dynamically constrained four-dimensional DA methods in both analysis and forecast skill, while achieving end-to-end inference-level efficiency and theoretical flexibility applies to any forecasting model. Moreover, by exploiting the error decorrelation property of latent variables, HLOBA enables element-wise uncertainty estimates for its latent analysis and propagates them to model space via the decoder. Idealized experiments show that this uncertainty highlights large-error regions and captures their seasonal variability.

AI.tldr: HLOBA is a 3D hybrid-ensemble data assimilation method that operates in a latent space learned by an autoencoder. It maps forecasts and observations into a shared latent representation via an encoder and an Observation-to-Latent-space network, respectively, and fuses them with a Bayesian update whose weights are inferred from time-lagged ensembles. It achieves performance on par with dynamically constrained 4D-DA methods while offering end-to-end inference efficiency and latent-space uncertainty quantification that propagates to model space through the decoder.
AI.motivation: Traditional and machine-learning data assimilation methods struggle to simultaneously achieve accuracy, efficiency, and uncertainty quantification. A latent-space, end-to-end learned DA framework could unite these goals and provide flexible applicability to various forecasting models.
AI.method: Train an autoencoder to learn a latent space of atmospheric states. Use the AE encoder for model forecasts and a dedicated O2Lnet to map observations into the latent space. Perform a Bayesian update in latent space with weights inferred from time-lagged ensemble forecasts, constituting a 3D hybrid-ensemble DA. Leverage the error decorrelation property of latent variables to obtain element-wise latent analysis uncertainties and propagate them to model space via the decoder.
AI.result: In idealized and real-observation experiments, HLOBA matches dynamically constrained four-dimensional DA methods in both analysis and forecast skill, while delivering end-to-end inference efficiency. It also enables latent-space uncertainties that highlight large-error regions and show seasonal variability.
AI.conclusion: HLOBA provides a flexible, end-to-end framework for latent-space data assimilation that can be applied to any forecasting model, offering accurate analysis/forecast skill, efficiency, and meaningful uncertainty quantification that transfers to physical model space.

title: EmbodiedSplat: Online Feed-Forward Semantic 3DGS for Open-Vocabulary 3D Scene Understanding
summary: Understanding a 3D scene immediately with its exploration is essential for embodied tasks, where an agent must construct and comprehend the 3D scene in an online and nearly real-time manner. In this study, we propose EmbodiedSplat, an online feed-forward 3DGS for open-vocabulary scene understanding that enables simultaneous online 3D reconstruction and 3D semantic understanding from the streaming images. Unlike existing open-vocabulary 3DGS methods which are typically restricted to either offline or per-scene optimization setting, our objectives are two-fold: 1) Reconstructs the semantic-embedded 3DGS of the entire scene from over 300 streaming images in an online manner. 2) Highly generalizable to novel scenes with feed-forward design and supports nearly real-time 3D semantic reconstruction when combined with real-time 2D models. To achieve these objectives, we propose an Online Sparse Coefficients Field with a CLIP Global Codebook where it binds the 2D CLIP embeddings to each 3D Gaussian while minimizing memory consumption and preserving the full semantic generalizability of CLIP. Furthermore, we generate 3D geometric-aware CLIP features by aggregating the partial point cloud of 3DGS through 3D U-Net to compensate the 3D geometric prior to 2D-oriented language embeddings. Extensive experiments on diverse indoor datasets, including ScanNet, ScanNet++, and Replica, demonstrate both the effectiveness and efficiency of our method. Check out our project page in https://0nandon.github.io/EmbodiedSplat/.

AI.tldr: Online feed-forward open-vocabulary 3D scene understanding (EmbodiedSplat) reconstructs a semantically embedded 3D scene from streaming images using an Online Sparse Coefficients Field with a CLIP Global Codebook for near real-time, scalable open-vocabulary semantics.
AI.motivation: Embodied agents need online, scalable 3D scene reconstruction and semantic understanding in unknown environments; existing methods often rely on offline or per-scene optimization and struggle to generalize to novel scenes.
AI.method: Introduce Online Sparse Coefficients Field that binds 2D CLIP embeddings to 3D Gaussians via a CLIP Global Codebook, reducing memory and preserving CLIP generalization. Generate 3D geometric-aware CLIP features by aggregating partial point clouds with a 3D U-Net to compensate 3D geometric priors for language embeddings. Operates in an online/streaming setting, assembling semantic-embedded 3DGS from 300+ images, and supports near real-time 3D semantic reconstruction when paired with real-time 2D models.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on ScanNet, ScanNet++, and Replica demonstrate effectiveness and efficiency; method reconstructs semantic-embedded 3DGS online from streaming images, shows strong generalization to novel scenes, and is memory-efficient.
AI.conclusion: EmbodiedSplat provides a practical, online, open-vocabulary 3D scene understanding pipeline that generalizes to new scenes and achieves near real-time semantic reconstruction when combined with fast 2D models.

title: A Hypertoroidal Covering for Perfect Color Equivariance
summary: When the color distribution of input images changes at inference, the performance of conventional neural network architectures drops considerably. A few researchers have begun to incorporate prior knowledge of color geometry in neural network design. These color equivariant architectures have modeled hue variation with 2D rotations, and saturation and luminance transformations as 1D translations. While this approach improves neural network robustness to color variations in a number of contexts, we find that approximating saturation and luminance (interval valued quantities) as 1D translations introduces appreciable artifacts. In this paper, we introduce a color equivariant architecture that is truly equivariant.
