Instead of approximating the interval with the real line, we lift values on the interval to values on the circle (a double-cover) and build equivariant representations there. Our approach resolves the approximation artifacts of previous methods, improves interpretability and generalizability, and achieves better predictive performance than conventional and equivariant baselines on tasks such as fine-grained classification and medical imaging tasks. Going beyond the context of color, we show that our proposed lifting can also extend to geometric transformations such as scale.

AI.tldr: Color-equivariant architecture that lifts saturation and luminance from an interval to a circle (double-cover) to achieve true equivariance under color transformations, avoiding artifacts of 1D translations; improves robustness, interpretability, and generalization; extends to scale and other interval-valued quantities.
AI.motivation: Existing color-equivariant networks model hue via 2D rotations and satur/ luminance via 1D translations, but treating interval-valued satur/ luminance as real-line translations introduces artifacts and imperfect equivariance. A true, mathematically consistent equivariant design is needed to better handle color distribution shifts.
AI.method: Propose lifting interval-valued saturation and luminance to circle (double-cover) and build equivariant representations there, yielding a color-equivariant architecture that reduces approximation artifacts. Demonstrate improvements in interpretability, generalization, and predictive performance on tasks like fine-grained classification and medical imaging; show extension of the lifting concept to geometric transformations such as scale.
AI.result: Outperforms conventional and prior equivariant baselines on targeted tasks; reduces artifacts associated with interval-to-real approximations; enhances interpretability and generalization; demonstrates practical gains in fine-grained classification and medical imaging; scalable to scale transformations.
AI.conclusion: Lifting interval-valued quantities to a circular domain yields true color equivariance, resolves approximation artifacts, and generalizes to other geometric transformations such as scale. The approach strengthens robustness and broadens applicability of equivariant architectures beyond color.

title: Geographically-Weighted Weakly Supervised Bayesian High-Resolution Transformer for 200m Resolution Pan-Arctic Sea Ice Concentration Mapping and Uncertainty Estimation using Sentinel-1, RCM, and AMSR2 Data
summary: Although high-resolution mapping of pan-Arctic sea ice with reliable corresponding uncertainty is essential for operational sea ice concentration (SIC) charting, it is a difficult task due to key challenges, such as the subtle nature of ice signature features, inexact SIC labels, model uncertainty, and data heterogeneity. This study presents a novel Bayesian High-Resolution Transformer approach for 200 meter resolution pan-Arctic SIC mapping and uncertainty quantification using Sentinel-1, RADARSAT Constellation Mission (RCM), and Advanced Microwave Scanning Radiometer 2 (AMSR2) data. First, to improve small and subtle sea ice feature (e.g., cracks/leads, ponds, and ice floes) extraction, we design a novel high-resolution Transformer model with both global and local modules that can better discern the subtle differences in sea ice patterns. Second, to address low-resolution and inexact SIC labels, we design a geographically-weighted weakly supervised loss function to supervise the model at region level instead of pixel level, and to prioritize pure open water and ice pack signatures while mitigating the impact of ambiguity in the marginal ice zone (MIZ). Third, to improve uncertainty quantification, we design a Bayesian extension of the proposed Transformer model, treating its parameters as random variables to more effectively capture uncertainties. Fourth, to address data heterogeneity, we fuse three different data types (Sentinel-1, RCM, and AMSR2) at decision-level to improve both SIC mapping and uncertainty quantification. The proposed approach is evaluated under pan-Arctic minimum-extent conditions in 2021 and 2025. Results demonstrate that the proposed model achieves 0.70 overall feature detection accuracy using Sentinel-1 data, while also preserving pan-Arctic SIC patterns (Sentinel-1 R\textsuperscript{2} = 0.90 relative to the ARTIST Sea Ice product).

AI.tldr: A Bayesian High-Resolution Transformer framework for 200 m pan-Arctic sea-ice concentration (SIC) mapping with uncertainty, leveraging multi-sensor data (Sentinel-1, RCM, AMSR2), weakly supervised region-level labels, and decision-level fusion to handle data heterogeneity and label inexactness; achieves 0.70 feature detection accuracy and 0.90 R^2 against ARTIST under 2021/2025 minimal-extent conditions.
AI.motivation: Reliable high-resolution SIC maps with quantified uncertainty are essential for operational Arctic navigation and climate monitoring, but are hindered by subtle ice signatures, noisy/ambiguous labels, model/ Data uncertainty, and heterogeneous data sources.
AI.method: (1) Develop a high-resolution Transformer with global and local modules to better extract subtle sea-ice features (cracks/leads/ponds/ice floes). (2) Propose a geographically-weighted weakly supervised loss operating at region level to reduce label noise and emphasize pure open-water vs ice-pack signatures, mitigating marginal ice zone ambiguity. (3) Extend the model Bayesianly by treating parameters as random variables to enhance uncertainty quantification. (4) Fuse Sentinel-1, RCM, and AMSR2 data at decision level to address data heterogeneity. (5) Evaluate under pan-Arctic minimum-extent conditions in 2021 and 2025.
AI.result: The approach yields 0.70 overall feature-detection accuracy with Sentinel-1 data and preserves pan-Arctic SIC patterns (Sentinel-1 R^2 = 0.90 relative to ARTIST Sea Ice product).
AI.conclusion: A unified Bayesian high-resolution Transformer framework can achieve accurate 200 m SIC mapping with robust uncertainty quantification while effectively handling label noise and multi-sensor data heterogeneity, demonstrated under challenging minimum-extent Arctic conditions.

title: ViterbiPlanNet: Injecting Procedural Knowledge via Differentiable Viterbi for Planning in Instructional Videos
summary: Procedural planning aims to predict a sequence of actions that transforms an initial visual state into a desired goal, a fundamental ability for intelligent agents operating in complex environments. Existing approaches typically rely on large-scale models that learn procedural structures implicitly, resulting in limited sample-efficiency and high computational cost. In this work we introduce ViterbiPlanNet, a principled framework that explicitly integrates procedural knowledge into the learning process through a Differentiable Viterbi Layer (DVL). The DVL embeds a Procedural Knowledge Graph (PKG) directly with the Viterbi decoding algorithm, replacing non-differentiable operations with smooth relaxations that enable end-to-end optimization. This design allows the model to learn through graph-based decoding. Experiments on CrossTask, COIN, and NIV demonstrate that ViterbiPlanNet achieves state-of-the-art performance with an order of magnitude fewer parameters than diffusion- and LLM-based planners. Extensive ablations show that performance gains arise from our differentiable structure-aware training rather than post-hoc refinement, resulting in improved sample efficiency and robustness to shorter unseen horizons. We also address testing inconsistencies establishing a unified testing protocol with consistent splits and evaluation metrics. With this new protocol, we run experiments multiple times and report results using bootstrapping to assess statistical significance.

AI.tldr: ViterbiPlanNet uses a Differentiable Viterbi Layer to integrate a Procedural Knowledge Graph into planning, enabling end-to-end, structure-aware learning with strong performance and high sample efficiency using far fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: Procedural planning currently relies on large-scale models that implicitly learn procedural structures, which hurts sample efficiency and incurs high computational costs; there is a need for explicit, trainable structure-aware methods and standardized evaluation.
AI.method: Introduce a Differentiable Viterbi Layer (DVL) that embeds a Procedural Knowledge Graph (PKG) with Viterbi decoding, replacing non-differentiable steps with smooth relaxations for end-to-end optimization; enables graph-based decoding within learning; validate under a unified testing protocol.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on CrossTask, COIN, and NIV with an order of magnitude fewer parameters than diffusion- and LLM-based planners; ablations show gains derive from differentiable, structure-aware training rather than post-hoc refinement; improved sample efficiency and robustness to shorter unseen horizons; uses bootstrapping for statistical significance under a unified protocol.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the value of explicitly incorporating procedural knowledge via differentiable, graph-based decoding for efficient, robust procedural planning; supports adopting a standardized, bootstrapped evaluation protocol for fair comparisons.

title: Real-time loosely coupled GNSS and IMU integration via Factor Graph Optimization
summary: Accurate positioning, navigation, and timing (PNT) is fundamental to the operation of modern technologies and a key enabler of autonomous systems. A very important component of PNT is the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) which ensures outdoor positioning. Modern research directions have pushed the performance of GNSS localization to new heights by fusing GNSS measurements with other sensory information, mainly measurements from Inertial Measurement Units (IMU). In this paper, we propose a loosely coupled architecture to integrate GNSS and IMU measurements using a Factor Graph Optimization (FGO) framework. Because the FGO method can be computationally challenging and often used as a post-processing method, our focus is on assessing its localization accuracy and service availability while operating in real-time in challenging environments (urban canyons). Experimental results on the UrbanNav-HK-MediumUrban-1 dataset show that the proposed approach achieves real-time operation and increased service availability compared to batch FGO methods. While this improvement comes at the cost of reduced positioning accuracy, the paper provides a detailed analysis of the trade-offs between accuracy, availability, and computational efficiency that characterize real-time FGO-based GNSS/IMU fusion.

AI.tldr: Loosely coupled GNSS/IMU fusion via Factor Graph Optimization enables real-time operation in urban environments, trading some positioning accuracy for increased service availability, with a comparative analysis against batch FGO methods.
AI.motivation: To enable real-time GNSS/IMU fusion in challenging urban canyons by balancing localization accuracy, service availability, and computational efficiency, addressing the heavy computational load of batch FGO.
AI.method: Implement a loosely coupled GNSS/IMU fusion within a Factor Graph Optimization (FGO) framework designed for real-time operation. Evaluate on the UrbanNav-HK-MediumUrban-1 dataset and compare against batch FGO methods focusing on accuracy, availability, and computation.
AI.result: The approach achieves real-time operation and higher service availability than batch FGO, albeit with reduced positioning accuracy. The work includes a detailed analysis of the trade-offs among accuracy, availability, and computational efficiency in real-time FGO-based GNSS/IMU fusion.
AI.conclusion: Real-time FGO-based GNSS/IMU fusion is feasible in urban environments, offering improved service availability at the cost of some accuracy. The results inform design choices for real-time GNSS/IMU systems and guide future optimizations.

title: CubeComposer: Spatio-Temporal Autoregressive 4K 360° Video Generation from Perspective Video
summary: Generating high-quality 360° panoramic videos from perspective input is one of the crucial applications for virtual reality (VR), whereby high-resolution videos are especially important for immersive experience. Existing methods are constrained by computational limitations of vanilla diffusion models, only supporting $\leq$ 1K resolution native generation and relying on suboptimal post super-resolution to increase resolution. We introduce CubeComposer, a novel spatio-temporal autoregressive diffusion model that natively generates 4K-resolution 360° videos. By decomposing videos into cubemap representations with six faces, CubeComposer autoregressively synthesizes content in a well-planned spatio-temporal order, reducing memory demands while enabling high-resolution output. Specifically, to address challenges in multi-dimensional autoregression, we propose: (1) a spatio-temporal autoregressive strategy that orchestrates 360° video generation across cube faces and time windows for coherent synthesis; (2) a cube face context management mechanism, equipped with a sparse context attention design to improve efficiency; and (3) continuity-aware techniques, including cube-aware positional encoding, padding, and blending to eliminate boundary seams. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that CubeComposer outperforms state-of-the-art methods in native resolution and visual quality, supporting practical VR application scenarios. Project page: https://lg-li.github.io/project/cubecomposer

AI.tldr: CubeComposer enables native 4K 360° video generation via a cubemap-based spatio-temporal autoregressive diffusion model, featuring sparse context attention and seam-free continuity techniques, outperforming prior methods.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models are limited by compute/memory, restricting native resolutions to ~1K for 360° videos and forcing suboptimal post-upscaling. A native 4K solution is needed for high-quality VR experiences with feasible resources.
AI.method: Decompose 360° video into six cubemap faces. Employ a spatio-temporal autoregressive diffusion process that synthesizes across cube faces and time windows. Use a cube face context management with sparse context attention for efficiency. Implement continuity-aware techniques (cube-aware positional encoding, padding, blending) to remove boundary seams.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets show CubeComposer achieves native 4K resolution with superior visual quality, outperforming state-of-the-art methods and enabling practical VR applications.
AI.conclusion: CubeComposer demonstrates that native high-resolution 360° video generation is feasible with a cubemap-based autoregressive diffusion framework, delivering coherent, high-quality outputs suitable for VR.

title: SSR: A Generic Framework for Text-Aided Map Compression for Localization
summary: Mapping is crucial in robotics for localization and downstream decision-making. As robots are deployed in ever-broader settings, the maps they rely on continue to increase in size. However, storing these maps indefinitely (cold storage), transferring them across networks, or sending localization queries to cloud-hosted maps imposes prohibitive memory and bandwidth costs. We propose a text-enhanced compression framework that reduces both memory and bandwidth footprints while retaining high-fidelity localization. The key idea is to treat text as an alternative modality: one that can be losslessly compressed with large language models. We propose leveraging lightweight text descriptions combined with very small image feature vectors, which capture "complementary information" as a compact representation for the mapping task. Building on this, our novel technique, Similarity Space Replication (SSR), learns an adaptive image embedding in one shot that captures only the information "complementary" to the text descriptions. We validate our compression framework on multiple downstream localization tasks, including Visual Place Recognition as well as object-centric Monte Carlo localization in both indoor and outdoor settings. SSR achieves 2 times better compression than competing baselines on state-of-the-art datasets, including TokyoVal, Pittsburgh30k, Replica, and KITTI.

AI.tldr: A text-enhanced compression framework for robotic mapping using text descriptions plus compact image features, with a novel Similarity Space Replication (SSR) that learns an adaptive embedding to capture information complementary to text, achieving ~2x compression while preserving localization accuracy on indoor/outdoor benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Reduce memory footprint and bandwidth for large-scale robotic maps in cloud/offline storage settings, by leveraging text as a losslessly compressible modality and combining it with compact image features to retain localization fidelity.
AI.method: Introduce a framework that treats text descriptions as an alternative modality and uses small image feature vectors to capture complementary information. Propose Similarity Space Replication (SSR), a one-shot adaptive image embedding that encodes information complementary to the text descriptions. Validate on Visual Place Recognition and object-centric Monte Carlo localization across indoor/outdoor datasets.
AI.result: SSR achieves about 2x better compression than competitive baselines on TokyoVal, Pittsburgh30k, Replica, and KITTI, while maintaining high localization performance across tasks.
AI.conclusion: Text-driven compression with SSR can substantially reduce memory and bandwidth requirements for mapping in robotics without sacrificing localization accuracy, enabling scalable deployment; potential extensions include richer text prompts and further refinement of cross-modal embeddings.

title: Real-time tightly coupled GNSS and IMU integration via Factor Graph Optimization
summary: Reliable positioning in dense urban environments remains challenging due to frequent GNSS signal blockage, multipath, and rapidly varying satellite geometry. While factor graph optimization (FGO)-based GNSS-IMU fusion has demonstrated strong robustness and accuracy, most formulations remain offline. In this work, we present a real-time tightly coupled GNSS-IMU FGO method that enables causal state estimation via incremental optimization with fixed-lag marginalization, and we evaluate its performance in a highly urbanized GNSS-degraded environment using the UrbanNav dataset.

AI.tldr: Real-time, tightly coupled GNSS-IMU fusion via incremental FGO with fixed-lag marginalization for urban GNSS-degraded environments; tested on UrbanNav.
AI.motivation: Urban canyons cause GNSS outages, multipath, and changing satellite geometry; offline FGO methods lack real-time capability; need causal, real-time estimation in dense cities.
AI.method: Incremental factor-graph optimization with fixed-lag marginalization enabling causal, real-time GNSS-IMU fusion; tightly couples GNSS and IMU measurements in a real-time framework.
AI.result: Evaluation conducted on the UrbanNav dataset in a GNSS-degraded urban environment; demonstrates feasibility of real-time causal estimation and robustness in challenging settings.
AI.conclusion: Presents a real-time, causal GNSS-IMU fusion framework via incremental FGO with fixed-lag marginalization, validated on UrbanNav; suitable for dense urban positioning where GNSS is unreliable.

title: A multi-center analysis of deep learning methods for video polyp detection and segmentation
summary: Colonic polyps are well-recognized precursors to colorectal cancer (CRC), typically detected during colonoscopy. However, the variability in appearance, location, and size of these polyps complicates their detection and removal, leading to challenges in effective surveillance, intervention, and subsequently CRC prevention. The processes of colonoscopy surveillance and polyp removal are highly reliant on the expertise of gastroenterologists and occur within the complexities of the colonic structure. As a result, there is a high rate of missed detections and incomplete removal of colonic polyps, which can adversely impact patient outcomes. Recently, automated methods that use machine learning have been developed to enhance polyps detection and segmentation, thus helping clinical processes and reducing missed rates. These advancements highlight the potential for improving diagnostic accuracy in real-time applications, which ultimately facilitates more effective patient management. Furthermore, integrating sequence data and temporal information could significantly enhance the precision of these methods by capturing the dynamic nature of polyp growth and the changes that occur over time. To rigorously investigate these challenges, data scientists and experts gastroenterologists collaborated to compile a comprehensive dataset that spans multiple centers and diverse populations. This initiative aims to underscore the critical importance of incorporating sequence data and temporal information in the development of robust automated detection and segmentation methods. This study evaluates the applicability of deep learning techniques developed in real-time clinical colonoscopy tasks using sequence data, highlighting the critical role of temporal relationships between frames in improving diagnostic precision.

AI.tldr: Real-time deep learning for polyp detection/segmentation in colonoscopy using temporal/sequence data to exploit frame-to-frame dynamics across a multicenter dataset.
AI.motivation: Reduce missed polyps and incomplete removals by leveraging temporal information and heterogeneous polyp appearances across diverse populations.
AI.method: Evaluation of deep learning models designed for real-time colonoscopy tasks, incorporating sequence/temporal data to capture frame-to-frame relationships; multicenter dataset collected by gastroenterologists and data scientists.
AI.result: Temporal relationships between frames improve diagnostic precision in real-time detection/segmentation of colonic polyps.
AI.conclusion: Temporal information is essential for robust automated colonoscopy analysis, supporting improved surveillance and CRC prevention; dataset diversity across centers enhances generalizability.

title: Gaussian Wardrobe: Compositional 3D Gaussian Avatars for Free-Form Virtual Try-On
summary: We introduce Gaussian Wardrobe, a novel framework to digitalize compositional 3D neural avatars from multi-view videos. Existing methods for 3D neural avatars typically treat the human body and clothing as an inseparable entity. However, this paradigm fails to capture the dynamics of complex free-form garments and limits the reuse of clothing across different individuals. To overcome these problems, we develop a novel, compositional 3D Gaussian representation to build avatars from multiple layers of free-form garments. The core of our method is decomposing neural avatars into bodies and layers of shape-agnostic neural garments. To achieve this, our framework learns to disentangle each garment layer from multi-view videos and canonicalizes it into a shape-independent space. In experiments, our method models photorealistic avatars with high-fidelity dynamics, achieving new state-of-the-art performance on novel pose synthesis benchmarks. In addition, we demonstrate that the learned compositional garments contribute to a versatile digital wardrobe, enabling a practical virtual try-on application where clothing can be freely transferred to new subjects. Project page: https://ait.ethz.ch/gaussianwardrobe

AI.tldr: A compositional 3D Gaussian framework (Gaussian Wardrobe) models avatars as body plus multiple layers of shape-agnostic garments, enabling reusable clothing and state-of-the-art novel pose synthesis from multi-view videos.
AI.motivation: Current 3D neural avatar methods treat body and clothing as inseparable, hindering realistic garment dynamics and cross-subject garment reuse.
AI.method: Proposes a compositional 3D Gaussian representation, disentangling each garment layer from multi-view videos and canonicalizing them into a shape-independent space, enabling layering of free-form garments on a separable body.
AI.result: Photorealistic avatars with high-fidelity dynamics achieving state-of-the-art performance on novel pose synthesis benchmarks; demonstrates a versatile digital wardrobe with transferable clothing for virtual try-on.
AI.conclusion: A modular avatar framework that decouples bodies and garments, enabling garment reuse across subjects and practical clothing transfer, with strong performance in pose-generalization tasks.

title: MEM: Multi-Scale Embodied Memory for Vision Language Action Models
summary: Conventionally, memory in end-to-end robotic learning involves inputting a sequence of past observations into the learned policy. However, in complex multi-stage real-world tasks, the robot's memory must represent past events at multiple levels of granularity: from long-term memory that captures abstracted semantic concepts (e.g., a robot cooking dinner should remember which stages of the recipe are already done) to short-term memory that captures recent events and compensates for occlusions (e.g., a robot remembering the object it wants to pick up once its arm occludes it). In this work, our main insight is that an effective memory architecture for long-horizon robotic control should combine multiple modalities to capture these different levels of abstraction. We introduce Multi-Scale Embodied Memory (MEM), an approach for mixed-modal long-horizon memory in robot policies. MEM combines video-based short-horizon memory, compressed via a video encoder, with text-based long-horizon memory. Together, they enable robot policies to perform tasks that span up to fifteen minutes, like cleaning up a kitchen, or preparing a grilled cheese sandwich. Additionally, we find that memory enables MEM policies to intelligently adapt manipulation strategies in-context.

AI.tldr: Proposes Multi-Scale Embodied Memory (MEM) that fuses video-based short-term memory with text-based long-term memory to support long-horizon robotic control (up to ~15 minutes) and adaptive manipulation strategies.
AI.motivation: Robots in real-world tasks require memory across multiple timescales and modalities; current approaches struggle with long-horizon planning and occlusions, limiting robustness in multi-stage tasks.
AI.method: MEM architecture combining a video encoder-based short-horizon memory with a text-based long-horizon memory; integrated into end-to-end policies to handle tasks like cooking and cleaning; memory enables adaptation in-context and improves stratagem selection.
AI.result: Demonstrates capability to perform tasks spanning up to 15 minutes; memory contributes to in-context adaptation and more robust manipulation strategies; the abstract does not provide quantitative metrics.
AI.conclusion: Multi-modal long-horizon memory is effective for complex, multi-stage robotic tasks; integrating video-based short-term memory with text-based long-term memory enhances abstraction across scales and improves policy robustness.

title: MOO: A Multi-view Oriented Observations Dataset for Viewpoint Analysis in Cattle Re-Identification
summary: Animal re-identification (ReID) faces critical challenges due to viewpoint variations, particularly in Aerial-Ground (AG-ReID) settings where models must match individuals across drastic elevation changes. However, existing datasets lack the precise angular annotations required to systematically analyze these geometric variations. To address this, we introduce the Multi-view Oriented Observation (MOO) dataset, a large-scale synthetic AG-ReID dataset of $1,000$ cattle individuals captured from $128$ uniformly sampled viewpoints ($128,000$ annotated images). Using this controlled dataset, we quantify the influence of elevation and identify a critical elevation threshold, above which models generalize significantly better to unseen views. Finally, we validate the transferability to real-world applications in both zero-shot and supervised settings, demonstrating performance gains across four real-world cattle datasets and confirming that synthetic geometric priors effectively bridge the domain gap. Collectively, this dataset and analysis lay the foundation for future model development in cross-view animal ReID. MOO is publicly available at https://github.com/TurtleSmoke/MOO.

AI.tldr: A synthetic multi-view AG-ReID dataset for cattle (MOO) with 1,000 identities and 128 viewpoints (128k images) is introduced to study elevation effects on cross-view re-identification. It identifies a critical elevation threshold where generalization to unseen views improves and demonstrates transferability to four real cattle datasets in zero-shot and supervised settings, indicating synthetic geometric priors reduce the domain gap. The dataset is publicly available.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of precise angular annotations and the need to systematically analyze geometric variations (elevation) in aerial-ground cattle ReID, enabling controlled experiments and better understanding of cross-view generalization.
AI.method: Construct a large-scale synthetic AG-ReID dataset (MOO) with 1,000 cattle identities captured from 128 uniformly sampled viewpoints (128k annotated images). Quantify the influence of elevation on cross-view ReID performance, identify a critical elevation threshold where generalization to unseen views improves, and validate transferability to real-world cattle datasets in zero-shot and supervised settings.
AI.result: Elevation has a measurable influence on cross-view ReID; a critical elevation threshold exists beyond which models generalize significantly better to unseen views. In real-world validation, models trained or tested with the synthetic geometric priors show performance gains across four cattle datasets, in both zero-shot and supervised scenarios, indicating effective domain gap bridging.
AI.conclusion: The MOO dataset and the accompanying analysis provide a foundation for developing cross-view animal ReID methods and demonstrate that synthetic geometric priors can improve real-world performance. The dataset is publicly available for further research.

title: Machine Pareidolia: Protecting Facial Image with Emotional Editing
comment: Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence 40
summary: The proliferation of facial recognition (FR) systems has raised privacy concerns in the digital realm, as malicious uses of FR models pose a significant threat. Traditional countermeasures, such as makeup style transfer, have suffered from low transferability in black-box settings and limited applicability across various demographic groups, including males and individuals with darker skin tones. To address these challenges, we introduce a novel facial privacy protection method, dubbed \textbf{MAP}, a pioneering approach that employs human emotion modifications to disguise original identities as target identities in facial images. Our method uniquely fine-tunes a score network to learn dual objectives, target identity and human expression, which are jointly optimized through gradient projection to ensure convergence at a shared local optimum. Additionally, we enhance the perceptual quality of protected images by applying local smoothness regularization and optimizing the score matching loss within our network. Empirical experiments demonstrate that our innovative approach surpasses previous baselines, including noise-based, makeup-based, and freeform attribute methods, in both qualitative fidelity and quantitative metrics. Furthermore, MAP proves its effectiveness against an online FR API and shows advanced adaptability in uncommon photographic scenarios.

AI.tldr: MAP is a facial privacy method that disguises identities by modifying human expressions to target identities, trained with dual objectives (target identity and expression) via gradient projection, with perceptual quality aided by local smoothness regularization; it outperforms prior baselines and resists online FR API in diverse scenarios.
AI.motivation: Address privacy risks from facial recognition and limitations of prior defenses (low transferability in black-box settings, poor demographic coverage, limited applicability across male and darker-skinned individuals).
AI.method: Fine-tune a score network to jointly optimize target identity and human expression. Use gradient projection to converge to a shared local optimum. Improve perceptual quality with local smoothness regularization and by optimizing the score-matching loss within the network.
AI.result: Outperforms noise-, makeup-, and freeform-attribute baselines in qualitative fidelity and quantitative metrics. Effective against an online FR API and adaptable to uncommon photographic scenarios.
AI.conclusion: MAP provides robust facial privacy protection by leveraging expression-based identity disguise, offering strong transferability across black-box FR systems and diverse demographics while maintaining image quality.

title: SPRINT: Semi-supervised Prototypical Representation for Few-Shot Class-Incremental Tabular Learning
summary: Real-world systems must continuously adapt to novel concepts from limited data without forgetting previously acquired knowledge. While Few-Shot Class-Incremental Learning (FSCIL) is established in computer vision, its application to tabular domains remains largely unexplored. Unlike images, tabular streams (e.g., logs, sensors) offer abundant unlabeled data, a scarcity of expert annotations and negligible storage costs, features ignored by existing vision-based methods that rely on restrictive buffers. We introduce SPRINT, the first FSCIL framework tailored for tabular distributions. SPRINT introduces a mixed episodic training strategy that leverages confidence-based pseudo-labeling to enrich novel class representations and exploits low storage costs to retain base class history. Extensive evaluation across six diverse benchmarks spanning cybersecurity, healthcare, and ecological domains, demonstrates SPRINT's cross-domain robustness. It achieves a state-of-the-art average accuracy of 77.37% (5-shot), outperforming the strongest incremental baseline by 4.45%.

AI.tldr: SPRINT is a FSCIL framework for tabular data that uses mixed episodic training and confidence-based pseudo-labeling to learn novel classes from limited data while retaining base-class knowledge, achieving state-of-the-art 5-shot accuracy across diverse benchmarks.
AI.motivation: There is a gap in Few-Shot Class-Incremental Learning (FSCIL) for tabular data. Unlike images, tabular streams have abundant unlabeled data, few expert labels, and low storage costs, yet current vision-oriented FSCIL methods rely on restrictive memory buffers and do not exploit unlabeled data inherent in tabular streams.
AI.method: SPRINT employs a mixed episodic training strategy to simulate FSCIL scenarios and uses confidence-based pseudo-labeling to expand novel class representations. It also exploits low storage costs to retain base-class history, mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across six benchmarks in cybersecurity, healthcare, and ecological domains demonstrates cross-domain robustness. SPRINT achieves a 5-shot average accuracy of 77.37%, outperforming the strongest incremental baseline by 4.45%.
AI.conclusion: SPRINT successfully extends FSCIL to tabular distributions, enabling robust, near real-time adaptation to new concepts in tabular streams while maintaining knowledge of base classes, and showing strong cross-domain performance.

title: Motion Manipulation via Unsupervised Keypoint Positioning in Face Animation
summary: Face animation deals with controlling and generating facial features with a wide range of applications. The methods based on unsupervised keypoint positioning can produce realistic and detailed virtual portraits. However, they cannot achieve controllable face generation since the existing keypoint decomposition pipelines fail to fully decouple identity semantics and intertwined motion information (e.g., rotation, translation, and expression). To address these issues, we present a new method, Motion Manipulation via unsupervised keypoint positioning in Face Animation (MMFA). We first introduce self-supervised representation learning to encode and decode expressions in the latent feature space and decouple them from other motion information. Secondly, we propose a new way to compute keypoints aiming to achieve arbitrary motion control. Moreover, we design a variational autoencoder to map expression features to a continuous Gaussian distribution, allowing us for the first time to interpolate facial expressions in an unsupervised framework. We have conducted extensive experiments on publicly available datasets to validate the effectiveness of MMFA, which show that MMFA offers pronounced advantages over prior arts in creating realistic animation and manipulating face motion.

AI.tldr: MMFA introduces unsupervised, disentangled facial motion control by learning self-supervised representations of expressions, a novel keypoint computation for arbitrary motion, and a variational autoencoder to interpolate expressions, achieving controllable and realistic face animation with improved motion separation.
AI.motivation: Current unsupervised keypoint-based face animation struggles to separate identity from motion (rotation, translation, expression), limiting controllability and expression interpolation.
AI.method: 1) Self-supervised representation learning to encode/decode expressions in latent space and decouple from other motion; 2) New keypoint computation to enable arbitrary motion control; 3) Variational autoencoder mapping expression features to a Gaussian distribution to allow unsupervised expression interpolation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on public datasets show MMFA outperforms prior methods in realism and ability to manipulate facial motion.
AI.conclusion: MMFA enables controllable, realistic face animation with unsupervised expression manipulation and interpolation, validated by comprehensive experiments.

title: Dual Diffusion Models for Multi-modal Guided 3D Avatar Generation
summary: Generating high-fidelity 3D avatars from text or image prompts is highly sought after in virtual reality and human-computer interaction. However, existing text-driven methods often rely on iterative Score Distillation Sampling (SDS) or CLIP optimization, which struggle with fine-grained semantic control and suffer from excessively slow inference. Meanwhile, image-driven approaches are severely bottlenecked by the scarcity and high acquisition cost of high-quality 3D facial scans, limiting model generalization. To address these challenges, we first construct a novel, large-scale dataset comprising over 100,000 pairs across four modalities: fine-grained textual descriptions, in-the-wild face images, high-quality light-normalized texture UV maps, and 3D geometric shapes. Leveraging this comprehensive dataset, we propose PromptAvatar, a framework featuring dual diffusion models. Specifically, it integrates a Texture Diffusion Model (TDM) that supports flexible multi-condition guidance from text and/or image prompts, alongside a Geometry Diffusion Model (GDM) guided by text prompts. By learning the direct mapping from multi-modal prompts to 3D representations, PromptAvatar eliminates the need for time-consuming iterative optimization, successfully generating high-fidelity, shading-free 3D avatars in under 10 seconds. Extensive quantitative and qualitative experiments demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches in generation quality, fine-grained detail alignment, and computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: PromptAvatar uses dual diffusion models to map multi-modal prompts to 3D avatars, achieving fast, high-fidelity generation without iterative optimization.
AI.motivation: Address slow, optimization-heavy text-driven methods (SDS/CLIP) and limited generalization due to scarce 3D facial data in image-driven approaches.
AI.method: Two diffusion models: Texture Diffusion Model (TDM) guided by text and image prompts to produce shading-free UV textures, and Geometry Diffusion Model (GDM) guided by text to produce 3D geometry; trained on a large, multi-modal dataset (>100k pairs) with textual descriptions, in-the-wild face images, light-normalized UV maps, and 3D shapes; learns direct prompt-to-3D mapping to bypass optimization.
AI.result: Outperforms prior methods in quality, fine-grained detail alignment, and speed, delivering high-fidelity avatars with shading-free textures in under 10 seconds.
AI.conclusion: PromptAvatar demonstrates the viability of multi-modal prompt-conditioned diffusion for direct 3D avatar generation, offering fast inference and improved control; the large dataset and dual-model design underpin strong performance.

title: RANGER: Sparsely-Gated Mixture-of-Experts with Adaptive Retrieval Re-ranking for Pathology Report Generation
summary: Pathology report generation remains a relatively under-explored downstream task, primarily due to the gigapixel scale and complex morphological heterogeneity of Whole Slide Images (WSIs). Existing pathology report generation frameworks typically employ transformer architectures, relying on a homogeneous decoder architecture and static knowledge retrieval integration. Such architectures limit generative specialization and may introduce noisy external guidance during the report generation process. To address these limitations, we propose RANGER, a sparsely-gated Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) framework with adaptive retrieval re-ranking for pathology report generation. Specifically, we integrate a sparsely gated MoE into the decoder, along with noisy top-$k$ routing and load-balancing regularization, to enable dynamic expert specialization across various diagnostic patterns. Additionally, we introduce an adaptive retrieval re-ranking module that selectively refines retrieved memory from a knowledge base before integration, reducing noise and improving semantic alignment based on visual feature representations. We perform extensive experiments on the PathText-BRCA dataset and demonstrate consistent improvements over existing approaches across standard natural language generation metrics. Our full RANGER model achieves optimal performance on PathText dataset, reaching BLEU-1 to BLEU-4 scores of 0.4598, 0.3044, 0.2036, and 0.1435, respectively, with METEOR of 0.1883, and ROUGE-L of 0.3038, validating the effectiveness of dynamic expert routing and adaptive knowledge refinement for semantically grounded pathology report generation.

AI.tldr: RANGER introduces sparsely gated Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) in the decoder with adaptive retrieval re-ranking to improve pathology report generation from gigapixel WSIs, addressing static decoders and noisy external guidance; achieves strong NLG metrics on PathText-BRCA.
AI.motivation: Pathology report generation from whole slide images (WSIs) is challenged by gigapixel scale and morphological heterogeneity. Existing transformer-based pipelines use homogeneous decoders and static knowledge retrieval, limiting specialization and introducing noisy guidance.
AI.method: Incorporate a sparsely gated Mixture-of-Experts into the decoder with noisy top-k routing and load-balancing regularization to enable dynamic expert specialization. Add an adaptive retrieval re-ranking module to refine retrieved memory from a knowledge base based on visual features before integration.
AI.result: On PathText-BRCA, RANGER yields consistent improvements over baselines on standard NLG metrics. Full model achieves BLEU-1/2/3/4 = 0.4598, 0.3044, 0.2036, 0.1435; METEOR = 0.1883; ROUGE-L = 0.3038, demonstrating effectiveness of dynamic routing and adaptive knowledge refinement.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic expert routing coupled with adaptive knowledge refinement enhances semantically grounded pathology report generation, with state-of-the-art performance on PathText, validating the MoE + re-ranking approach.

title: Scalable Evaluation of the Realism of Synthetic Environmental Augmentations in Images
summary:
Evaluation of AI systems often requires synthetic test cases, particularly for rare or safety-critical conditions that are difficult to observe in operational data. Generative AI offers a promising approach for producing such data through controllable image editing, but its usefulness depends on whether the resulting images are sufficiently realistic to support meaningful evaluation.
  We present a scalable framework for assessing the realism of synthetic image-editing methods and apply it to the task of adding environmental conditions-fog, rain, snow, and nighttime-to car-mounted camera images. Using 40 clear-day images, we compare rule-based augmentation libraries with generative AI image-editing models. Realism is evaluated using two complementary automated metrics: a vision-language model (VLM) jury for perceptual realism assessment, and embedding-based distributional analysis to measure similarity to genuine adverse-condition imagery.
  Generative AI methods substantially outperform rule-based approaches, with the best generative method achieving approximately 3.6 times the acceptance rate of the best rule-based method. Performance varies across conditions: fog proves easiest to simulate, while nighttime transformations remain challenging. Notably, the VLM jury assigns imperfect acceptance even to real adverse-condition imagery, establishing practical ceilings against which synthetic methods can be judged. By this standard, leading generative methods match or exceed real-image performance for most conditions.
  These results suggest that modern generative image-editing models can enable scalable generation of realistic adverse-condition imagery for evaluation pipelines. Our framework therefore provides a practical approach for scalable realism evaluation, though validation against human studies remains an important direction for future work.

AI.tldr: Generative AI-based image editing outperforms rule-based augmentation in realism for adverse-condition car imagery; a scalable framework using a vision-language model jury and embedding-based analysis shows generative methods approach or exceed real images for most conditions, with some ceilings and nighttime remaining challenging.
AI.motivation: Need scalable synthetic test data for evaluating AI systems under rare or safety-critical adverse conditions; assess realism of synthetic imagery to support meaningful evaluation.
AI.method: Apply a scalable realism-evaluation framework to adding adverse environmental conditions (fog, rain, snow, nighttime) to 40 clear-day car-mounted camera images. Compare rule-based augmentation libraries with generative AI image-editing models. Use two automated metrics: (1) a vision-language model jury for perceptual realism, (2) embedding-based distributional analysis comparing synthetic images to genuine adverse-condition imagery.
AI.result: Generative AI methods substantially outperform rule-based approaches; the best generative method yields ~3.6x higher acceptance than the best rule-based method. Fog is easier to simulate; nighttime transformations are more challenging. The VLM jury does not perfectly accept real adverse-condition images, indicating a practical realism ceiling. Overall, leading generative methods match or exceed real-image realism for most conditions.
AI.conclusion: Modern generative image-editing models enable scalable generation of realistic adverse-condition imagery for evaluation pipelines. The proposed framework provides a practical realism-evaluation approach, with validation against human studies remaining an important future direction.

title: Pointer-CAD: Unifying B-Rep and Command Sequences via Pointer-based Edges & Faces Selection
summary: Constructing computer-aided design (CAD) models is labor-intensive but essential for engineering and manufacturing. Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) have inspired the LLM-based CAD generation by representing CAD as command sequences. But these methods struggle in practical scenarios because command sequence representation does not support entity selection (e.g. faces or edges), limiting its ability to support complex editing operations such as chamfer or fillet. Further, the discretization of a continuous variable during sketch and extrude operations may result in topological errors. To address these limitations, we present Pointer-CAD, a novel LLM-based CAD generation framework that leverages a pointer-based command sequence representation to explicitly incorporate the geometric information of B-rep models into sequential modeling. In particular, Pointer-CAD decomposes CAD model generation into steps, conditioning the generation of each subsequent step on both the textual description and the B-rep generated from previous steps. Whenever an operation requires the selection of a specific geometric entity, the LLM predicts a Pointer that selects the most feature-consistent candidate from the available set. Such a selection operation also reduces the quantization error in the command sequence-based representation. To support the training of Pointer-CAD, we develop a data annotation pipeline that produces expert-level natural language descriptions and apply it to build a dataset of approximately 575K CAD models. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that Pointer-CAD effectively supports the generation of complex geometric structures and reduces segmentation error to an extremely low level, achieving a significant improvement over prior command sequence methods, thereby significantly mitigating the topological inaccuracies introduced by quantization error.

AI.tldr: Pointer-CAD introduces a pointer-based command sequence to embed geometric information from B-rep models into sequential CAD generation, enabling entity selection and reducing quantization/topology errors, and outperforms prior command-sequence methods on a large dataset.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-driven CAD pipelines rely on plain command sequences that cannot select specific geometric entities (faces/edges) and discretize continuous sketch/extrude steps, causing editing limitations and topological errors. There is a need to integrate explicit geometric information (B-rep) and entity selection into the generative process.
AI.method: Decompose CAD generation into iterative steps. Each step conditions on textual description and the B-rep from earlier steps. When an operation requires choosing a geometric entity, the LLM predicts a Pointer to select the most geometry-consistent candidate from a candidate set. This reduces quantization error and enables precise editing. A data annotation pipeline creates expert-level natural language descriptions, yielding a dataset of ~575k CAD models.
AI.result: Experimental results show Pointer-CAD effectively supports complex geometric structures and drastically lowers segmentation/topology errors compared to prior command-sequence approaches, significantly mitigating topological inaccuracies from quantization and achieving improved generation quality on a large dataset.
AI.conclusion: Pointer-CAD demonstrates that combining an LLM with a pointer-based, B-rep–aware representation enables precise, editable CAD generation and robust handling of complex features, offering substantial performance gains over traditional command-sequence methods.

title: ArtHOI: Articulated Human-Object Interaction Synthesis by 4D Reconstruction from Video Priors
summary: Synthesizing physically plausible articulated human-object interactions (HOI) without 3D/4D supervision remains a fundamental challenge. While recent zero-shot approaches leverage video diffusion models to synthesize human-object interactions, they are largely confined to rigid-object manipulation and lack explicit 4D geometric reasoning. To bridge this gap, we formulate articulated HOI synthesis as a 4D reconstruction problem from monocular video priors: given only a video generated by a diffusion model, we reconstruct a full 4D articulated scene without any 3D supervision. This reconstruction-based approach treats the generated 2D video as supervision for an inverse rendering problem, recovering geometrically consistent and physically plausible 4D scenes that naturally respect contact, articulation, and temporal coherence. We introduce ArtHOI, the first zero-shot framework for articulated human-object interaction synthesis via 4D reconstruction from video priors. Our key designs are: 1) Flow-based part segmentation: leveraging optical flow as a geometric cue to disentangle dynamic from static regions in monocular video; 2) Decoupled reconstruction pipeline: joint optimization of human motion and object articulation is unstable under monocular ambiguity, so we first recover object articulation, then synthesize human motion conditioned on the reconstructed object states. ArtHOI bridges video-based generation and geometry-aware reconstruction, producing interactions that are both semantically aligned and physically grounded. Across diverse articulated scenes (e.g., opening fridges, cabinets, microwaves), ArtHOI significantly outperforms prior methods in contact accuracy, penetration reduction, and articulation fidelity, extending zero-shot interaction synthesis beyond rigid manipulation through reconstruction-informed synthesis.

AI.tldr: ArtHOI: zero-shot articulated HOI synthesis via 4D reconstruction from monocular video priors; uses flow-based segmentation and a decoupled reconstruction pipeline to produce physically plausible 4D scenes, outperforming prior diffusion-based methods in contact accuracy, penetration reduction, and articulation fidelity.
AI.motivation: There is a fundamental challenge to synthesize physically plausible articulated human–object interactions (HOI) without 3D/4D supervision. Existing zero-shot methods rely on video diffusion models and are mostly limited to rigid objects and lack explicit 4D geometry reasoning; a reconstruction-based approach using monocular video priors can enforce contact, articulation, and temporal coherence.
AI.method: Formulate articulated HOI synthesis as a 4D reconstruction problem from monocular video priors generated by a diffusion model. Treat the 2D video as supervision for inverse rendering to recover geometrically consistent and physically plausible 4D scenes. Key components: (1) flow-based part segmentation using optical flow to separate dynamic vs static regions; (2) decoupled reconstruction pipeline that first recovers object articulation and then synthesizes human motion conditioned on the reconstructed object states.
AI.result: ArtHOI significantly improves contact accuracy, reduces interpenetration, and enhances articulation fidelity across diverse articulated scenes (e.g., opening fridges, cabinets, microwaves) compared to prior methods, demonstrating zero-shot articulation beyond rigid manipulation.
AI.conclusion: First zero-shot framework for articulated HOI synthesis via 4D reconstruction from video priors; demonstrates the viability of combining diffusion-based video generation with geometry-aware reconstruction to produce semantically aligned and physically grounded HOI, with potential for broader articulated interactions.

title: Balancing Fidelity, Utility, and Privacy in Synthetic Cardiac MRI Generation: A Comparative Study
summary: Deep learning in cardiac MRI (CMR) is fundamentally constrained by both data scarcity and privacy regulations. This study systematically benchmarks three generative architectures: Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM), Latent Diffusion Models (LDM), and Flow Matching (FM) for synthetic CMR generation. Utilizing a two-stage pipeline where anatomical masks condition image synthesis, we evaluate generated data across three critical axes: fidelity, utility, and privacy. Our results show that diffusion-based models, particularly DDPM, provide the most effective balance between downstream segmentation utility, image fidelity, and privacy preservation under limited-data conditions, while FM demonstrates promising privacy characteristics with slightly lower task-level performance. These findings quantify the trade-offs between cross-domain generalization and patient confidentiality, establishing a framework for safe and effective synthetic data augmentation in medical imaging.

AI.tldr: DDPM offers the strongest balance of image fidelity, downstream segmentation utility, and privacy preservation for synthetic CMR under data scarcity; FM yields better privacy but slightly lower task performance; LDM falls between or underperforms DDPM in this setup; the work provides a framework for safe, privacy-aware synthetic data augmentation in cardiac MRI.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity and privacy constraints in deep learning for cardiac MRI by evaluating privacy-preserving synthetic data generation methods.
AI.method: Two-stage pipeline using anatomical masks to condition image synthesis; systematic benchmarking of three generative architectures (DDPM, LDM, FM) for synthetic CMR; evaluation along three axes—fidelity, utility (downstream segmentation), and privacy—under limited-data conditions.
AI.result: Diffusion-based models (DDPM) give the best overall performance, achieving favorable trade-offs across fidelity, utility, and privacy; FM exhibits strong privacy characteristics with slightly lower task performance; LDM performance is not highlighted as superior; the study quantifies cross-domain generalization versus confidentiality and proposes a framework for safe synthetic data augmentation in medical imaging.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion models, particularly DDPM, are favored for safe, effective synthetic CMR under data constraints, offering a principled balance between data utility and patient privacy; the paper contributes a framework to compare privacy and utility trade-offs across generative architectures for medical imaging.

title: BeamPERL: Parameter-Efficient RL with Verifiable Rewards Specializes Compact LLMs for Structured Beam Mechanics Reasoning
summary: Can reinforcement learning with hard, verifiable rewards teach a compact language model to reason about physics, or does it primarily learn to pattern-match toward correct answers? We study this question by training a 1.5B-parameter reasoning model on beam statics, a classic engineering problem, using parameter-efficient RLVR with binary correctness rewards from symbolic solvers, without teacher-generated reasoning traces. The best BeamPERL checkpoint achieves a 66.7% improvement in Pass@1 over the base model. However, the learned competence is anisotropic: the model generalizes compositionally (more loads) but fails under topological shifts (moved supports) that require the same equilibrium equations. Intermediate checkpoints yield the strongest reasoning, while continued optimization degrades robustness while maintaining reward. These findings reveal a key limitation of outcome-level alignment: reinforcement learning with exact physics rewards induces procedural solution templates rather than internalization of governing equations. The precision of the reward signal - even when analytically exact - does not by itself guarantee transferable physical reasoning. Our results suggest that verifiable rewards may need to be paired with structured reasoning scaffolding to move beyond template matching toward robust scientific reasoning.

AI.tldr: RLVR with exact physics rewards improves Pass@1 but yields anisotropic, template-like reasoning; gains in composition but poor topological generalization; intermediate checkpoints best; rewards alone insufficient for robust physics reasoning; need reasoning scaffolding.
AI.motivation: Determine whether hard, verifiable rewards foster genuine physical reasoning in LMs or simply enable pattern matching; assess generalization to compositional versus topological changes in a classical physics problem.
AI.method: Train a 1.5B-parameter reasoning LM on beam statics using parameter-efficient RL with binary correctness rewards from symbolic solvers, without teacher traces; evaluate Pass@1 and generalization across loads and topology.
AI.result: BeamPERL yields ~66.7% improvement in Pass@1 over base; anisotropic generalization: better with more loads, worse with moved supports; intermediate checkpoints show strongest reasoning, continued optimization reduces robustness; reward-driven training does not internalize governing equations.
AI.conclusion: Outcome-level alignment with exact physics rewards tends to induce procedural templates; precise rewards do not guarantee transferable physical reasoning; verifiable rewards should be paired with structured reasoning scaffolding to foster robust scientific reasoning.

title: ZipMap: Linear-Time Stateful 3D Reconstruction with Test-Time Training
summary: Feed-forward transformer models have driven rapid progress in 3D vision, but state-of-the-art methods such as VGGT and $π^3$ have a computational cost that scales quadratically with the number of input images, making them inefficient when applied to large image collections. Sequential-reconstruction approaches reduce this cost but sacrifice reconstruction quality. We introduce ZipMap, a stateful feed-forward model that achieves linear-time, bidirectional 3D reconstruction while matching or surpassing the accuracy of quadratic-time methods. ZipMap employs test-time training layers to zip an entire image collection into a compact hidden scene state in a single forward pass, enabling reconstruction of over 700 frames in under 10 seconds on a single H100 GPU, more than $20\times$ faster than state-of-the-art methods such as VGGT. Moreover, we demonstrate the benefits of having a stateful representation in real-time scene-state querying and its extension to sequential streaming reconstruction.

AI.tldr: ZipMap is a stateful, linear-time 3D reconstruction model that compresses an entire image collection into a hidden scene state using test-time training layers, enabling bidirectional reconstruction and real-time querying with >20x speedups over VGGT while matching or surpassing accuracy.
AI.motivation: Current SOTA transformers (e.g., VGGT, π^3) for 3D vision scale quadratically with the number of input images, making large datasets slow. There is a need for efficient, real-time reconstruction and streaming capabilities without sacrificing quality.
AI.method: Introduce ZipMap, a stateful feed-forward model. It uses test-time training layers to zip an entire image collection into a compact hidden scene state in a single forward pass. Supports linear-time bidirectional reconstruction and real-time scene-state querying; extends to sequential streaming reconstruction.
AI.result: Demonstrates reconstruction of 700+ frames in under 10 seconds on a single H100 GPU, >20x faster than VGGT, with accuracy matching or surpassing quadratic-time methods.
AI.conclusion: Stateful representations enable practical real-time and streaming 3D reconstruction with substantial speedups and preserved accuracy; warrants further exploration of streaming pipelines and broader dataset scalability.

title: Hold-One-Shot-Out (HOSO) for Validation-Free Few-Shot CLIP Adapters
summary: In many CLIP adaptation methods, a blending ratio hyperparameter controls the trade-off between general pretrained CLIP knowledge and the limited, dataset-specific supervision from the few-shot cases. Most few-shot CLIP adaptation techniques report results by ablation of the blending ratio on the test set or require additional validation sets to select the blending ratio per dataset, and thus are not strictly few-shot. We present a simple, validation-free method for learning the blending ratio in CLIP adaptation. Hold-One-Shot-Out (HOSO) presents a novel approach for CLIP-Adapter-style methods to compete in the newly established validation-free setting. CLIP-Adapter with HOSO (HOSO-Adapter) learns the blending ratio using a one-shot, hold-out set, while the adapter trains on the remaining few-shot support examples. Under the validation-free few-shot protocol, HOSO-Adapter outperforms the CLIP-Adapter baseline by more than 4 percentage points on average across 11 standard few-shot datasets. Interestingly, in the 8- and 16-shot settings, HOSO-Adapter outperforms CLIP-Adapter even with the optimal blending ratio selected on the test set. Ablation studies validate the use of a one-shot hold-out mechanism, decoupled training, and improvements over the naively learnt blending ratio baseline. Code is released here: https://github.com/chris-vorster/HOSO-Adapter

AI.tldr: Proposes Hold-One-Shot-Out (HOSO) to learn the CLIP blending ratio without a validation split, improving CLIP-Adapter performance in few-shot settings.
AI.motivation: Existing few-shot CLIP adaptation relies on a blending ratio between pretrained CLIP knowledge and dataset-specific supervision. This ratio is typically tuned via validation data or ablated on the test set, undermining the strict few-shot setting.
AI.method: Introduce HOSO-Adapter: a CLIP-Adapter-style method that learns the blending ratio using a one-shot hold-out set while training the adapter on the remaining few-shot support examples. The approach uses decoupled training and validates the necessity of a one-shot hold-out via ablations.
AI.result: Under a validation-free few-shot protocol, HOSO-Adapter surpasses the CLIP-Adapter baseline by >4 percentage points on 11 standard datasets. In 8- and 16-shot regimes, it also outperforms the baseline even when the blending ratio is optimally chosen on the test set. Code is released.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Hold-One-Shot-Out approach provides an effective, validation-free mechanism to learn the blending ratio in CLIP adaptation, enabling better few-shot performance and robust decoupled training.

title: Enhancing Authorship Attribution with Synthetic Paintings
comment: Accepted for publication at the 24th IEEE International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA 2025)
summary: Attributing authorship to paintings is a historically complex task, and one of its main challenges is the limited availability of real artworks for training computational models. This study investigates whether synthetic images, generated through DreamBooth fine-tuning of Stable Diffusion, can improve the performance of classification models in this context. We propose a hybrid approach that combines real and synthetic data to enhance model accuracy and generalization across similar artistic styles. Experimental results show that adding synthetic images leads to higher ROC-AUC and accuracy compared to using only real paintings. By integrating generative and discriminative methods, this work contributes to the development of computer vision techniques for artwork authentication in data-scarce scenarios.

AI.tldr: Synthetic data via DreamBooth-enhanced Stable Diffusion improves author attribution in paintings by combining real and synthetic images, yielding higher ROC-AUC and accuracy in data-scarce settings.
AI.motivation: Tackle the scarcity of authentic artworks for training author-attribution models and investigate whether synthetic data can boost discriminative performance and generalization across similar styles.
AI.method: Generate synthetic paintings by DreamBooth fine-tuning of Stable Diffusion to resemble target artists/styles; combine synthetic and real paintings for training a classifier; evaluate performance using ROC-AUC and accuracy, comparing against real-only baselines and assessing generalization.
AI.result: Augmenting training with synthetic images improves ROC-AUC and accuracy compared to real-only training, indicating that integrating generative and discriminative approaches benefits artwork authentication under data scarcity.
AI.conclusion: A hybrid real+synthetic data strategy is promising for author attribution in paintings, enhancing performance and generalization and contributing to data-efficient computer vision methods for art authentication.

title: Underrepresented in Foundation Model Pretraining Data? A One-Shot Probe
summary: Large-scale Vision-Language Foundation Models (VLFMs), such as CLIP, now underpin a wide range of computer vision research and applications. VLFMs are often adapted to various domain-specific tasks. However, VLFM performance on novel, specialised, or underrepresented domains remains inconsistent. Evaluating VLFMs typically requires labelled test sets, which are often unavailable for niche domains of interest, particularly those from the Global South. We address this gap by proposing a highly data-efficient method to predict a VLFM's zero-shot accuracy on a target domain using only a single labelled image per class. Our approach uses a Large Language Model to generate plausible counterfactual descriptions of a given image. By measuring the VLFM's ability to distinguish the correct description from these hard negatives, we engineer features that capture the VLFM's discriminative power in its shared embedding space. A linear regressor trained on these similarity scores estimates the VLFM's zero-shot test accuracy across various visual domains with a Pearson-r correlation of 0.96. We demonstrate our method's performance across five diverse datasets, including standard benchmark datasets and underrepresented datasets from Africa. Our work provides a low-cost, reliable tool for probing VLFMs, enabling researchers and practitioners to make informed decisions about data annotation efforts before committing significant resources. The model training code, generated captions and counterfactuals are released here: https://github.com/chris-vorster/PreLabellingProbe.

AI.tldr: A data-efficient method to predict a VLFM's zero-shot accuracy on a target domain using one labeled image per class, leveraging LLM-generated counterfactual descriptions and a linear regressor; achieves Pearson r=0.96 across five diverse datasets including Africa.
AI.motivation: Addresses lack of labelled test sets for niche or Global South domains and the need to estimate VLFM performance with minimal data, enabling cost-effective annotation decisions.
AI.method: For each class with a single labeled image, use a Large Language Model to generate plausible counterfactual captions of the image. Measure the VLFM's discriminative power by how well it distinguishes the correct caption from hard negatives. Extract features from embedding-space similarity scores and train a linear regressor to predict zero-shot accuracy; evaluate across multiple domains.
AI.result: The approach achieves a Pearson correlation of 0.96 between predicted and actual zero-shot accuracy across five diverse datasets, including Africa; demonstrates data-efficient prediction of VLFM performance; code and generated captions/counterfactuals released.
AI.conclusion: Provides a low-cost, reliable probe for VLFMs, guiding data annotation decisions prior to large-scale labeling.

title: FocusGraph: Graph-Structured Frame Selection for Embodied Long Video Question Answering
summary:
The ability to understand long videos is vital for embodied intelligent agents, because their effectiveness depends on how well they can accumulate, organize, and leverage long-horizon perceptual memories. Recently, multimodal LLMs have been gaining popularity for solving the long video understanding task due to their general ability to understand natural language and to leverage world knowledge. However, as the number of frames provided to an MLLM increases, the quality of its responses tends to degrade, and inference time grows. Therefore, when using MLLMs for long video understanding, a crucial step is selecting key frames from the video to answer user queries.
  In this work, we develop FocusGraph, a framework for keyframe selection for question answering over long egocentric videos. It leverages a lightweight trainable Scene-Caption LLM Selector that selects query-relevant clips based on their graph-based captions, and a training-free method for selecting keyframes from these clips. Unlike existing methods, the proposed Scene-Caption LLM Selector does not rely on the original sequence of low-resolution frames; instead, it operates on a compact textual representation of the scene. We then design a training-free Patch-wise Sparse-Flow Retention (PSFR) method to select keyframes from the resulting sequence of clips, which are fed into an MLLM to produce the final answer. Together, these components enable FocusGraph to achieve state-of-the-art results on challenging egocentric long-video question answering benchmarks, including FindingDory and HourVideo, while significantly reducing inference time relative to baseline approaches.

AI.tldr: FocusGraph introduces a two-stage keyframe selection for long video QA: a lightweight Scene-Caption LLM Selector operating on textual scene representations, and a training-free Patch-wise Sparse-Flow Retention (PSFR) for keyframe sampling, achieving state-of-the-art results on egocentric long-video QA benchmarks with reduced inference time.
AI.motivation: Long videos challenge multimodal LLMs due to memory/computation constraints; efficient keyframe selection is essential for accurate long-horizon understanding in embodied agents.
AI.method: Two components: (1) Scene-Caption LLM Selector that uses graph-based captions to select query-relevant clips without relying on the raw low-resolution frame sequence; (2) PSFR, a training-free method to extract keyframes from the selected clips; final answers are produced by an MLLM.
AI.result: State-of-the-art on egocentric long-video QA benchmarks (FindingDory, HourVideo); substantial reduction in inference time compared with baselines.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that compact textual scene representations combined with a training-free keyframe retention strategy can yield accurate and efficient long-video QA, with potential applicability to broader long-horizon perception tasks.

title: Helios: Real Real-Time Long Video Generation Model
summary: We introduce Helios, the first 14B video generation model that runs at 19.5 FPS on a single NVIDIA H100 GPU and supports minute-scale generation while matching the quality of a strong baseline. We make breakthroughs along three key dimensions: (1) robustness to long-video drifting without commonly used anti-drifting heuristics such as self-forcing, error-banks, or keyframe sampling; (2) real-time generation without standard acceleration techniques such as KV-cache, sparse/linear attention, or quantization; and (3) training without parallelism or sharding frameworks, enabling image-diffusion-scale batch sizes while fitting up to four 14B models within 80 GB of GPU memory. Specifically, Helios is a 14B autoregressive diffusion model with a unified input representation that natively supports T2V, I2V, and V2V tasks. To mitigate drifting in long-video generation, we characterize typical failure modes and propose simple yet effective training strategies that explicitly simulate drifting during training, while eliminating repetitive motion at its source. For efficiency, we heavily compress the historical and noisy context and reduce the number of sampling steps, yielding computational costs comparable to -- or lower than -- those of 1.3B video generative models. Moreover, we introduce infrastructure-level optimizations that accelerate both inference and training while reducing memory consumption. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Helios consistently outperforms prior methods on both short- and long-video generation. We plan to release the code, base model, and distilled model to support further development by the community.

AI.tldr: Helios is a 14B autoregressive diffusion model for video generation that runs at 19.5 FPS on a single NVIDIA H100, enabling minute-scale generation with quality matching a strong baseline, and introduces drift-robust training and efficiency improvements to support T2V, I2V, and V2V without standard acceleration techniques.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in real-time, long-video generation with high-quality output, addressing drifting artifacts and computational bottlenecks that limit larger models from single-GPU deployment.
AI.method: A 14B autoregressive diffusion model with a unified input representation supporting text-to-video (T2V), image-to-video (I2V), and video-to-video (V2V). It mitigates drifting by training strategies that simulate drift and remove repetitive motion, compresses historical context, reduces sampling steps, and applies infrastructure-level optimizations. It achieves real-time generation without KV-cache, sparse/linear attention, or quantization, and trains without parallelism or sharding, enabling large batch sizes and running multiple 14B models within 80 GB.
AI.result: Empirical results show Helios consistently outperforms prior methods on both short- and long-video generation, achieving 19.5 FPS on a single H100, minute-scale generation with quality comparable to strong baselines, and memory-efficient training enabling four 14B models within 80 GB. Code, base, and distilled models are planned for release.
AI.conclusion: Helios establishes a new capability frontier for high-quality, real-time long-video generation on commodity multi-GPU hardware (single-GPU in this case) by combining drift-robust training, aggressive efficiency optimizations, and a unified 14B diffusion framework, with an emphasis on accessibility via planned public releases.

title: TaxonRL: Reinforcement Learning with Intermediate Rewards for Interpretable Fine-Grained Visual Reasoning
summary: Traditional vision-language models struggle with contrastive fine-grained taxonomic reasoning, particularly when distinguishing between visually similar species within the same genus or family. We introduce TaxonRL, a reinforcement learning approach using Group Relative Policy Optimization with intermediate rewards that decomposes the reasoning process into hierarchical taxonomic predictions. Our method incentivizes models to explicitly reason about species-level, genus-level, and family-level features before making final classifications. This structured approach is designed not only to boost accuracy but also to yield a transparent, verifiable decision-making process. On the challenging Birds-to-Words dataset, TaxonRL achieves 91.7\% average accuracy, exceeding human performance (77.3\%) while generating interpretable reasoning traces. We demonstrate strong cross-domain generalization, showing substantial gains in primate and marine species verification. Our results establish that enforcing structured, hierarchical reasoning provides a powerful and transferable framework for fine-grained visual discrimination.

AI.tldr: TaxonRL uses reinforcement learning with hierarchical taxonomic reasoning (species/genus/family) via Group Relative Policy Optimization and intermediate rewards to improve fine-grained visual discrimination, yielding interpretable traces and state-of-the-art accuracy.
AI.motivation: Fill gaps in vision-language models for fine-grained taxonomic reasoning and interpretability; enable transparent decision-making and better transferability across related species.
