AI.method: Group Relative Policy Optimization with intermediate rewards that decompose classification into sequential species-, genus-, and family-level predictions before final decision; produces interpretable reasoning traces; evaluated across birds (Birds-to-Words) and cross-domain taxa.
AI.result: Achieves 91.7% average accuracy on Birds-to-Words, surpassing human performance at 77.3%; provides interpretable traces; shows strong cross-domain generalization to primates and marine species.
AI.conclusion: Structured, hierarchical reasoning enhances fine-grained visual discrimination and yields transferable, transparent models that can outperform humans on specific datasets.

title: SimpliHuMoN: Simplifying Human Motion Prediction
summary: Human motion prediction combines the tasks of trajectory forecasting and human pose prediction. For each of the two tasks, specialized models have been developed. Combining these models for holistic human motion prediction is non-trivial, and recent methods have struggled to compete on established benchmarks for individual tasks. To address this, we propose a simple yet effective transformer-based model for human motion prediction. The model employs a stack of self-attention modules to effectively capture both spatial dependencies within a pose and temporal relationships across a motion sequence. This simple, streamlined, end-to-end model is sufficiently versatile to handle pose-only, trajectory-only, and combined prediction tasks without task-specific modifications. We demonstrate that this approach achieves state-of-the-art results across all tasks through extensive experiments on a wide range of benchmark datasets, including Human3.6M, AMASS, ETH-UCY, and 3DPW.

AI.tldr: A transformer-based, end-to-end model unifies pose and trajectory prediction for holistic human motion forecasting, achieving state-of-the-art results across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches separate pose and trajectory prediction, making holistic motion forecasting challenging. A unified, simple transformer could capture both intra-pose spatial structure and inter-frame temporal dynamics more effectively.
AI.method: A stack of self-attention modules (transformer) is used to model spatial dependencies within a pose and temporal relationships across a motion sequence. The model is end-to-end and can handle pose-only, trajectory-only, and combined predictions without task-specific modifications.
AI.result: The approach achieves state-of-the-art performance across pose, trajectory, and combined prediction tasks on standard benchmarks, including Human3.6M, AMASS, ETH-UCY, and 3DPW.
AI.conclusion: A simple, versatile transformer-based framework can unify holistic human motion prediction and deliver strong results across diverse tasks without task-specific adjustments.

title: Impact of Localization Errors on Label Quality for Online HD Map Construction
comment: Accepted for the 36th IEEE Intelligent Vehicles Symposium (IV 2025), 8 pages
summary: High-definition (HD) maps are crucial for autonomous vehicles, but their creation and maintenance is very costly. This motivates the idea of online HD map construction. To provide a continuous large-scale stream of training data, existing HD maps can be used as labels for onboard sensor data from consumer vehicle fleets. However, compared to current, well curated HD map perception datasets, this fleet data suffers from localization errors, resulting in distorted map labels. We introduce three kinds of localization errors, Ramp, Gaussian, and Perlin noise, to examine their influence on generated map labels. We train a variant of MapTRv2, a state-of-the-art online HD map construction model, on the Argoverse 2 dataset with various levels of localization errors and assess the degradation of model performance. Since localization errors affect distant labels more severely, but are also less significant to driving performance, we introduce a distance-based map construction metric. Our experiments reveal that localization noise affects the model performance significantly. We demonstrate that errors in heading angle exert a more substantial influence than position errors, as angle errors result in a greater distortion of labels as distance to the vehicle increases. Furthermore, we can demonstrate that the model benefits from non-distorted ground truth (GT) data and that the performance decreases more than linearly with the increase in noisy data. Our study additionally provides a qualitative evaluation of the extent to which localization errors influence the construction of HD maps.

AI.tldr: This work analyzes how localization noise in fleet-based labels affects online HD map construction with MapTRv2 on Argoverse 2. It injects three error types (Ramp, Gaussian, Perlin) to study their impact, introduces a distance-based evaluation metric, and finds that heading errors significantly distort distant labels, causing non-linear performance degradation—even with more noisy data—while GT data remains beneficial.
AI.motivation: HD maps are expensive to create and maintain. Online construction using consumer-vehicle fleets could provide scalable labels, but localization errors contaminate labels. Understanding and quantifying this impact is crucial for robust online HD map learning.
AI.method: Simulate localization errors by adding Ramp, Gaussian, and Perlin noise to ground-truth vehicle poses. Train a MapTRv2-based online HD map construction model on the Argoverse 2 dataset under varying noise levels. Introduce a distance-based map construction metric to reflect label distortion with distance and perform qualitative analyses of label degradation.
AI.result: Localization noise substantially degrades model performance. Heading (angle) errors have a larger impact than position errors as distance increases. Distortion grows with distance; the effect of noise on performance is nonlinear and more pronounced with more noisy data.GT data improves performance and non-distorted data is beneficial; qualitative evaluation confirms the extent of label degradation due to localization errors.
AI.conclusion: Accurate heading information is critical for reliable online HD map construction. The study highlights the need for noise-robust methods, data cleaning, or better labeling pipelines, and supports using a distance-based metric to evaluate label quality. GT data remains valuable for improving performance.

title: Structural Action Transformer for 3D Dexterous Manipulation
summary: Achieving human-level dexterity in robots via imitation learning from heterogeneous datasets is hindered by the challenge of cross-embodiment skill transfer, particularly for high-DoF robotic hands. Existing methods, often relying on 2D observations and temporal-centric action representation, struggle to capture 3D spatial relations and fail to handle embodiment heterogeneity. This paper proposes the Structural Action Transformer (SAT), a new 3D dexterous manipulation policy that challenges this paradigm by introducing a structural-centric perspective. We reframe each action chunk not as a temporal sequence, but as a variable-length, unordered sequence of joint-wise trajectories. This structural formulation allows a Transformer to natively handle heterogeneous embodiments, treating the joint count as a variable sequence length. To encode structural priors and resolve ambiguity, we introduce an Embodied Joint Codebook that embeds each joint's functional role and kinematic properties. Our model learns to generate these trajectories from 3D point clouds via a continuous-time flow matching objective. We validate our approach by pre-training on large-scale heterogeneous datasets and fine-tuning on simulation and real-world dexterous manipulation tasks. Our method consistently outperforms all baselines, demonstrating superior sample efficiency and effective cross-embodiment skill transfer. This structural-centric representation offers a new path toward scaling policies for high-DoF, heterogeneous manipulators.

AI.tldr: SAT introduces a 3D dexterous manipulation policy that uses a structural, variable-length, unordered sequence of joint trajectories, encoded by an Embodied Joint Codebook and learned from 3D point clouds via a continuous-time flow matching objective; it enables cross-embodiment transfer and improves sample efficiency on sim and real tasks.
AI.motivation: Cross-embodiment skill transfer for high-DoF manipulators is hampered by reliance on 2D observations and temporal action representations that struggle with 3D spatial relations and varying joint counts.
AI.method: Propose Structural Action Transformer (SAT) with a structural-centric action chunk: a variable-length, unordered sequence of joint trajectories. A Transformer processes varying joint counts; an Embodied Joint Codebook encodes each joint's functional role and kinematic properties. Trajectories are learned from 3D point clouds using a continuous-time flow matching objective. Pre-train on large heterogeneous datasets and fine-tune on simulation and real-world dexterous tasks.
AI.result: SAT consistently outperforms baselines, offering superior sample efficiency and effective cross-embodiment skill transfer across simulated and real dexterous manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: A structural-centric representation provides a scalable path to policies for high-DoF, heterogeneous manipulators and enables more robust cross-embodiment transfer.

title: Phi-4-reasoning-vision-15B Technical Report
summary: We present Phi-4-reasoning-vision-15B, a compact open-weight multimodal reasoning model, and share the motivations, design choices, experiments, and learnings that informed its development. Our goal is to contribute practical insight to the research community on building smaller, efficient multimodal reasoning models and to share the result of these learnings as an open-weight model that is good at common vision and language tasks and excels at scientific and mathematical reasoning and understanding user interfaces. Our contributions include demonstrating that careful architecture choices and rigorous data curation enable smaller, open-weight multimodal models to achieve competitive performance with significantly less training and inference-time compute and tokens. The most substantial improvements come from systematic filtering, error correction, and synthetic augmentation -- reinforcing that data quality remains the primary lever for model performance. Systematic ablations show that high-resolution, dynamic-resolution encoders yield consistent improvements, as accurate perception is a prerequisite for high-quality reasoning. Finally, a hybrid mix of reasoning and non-reasoning data with explicit mode tokens allows a single model to deliver fast direct answers for simpler tasks and chain-of-thought reasoning for complex problems.

AI.tldr: A compact open-weight multimodal reasoning model (Phi-4-reasoning-vision-15B) demonstrates that careful architecture choices and rigorous data curation enable smaller models to rival larger models with significantly reduced training and inference compute. Data quality, via systematic filtering, error correction, and synthetic augmentation, is the primary performance lever. High-resolution/dynamic-resolution encoders and a hybrid data mix with explicit mode tokens enable fast direct answers for simple tasks and chain-of-thought reasoning for complex problems.
AI.motivation: Address the need for practical, smaller, efficient multimodal reasoning models that are open-weight, competitive on vision-language tasks, and capable of scientific/mathematical reasoning and user-interface understanding. Provide actionable insights on architecture and data practices that scale down compute while preserving reasoning performance.
AI.method: Systematic data curation and augmentation (filtering, error correction, synthetic data), high-resolution and dynamic-resolution encoders, and a hybrid training mix that includes both reasoning and non-reasoning data with explicit mode tokens to support both fast direct answers and chain-of-thought reasoning.
AI.result: Demonstrates competitive performance with substantially less training and inference-time compute and tokens compared to larger baselines; highlights data quality as the main lever for performance; shows consistent improvements from high-resolution encoders; validates the feasibility of a single model performing both fast answers and chain-of-thought reasoning via mode tokens.
AI.conclusion: Architectural choices and rigorous data curation enable smaller open-weight multimodal models to perform competitively; high-quality perception is foundational for reasoning; a unified model can adapt to task difficulty by switching between fast direct answers and chain-of-thought reasoning through explicit mode tokens.
